topics.title: Introduction (Overview of Network Security Issues, Cryptographic Algorithms, Authentication Techniques)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT633 - SECURE COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction (Overview of Network Security Issues, Cryptographic Algorithms, Authentication Techniques)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Network Security — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Network Security** is the discipline of engineering protective measures to prevent, detect, and recover from unauthorized access, misuse, modification, or denial of a computer network and its accessible resources. It is formally defined in **ITU-T X.800** as the set of mechanisms, services, and policies that ensure confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data in transit across communication networks.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Postal Mail System

Imagine sending a registered letter through the postal system. Network security works in the same way:

| Real-World Post System | Network Security Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Sealed tamper-proof envelope | **Encryption** of the message payload |
| Registered post receipt with signature | **Digital Signature** & non-repudiation |
| Verified identity at the post office | **Authentication** of the sender/receiver |
| Locked mailbox with a key | **Access Control Lists (ACLs)** and firewalls |
| Tracking number & insurance | **Integrity checks** (Hashes/MACs) |
| Backup courier if the postman fails | **Redundancy & Availability** mechanisms |

Without these services, an adversary (a "man-in-the-middle" like a corrupt postal worker) could read, copy, alter, or destroy your message — which is precisely the threat model that cryptography and security protocols are designed to neutralize.

---

## 1.2 The CIA Triad — The Trinity of Security

Every security problem, regardless of complexity, ultimately boils down to preserving three fundamental properties known as the **CIA Triad** (formally endorsed by the KTU 2024 syllabus):

> [!NOTE]
> **CIA Triad Definition (X.800 / RFC 4949):**
> 1. **Confidentiality** — Ensures that information is disclosed only to authorized parties.
> 2. **Integrity** — Guarantees that data is not altered or destroyed in an unauthorized manner.
> 3. **Availability** — Ensures that systems and data are accessible to authorized users when required.

Extended properties that the syllabus also highlights:
- **Authentication** — Verification of the claimed identity of an entity.
- **Authorization** — Granting of access rights based on identity.
- **Non-Repudiation** — Prevents an entity from denying previous commitments or actions.
- **Accountability** — Tracing actions uniquely to an entity.

---

## 1.3 Overview of Network Security Issues

A **security issue / threat** is any potential circumstance, capability, action, or event that can breach security and cause harm. Security issues are categorized along two axes: **passive vs. active** attacks and **threats vs. vulnerabilities vs. attacks**.

### Threats, Vulnerabilities, and Attacks (KTU High-Yield Distinction)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Threat** = A potential danger of a security breach (e.g., a hacker on the network).
> - **Vulnerability** = A weakness in the system that can be exploited (e.g., unpatched OS).
> - **Attack** = The actual act of exploiting a vulnerability to breach security.

### Passive Attacks (Eavesdropping Class)
These attacks **observe or monitor** transmissions without altering them. They are hard to detect because they leave data intact.

- **Release of message content:** Adversary reads the plaintext (e.g., intercepted email).
- **Traffic analysis:** Adversary observes the pattern, frequency, length, and source/destination of messages to infer information, even if content is encrypted.

### Active Attacks (Modification Class)
These attacks involve **modification of the data stream** or creation of a false stream. They are detectable but hard to prevent absolutely.

- **Masquerade:** An entity pretends to be a different entity (e.g., IP spoofing).
- **Replay:** Passive capture and retransmission of a valid message to produce an unauthorized effect.
- **Modification of messages:** Partial alteration of a legitimate message (e.g., man-in-the-middle).
- **Denial of Service (DoS / DDoS):** Disruption of an entire system; e.g., SYN flood, Smurf attack, Ping of Death.

---

## 1.4 Cryptographic Algorithms — Formal Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cryptography** is the science of transforming intelligible information (plaintext) into an unintelligible form (ciphertext) using a mathematical transformation and a secret value (key), in order to provide confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation over an insecure channel.

### The Five Pillars of Cryptographic Functions (KTU Module 1 Focus)

1. **Symmetric-Key Encryption** — Same key for encryption and decryption (e.g., AES, DES, 3DES).
2. **Asymmetric-Key Encryption** — Different mathematical keys: public key for encryption, private key for decryption (e.g., RSA, ECC).
3. **Hash Functions** — One-way functions producing a fixed-size digest (e.g., SHA-256, MD5).
4. **Message Authentication Codes (MAC)** — Symmetric-key integrity check (e.g., HMAC-SHA256).
5. **Digital Signatures** — Asymmetric analog of MAC providing non-repudiation (e.g., RSA-PSS, ECDSA, EdDSA).

### Conceptual Analogy — Locking a Treasure Chest

Think of cryptography as locking a chest:
- **Symmetric-key** = You and your friend share the *same physical key* (must exchange it secretly beforehand).
- **Asymmetric-key** = You publish an *open padlock* on the noticeboard; anyone can lock the chest, but only you (with the private key) can open it.
- **Hash** = A unique fingerprint of the chest's contents; if anyone tampers with the chest, the fingerprint changes.
- **Digital Signature** = You sign the chest with your private seal; anyone with your *public seal verification card* can verify you signed it.

---

## 1.5 Authentication Techniques — Formal Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Authentication** is the security service whose primary purpose is to verify the identity of a communicating entity (peer-entity authentication) or the source of a data unit (data-origin authentication), thereby providing assurance that the entity is what it claims to be.

### Conceptual Analogy — Airport Security Checkpoint

Authentication in networks is exactly like airport security:
1. You present an **identity document** (something you *have*).
2. You type in a **password or PIN** (something you *know*).
3. The system may also check your **fingerprint or face** (something you *are*).

If any one (or a combination) of these checks passes, you are authenticated. In networks, this happens digitally via cryptographic protocols.

### The Three Authentication Factors (Knowledge-Based, Possession-Based, Biometric)

- **Type-1: Something You Know** — Passwords, PINs, passphrases, security questions.
- **Type-2: Something You Have** — Smart cards, hardware tokens (RSA SecurID), mobile OTPs.
- **Type-3: Something You Are** — Biometrics: fingerprints, iris, voice, facial geometry.

**Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)** combines two or more factors for stronger security.

### Common Authentication Techniques in Practice

| Technique | Class | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Password Authentication | Type-1 | User submits a secret string |
| Challenge-Response | Cryptographic | Server sends nonce; user proves knowledge of secret via computation |
| Token-Based (OTP/TOTP) | Type-2 | One-time codes generated by hardware/software tokens |
| Biometric | Type-3 | Physiological or behavioral characteristic matching |
| Kerberos (Ticket-Based) | Cryptographic | Trusted third-party symmetric-key ticket system (used in Windows AD) |
| Public-Key (Certificate) | Asymmetric | Mutual authentication via X.509 certificates and PKI |
| Zero-Knowledge Proofs | Advanced | Prover convinces verifier without revealing the secret |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The OSI Security Architecture (X.800 Framework)

The **ITU-T Recommendation X.800 (1991)** — adopted by KTU as the canonical reference — defines security in three hierarchical layers. This framework is mandatory in Module 1 and frequently tested.

### Layer 1: Security Attack
Any action that **compromises** the security of information owned by an organization. (Discussed in §1.3.)

### Layer 2: Security Mechanism
A **process or device** designed to detect, prevent, or recover from a security attack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Eight Specific Security Mechanisms (X.800):**
> 1. **Encipherment** — Use of mathematical algorithms to transform data (the core of cryptography).
> 2. **Digital Signature** — Appends a cryptographic value to data for source verification.
> 3. **Access Control** — Enforcement of authorization rules (RBAC, ABAC, DAC, MAC).
> 4. **Data Integrity** — Hash/MAC-based detection of message alteration.
> 5. **Authentication Exchange** — Two-party message exchange proving identity.
> 6. **Traffic Padding** — Insertion of bits to mask traffic-flow patterns.
> 7. **Routing Control** — Selecting secure routes for certain data.
> 8. **Notarization** — Trusted third party validates communication.

### Layer 3: Security Service
A **service that enhances the security** of data processing systems and information transfers, using one or more mechanisms.

The five core X.800 security services:
1. **Data Confidentiality** — Protection against unauthorized disclosure.
2. **Data Integrity** — Detection of modification, insertion, deletion, or replay.
3. **Authentication** — Assurance of identity (peer or data-origin).
4. **Non-Repudiation** — Protection against false denial by either sender or receiver.
5. **Access Control** — Prevention of unauthorized use of resources.

Additional context services: **Availability**, **Accountability**.

---

## 2.2 Cryptographic Algorithm Classification (Module 1 Core)

### Symmetric-Key (Secret-Key) Cryptography
- **Same secret key K** used for both encryption $E_K$ and decryption $D_K$.
- Mathematical representation:

$$C = E_K(P) \quad \text{and} \quad P = D_K(C)$$

- Sub-classes:
  - **Block Ciphers** — Operate on fixed-size blocks (e.g., AES uses 128-bit blocks).
  - **Stream Ciphers** — Operate on one bit/byte at a time (e.g., RC4, ChaCha20).

### Asymmetric-Key (Public-Key) Cryptography
- Each user has a **key pair** $(K_{pub}, K_{priv})$ mathematically linked.
- The relationship:

$$C = E_{K_{pub}}(P) \quad \text{and} \quad P = D_{K_{priv}}(C)$$

- Mathematical primitives:
  - **Integer Factorization** (RSA)
  - **Discrete Logarithm Problem** (Diffie-Hellman, ElGamal, DSA)
  - **Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm** (ECC, ECDSA)

### Cryptographic Hash Functions
A hash function $H$ maps an arbitrary-length input $M$ to a fixed-length output $h$:

$$h = H(M), \quad h \in \{0,1\}^n$$

For cryptographic use, $H$ must satisfy:
- **Pre-image resistance** — Given $h$, infeasible to find $M$ such that $H(M)=h$.
- **Second pre-image resistance** — Given $M_1$, infeasible to find $M_2 \neq M_1$ with $H(M_1)=H(M_2)$.
- **Collision resistance** — Infeasible to find any $M_1 \neq M_2$ with $H(M_1)=H(M_2)$.

### The Famous Caesar Cipher — A Foundational Worked Model

The Caesar cipher is the simplest introduction to encryption. Each plaintext letter is shifted by a fixed key $K$:

$$C = (P + K) \bmod 26$$

$$P = (C - K) \bmod 26$$

For example, with $K = 3$ (the original Caesar shift), the plaintext **"HELLO"** becomes **"KHOOR"**.

---

## 2.3 Authentication Techniques — Theoretical Layering

### Password-Based Authentication (Simplest)
The user transmits the password to the server (often hashed over TLS):

$$\text{Client} \xrightarrow{H(\text{pwd})} \text{Server}$$

**Threats:** Replay attacks, offline dictionary attacks, shoulder-surfing, credential stuffing.

### Challenge-Response Authentication (Cryptographic)
To prevent replay, the server sends a **random nonce** $N$ that the client must transform using a shared secret $K$:

$$\text{Server} \rightarrow \text{Client}: \quad N$$

$$\text{Client} \rightarrow \text{Server}: \quad f_K(N)$$

Common forms:
- **Using symmetric key:** $f_K(N) = E_K(N)$ (e.g., in Kerberos pre-authentication).
- **Using hash:** $f_K(N) = H(K \mid\mid N)$ (used in MS-CHAPv2).
- **Using public key:** $f_{K_{priv}}(N) = \text{Sign}_{K_{priv}}(N)$ (e.g., TLS client certificates).

### One-Time Password (OTP) — TOTP / HOTP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HOTP (RFC 4226):** $\text{OTP} = \text{Truncate}(\text{HMAC-SHA1}(K, C))$, where $C$ is an 8-byte counter.
> **TOTP (RFC 6238):** $\text{OTP} = \text{Truncate}(\text{HMAC-SHA1}(K, T))$, where $T = \lfloor (t - t_0) / X \rfloor$ and $X$ is the time step (usually 30 s).

This is the algorithm behind Google Authenticator, Authy, and most banking tokens.

### Zero-Knowledge Proof Concept (Theoretical Introduction)
A zero-knowledge proof allows a prover $P$ to convince a verifier $V$ that they know a secret $s$ **without revealing any information about $s$**. It must satisfy:
- **Completeness** — If $P$ knows $s$, $V$ is convinced.
- **Soundness** — If $P$ does not know $s$, $V$ cannot be fooled.
- **Zero-knowledge** — $V$ learns nothing about $s$ beyond the truth of the statement.

Examples in KTU syllabus context: **Fiat-Shamir**, **Feige-Fiat-Shamir**, **Schnorr**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula a KTU 2024 student is expected to know for Module 1 introductory problems. Use the `\vert` notation — never the literal pipe — for absolute value or set-membership operations inside tables.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Caesar Cipher Encryption | $C = (P + K) \bmod 26$ | Classical encryption |
| 2 | Caesar Cipher Decryption | $P = (C - K) \bmod 26$ | Classical decryption |
| 3 | Modular Inverse | $a \cdot a^{-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ | RSA, ECC math |
| 4 | Euler's Totient | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ for $n = pq$ | RSA key gen |
| 5 | Fermat's Little Theorem | $a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ | Primality tests |
| 6 | RSA Encryption | $C = M^{e} \bmod n$ | Public-key encryption |
| 7 | RSA Decryption | $M = C^{d} \bmod n$ | Private-key decryption |
| 8 | RSA Key Constraint | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | Public/Private pair |
| 9 | Hash Function | $h = H(M), \quad h \in \{0,1\}^{n}$ | Integrity, signatures |
| 10 | Birthday Bound (Collisions) | $\sqrt{2^{n}} = 2^{n/2}$ trials | MD5/SHA1 security |
| 11 | Entropy of Password | $H = \log_2(S^{L})$ bits | Password strength |
| 12 | TOTP Time Step | $T = \lfloor (t - t_0) / X \rfloor$ | OTP generation |
| 13 | Information Leakage Bound | $I(M;C) \le H(K)$ | Shannon's perfect secrecy |
| 14 | Perfect Secrecy (Shannon) | $\Pr(M \mid C) = \Pr(M)$ | One-time pad property |
| 15 | Avalanche Effect | $\approx 50\%$ bits flip per input bit | Hash/Block cipher quality |
| 16 | Authentication Bit Cost | $\ge \log_2(S)$ bits for alphabet size $S$ | Min entropy for identity |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **TLS 1.3 handshake** uses challenge-response with Diffie-Hellman + RSA signatures.
- **Banking apps** use TOTP for transaction authorization (Layer 2 of authentication).
- **Blockchain** uses SHA-256 + ECDSA for transaction integrity and signing.
- **e-Governance (India's DigiLocker, Aadhaar)** uses PKI with X.509 certificates.
- **5G AKA (Authentication and Key Agreement)** uses challenge-response with symmetric keys on USIM cards.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Worked Example — Caesar Cipher (Full Round-Trip)

We now walk through every step of an end-to-end Caesar cipher to demonstrate the encryption-decryption cycle, exactly as expected in a KTU 14-mark problem.

### Given
- Plaintext: **"ATTACKATDAWN"** (12 letters)
- Key: **$K = 7$** (shift by 7 positions)
- Alphabet: English uppercase A–Z, mapped $A=0, B=1, \dots, Z=25$

### Step 1: Map each plaintext letter to its numeric value
$A \to 0$, $T \to 19$, $T \to 19$, $A \to 0$, $C \to 2$, $K \to 10$, $A \to 0$, $T \to 19$, $D \to 3$, $A \to 0$, $W \to 22$, $N \to 13$

So the numeric plaintext vector is:

$$P = [0, 19, 19, 0, 2, 10, 0, 19, 3, 0, 22, 13]$$

### Step 2: Apply the encryption formula $C = (P + K) \bmod 26$ element-wise

For $i=0$: $C_0 = (0 + 7) \bmod 26 = 7$
For $i=1$: $C_1 = (19 + 7) \bmod 26 = 26 \bmod 26 = 0$
For $i=2$: $C_2 = (19 + 7) \bmod 26 = 0$
For $i=3$: $C_3 = (0 + 7) \bmod 26 = 7$
For $i=4$: $C_4 = (2 + 7) \bmod 26 = 9$
For $i=5$: $C_5 = (10 + 7) \bmod 26 = 17$
For $i=6$: $C_6 = (0 + 7) \bmod 26 = 7$
For $i=7$: $C_7 = (19 + 7) \bmod 26 = 0$
For $i=8$: $C_8 = (3 + 7) \bmod 26 = 10$
For $i=9$: $C_9 = (0 + 7) \bmod 26 = 7$
For $i=10$: $C_{10} = (22 + 7) \bmod 26 = 29 \bmod 26 = 3$
For $i=11$: $C_{11} = (13 + 7) \bmod 26 = 20$

Thus the numeric ciphertext vector is:

$$C = [7, 0, 0, 7, 9, 17, 7, 0, 10, 7, 3, 20]$$

### Step 3: Map numeric ciphertext back to letters

$7 \to H$, $0 \to A$, $0 \to A$, $7 \to H$, $9 \to J$, $17 \to R$, $7 \to H$, $0 \to A$, $10 \to K$, $7 \to H$, $3 \to D$, $20 \to U$

$$\boxed{\text{Ciphertext} = \text{"HAAHJRHAKHDU"}}$$

### Step 4: Verify decryption using $P = (C - K) \bmod 26$

We can verify by applying the inverse transformation to the first letter:

$$P_0 = (7 - 7) \bmod 26 = 0 \to A \checkmark$$

And for the last letter:

$$P_{11} = (20 - 7) \bmod 26 = 13 \to N \checkmark$$

The decryption successfully recovers the plaintext.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — RSA Toy Key Generation & Encryption (Pedagogical)

The RSA algorithm is introduced in Module 1 to ground the public-key concept. We now walk through a complete toy RSA cycle using very small primes, exactly as KTU examiners like to test.

### Step 1: Select two small primes
Choose $p = 5$ and $q = 11$.

### Step 2: Compute the modulus $n$
$$n = p \times q = 5 \times 11 = 55$$

### Step 3: Compute Euler's totient $\phi(n)$
Because $p$ and $q$ are distinct primes:

$$\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1) = (5-1)(11-1) = 4 \times 10 = 40$$

### Step 4: Choose a public exponent $e$
Pick $e$ such that $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$. Choosing $e = 7$:

$$\gcd(7, 40) = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 5: Compute the private exponent $d$
We need $d$ such that:

$$e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$$

That is, $7d \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$. Solve via extended Euclidean algorithm:

$$40 = 5 \cdot 7 + 5$$
$$7 = 1 \cdot 5 + 2$$
$$5 = 2 \cdot 2 + 1$$
$$2 = 2 \cdot 1 + 0$$

Back-substitute:

$$1 = 5 - 2 \cdot 2$$
$$1 = 5 - 2 \cdot (7 - 1 \cdot 5) = 3 \cdot 5 - 2 \cdot 7$$
$$1 = 3 \cdot (40 - 5 \cdot 7) - 2 \cdot 7 = 3 \cdot 40 - 17 \cdot 7$$

So $-17 \cdot 7 \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$, giving $d \equiv -17 \equiv 23 \pmod{40}$.

Thus:

$$\boxed{d = 23, \quad e = 7, \quad n = 55}$$

The public key is $(e, n) = (7, 55)$ and the private key is $(d, n) = (23, 55)$.

### Step 6: Encrypt plaintext $M = 9$
$$C = M^{e} \bmod n = 9^{7} \bmod 55$$

Compute step-by-step using successive squaring:

$$9^2 = 81 \equiv 26 \pmod{55}$$
$$9^4 \equiv 26^2 = 676 \equiv 676 - 12 \cdot 55 = 676 - 660 = 16 \pmod{55}$$
$$9^7 = 9^4 \cdot 9^2 \cdot 9^1 \equiv 16 \cdot 26 \cdot 9 \pmod{55}$$
$$16 \cdot 26 = 416 \equiv 416 - 7 \cdot 55 = 416 - 385 = 31 \pmod{55}$$
$$31 \cdot 9 = 279 \equiv 279 - 5 \cdot 55 = 279 - 275 = 4 \pmod{55}$$

So the ciphertext is $C = 4$.

### Step 7: Decrypt $C = 4$ using the private key

$$M = C^{d} \bmod n = 4^{23} \bmod 55$$

Using repeated squaring:

$$4^2 = 16$$
$$4^4 = 16^2 = 256 \equiv 256 - 4 \cdot 55 = 36 \pmod{55}$$
$$4^8 \equiv 36^2 = 1296 \equiv 1296 - 23 \cdot 55 = 1296 - 1265 = 31 \pmod{55}$$
$$4^{16} \equiv 31^2 = 961 \equiv 961 - 17 \cdot 55 = 961 - 935 = 26 \pmod{55}$$

Now $4^{23} = 4^{16} \cdot 4^4 \cdot 4^2 \cdot 4^1$:

$$26 \cdot 36 = 936 \equiv 936 - 17 \cdot 55 = 936 - 935 = 1 \pmod{55}$$
$$1 \cdot 16 = 16$$
$$16 \cdot 4 = 64 \equiv 64 - 1 \cdot 55 = 9 \pmod{55}$$

So $M = 9$, which matches the original plaintext. The toy RSA round-trip is verified.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** In real RSA, $p, q$ must be at least 1024 bits each. The toy values used here (5 and 11) are for illustration only — anyone can factor 55 in milliseconds!

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Symmetric vs. Asymmetric vs. Hash

This comparison matrix is a high-yield KTU table. Students are often asked to choose the right primitive for a given scenario.

| Property | Symmetric-Key | Asymmetric-Key | Cryptographic Hash |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Key(s) used** | Single shared secret $K$ | Public $K_{pub}$, Private $K_{priv}$ | No key (or keyed for MAC) |
| **Speed** | Very fast (100 MB/s – GB/s) | Slow (1000× slower than symmetric) | Fast (hundreds of MB/s) |
| **Confidentiality?** | Yes | Yes | No (one-way) |
| **Integrity?** | Yes (via MAC mode) | Yes (via signatures) | Yes (collision resistance) |
| **Authentication?** | Limited (shared key) | Strong (digital signatures) | Indirect |
| **Non-repudiation?** | No | Yes | No |
| **Key distribution problem** | Severe ($n^2$ keys for $n$ users) | Solved via PKI | N/A |
| **Typical key size (2024)** | 128–256 bits | 2048–4096 bits (RSA), 256 bits (EC) | 256–512 bits output |
| **Examples** | AES, 3DES, ChaCha20 | RSA, ECC, ElGamal | SHA-256, SHA-3, BLAKE2 |
| **Primary use case** | Bulk data encryption | Key exchange, signatures | Integrity, fingerprints |

---

## 3.4 Authentication Decision Matrix

| Scenario | Recommended Technique | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Local OS login | Password (hashed via bcrypt) | Simple, low infrastructure |
| Bank transaction OTP | TOTP (RFC 6238) | Time-bound, replay-resistant |
| SSH remote login | Public-key + Challenge-Response | No password on the wire |
| Enterprise SSO (e.g., Active Directory) | Kerberos | Scalable, ticket-based |
| Web HTTPS | X.509 Certificates (PKI) | Industry standard, mutual auth |
| Mobile app login | MFA (Password + SMS OTP + Biometric) | Defense in depth |
| IoT constrained device | Pre-shared key + HMAC challenge | Lightweight crypto |
| Blockchain wallet | ECDSA + BIP-39 mnemonic | Trustless verification |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The CIA Triad and Extended Security Goals

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[Secure Communication System]
    root --> C[Confidentiality]
    root --> I[Integrity]
    root --> A[Availability]
    root --> Auth[Authentication]
    root --> AuthZ[Authorization]
    root --> NR[Non Repudiation]
    root --> Acc[Accountability]

    C --> C1[Encryption symmetric asymmetric]
    C --> C2[Access Control Lists]
    C --> C3[Traffic Padding]

    I --> I1[Hash Functions SHA 256]
    I --> I2[MAC HMAC]
    I --> I3[Digital Signatures]

    A --> A1[Redundancy and Replication]
    A --> A2[DoS Mitigation Firewalls]
    A --> A3[Backup and Recovery]

    Auth --> Auth1[Password Based]
    Auth --> Auth2[Challenge Response]
    Auth --> Auth3[Token and Certificate]
    Auth --> Auth4[Biometric MFA]

    NR --> NR1[Digital Signatures RSA ECDSA]
    NR --> NR2[Trusted Timestamping]
    NR --> NR3[Notarization Services]
```

---

## 4.2 Taxonomy of Network Security Attacks (Module 1 Must-Know)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Attacks[Network Security Attacks]
    Attacks --> Passive[Passive Attacks]
    Attacks --> Active[Active Attacks]

    Passive --> P1[Release of Message Content]
    Passive --> P2[Traffic Analysis]

    Active --> AC1[Masquerade and Spoofing]
    Active --> AC2[Replay Attack]
    Active --> AC3[Message Modification]
    Active --> AC4[Denial of Service DoS DDoS]

    AC1 --> AC1a[IP Spoofing]
    AC1 --> AC1b[DNS Spoofing]
    AC1 --> AC1c[Email Forging]

    AC2 --> AC2a[Session Hijacking]
    AC2 --> AC2b[Nonce Reuse]

    AC3 --> AC3a[Man in the Middle]
    AC3 --> AC3b[Bit Flipping in Ciphertext]

    AC4 --> AC4a[SYN Flood]
    AC4 --> AC4b[Smurf Attack]
    AC4 --> AC4c[Ping of Death]
    AC4 --> AC4d[Botnet DDoS]
```

---

## 4.3 Cryptographic Algorithm Classification

```mermaid
graph TD
    Crypto[Cryptographic Algorithms]
    Crypto --> Sym[Symmetric Key]
    Crypto --> Asym[Asymmetric Key]
    Crypto --> Hash[Hash Functions]
    Crypto --> MAC[Message Auth Codes]
    Crypto --> Sig[Digital Signatures]

    Sym --> SymB[Block Ciphers]
    Sym --> SymS[Stream Ciphers]

    SymB --> DES[DES 56 bit weak]
    SymB --> TripleDES[3DES Deprecated]
    SymB --> AES[AES 128 192 256 bit standard]
    SymB --> Blow[Blowfish and Twofish]

    SymS --> RC4[RC4 Legacy]
    SymS --> ChaCha[ChaCha20 Modern]

    Asym --> RSA[RSA 2048 bit minimum]
    Asym --> DH[Diffie Hellman Key Exchange]
    Asym --> ECC[Elliptic Curve ECC 256 bit]
    Asym --> ElGamal[ElGamal]

    Hash --> MD5[MD5 Broken]
    Hash --> SHA1[SHA1 Deprecated]
    Hash --> SHA2[SHA 224 256 384 512]
    Hash --> SHA3[SHA 3 Keccak]
    Hash --> Blake[BLAKE2 and BLAKE3]

    MAC --> HMAC[HMAC SHA256]
    MAC --> CMAC[CMAC AES]
    MAC --> GMAC[GCM Galois Counter]

    Sig --> RSApss[RSA PSS]
    Sig --> ECDSA[ECDSA]
    Sig --> EdDSA[EdDSA Ed25519]
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture of a Secure Communication Session

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Sender[Sender Client] -->|Step1 Encrypt| EncLayer[Encryption Module AES RSA]
    EncLayer -->|Step2 Sign| SigLayer[Digital Signature Module ECDSA]
    SigLayer -->|Step3 Hash| HashLayer[Hash Computation SHA 256]
    HashLayer -->|Step4 Wrap| Protocol[Transport Layer TLS 1.3]
    Protocol -->|Step5 Send| Net[Insecure Network Internet]

    Net -->|Step6 Receive| ProtocolR[Receiver Transport Layer]
    ProtocolR -->|Step7 Unwrap| HashLayerR[Hash Verification]
    HashLayerR -->|Step8 Verify| SigLayerR[Signature Verification]
    SigLayerR -->|Step9 Decrypt| EncLayerR[Decryption Module]
    EncLayerR -->|Step10 Deliver| Receiver[Receiver Server]

    HashLayerR -.Fail.-> Reject[Reject and Log]
    SigLayerR -.Fail.-> Reject
    EncLayerR -.Fail.-> Reject
```

This flow mirrors a real **TLS 1.3** session: encryption, integrity (via AEAD), and signature-based authentication of the server (and optionally the client). Any cryptographic verification failure halts the session and triggers an alert.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between passive and active network attacks. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Passive attacks** attempt to **learn or make use of information** from the system but **do not affect** system resources. They are difficult to detect because they leave no trace of their presence. *Example:* Eavesdropping on a wireless network to capture plaintext credentials.

> **Active attacks** attempt to **alter system resources** or **affect their operation**. They involve modification of the data stream or creation of a false stream, and are detectable (but not always preventable). *Example:* A man-in-the-middle attack that modifies a wire transfer's destination account number in transit.

| Aspect | Passive | Active |
|---|---|---|
| Goal | Read / observe | Modify / disrupt |
| Detectability | Hard | Easier (via integrity checks) |
| Effect on data | None | Alters, inserts, deletes |
| Countermeasure | Encryption | Authentication + Integrity |
| Examples | Eavesdropping, Traffic analysis | DoS, Masquerade, Replay |

**(3 Marks — allocation: 1 mark definition each, 1 mark example each, optional comparative table.)**

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**List and briefly explain any three specific security mechanisms defined in ITU-T X.800.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Encipherment (Encryption):** The use of mathematical algorithms to transform plaintext into ciphertext, ensuring data confidentiality. Both symmetric (AES) and asymmetric (RSA) algorithms are used.

2. **Digital Signature:** A cryptographic mechanism that allows the recipient to verify the source and integrity of a message. The sender signs with a private key; the receiver verifies with the public key. Provides authentication, integrity, and non-repudiation.

3. **Access Control:** A set of rules and policies that determine who can access which resources and what operations they can perform. Implemented via Access Control Lists (ACLs), Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), or capability-based systems.

*(Optional alternatives: Data Integrity, Authentication Exchange, Traffic Padding, Routing Control, Notarization.)*

**(3 Marks — 1 mark per mechanism.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **CIA Triad** in detail. Discuss how each property is ensured using appropriate cryptographic techniques. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain **symmetric-key** and **asymmetric-key** cryptographic systems with suitable diagrams. Compare their features. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) — CIA Triad

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Confidentiality:** Ensures that information is accessible only to those authorized to view it. *Techniques:* AES, RSA, TLS encryption; access control lists; traffic padding.
>
> **Integrity:** Guarantees that data has not been tampered with during transit or storage. *Techniques:* SHA-256 hashing, HMAC, digital signatures, checksums.
>
> **Availability:** Ensures systems and data are accessible when needed by authorized users. *Techniques:* Redundancy, DDoS protection, backups, fault-tolerant design.

The triad is often extended to include **Authentication, Authorization, Non-repudiation, and Accountability**, forming the **Parkerian Hexad** (added by Donn Parker).

#### Model Solution for (b) — Symmetric vs. Asymmetric Cryptography

**Symmetric-Key Cryptography:**
- Uses **one shared secret key** for both encryption and decryption.
- $C = E_K(P)$ and $P = D_K(C)$.
- **Examples:** AES (128/256-bit), 3DES, ChaCha20, Twofish.
- **Advantages:** Very fast, suitable for bulk data.
- **Disadvantages:** Key distribution problem — $n$ users require $n(n-1)/2$ unique keys.

**Asymmetric-Key Cryptography:**
- Uses a **key pair** $(K_{pub}, K_{priv})$.
- $C = E_{K_{pub}}(P)$ and $P = D_{K_{priv}}(C)$.
- **Examples:** RSA (based on integer factorization), ECC (elliptic curves), Diffie-Hellman.
- **Advantages:** Solves key distribution, enables digital signatures.
- **Disadvantages:** 1000× slower than symmetric.

**Modern systems (hybrid):** Use asymmetric crypto to **exchange a symmetric session key**, then use symmetric crypto for the bulk data. This is exactly how **TLS 1.3** works.

**(14 Marks — 7 + 7, awarded as: definitions, diagrams, examples, comparisons, applications.)**

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **OSI Security Architecture (X.800)**. List and define the specific security attacks and services. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** What is **authentication**? Explain the following authentication techniques with examples: (i) Password-based, (ii) Challenge-Response, (iii) Token-based (TOTP). *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) — X.800 Architecture

**Three layers of the X.800 security architecture:**

1. **Security Attacks:** Any action that compromises security. Categorized as:
   - **Passive:** Release of message content, traffic analysis.
   - **Active:** Masquerade, replay, modification, denial of service.

2. **Security Mechanisms:** Processes/devices to detect/prevent/recover:
   - Encipherment, Digital Signature, Access Control, Data Integrity, Authentication Exchange, Traffic Padding, Routing Control, Notarization.

3. **Security Services:** Enhance security using mechanisms:
   - Data Confidentiality, Data Integrity, Authentication, Non-repudiation, Access Control (Availability is sometimes included).

**Mapping:** Each service is realized by one or more mechanisms. E.g., *Data Confidentiality* uses *Encipherment*; *Authentication* uses *Authentication Exchange* + *Digital Signature*.

#### Model Solution for (b) — Authentication Techniques

**Definition:** Authentication is the process of verifying the **claimed identity** of an entity (user, device, or process).

**(i) Password-Based:**
- User submits a secret string; server compares against a stored (hashed) version.
- Vulnerable to: brute force, dictionary, phishing, credential stuffing.
- Hardening: bcrypt/Argon2 hashing, salting, account lockout.

**(ii) Challenge-Response:**
- Server sends a **random nonce** $N$. Client computes $f_K(N)$ where $K$ is the shared secret and returns it.
- Variants:
  - **Symmetric:** $f_K(N) = E_K(N)$ (e.g., Kerberos pre-auth).
  - **Hash-based:** $f_K(N) = H(K \mid\mid N)$ (e.g., MS-CHAPv2).
  - **Asymmetric:** $f_{K_{priv}}(N) = \text{Sign}(N)$ (e.g., SSH key login).
- **Property:** Each session is unique — replay is impossible.

**(iii) Token-Based (TOTP):**
- A **hardware or software token** generates a one-time password (OTP) that changes every 30 seconds.
- Algorithm: $\text{TOTP} = \text{Truncate}(\text{HMAC-SHA1}(K, T))$, where $T = \lfloor (t - t_0)/X \rfloor$.
- Used in: Google Authenticator, banking 2FA, employee VPN access.

**(14 Marks — 7 + 7; mark split as: definitions 2 + diagrams 2 + examples/advantages 2 + comparison 1.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Module 1 Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "threat" with "attack":** Threats are *potential*; attacks are *actual*. Examiners dock 1 mark if you use them interchangeably.
> 2. **Writing only "AES" without explaining why it's secure:** Mention block size (128 bits) and key sizes (128/192/256 bits). Just naming an algorithm is worth only half marks.
> 3. **Forgetting "non-repudiation":** When comparing symmetric vs. asymmetric, students often forget that *only asymmetric* (via digital signatures) provides non-repudiation. Examiners specifically check for this.
> 4. **Mixing up the OTP equations:** HOTP uses a *counter*; TOTP uses *time*. Writing them interchangeably loses marks.
> 5. **Skipping the "CIA triad" in answers:** Many Module 1 questions implicitly require you to map your answer back to CIA. Always anchor your conclusions to one of C, I, or A.
> 6. **Not distinguishing peer-entity vs. data-origin authentication:** Peer = connection-level (e.g., TLS handshake); Data-origin = message-level (e.g., signed email).
> 7. **Omitting "Kerberos"** when asked about enterprise authentication — it is a KTU-favorite example.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — High-Density Summary (Print Before Exam):**

- [ ] **CIA Triad:** Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability. Plus extensions: Authentication, Non-Repudiation, Access Control, Accountability.
- [ ] **X.800 Architecture:** 3 layers — Attacks, Mechanisms, Services. Each service uses one or more mechanisms.
- [ ] **Passive attacks** observe only (eavesdropping, traffic analysis); **Active attacks** modify data (DoS, replay, MITM, masquerade).
- [ ] **Cryptography primitives:** Symmetric (AES), Asymmetric (RSA, ECC), Hash (SHA-256), MAC (HMAC), Signatures (RSA-PSS, EdDSA).
- [ ] **Caesar Cipher:** $C = (P+K) \bmod 26$. Classic example of monoalphabetic substitution.
- [ ] **RSA math:** $n = pq$, $\phi = (p-1)(q-1)$, $ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$. Encrypt $M^e \bmod n$, decrypt $C^d \bmod n$.
- [ ] **Hash requirements:** Pre-image, second pre-image, collision resistance.
- [ ] **Authentication factors:** Type-1 (know), Type-2 (have), Type-3 (are). MFA combines them.
- [ ] **Authentication techniques:** Password, Challenge-Response (nonce-based), Token/TOTP, Biometric, Kerberos, PKI/Certificates, Zero-Knowledge Proofs.
- [ ] **OTP equations:** HOTP uses counter $C$; TOTP uses $T = \lfloor (t-t_0)/X \rfloor$.
- [ ] **Symmetric vs. Asymmetric trade-off:** Symmetric is fast but has a key-distribution problem; Asymmetric is slow but solves distribution and enables signatures.
- [ ] **Hybrid encryption** = Asymmetric key exchange + Symmetric bulk encryption (used in TLS).
- [ ] **Non-repudiation** is provided only by **digital signatures** (asymmetric). Symmetric crypto cannot provide it alone.
- [ ] **Modern standards to remember:** AES-256, RSA-2048+, SHA-256+, TLS 1.3, TOTP (RFC 6238), HOTP (RFC 4226), X.509 certificates.
- [ ] **Real-world analogy to remember:** Network security = registered post office with sealed envelopes, signatures, ID verification, and locked mailboxes.
- [ ] **Always anchor your answer to the CIA Triad** when asked "why is this needed?"

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Introduction to Network Security, Cryptographic Algorithms & Authentication Techniques.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Layer 2/3 Security and Authentication Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT633 - SECURE COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Layer 2/3 Security and Authentication Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Layer 2/3 Security and Authentication Systems** refers to a coordinated set of protocols, mechanisms, and architectural controls that protect the **Data Link Layer (OSI Layer 2)** and the **Network Layer (OSI Layer 3)** of the TCP/IP stack, while providing identity verification for users, devices, and packets traversing the network.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Layer 2 security focuses on protecting LAN-based broadcast domains, switch forwarding tables, and MAC-layer identity. Layer 3 security focuses on protecting the routing/forwarding plane, IP packet integrity, and inter-domain trust. Authentication systems (RADIUS, TACACS+, Kerberos, 802.1X) sit on top of these layers to provide the **AAA framework** — *Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting*.

The three pillars of this topic are:

1. **Layer 2 Defense** — Protect the switch fabric, MAC tables, and VLAN boundaries.
2. **Layer 3 Defense** — Protect IP header integrity, routing protocols, and forwarding decisions.
3. **AAA Authentication** — Verify *who* is connecting, *what* they are allowed to do, and *record* what they did.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a large office building:

- **Layer 2 Security** is like the **reception desk on every floor**. It checks the employee's *badge number* (MAC address), stops strangers from wandering into offices (port security), and prevents one floor from impersonating another (VLAN hopping defense).
- **Layer 3 Security** is like the **main mailroom**. Every parcel (IP packet) is inspected for a valid return address (IP spoofing defense) and routed only along approved corridors (BGP/OSPF authentication).
- **Authentication Systems** are the **centralized ID card issuance office** — RADIUS, TACACS+, and Kerberos — that hand out credentials and decide which doors open for whom.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** In the 2024 Scheme, Module 1 establishes the *perimeter* and *identity* foundation. Without Layer 2/3 hardening and strong authentication, all higher-layer crypto (IPsec, TLS, SSH) can be bypassed or downgraded. Expect direct 3-mark questions on **RADIUS vs. TACACS+** and **ARP Spoofing mitigation**.

## 1.3 Key Entities & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **MAC Address** — 48-bit hardware identifier (e.g., `AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF`).
- **IP Address** — 32-bit (IPv4) or 128-bit (IPv6) logical identifier.
- **EAP** — Extensible Authentication Protocol (RFC 3748).
- **RADIUS** — Remote Authentication Dial-In User Service (UDP **1812/1813**).
- **TACACS+** — Terminal Access Controller Access-Control System Plus (TCP **49**).
- **802.1X** — Port-Based Network Access Control (PNAC) standard.
- **AAA** — Authentication, Authorization, Accounting.
- **DHCP Snooping** — L2 feature that filters untrusted DHCP messages.
- **DAI** — Dynamic ARP Inspection.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layer 2 Attack Surface and Defenses

Layer 2 is inherently a **broadcast medium** in switched Ethernet. Several attacks exploit this:

### 2.1.1 MAC Flooding Attack
- **How it works:** Attacker sends thousands of frames with *random source MAC addresses* to fill the switch's **CAM (Content Addressable Memory) table**.
- **Effect:** Once the CAM table overflows, the switch fails open and begins behaving like a **hub**, broadcasting all frames to all ports → attacker can sniff all traffic.
- **Defense:** **Port Security** — limits the number of MAC addresses allowed on a port (e.g., `switchport port-security maximum 2`).

### 2.1.2 ARP Spoofing / ARP Poisoning
- **How it works:** Attacker sends gratuitous ARP replies claiming, *"I am 192.168.1.1, my MAC is AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA"*. Victim's ARP cache gets poisoned, redirecting traffic to the attacker (Man-in-the-Middle).
- **Defense:** **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)** — cross-references each ARP packet with the **DHCP Snooping binding table**; invalid bindings are dropped.

### 2.1.3 DHCP Starvation & Rogue DHCP
- **Starvation:** Attacker floods DHCPDISCOVER with random MACs to exhaust the DHCP pool.
- **Rogue Server:** Attacker runs a fake DHCP server handing out malicious default gateway/DNS.
- **Defense:** **DHCP Snooping** — classifies ports as *trusted* (uplink to legit DHCP server) or *untrusted* (host ports); blocks DHCP server replies on untrusted ports.

### 2.1.4 VLAN Hopping
- **Switched Spoofing:** Attacker negotiates a trunk link (DTP) and gains access to all VLANs.
- **Double Tagging:** Attacker sends a frame with two 802.1Q tags; the outer tag is stripped by the first switch, and the inner tag places the frame in the target VLAN.
- **Defense:** Disable DTP on user ports (`switchport mode access`), assign unused ports to a *blackhole VLAN*, and never use VLAN 1 for user traffic.

### 2.1.5 STP Manipulation
- Attacker sends superior BPDUs to become the **Root Bridge**, capturing all traffic.
- **Defense:** **BPDU Guard** and **Root Guard** on access/edge ports.

## 2.2 Layer 3 Attack Surface and Defenses

### 2.2.1 IP Spoofing
- Attacker forges the source IP to bypass ACLs or launch reflection attacks.
- **Defenses:**
  - **Ingress Filtering (RFC 2827 / BCP 38)** — ISP edge routers drop packets with source IPs outside the customer's allocated range.
  - **uRPF (Unicast Reverse Path Forwarding)** — router verifies that a reverse path to the source IP exists in the FIB.
  - **IP Source Guard** — L2/L3 hybrid that uses the DHCP Snooping binding table to filter packets with invalid source IPs.

### 2.2.2 Routing Protocol Authentication
- **OSPF:** Uses **MD5** or **SHA** authentication in the OSPF header (Type 2 Authentication for MD5, or Cryptographic Authentication for SHA-1/2).
- **BGP:** Uses **TCP MD5 Signature Option (RFC 2385)** or the newer **TCP AO (RFC 5925)**.
- **RIPv2:** Supports simple plaintext or MD5 authentication.
- **Why?** Prevents route hijacking where an attacker injects false routes to redirect traffic.

### 2.2.3 ICMP-Based Attacks
- **Smurf Attack:** Attacker sends ICMP Echo to broadcast address with spoofed source IP.
- **Ping of Death:** Oversized ICMP packet.
- **Defense:** Disable directed broadcast forwarding; rate-limit ICMP.

## 2.3 Authentication Systems — The AAA Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AAA is the heart of any enterprise authentication question. Master this for 14-mark answers.**

### 2.3.1 Authentication
Verifies the identity of a user/device. Methods: passwords, certificates, tokens, biometrics.

### 2.3.2 Authorization
Determines what an authenticated identity is permitted to do (read-only, admin, etc.).

### 2.3.3 Accounting
Logs *what* the user did, *when*, and for *how long* — used for billing, auditing, forensics.

### 2.3.4 RADIUS (Remote Authentication Dial-In User Service)
- **Transport:** UDP (connectionless, faster but less reliable).
- **Ports:** **1812** (authentication/authorization), **1813** (accounting).
- **Encryption:** Only the **password field** is encrypted (using a shared secret + MD5). The username and other attributes are sent in **cleartext**.
- **Architecture:** Combines Authentication and Authorization in one packet; accounting is separate.
- **Model:** Client–Server — the **NAS (Network Access Server)** is the RADIUS client.

### 2.3.5 TACACS+ (Terminal Access Controller Access-Control System Plus)
- **Transport:** **TCP** (reliable, connection-oriented).
- **Port:** **49**.
- **Encryption:** **Entire payload** is encrypted (using the shared secret + a per-packet random pad).
- **Architecture:** Separates Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting into distinct phases (more granular control).
- **Use Case:** Cisco-centric, ideal for device administration (router/switch login).
- **Model:** Also Client–Server.

### 2.3.6 Kerberos
- **Architecture:** Trusted third-party model using a **Key Distribution Center (KDC)** = **AS (Authentication Server)** + **TGS (Ticket Granting Server)**.
- **Tickets:** TGT (Ticket Grantting Ticket) → Service Ticket.
- **Encryption:** Symmetric (typically AES).
- **Single Sign-On (SSO):** User authenticates once, then accesses multiple services without re-entering credentials.
- **Mutual Authentication:** Both client and server prove identity to each other.

### 2.3.7 802.1X / EAP
- **Role:** **Port-based Network Access Control (PNAC)** — blocks all traffic on a switch port until the device authenticates.
- **Roles:** *Supplicant* (client) ↔ *Authenticator* (switch/AP) ↔ *Authentication Server* (RADIUS).
- **EAP Variants:** EAP-TLS (cert-based), EAP-TTLS, PEAP, EAP-FAST, EAP-MD5 (weak — never use).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Value / Equation | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| MAC Address length | 48 bits (6 octets) | First 24 bits = OUI |
| IPv4 Header Length | 20–60 bytes | Min if no options |
| CAM Table size (typical) | 8K–128K entries | Overflow → flooding |
| RADIUS Auth Port | UDP **1812** | Old standard was 1645 |
| RADIUS Acct Port | UDP **1813** | Old standard was 1646 |
| TACACS+ Port | TCP **49** | Single port for all AAA |
| Kerberos Default Port | UDP/TCP **88** | KDC service |
| 802.1X EAPOL Frame | EtherType **0x888E** | EAP over LAN |
| MD5 Digest Size | 128 bits | Used in RADIUS, BGP-MD5 |
| SHA-1 Digest Size | 160 bits | Deprecated for new use |
| SHA-256 Digest Size | 256 bits | Current standard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** When asked *"Compare RADIUS and TACACS+"*, always include **Transport (UDP vs TCP)**, **Encryption Scope (password only vs entire payload)**, **AAA Separation (combined vs separate)**, and **Typical Use (Network Access vs Device Admin)**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise Networks:** Cisco switches use **802.1X + RADIUS** to authenticate every wired and wireless device.
- **Service Providers:** Use **RADIUS** for ISP subscriber billing (accounting).
- **Data Centers:** Use **BGP TCP-AO** to protect route advertisements from peers.
- **Government/Defense:** Use **Kerberos** for SSO across classified systems.
- **IoT/OT Security:** **Port security + DHCP snooping + DAI** are baseline Layer 2 hardening in any industrial network.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: RADIUS Packet Authentication Exchange

RADIUS uses a **shared secret + MD5** to authenticate response packets. The mathematical derivation of the *Response Authenticator* is the most-tested formula in this module.

**Given:**
- Code = Access-Request (1) or Access-Accept (2)
- Identifier = 1-byte session ID
- Length = 2-byte packet length
- Attributes = AVPs
- Shared Secret = $S$ (between NAS and RADIUS server)
- Request Authenticator = 16-byte random nonce $R_{req}$ (chosen by NAS)

**To Find:** Response Authenticator $R_{res}$

**Step 1 — Compute MD5 Digest**
We concatenate the entire response packet (Code $\vert$ Identifier $\vert$ Length $\vert$ Request Authenticator $\vert$ Attributes) with the shared secret $S$:

$$M_1 \;=\; \text{Code} \;\vert\; \text{Identifier} \;\vert\; \text{Length} \;\vert\; R_{req} \;\vert\; \text{Attributes} \;\vert\; S$$

**Step 2 — Apply MD5 Hash**
The 128-bit MD5 hash is computed:

$$D_1 \;=\; \text{MD5}(M_1)$$

**Step 3 — Form the Response Authenticator**
The 16-byte Response Authenticator $R_{res}$ is the binary representation of $D_1$:

$$R_{res} \;=\; D_1 \;\in\; \{0,1\}^{128}$$

**Step 4 — Password Encryption (User-Password AVP)**
For each block of 16 bytes of the password $P$ (padded with nulls), we compute:

$$C_i \;=\; P_i \;\oplus\; \text{MD5}(S \;\vert\; R_{req})$$

$$C_{i+1} \;=\; P_{i+1} \;\oplus\; \text{MD5}(S \;\vert\; C_i)$$

The ciphertext blocks $C_0, C_1, \ldots, C_n$ form the encrypted password attribute.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** The MD5 chain ensures that two users with the same password get *different* ciphertexts because $R_{req}$ is a fresh random nonce per session — this is known as a **salt**.

## 3.2 Derivation: EAPOL 4-Way Handshake (used in WPA2/802.1X)

The 4-way handshake proves the client and AP know the **PMK (Pairwise Master Key)** without sending it over the air.

**Step 1 — AP → Client:** Sends **ANonce** (AP's random nonce).
Client now derives the **PTK** using:

$$\text{PTK} \;=\; \text{PRF}(\text{PMK} \;\vert\; \text{ANonce} \;\vert\; \text{SNonce} \;\vert\; \text{MAC}_{AP} \;\vert\; \text{MAC}_{Client})$$

**Step 2 — Client → AP:** Sends **SNonce** and the **MIC** (Message Integrity Code):

$$\text{MIC} \;=\; \text{PRF}(\text{PTK}, \text{EAPOL-Frame})$$

**Step 3 — AP → Client:** Sends **GTK** (Group Temporal Key, encrypted) and confirms the MIC. AP also derives PTK and verifies the MIC.

**Step 4 — Client → AP:** Sends final ACK, encrypted with the new PTK. Secure data exchange begins.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Layer 2/3 Attack vs. Mitigation

| Attack | OSI Layer | Primary Mitigation | Secondary Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| MAC Flooding | L2 | Port Security (limit MAC count) | Storm Control, Static MAC entries |
| ARP Spoofing | L2 | Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) | Static ARP entries, ARP Watch |
| DHCP Starvation | L2 | DHCP Snooping + Port Security | 802.1X device authentication |
| Rogue DHCP | L2 | DHCP Snooping (trusted ports) | DHCP option 82 insertion |
| VLAN Hopping | L2 | Disable DTP, set access mode | Native VLAN mismatch, prune VLANs |
| STP Attack | L2 | BPDU Guard, Root Guard | Disable STP on edge ports |
| IP Spoofing | L3 | uRPF, Ingress Filtering (BCP 38) | IP Source Guard |
| Route Hijacking | L3 | BGP TCP-AO / MD5, Route Filtering | RPKI, ROA validation |
| ICMP Smurf | L3 | Disable directed broadcast | ICMP rate-limiting |
| Unauthorized Access | Multi | 802.1X, RADIUS, TACACS+ | ACLs, NAC (Network Access Control) |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Kerberos Authentication Exchange

**Step 1 — Client → AS (Authentication Server):**
Client sends plaintext username (no password) in the **AS-REQ**.

**Step 2 — AS → Client:** **AS-REP**
AS generates a session key $K_{C,TGS}$ and encrypts it with the user's secret key $K_C$ (derived from password). It also issues a **TGT** encrypted with the TGS's secret key $K_{TGS}$:

$$TGT \;=\; \{K_{C,TGS}\}_{K_{TGS}}$$

The client decrypts $\{K_{C,TGS}\}_{K_C}$ using her password-derived key, proving she knows the password.

**Step 3 — Client → TGS:** **TGS-REQ**
Client presents the TGT and an **Authenticator** $= \{ClientID\}_{K_{C,TGS}}$, requesting a service ticket for, say, a file server.

**Step 4 — TGS → Client:** **TGS-REP**
TGS issues a **Service Ticket** $= \{K_{C,S}\}_{K_S}$ (encrypted with the server's key) and a session key $K_{C,S}$ for client–server communication.

**Step 5 — Client → Service Server:** **AP-REQ**
Client sends the Service Ticket plus a fresh Authenticator $= \{ClientID, Timestamp\}_{K_{C,S}}$.

**Step 6 — Server → Client (optional):** **AP-REP**
Server sends a timestamp encrypted with $K_{C,S}$, completing **mutual authentication**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The password never traverses the network. Only encrypted tickets do. This is why Kerberos resists replay attacks (via timestamps in the Authenticator, which must be within 5 minutes of KDC time).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: AAA Framework with RADIUS/TACACS+

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Device or Supplicant] -->|1. Login Request| B[NAS Network Access Server]
    B -->|2. RADIUS Access-Request UDP 1812| C[RADIUS Server]
    B -.->|2 alt TACACS+ TCP 49| D[TACACS+ Server]
    C -->|3. Access Accept Reject| B
    D -.->|3 alt| B
    B -->|4. Grant or Deny Access| A
    C -->|5. RADIUS Accounting UDP 1813| E[Accounting Log]
    D -.->|5 alt Accounting| E
    E -->|Audit and Billing| F[SIEM and Admin]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: ARP Spoofing Attack and DAI Defense

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Attacker[Attacker Host] -->|1. Gratuitous ARP Reply claiming Gateway MAC| Victim[Victim Host]
    Victim -->|2. Updates ARP Cache with fake Gateway MAC| Cache[Poisoned ARP Table]
    Cache -->|3. All traffic to Gateway sent to Attacker| Attacker
    Attacker -->|4. Forwards to real Gateway and back| Gateway[Real Gateway Router]

    subgraph Defense Layer
        D1[DHCP Snooping builds trusted binding table] --> D2[Dynamic ARP Inspection DAI]
        D2 -->|Drop ARP with MAC IP mismatch| Drop[Invalid ARP packets discarded]
    end

    Attacker -.->|Blocked by| D2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: 802.1X Port-Based Network Access Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Supplicant[Supplicant Laptop] -->|EAPOL Start| Authenticator[Authenticator Switch or AP]
    Authenticator -->|EAP Request Identity| Supplicant
    Supplicant -->|EAP Response Identity| Authenticator
    Authenticator -->|RADIUS Access Request UDP 1812| AuthServer[RADIUS Authentication Server]
    AuthServer -->|RADIUS Access Challenge with EAP Type| Authenticator
    Authenticator -->|EAP Request from Server| Supplicant
    Supplicant -->|EAP Response with Credentials| Authenticator
    Authenticator -->|RADIUS Access Request updated| AuthServer
    AuthServer -->|RADIUS Access Accept Success| Authenticator
    Authenticator -->|EAP Success MAC Learned Port Open| Supplicant
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Kerberos Ticket Exchange

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C[Client] -->|1 AS-REQ username only| AS[Authentication Server]
    AS -->|2 AS-REP TGT encrypted with TGS key| C
    C -->|3 TGS-REQ TGT plus Authenticator| TGS[Ticket Granting Server]
    TGS -->|4 TGS-REP Service Ticket encrypted with Server key| C
    C -->|5 AP-REQ Service Ticket plus Authenticator| S[Service Server]
    S -->|6 AP-REP Timestamp proves mutual auth| C
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Layer 2/3 Security Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L7_AAA[AAA and Authentication Plane]
        A1[RADIUS Server]
        A2[TACACS+ Server]
        A3[Kerberos KDC]
    end

    subgraph L3_Defense[Layer 3 Security Plane]
        L3a[uRPF and Ingress Filtering]
        L3b[BGP TCP-AO Authentication]
        L3c[OSPF MD5 or SHA Authentication]
        L3d[IP Source Guard]
        L3e[ACLs and Route Maps]
    end

    subgraph L2_Defense[Layer 2 Security Plane]
        L2a[Port Security and 802.1X]
        L2b[DHCP Snooping]
        L2c[Dynamic ARP Inspection]
        L2d[BPDU Guard and Root Guard]
        L2e[VLAN Pruning and DTP Disabled]
    end

    L2_Defense --> L3_Defense
    L3_Defense --> L7_AAA
    L7_AAA -->|Identity and Policy| L2_Defense
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between RADIUS and TACACS+ in terms of transport protocol and encryption.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **RADIUS** uses **UDP** (ports 1812/1813) as the transport protocol and encrypts **only the password field** of the packet using a shared secret and MD5. The username and other AVPs are sent in cleartext.
- **TACACS+** uses **TCP** (port 49) and encrypts the **entire payload** of every packet using the shared secret and a per-packet random pad. (1 mark each for transport; 1 mark for encryption scope).

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is ARP Spoofing? Name ONE mitigation technique.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
ARP Spoofing (or ARP Poisoning) is a Layer 2 attack in which an attacker sends forged gratuitous ARP reply messages onto a LAN, associating the attacker's MAC address with the IP address of a legitimate host (typically the default gateway). This causes victim hosts to forward traffic through the attacker, enabling a Man-in-the-Middle attack. **Mitigation:** **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)**, which validates ARP packets against the trusted DHCP Snooping binding table and drops any ARP packet whose MAC-to-IP binding is inconsistent (1 mark for definition; 1 mark for attack impact; 1 mark for mitigation).

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Authentication Systems Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **AAA framework** in detail. With a neat diagram, describe how **RADIUS** implements the AAA model for a network access scenario. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **RADIUS, TACACS+, and Kerberos** in terms of architecture, transport, encryption, use case, and SSO capability. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**AAA Framework Definition (2 marks):**
- **Authentication:** Verifies the identity of a user or device (e.g., username/password, certificate).
- **Authorization:** Determines what an authenticated entity is allowed to do (e.g., read-only access, admin rights).
- **Accounting:** Tracks and logs user activities (login time, duration, data transferred) for billing and auditing.

**RADIUS Architecture (3 marks):**
RADIUS follows a **client–server** model:
- **RADIUS Client** = **NAS (Network Access Server)** — typically a switch, wireless controller, or VPN concentrator that the user initially contacts.
- **RADIUS Server** = A centralized policy/identity server that holds user credentials (often backed by LDAP or Active Directory).

**Operational Flow with Diagram (2 marks):**
1. User connects to the NAS and submits credentials (802.1X EAPOL or PPP PAP/CHAP).
2. NAS creates a **RADIUS Access-Request** packet (with username, password, NAS IP, NAS port) and sends it to the RADIUS server over **UDP 1812**.
3. RADIUS server validates credentials against its database and replies with **Access-Accept**, **Access-Reject**, or **Access-Challenge** (for MFA).
4. On Accept, NAS opens the port and may start **RADIUS Accounting** (UDP 1813) to log session start/stop and traffic counters.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Parameter | RADIUS | TACACS+ | Kerberos |
|---|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Client–Server (NAS + Server) | Client–Server (NAS + Server) | Trusted Third-Party (KDC) |
| Transport | UDP (1812/1813) | TCP (49) | UDP/TCP (88) |
| Encryption | Only password (MD5) | Entire payload | Symmetric (AES), tickets only |
| AAA Separation | Auth+Authz combined, Acct separate | All three fully separate | Authentication only; authz implicit in ticket |
| Primary Use | Network Access (WiFi, VPN) | Device Administration (router/switch) | Single Sign-On (SSO) in enterprises |
| SSO Support | No | No | Yes (TGT + Service Tickets) |
| Mutual Auth | No (server only authenticates client) | No | Yes |

*(1 mark per correct row × 6 rows = 6 marks; 1 mark for any valid extra insight.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Layer 2/3 Attack & Defense Focus

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Describe the following Layer 2 attacks with their mitigation techniques: **(7 marks)**
   (i) MAC Flooding
   (ii) ARP Spoofing
   (iii) DHCP Starvation
   (iv) VLAN Hopping

**(b)** Explain **IP Spoofing** and discuss how **uRPF, Ingress Filtering, and IP Source Guard** mitigate it. Show the configuration steps for enabling uRPF on a Cisco router interface. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**(i) MAC Flooding (1.5 marks):**
- **Attack:** Attacker blasts frames with spoofed random source MACs to overflow the switch's CAM table. The switch, unable to learn new entries, begins flooding unicast frames out all ports (hub-like behavior), allowing sniffing.
- **Mitigation:** **Port Security** — set a maximum MAC count per port (e.g., `switchport port-security maximum 2`) and use violation modes (*protect*, *restrict*, *shutdown*). Enable **Storm Control** to limit broadcast/multicast rates.

**(ii) ARP Spoofing (2 marks):**
- **Attack:** Attacker sends gratuitous ARP replies associating his MAC with the IP of the legitimate gateway. Victims send traffic to attacker first → MITM.
- **Mitigation:** **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)** enabled on VLANs, with **DHCP Snooping** providing the trusted binding table. Optionally use static ARP entries for critical hosts.

**(iii) DHCP Starvation (1.5 marks):**
- **Attack:** Attacker floods DHCPDISCOVER with random MACs to exhaust the DHCP pool, denying IP leases to legitimate users.
- **Mitigation:** **Port Security** to limit MACs per port; **802.1X** to authenticate devices before they can request IP.

**(iv) VLAN Hopping (2 marks):**
- **Attack (Switch Spoofing):** Host negotiates a trunk (DTP) to access all VLANs.
- **Attack (Double Tagging):** Two 802.1Q tags; outer stripped, inner tag lands in target VLAN.
- **Mitigation:** Set all user ports to `switchport mode access` (disable DTP); set unused ports to a *blackhole VLAN*; prune unused VLANs from trunks; never use VLAN 1 for user data.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**IP Spoofing Explanation (2 marks):**
IP Spoofing is a Layer 3 attack where the attacker forges the source IP address in an IP header. The attacker uses a source IP belonging to a trusted network (or the victim's own IP) to bypass ACLs, evade logging, or launch reflection-based DDoS attacks (e.g., DNS amplification).

**Mitigation 1 — uRPF (2 marks):**
**Unicast Reverse Path Forwarding** verifies that, for any incoming packet on an interface, a reverse path exists in the routing table back to the source IP via the *same* interface. If no such route exists, the packet is dropped. Two modes:
- **Strict mode:** Packet dropped unless the source IP is reachable *exclusively* through the receiving interface.
- **Loose mode:** Packet dropped only if no route to the source IP exists at all.

**Configuration on Cisco IOS:**
```
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip verify unicast reverse-path
```
*(1 mark for explanation, 1 mark for configuration.)*

**Mitigation 2 — Ingress Filtering (BCP 38 / RFC 2827) (1.5 marks):**
ISP edge routers drop any packet whose source IP does not belong to the customer's allocated address block. Implemented with ACLs:
```
access-list 100 permit ip 203.0.113.0 0.0.0.255 any
access-list 100 deny   ip any any
interface Serial0/0
 ip access-group 100 in
```

**Mitigation 3 — IP Source Guard (1.5 marks):**
A Layer 2/3 hybrid that uses the **DHCP Snooping binding table** to permit only those source IPs and MACs that have a valid lease. Packets with untrusted source IPs are dropped at the switch port:
```
interface FastEthernet0/1
 ip verify source
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing RADIUS ports:** Many students write 1645/1646 (the *old* IANA-assigned ports). The current standard is **1812/1813**. Using old ports costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up "encrypts password" vs "encrypts entire payload":** This is the single most-tested RADIUS vs TACACS+ differentiator.
> 3. **Confusing DAI with Port Security:** Port Security = MAC limit; DAI = ARP validation via DHCP Snooping. They are *complementary*, not substitutes.
> 4. **Forgetting strict vs loose uRPF mode:** Examiners love asking this.
> 5. **Writing "Kerberos uses asymmetric crypto":** It does NOT — it uses **symmetric** keys (AES).
> 6. **In Layer 2 questions, students often forget to disable DTP** when mitigating VLAN hopping — that is the most common omission.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Layer 2/3 Security & Authentication:**

- **Layer 2 Attacks** to memorize: **MAC Flooding, ARP Spoofing, DHCP Starvation, Rogue DHCP, VLAN Hopping, STP Manipulation**.
- **Layer 2 Defenses:** **Port Security, DHCP Snooping, Dynamic ARP Inspection, BPDU Guard, Root Guard, DTP disabled, private VLANs**.
- **Layer 3 Attacks:** **IP Spoofing, Route Hijacking, ICMP Smurf, Ping of Death**.
- **Layer 3 Defenses:** **uRPF (strict & loose), Ingress Filtering (BCP 38), IP Source Guard, BGP TCP-AO, OSPF MD5/SHA, ACLs**.
- **AAA = Authentication + Authorization + Accounting** — always cite all three.
- **RADIUS:** UDP, ports **1812/1813**, password-only encryption, combined Auth+Authz, used for **network access**.
- **TACACS+:** TCP, port **49**, full payload encryption, **separated AAA**, used for **device administration**.
- **Kerberos:** KDC = AS + TGS, uses **TGT → Service Ticket**, symmetric encryption, supports **SSO** and **mutual authentication**, password never travels.
- **802.1X:** **Supplicant ↔ Authenticator ↔ Authentication Server**, EAPOL frames use EtherType **0x888E**, back-end server is usually **RADIUS**.
- **EAP variants:** EAP-TLS (strongest, cert-based), PEAP, EAP-TTLS, EAP-FAST — **avoid EAP-MD5**.
- **CAM table overflow** → switch becomes a hub → enables sniffing.
- **VLAN 1** should never carry user traffic — always prune it.
- **DTP** (Dynamic Trunking Protocol) must be **disabled** on all user/access ports.
- **BGP authentication** has evolved: **TCP MD5 (RFC 2385)** → **TCP AO (RFC 5925)**; both are KTU-relevant.
- **Kerberos timestamps** must be within **5 minutes** of KDC time (replay defense).
- **MD5** in RADIUS = legacy, still asked in exams despite SHA-256 being the modern recommendation.
- **High-yield 14-mark topics:** (1) RADIUS vs TACACS+ comparison, (2) Kerberos exchange, (3) ARP Spoofing + DAI, (4) IP Spoofing + uRPF, (5) 802.1X + EAP flow.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Key Establishment Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT633 - SECURE COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Key Establishment Protocols # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Key Establishment Protocol? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition** > A **Key Establishment Protocol** is a cryptographic procedure in which a shared secret key (the *session key*) is generated, exchanged, or agreed upon between two or more communicating entities such that subsequent secure communication (using symmetric ciphers like AES) becomes feasible over an otherwise insecure public channel. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, key establishment is the **first pillar** of the *cryptographic protocol stack* — without a securely shared secret, every symmetric cipher, MAC, or authenticated channel collapses. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Locked Suitcase Exchange" Imagine two strangers, **Alice** and **Bob**, sitting in a noisy airport lounge who wish to exchange a sealed suitcase of confidential papers, but the only people moving between them are untrusted couriers. - **Option A (Key Transport):** Alice puts her papers in a locked suitcase, but Bob does not have the key. She sends the key separately via a courier. If a malicious courier makes a copy, the lock is compromised. - **Option B (Key Agreement):** Alice and Bob each contribute ingredients to a *magic potion* that only turns into a usable key when combined. No courier ever sees the full potion. > [!NOTE] > This analogy maps directly onto two principal sub-classes of key establishment: > - **Key Transport** — One party generates the key and securely delivers it. > - **Key Agreement** — Both parties contribute entropy; no one alone knows the result. --- ## 1.3 Why Key Establishment is Indispensable | Reason | Engineering Justification | |---|---| | **Confidentiality** | Symmetric ciphers (AES-128, ChaCha20) demand a shared secret. | | **Authentication** | Session keys authenticate parties via challenge-response. | | **Forward Secrecy** | New keys per session prevent bulk decryption upon long-term key compromise. | | **Scalability** | Public-key exchange reduces $n^2$ key pairs to $n$ certificates. | | **Replay Protection** | Fresh keys bound to timestamps/nonces stop replay attacks. | --- ## 1.4 Classification Hierarchy Key Establishment Protocols fall under two broad families: 1. **Symmetric (Secret Key) Techniques** - Key Distribution Center (KDC) model — *Needham–Schroeder*, *Kerberos*. - Key Translation Center model. 2. **Asymmetric (Public Key) Techniques** - **Key Transport** using public-key encryption (e.g., hybrid encryption with RSA). - **Key Agreement** — **Diffie–Hellman (DH)** and its variants (ECDH, X3DH). > [!TIP] > **KTU High-Yield Pearl:** For 14-mark questions, you will almost always be asked to compare *Needham–Schroeder Symmetric Key Protocol* with *Diffie–Hellman Key Agreement* — be prepared with a tabular comparison of trust models, attack surfaces, and computational costs. --- ## 1.5 Glossary of Standard KTU 2024 Terms | Symbol | Meaning | |---|---| | $K_{AB}$ | Long-term shared key between A and B | | $K_S$ | Fresh session key | | $N_A, N_B$ | Nonces (numbers used once) | | $T_A, T_B$ | Timestamps | | $E_X(\cdot)$ | Encryption under $X$'s public key | | $E_K(\cdot)$ | Symmetric encryption with key $K$ | | $S_A$ | Digital signature of A | | $Cert_A$ | Public-key certificate of A | | KDC | Key Distribution Center | | PKI | Public Key Infrastructure | --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Threat Model & Design Goals A robust key establishment protocol must satisfy the following **KTU security triad**: - **Confidentiality** — No eavesdropper ($E$) learns $K_S$. - **Authentication** — Each party is sure of the other's identity. - **Integrity / Freshness** — The key is fresh, never replayed. Auxiliary goals include **forward secrecy** (past keys stay safe even if long-term keys leak) and **known-key security** (leaking one session key does not compromise others). --- ## 2.2 The Three Architectural Pillars ### Pillar 1 — Key Distribution Using Symmetric Techniques (KDC Model) **Working Principle:** A trusted online server — the **Key Distribution Center (KDC)** — shares a long-term symmetric key with every registered user. When Alice wants to talk to Bob: 1. Alice requests the KDC for a session key with Bob. 2. KDC generates $K_S$ and dispatches it twice: - Once encrypted to Alice (so she can read it). - Once encrypted to Bob (passed *through* Alice in a sealed envelope). 3. Alice forwards Bob's envelope. Bob decrypts and retrieves $K_S$. **Canonical Example: Needham–Schroeder Symmetric Key Protocol (1978)** The 7-step canonical form (with nonces for freshness): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{1. } A \rightarrow S : &\ A, B, N_A \\ \text{2. } S \rightarrow A : &\ E_{K_{AS}}(N_A, B, K_S, E_{K_{BS}}(K_S, A)) \\ \text{3. } A \rightarrow B : &\ E_{K_{BS}}(K_S, A) \\ \text{4. } B \rightarrow A : &\ E_{K_S}(N_B) \\ \text{5. } A \rightarrow B : &\ E_{K_S}(N_B - 1) \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > The **-1** in step 5 is a classic KTU exam question: it prevents a *reflection attack* by ensuring the response is not identical to the challenge. **Denning–Sacco Attack (1981):** If $K_S$ is compromised, an attacker can replay step 3 indefinitely because there is no timestamp tying the session to a specific time window. Kerberos fixed this by adding timestamps. --- ### Pillar 2 — Key Distribution Using Asymmetric Techniques (Key Transport) The simplest hybrid scheme: $$ \begin{aligned} A \rightarrow B : &\ E_B(K_S) \end{aligned} $$ Where $E_B$ denotes encryption under Bob's public key. Only Bob (holder of the private key) can recover $K_S$. **Enhanced version with authentication and freshness:** $$ \begin{aligned} A \rightarrow B : &\ A, E_B(K_S, T_A) \end{aligned} $$ Here $T_A$ is a timestamp protecting against replay. For stronger guarantees, $K_S$ is signed: $$ A \rightarrow B : \ A, S_A(B, K_S, T_A), E_B(K_S) $$ --- ### Pillar 3 — Key Agreement (Diffie–Hellman, 1976) **The jewel of asymmetric key establishment.** Two parties jointly compute a shared secret without ever transmitting it. **Public Parameters (global, known to all):** - A large prime $p$ (typically 2048 bits). - A generator $g$ of the multiplicative group $\mathbb{Z}_p^{\star}$. **Protocol Flow:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Alice picks secret } a \in \mathbb{Z}_p, \quad & \text{computes } X = g^a \bmod p \\ \text{Bob picks secret } b \in \mathbb{Z}_p, \quad & \text{computes } Y = g^b \bmod p \\ A \rightarrow B : \ & X \\ B \rightarrow A : \ & Y \end{aligned} $$ **Shared secret computed by both:** $$ K_S = Y^a \bmod p = X^b \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p $$ The security relies on the **Computational Diffie–Hellman (CDH) assumption** — given $g^a, g^b$, computing $g^{ab}$ is infeasible. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Mathematical Formulation | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | DH Shared Secret | $K_S = g^{ab} \bmod p$ | Symmetric session key | | 2 | Discrete Log Hardness | Given $y = g^x \bmod p$, find $x$ | Hard problem (DL problem) | | 3 | RSA Encryption | $C = M^e \bmod n$ | Key transport | | 4 | RSA Decryption | $M = C^d \bmod n$ | Key recovery by receiver | | 5 | DH Order | $\vert g \vert = p - 1$ (if $g$ primitive) | Cycle length of group | | 6 | Key Freshness | $K_S \ne K_S'$ across sessions | Forward secrecy | | 7 | Nonce Bound | $N_A$ must be unique & unpredictable | Anti-replay | | 8 | Kerberos Lifetime | $\Delta t = T_{\text{exp}} - T_{\text{issue}}$ | Validity window | | 9 | ECDH Curve Size | $\vert \mathbb{G} \vert \approx 2^{256}$ | Equivalent to RSA-3072 | | 10 | Man-in-Middle Defense | Sign $X$ and $Y$ with long-term key | Authentication binding | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Note on Notation:** In KTU answer scripts, always write $g^{ab} \bmod p$ — never use $\mod$ (without braces). Examiners penalise ambiguous notation heavily. --- ## 2.4 Why These Equations Matter in Engineering - **DH in TLS 1.3:** Every HTTPS handshake uses (EC)DH to establish a fresh session key — protecting billions of daily transactions. - **KDC in Kerberos:** Active Directory in enterprises uses a derivative of Needham–Schroeder for single sign-on across millions of nodes. - **Key Transport in PGP / S/MIME:** Emails are encrypted with a random AES key wrapped by the recipient's RSA public key. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Why Diffie–Hellman Works (Algebraic Equivalence) We explicitly demonstrate that both parties arrive at the *same* session key. ### Setup (Public) $$ p = 23, \quad g = 5 $$ ### Step 1 — Alice's Secret Choice Alice secretly chooses: $$ a = 6 $$ She computes her public value: $$ X = g^a \bmod p = 5^6 \bmod 23 $$ ### Step 2 — Evaluate $5^6 \bmod 23$ By repeated squaring: $$ \begin{aligned} 5^1 \bmod 23 &= 5 \\ 5^2 \bmod 23 &= 25 \bmod 23 = 2 \\ 5^4 \bmod 23 &= (5^2)^2 = 2^2 = 4 \\ 5^6 \bmod 23 &= 5^4 \cdot 5^2 = 4 \cdot 2 = 8 \bmod 23 = 8 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore $X = 8$. Alice sends $X = 8$ to Bob over the (insecure) channel. ### Step 3 — Bob's Secret Choice Bob secretly chooses: $$ b = 15 $$ He computes: $$ Y = g^b \bmod p = 5^{15} \bmod 23 $$ ### Step 4 — Evaluate $5^{15} \bmod 23$ by Modular Exponentiation Using binary expansion $15 = 8 + 4 + 2 + 1$: $$ \begin{aligned} 5^1 \bmod 23 &= 5 \\ 5^2 \bmod 23 &= 2 \quad \text{(from before)} \\ 5^4 \bmod 23 &= 4 \quad \text{(from before)} \\ 5^8 \bmod 23 &= 4^2 = 16 \bmod 23 = 16 \\ 5^{15} \bmod 23 &= 5^8 \cdot 5^4 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 5^1 \\ &= 16 \cdot 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 \bmod 23 \\ &= 640 \bmod 23 \\ 640 \div 23 &= 27 \text{ remainder } 19 \\ \Rightarrow Y &= 19 \end{aligned} $$ Bob sends $Y = 19$ to Alice. ### Step 5 — Alice Computes the Shared Secret $$ \begin{aligned} K_S &= Y^a \bmod p \\ &= 19^6 \bmod 23 \end{aligned} $$ Compute $19^6 \bmod 23$. Note that $19 \equiv -4 \pmod{23}$. $$ \begin{aligned} 19^2 \bmod 23 &= (-4)^2 = 16 \\ 19^4 \bmod 23 &= 16^2 = 256 \bmod 23 \\ 256 \div 23 &= 11 \text{ remainder } 3 \\ \Rightarrow 19^4 \bmod 23 &= 3 \\ 19^6 \bmod 23 &= 19^4 \cdot 19^2 = 3 \cdot 16 = 48 \bmod 23 \\ 48 - 46 &= 2 \\ \Rightarrow K_S &= 2 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Bob Independently Computes the Same Shared Secret $$ \begin{aligned} K_S &= X^b \bmod p \\ &= 8^{15} \bmod 23 \end{aligned} $$ Compute $8^{15} \bmod 23$ using $15 = 8 + 4 + 2 + 1$: $$ \begin{aligned} 8^1 \bmod 23 &= 8 \\ 8^2 \bmod 23 &= 64 \bmod 23 = 18 \\ 8^4 \bmod 23 &= 18^2 = 324 \bmod 23 \\ 324 - 14 \cdot 23 &= 324 - 322 = 2 \\ \Rightarrow 8^4 \bmod 23 &= 2 \\ 8^8 \bmod 23 &= 2^2 = 4 \\ 8^{15} \bmod 23 &= 8^8 \cdot 8^4 \cdot 8^2 \cdot 8^1 \\ &= 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 18 \cdot 8 \bmod 23 \\ &= (4 \cdot 2) \cdot (18 \cdot 8) = 8 \cdot 144 \bmod 23 \\ 144 \bmod 23 &= 144 - 6 \cdot 23 = 144 - 138 = 6 \\ \Rightarrow 8 \cdot 6 &= 48 \bmod 23 = 2 \end{aligned} $$ Hence: $$ \boxed{K_S = 2} $$ Both Alice and Bob obtain $K_S = 2$ — confirming the algebraic equivalence $g^{ab} \equiv g^{ba} \pmod p$. > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** When asked to "demonstrate DH", always work out a *small numerical example* like this one. KTU examiners reward explicit intermediate modular arithmetic steps. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Pillars | Criterion | KDC / Needham–Schroeder | Asymmetric Key Transport | Diffie–Hellman Agreement | |---|---|---|---| | **Trust Anchor** | Online KDC | PKI / CA | Public parameters $p, g$ | | **Online Dependency** | Yes (KDC must be online) | No | No | | **Forward Secrecy** | No (long-term $K_{AS}$ compromise reveals past) | No (if private key leaks, past sessions lost) | Yes (with ephemeral keys) | | **Authentication** | Built-in (via shared keys) | Via certificate chain | Requires signed DH | | **Replay Defense** | Nonces / timestamps | Timestamps / nonces | Static DH is vulnerable | | **Computation** | Cheap (symmetric) | Expensive (RSA) | Moderate (modular exp) | | **Vulnerability** | KDC compromise = total | PKI compromise | Man-in-the-middle | | **Scalability** | Poor ($n$ keys per user) | Good ($n$ public keys) | Excellent | | **Real-World Use** | Kerberos, KDC in banks | PGP, S/MIME | TLS 1.3, Signal | --- ## 3.3 The Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) Attack on Plain DH — Analytical Walkthrough **Attack Vector:** An adversary $M$ intercepts and rewrites messages. **Normal flow:** $$ A \rightarrow B : \ g^a \bmod p \qquad B \rightarrow A : \ g^b \bmod p $$ **Under attack:** $$ \begin{aligned} A \rightarrow M : \ & g^a \bmod p \quad \text{(intercepted)} \\ M \rightarrow B : \ & g^m \bmod p \quad \text{(M's value sent to B)} \\ B \rightarrow M : \ & g^b \bmod p \quad \text{(intercepted)} \\ M \rightarrow A : \ & g^{m'} \bmod p \quad \text{(M's value sent to A)} \end{aligned} $$ **Resulting session keys:** $$ \begin{aligned} K_{AM} &= g^{am} \bmod p \quad \text{(between Alice and M)} \\ K_{BM} &= g^{bm'} \bmod p \quad \text{(between Bob and M)} \end{aligned} $$ Alice believes she shares $K_{AM}$ with Bob;
