Bob believes he shares $K_{BM}$ with Alice. $M$ sits in the middle, decrypting and re-encrypting transparently. **Defense — Station-to-Station (STS) Protocol:** Each party signs its DH public value with a long-term private key, binding identity to the ephemeral value: $$ A \rightarrow B : \ g^a, S_A(g^a, g^b) \\ B \rightarrow A : \ g^b, S_B(g^a, g^b) $$ $M$ cannot forge $S_A$ or $S_B$ without the long-term private keys — MITM defeated. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Needham–Schroeder Symmetric Key Protocol — Sequence Diagram ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant A as Alice participant S as KDC Server participant B as Bob A->>S: Request | A, B, N_A Note right of S: KDC generates fresh K_S S->>A: E_KAS( N_A, B, K_S, E_KBS(K_S, A) ) Note left of A: Alice decrypts,
verifies N_A,
extracts K_S A->>B: Forward | E_KBS(K_S, A) Note right of B: Bob decrypts with K_BS,
recovers K_S B->>A: Challenge | E_KS(N_B) Note left of A: Alice proves knowledge of K_S A->>B: Response | E_KS(N_B - 1) Note right of B: Verified - session open ``` --- ## 4.2 Diffie–Hellman Key Exchange — Block Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD pubParams[Public Parameters: prime p, generator g] pubParams --> alicePick pubParams --> bobPick alicePick["Alice picks secret a"] --> aliceCompute["Compute X = g^a mod p"] bobPick["Bob picks secret b"] --> bobCompute["Compute Y = g^b mod p"] aliceCompute -->|"Send X over insecure channel"| bobCompute bobCompute -->|"Send Y over insecure channel"| aliceCompute bobCompute --> bobSecret["K_S = X^b mod p = g^(ab) mod p"] aliceCompute --> aliceSecret["K_S = Y^a mod p = g^(ab) mod p"] bobSecret --> sharedKey[(Shared Session Key K_S)] aliceSecret --> sharedKey style pubParams fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608 style sharedKey fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d style aliceCompute fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21 style bobCompute fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d ``` --- ## 4.3 Hybrid Key Transport with Authentication — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR aliceGen[Alice generates random K_S] --> aliceWrap[Wrap: E_B K_S with Bob's public key] aliceWrap --> channel[Insecure Channel] channel --> bobUnwrap[Bob unwraps with private key d] bobUnwrap --> bobK[Bob recovers K_S] bobK --> secure[Secure symmetric channel established] pkca[PKI / CA issues Cert_B] -.-> aliceWrap pkca -.-> bobUnwrap style aliceGen fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#a04000 style bobK fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#1e8449 style channel fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,stroke-dasharray:5 5 style secure fill:#a9cce3,stroke:#1f618d ``` --- ## 4.4 MITM Attack Visualisation on Plain DH ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Alice: secret a] -->|g^a| M{Adversary M} M -->|g^m1| B[Bob: secret b] B -->|g^b| M M -->|g^m2| A A -.->|"Thinks K = g^(a m2)"| MISLED1((Alice-M key)) B -.->|"Thinks K = g^(b m1)"| MISLED2((Bob-M key)) M -->|"Owns both keys"| PWNED((Transparent Relay / Full Compromise)) style M fill:#f1948a,stroke:#7b241c style PWNED fill:#c0392b,stroke:#641e16,color:#fff style MISLED1 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21 style MISLED2 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > Always include a **legend** (a, b, m1, m2 are secrets known only to the respective party) when drawing this diagram in your answer script. --- ## 4.5 Comparative Architectural Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Need for shared secret key?] --> online{Is an online
trusted server
acceptable?} online -- Yes --> kdc[KDC Model
Needham-Schroeder / Kerberos] online -- No --> pki{PKI / Certificates
available?} pki -- Yes --> purpose{Purpose?} purpose -- Send a key --> transport[Asymmetric Key Transport
RSA wrap] purpose -- Joint derivation --> dh[Diffie-Hellman Agreement] pki -- No --> pake[Password-Authenticated
Key Exchange
e.g., SRP] dh -- Auth needed --> sts[Station-to-Station
or signed DH] style kdc fill:#a9dfbf style transport fill:#a9dfbf style dh fill:#a9dfbf style sts fill:#a9dfbf style pake fill:#f9e79f style start fill:#d6eaf8 ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define key establishment. Differentiate between key transport and key agreement with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Key Establishment** is the process by which a shared secret key is securely generated, distributed, or mutually computed between two or more parties to enable subsequent cryptographic operations. | Aspect | Key Transport | Key Agreement | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | One party generates the key and securely delivers it to the other(s). | Both parties jointly contribute to the key so that no single party alone knows it. | | **Example** | RSA-encrypted session key: $A \rightarrow B : E_B(K_S)$ | Diffie–Hellman key exchange: $K_S = g^{ab} \bmod p$ | | **Trust Model** | Relies on PKI certificates | Relies on hardness of CDH problem | **(1 mark** for definition, **1 mark** for tabulated distinction, **1 mark** for correct examples.) --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **List any three attacks possible on a key establishment protocol. How does a nonce prevent replay attacks?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Three attacks: 1. **Eavesdropping** — Adversary observes key-exchange messages on a public channel. 2. **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** — Adversary impersonates each party to the other, deriving separate keys with each. 3. **Replay Attack** — Adversary captures and re-sends a valid key-establishment message later. **Nonce defence:** A nonce (number-used-once) is a fresh, unpredictable value (e.g., $N_A$). The receiver stores the nonce and rejects any message bearing a previously seen nonce. This ensures liveness — a replayed message cannot match a freshly generated nonce. **(1 mark** per attack + **bonus 0 mark** for nonce, but examiners accept concise explanation as part of third point.) --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **(14 Marks)** #### (a) Describe the Needham–Schroeder Symmetric Key Protocol with all messages. Discuss the Denning–Sacco attack on it and explain how Kerberos resolves it. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **The Needham–Schroeder Protocol (1978):** Notation: $A$, $B$ — principals; $S$ — trusted server (KDC); $K_{AS}, K_{BS}$ — long-term keys shared with $S$; $K_S$ — fresh session key; $N_A$ — nonce generated by $A$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Message 1. } & A \rightarrow S : A, B, N_A \\ \text{Message 2. } & S \rightarrow A : E_{K_{AS}}(N_A, B, K_S, E_{K_{BS}}(K_S, A)) \\ \text{Message 3. } & A \rightarrow B : E_{K_{BS}}(K_S, A) \\ \text{Message 4. } & B \rightarrow A : E_{K_S}(N_B) \\ \text{Message 5. } & A \rightarrow B : E_{K_S}(N_B - 1) \end{aligned} $$ **Step-by-step explanation:** - **Message 1:** Alice requests a session key with Bob, contributing her nonce $N_A$ for freshness. - **Message 2:** KDC creates a fresh $K_S$, bundles Alice's nonce (so she knows the reply is fresh), and embeds a *sealed envelope* $E_{K_{BS}}(K_S, A)$ for Bob. - **Message 3:** Alice forwards Bob's sealed envelope — Bob decrypts using $K_{BS}$ and obtains $K_S$. - **Message 4:** Bob challenges Alice with a nonce $N_B$ encrypted under $K_S$ — proving Bob has the key. - **Message 5:** Alice responds with $N_B - 1$ — proving Alice has the key and preventing reflection attacks (Message 5 cannot simply be echoed back). **Denning–Sacco Attack (1981):** If an adversary compromises a *single* $K_S$ from a past session, the adversary can replay Message 3 to Bob arbitrarily later. Bob has no time-check and accepts it — re-establishing a session with the attacker. The original protocol lacks timestamps, so freshness cannot be validated. **Kerberos Resolution:** Kerberos extends Needham–Schroeder with: 1. **Timestamps** $T_A$ included in tickets. 2. **Lifetime** field $\Delta t$ bounding ticket validity. 3. **Replay cache** on the KDC rejecting previously seen authenticators. 4. **TGT (Ticket Granting Ticket)** mechanism to avoid repeated KDC contact. Mathematically, Kerberos adds: $$ \text{Ticket}_{AB} = E_{K_B}(K_S, A, T_{\text{issue}}, T_{\text{exp}}) $$ This binds the session to a time window — replay outside $\Delta t$ is rejected. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget to explain *why* the $-1$ in Message 5 is essential. If Alice simply returned $N_B$ unchanged, Bob could be tricked into acting as an *oracle* — he would reflect the nonce back, allowing a third party to mount a reflection attack. Always justify the $-1$ explicitly. **(2 marks** for protocol listing, **2 marks** for explanation, **2 marks** for Denning–Sacco, **1 mark** for Kerberos fix.) --- #### (b) Explain Diffie–Hellman Key Exchange with its mathematical foundation. State two attacks on it and their countermeasures. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Mathematical Foundation:** Given a cyclic group $\mathbb{Z}_p^{\star}$ with generator $g$ of prime order $q$: $$ \text{Hard Problems:} \begin{cases} \text{Discrete Log (DL): Given } y = g^x \bmod p, \text{ find } x. \\ \text{Computational DH (CDH): Given } g^a, g^b, \text{ find } g^{ab}. \\ \text{Decisional DH (DDH): Decide if } z = g^{ab}. \end{cases} $$ **Protocol:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Public: } & p, g \\ A : & \text{choose } a \in \mathbb{Z}_q \\ A \rightarrow B : & X = g^a \bmod p \\ B : & \text{choose } b \in \mathbb{Z}_q \\ B \rightarrow A : & Y = g^b \bmod p \\ A \text{ computes: } & K_S = Y^a \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p \\ B \text{ computes: } & K_S = X^b \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p \end{aligned} $$ **Numerical Example:** (Use the derivation from Section 3.1 with $p = 23, g = 5, a = 6, b = 15$, yielding $K_S = 2$.) **Attack 1 — Man-in-the-Middle:** Adversary $M$ replaces $g^a$ with $g^{m_1}$ to Bob, and $g^b$ with $g^{m_2}$ to Alice. $M$ derives two separate keys and proxies all traffic transparently. **Countermeasure:** Sign $X$ and $Y$ with long-term private keys (e.g., Station-to-Station protocol): $$ A \rightarrow B : g^a, S_A(g^a, g^b) $$ $M$ cannot forge $S_A$ without Alice's private key. **Attack 2 — Small Subgroup / Invalid Parameter Attack:** If $g$ has small order, $K_S$ takes only a few possible values — brute-forceable. Similarly, if $p$ is poorly chosen (smooth), index-calculus attacks succeed. **Countermeasure:** Verify that $p$ is a *safe prime* ($p = 2q + 1$ with $q$ prime) and that $g$ generates the full subgroup of order $q$. Use standards such as NIST P-256 or Curve25519 for ECDH. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Students frequently write "DH provides authentication" — this is *false* for plain DH. Authentication requires explicit signatures or MACs. Mis-stating this costs 1–2 marks in a 7-mark answer. **(2 marks** for math foundation, **2 marks** for protocol + example, **1.5 marks** for attacks, **1.5 marks** for countermeasures.) --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* #### (a) Compare and contrast symmetric key distribution (KDC) with public-key-based key agreement. Mention at least five parameters in your comparison. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | Parameter | KDC / Symmetric | Public-Key / DH | |---|---|---| | **1. Trust Anchor** | Online trusted server | PKI / Certificates | | **2. Number of Keys** | $n$ per user + KDC's $n$ keys | $n$ public keys only | | **3. Online Dependency** | KDC must be online | No online authority | | **4. Computation Cost** | Cheap (symmetric ops) | Expensive (modular exponentiation) | | **5. Forward Secrecy** | Not provided inherently | Provided if ephemeral (EC)DHE used | | **6. Authentication** | Built-in via shared keys | Requires certificate verification | | **7. Single Point of Failure** | KDC compromise = catastrophic | CA compromise = catastrophic | | **8. Replay Defence** | Nonces or timestamps | Signed ephemeral values | | **9. Real-World Use** | Kerberos, banking networks | TLS 1.3, Signal, WhatsApp | **Conclusion:** KDC is preferred in closed enterprise networks with high-trust infrastructure; public-key agreement is preferred for open internet-scale systems. **(1 mark** for identifying 5+ parameters, **1 mark** for accurate descriptions, **1 mark** for balanced conclusion.) --- #### (b) Explain the Station-to-Station (STS) protocol. How does it prevent man-in-the-middle attacks on plain DH? Provide the protocol flow with cryptographic notation. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The STS protocol (Diffie, van Oorschot, Wiener, 1992) binds authentication to DH by signing both ephemeral public values. **Protocol Flow:** $$ \begin{aligned} A \rightarrow B : & g^a \bmod p \\ B \rightarrow A : & g^b \bmod p, E_K(S_B(g^b, g^a)) \\ A \rightarrow B : & E_K(S_A(g^a, g^b)) \end{aligned} $$ Where: - $K$ is a key derived from the DH shared secret (often via a key derivation function). - $S_A, S_B$ are signatures using the long-term private keys of $A$ and $B$. - $E_K$ denotes symmetric encryption under the session key $K$ (e.g., AES). **Why STS defeats MITM:** Consider adversary $M$ trying to interpose: 1. $M$ sends $g^{m_1}$ to Bob pretending to be Alice. 2. Bob replies with $g^b$ and a signed envelope $E_K(S_B(g^b, g^{m_1}))$. 3. **Critical step:** To verify $S_B$, $M$ would need to recompute $K$ — but $M$ only knows $g^{m_1}$ and $g^b$, so $K = g^{b m_1}$. The encrypted signature is bound to $g^a$ (the *original* value), so $M$ cannot produce a signature $S_A(g^a, g^b)$ that Alice would have legitimately produced. 4. **Without Alice's private key**, $M$ cannot forge $S_A$. Bob detects the inconsistency and aborts. **Authentication guarantee:** Each signature ties the *identity* (via the long-term key) to the *fresh ephemeral value* (via DH). The two together produce a *forward-secret* authenticated key exchange. **Optional enhancements:** - Include certificates $\text{Cert}_A, \text{Cert}_B$ in the first message for explicit identity binding. - Use ephemeral ECDH keys (ECDHE) for efficiency. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students write STS with the signatures *outside* the encryption. The original STS paper encrypts the signature for additional confidentiality. Examiners may deduct 0.5 marks if encryption is omitted — verify against your syllabus module. **(2 marks** for protocol listing, **2 marks** for explaining signatures, **2 marks** for MITM defeat reasoning, **1 mark** for enhancements.) --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Key Establishment Protocols** - ✅ **Three families:** (i) Symmetric KDC distribution, (ii) Asymmetric Key Transport, (iii) Asymmetric Key Agreement. - ✅ **Needham–Schroeder** uses 7 messages with a sealed envelope for Bob; **$-$1** in message 5 prevents reflection attacks. - ✅ **Denning–Sacco Attack** exploits absence of timestamps — fixed by Kerberos via $T_{\text{issue}}$, $T_{\text{exp}}$, and replay cache. - ✅ **Diffie–Hellman** relies on the **CDH assumption**: $g^a, g^b \rightarrow g^{ab}$ is hard. - ✅ **Plain DH ≠ Authentication** — needs explicit signing for MITM defence (e.g., STS protocol). - ✅ **Forward Secrecy** is achieved only with **ephemeral** keys (EC)DH**E** in TLS 1.3. - ✅ **Safe primes** ($p = 2q + 1$) prevent small-subgroup attacks on DH. - ✅ **Key Transport** formula: $A \rightarrow B : E_B(K_S, T_A)$; vulnerable to replay if timestamp missing. - ✅ **Man-in-the-Middle** gives the adversary *two* keys ($K_{AM}$ and $K_{BM}$), enabling a transparent relay. - ✅ **Notation discipline:** Use $\bmod$ in math, $\pmod{}$ in LaTeX, never $\mod$. - ✅ **Real-world anchors to mention in answers:** Kerberos (KDC), TLS 1.3 (EC)DH(E), PGP (RSA key transport), Signal (X3DH). - ✅ **Common examiner traps:** Forgetting $-1$, claiming DH provides authentication, omitting timestamps in Needham–Schroeder, confusing $K_{AS}$ (long-term) with $K_S$ (session). - ✅ **Always include a numerical example** in 14-mark answers — a small $p = 23, g = 5$ DH calculation almost guarantees full marks. - ✅ **Memorise the KTU formula sheet table** in Section 2.3 — it covers 90% of Part A derivations. --- > **End of Module 1 Notes — Key Establishment Protocols** > *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme OBE framework and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.*

topics.title: Kerberos
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT633 - SECURE COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Kerberos

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Kerberos** is a **trusted third-party network authentication protocol** that uses **symmetric-key cryptography** to provide strong, mutual authentication between a client and a server over an insecure network. It was originally developed at the **Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT)** as part of **Project Athena** in the 1980s and is standardized in **RFC 4120** (Kerberos Version 5).

The protocol derives its name from the three-headed dog **Cerberus** (Greek mythology), which guards the gates of the underworld. The "three heads" metaphorically represent the three principal parties involved in every Kerberos transaction:

1. **The Client (Principal/User)** — the entity requesting access.
2. **The Application Server (Service)** — the resource being accessed.
3. **The Key Distribution Center (KDC)** — the trusted arbitrator that vouches for both parties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Kerberos is the **de-facto standard** for authentication in distributed systems, most notably implemented in **Microsoft Active Directory (AD)**, Apple OS X, and numerous UNIX/Linux enterprise environments (via MIT Kerberos or Heimdal).

### Formal Definition (RFC 4120)

Kerberos is an *authentication service*, the purpose of which is to provide a means of *verifying the identities of principals* (users or services) on an open and unprotected network, without sending unencrypted secrets over the network.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Security Analogy

Imagine you arrive at a heavily secured international airport. You cannot simply walk to your boarding gate — you must pass through multiple checkpoints:

1. **Check-in Counter (Authentication Server — AS):** You present your passport and ticket. The agent verifies your identity against the government database and gives you a **boarding pass** valid for the entire airport.
2. **Security Gate (Ticket Granting Server — TGS):** When you want to board a specific flight, you present your boarding pass at the gate. The gate agent issues a **flight-specific pass** (a service ticket) that is only valid for that one flight.
3. **Boarding Gate (Application Server):** You scan your flight-specific pass and board the plane. The flight attendant doesn't need to call the government to verify your identity — the pass itself is the proof.

In this analogy:
- **Your passport** = your long-term secret key (derived from your password).
- **The boarding pass** = the **Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT)**.
- **The flight pass** = the **Service Ticket (ST)**.
- **The airport authority (KDC)** = the trusted central authority.
- **The fact that you never show your passport again** = the principle of **Single Sign-On (SSO)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Insight:** Just as your passport is *never* sent over the airport's network of cameras in plaintext, your password is *never* transmitted over the network in Kerberos. Instead, the KDC issues **time-limited, cryptographically sealed tickets**.

## 1.3 Core Design Goals of Kerberos

The Kerberos protocol was designed to satisfy the following **KTU-mandated** security goals:

| Goal | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **Authentication** | Verify the identity of users and services |
| **Authorization-Ready** | Provide the framework for access control decisions |
| **Confidentiality** | Protect session data from eavesdropping |
| **Integrity** | Prevent tampering with authentication exchanges |
| **Mutual Authentication** | Both client *and* server prove their identities |
| **Single Sign-On (SSO)** | Authenticate once, access multiple services |
| **Replay Protection** | Prevent reuse of captured credentials |
| **Transparency** | User should not be aware authentication is happening |

## 1.4 Why Kerberos? The Threat Model

Consider a **plaintext password authentication** scenario:

```
Client → Server: "Hello, I am Alice. My password is: secret123"
```

An attacker on the same network (e.g., via ARP spoofing) can read this traffic trivially. Kerberos solves this by:

1. **Never sending the password** over the network after initial setup.
2. Using a **trusted KDC** to vouch for both parties.
3. Employing **symmetric-key cryptography** to seal short-lived tickets.
4. Using **timestamps and nonces** to prevent replay.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse *authentication* with *authorization*. Kerberos is strictly an **authentication** protocol. It identifies *who* you are, but it does not decide *what* you can do. Authorization is handled by access-control lists (ACLs) on the target server (or in extensions like Microsoft's PAC — Privilege Attribute Certificate).

## 1.5 Kerberos Versioning

| Version | Year | Status |
|---------|------|--------|
| Kerberos v1–v3 | 1980s | Internal MIT use only |
| **Kerberos v4** | 1989 | First public release; used DES in **ECB mode** |
| **Kerberos v5** | 1993–present | Current standard; RFC 1510 → **RFC 4120** (2005) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Differences between v4 and v5:**
> - v4 used **DES in ECB mode** (insecure); v5 supports **CBC, CTS, AES**.
> - v4 had **fixed ticket lifetime**; v5 supports **arbitrary ticket lifetimes**.
> - v4 lacked **renewable, forwardable, and postdated tickets**.
> - v4 didn't support **cross-realm authentication** properly.
> - v4 had a **single-DES dependency**; v5 is **algorithm-agnostic** (supports AES-256, Camellia, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Architectural Components of Kerberos

### 2.1.1 Key Distribution Center (KDC)

The KDC is the **heart of Kerberos**. It is a single, highly secured logical service that runs on a physically hardened server. Conceptually, it has two sub-services:

- **Authentication Server (AS):** Verifies the user's identity during the *first* login. It issues the **Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT)**.
- **Ticket Granting Server (TGS):** Issues **service tickets** for specific application servers, after the client presents a valid TGT.

```
        ┌───────────────── KDC ─────────────────┐
        │                                       │
        │   ┌───────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐  │
        │   │  AS (Auth.    │  │   TGS       │  │
        │   │   Server)     │  │  (Ticket    │  │
        │   │               │  │   Granting  │  │
        │   │ Issues TGT    │  │   Server)   │  │
        │   │               │  │             │  │
        │   │ Knows user    │  │ Knows all   │  │
        │   │ passwords     │  │ service     │  │
        │   │ (hashed)      │  │ secrets     │  │
        │   └───────────────┘  └─────────────┘  │
        │                                       │
        │   Database: Principal, Keys, Policies │
        └───────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 2.1.2 Realm

A **realm** is an administrative domain of authentication. It is conventionally written in **UPPERCASE** (e.g., `KTU.EDU`, `MIT.EDU`, `CORP.EXAMPLE.COM`). Every realm has:

- Its own **KDC**
- A unique **realm name**
- A database of all principals and their keys

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Realm Authentication:** Two realms can establish **inter-realm keys**. A client in realm A can obtain a ticket for a service in realm B by walking through a chain of TGS servers. This is the foundation of **federated trust** in Kerberos.

### 2.1.3 Principal

A **principal** is the unique name of any entity (user or service) that can authenticate. The format is:

```
primary/instance@REALM
```

Examples:
- `alice@KTU.EDU` — a user
- `admin/admin@KTU.EDU` — a privileged user
- `host/server1.ktu.edu@KTU.EDU` — a host principal
- `HTTP/web.ktu.edu@KTU.EDU` — a service principal

### 2.1.4 Ticket

A **ticket** is a sealed envelope issued by a Kerberos server. It contains:

| Field | Meaning |
|-------|---------|
| **S** | Service Principal Name (target server) |
| **c** | Client Principal Name |
| **addr** | IP addresses from which the ticket is valid |
| **t_valid** | Ticket start time |
| **t_until** | Ticket end time (lifetime) |
| **K_s,c** | Random session key shared between service and client |
| **Flags** | Renewable, forwardable, proxiable, etc. |
| **Authz-data** | Authorization data (e.g., group memberships, PAC) |

The ticket is **encrypted with the long-term secret key of the target service**, so only the service can decrypt it.

### 2.1.5 Authenticator

An **authenticator** is a record constructed *by the client* containing:

$$ A_c = E(K_{s,c}, \, c \, \| \, t_{current} \, \| \, \text{optional checksum}) $$

It proves the client knows the session key `K_s,c` and provides a fresh timestamp to prevent replay.

### 2.1.6 Session Key

A **session key** is a **short-lived symmetric key** generated by the KDC and shared between two parties. There are three types of session keys in any Kerberos exchange:

| Session Key | Shared Between | Purpose |
|-------------|----------------|---------|
| **K_c,tgs** | Client and TGS | Protects TGS exchange |
| **K_c,s** | Client and Service | Protects client/server exchange |
| **K_tgs,s** | TGS and Service | Encrypted into the service ticket |

## 2.2 Cryptographic Primitives Used

Kerberos v5 supports a **pluggable cryptography framework** (the *cryptosystem negotiation* is done via *etype* — encryption type):

| etype | Algorithm | Key Size | Status |
|-------|-----------|----------|--------|
| `des-cbc-crc` | DES-CBC + CRC-32 | **56-bit** | Deprecated |
| `des3-cbc-sha1` | 3DES-CBC + HMAC-SHA1 | **168-bit** | Legacy |
| `aes128-cts-hmac-sha1-96` | AES-128-CTS + HMAC-SHA1-96 | **128-bit** | Common |
| `aes256-cts-hmac-sha1-96` | AES-256-CTS + HMAC-SHA1-96 | **256-bit** | Recommended |
| `aes128-cts-hmac-sha256-128` | AES-128-CTS + HMAC-SHA256 | **128-bit** | Modern |
| `aes256-cts-hmac-sha384-192` | AES-256-CTS + HMAC-SHA384 | **256-bit** | Modern |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for KTU Exams:** The original Kerberos v4 used **DES in ECB mode**, which was found to be cryptographically weak. Kerberos v5 supports **Cipher-Text Stealing (CTS)** mode, which is a CBC variant that handles plaintext of arbitrary length without padding.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table summarizes every key notation and equation you must memorize for the exam.

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning |
|-------------------|---------|
| $E(K, M)$ | Symmetric encryption of message $M$ under key $K$ |
| $D(K, C)$ | Symmetric decryption of ciphertext $C$ under key $K$ |
| $H(M)$ | Cryptographic hash of $M$ |
| $K_c$ | Client's long-term secret key (derived from password) |
| $K_s$ | Server's long-term secret key |
| $K_{tgs}$ | TGS's long-term secret key |
| $K_{c,tgs}$ | Session key between client and TGS |
| $K_{c,s}$ | Session key between client and service $s$ |
| $T_{c,tgs}$ | TGT (Ticket Granting Ticket) |
| $T_{c,s}$ | Service ticket |
| $A_{c,tgs}$ | Authenticator from client to TGS |
| $A_{c,s}$ | Authenticator from client to service |
| $N_1, N_2$ | Nonces (random numbers) |
| $t_1, t_2$ | Timestamps |

### Core Cryptographic Equations

**Ticket Granting Ticket (issued by AS):**
$$ T_{c,tgs} = E(K_{tgs}, \, s_{tgs} \, \| \, c \, \| \, \text{addr} \, \| \, t_{valid} \, \| \, t_{until} \, \| \, K_{c,tgs} \, \| \, \text{flags}) $$

**Service Ticket (issued by TGS):**
$$ T_{c,s} = E(K_{s}, \, s \, \| \, c \, \| \, \text{addr} \, \| \, t_{valid} \, \| \, t_{until} \, \| \, K_{c,s} \, \| \, \text{flags}) $$

**Client Authenticator:**
$$ A_{c,s} = E(K_{c,s}, \, c \, \| \, t_{now}) $$

**Mutual Authentication Response (from server):**
$$ R_s = E(K_{c,s}, \, t_{now} + 1) $$

> [!NOTE]
> The server increments the client's timestamp by 1, encrypts it, and sends it back. This proves the server knew $K_{c,s}$ and therefore is genuine. This is the essence of **mutual authentication** in Kerberos.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Kerberos is not just a textbook protocol — it is the authentication backbone of:

| Domain | Use Case |
|--------|----------|
| **Windows Active Directory** | Default domain authentication since Windows 2000 |
| **Hadoop / Big Data** | Securing HDFS, HBase, Kafka in cluster environments |
| **NFSv4** | Securing Network File System access |
| **SSH (with GSSAPI)** | Kerberos-based SSH keyless login |
| **SMTP / IMAP** | Mail server authentication |
| **HTTP (SPNEGO)** | Web SSO via the Negotiate protocol |
| **Database (PostgreSQL, Oracle)** | Native Kerberos auth plugins |
| **Container Orchestration** | Kubernetes uses ServiceAccount tokens that can integrate with KDC |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Tip:** When designing enterprise systems, the KDC becomes a **single point of failure**. In production, multiple **read-only KDC replicas** are deployed (kprop + kpropd synchronization) and **load-balanced** using DNS SRV records (`_kerberos._tcp.REALM`). High-availability is achieved by **Kerberos slave propagation** every few minutes.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 The Three-Phase Kerberos Authentication Exchange — Complete Derivation

The Kerberos protocol (version 5) operates in **three logical phases**. We will derive each message and explain every cryptographic decision.

### 3.1.1 PHASE 1: Authentication Service Exchange (AS-REQ / AS-REP)

**Objective:** Authenticate the client *once* and issue a TGT that the client can later use to request service tickets — without re-sending the password.

#### Step 1: Client → AS Request

The client constructs an **AS-REQ** packet. This packet is sent in **cleartext** because it does not contain any secret information:

$$ \text{AS-REQ} = (c \, \| \, tgs \, \| \, \text{realm}_c \, \| \, N_1 \, \| \, \text{times} \, \| \, \text{etype-list}) $$

**Field-by-field explanation:**

- $c$ — the client principal name (e.g., `alice@KTU.EDU`). Sent in cleartext is acceptable because the KDC needs to know *who* is asking.
- $tgs$ — the TGS principal name (e.g., `krbtgt/KTU.EDU`).
- $\text{realm}_c$ — the realm the client is authenticating into.
- $N_1$ — a **nonce**, a fresh random number generated by the client to bind the response to this session and prevent replay.
- $\text{times}$ — requested ticket lifetime parameters: $t_{start}$, $t_{until}$, $t_{renew_till}$.
- $\text{etype-list}$ — list of supported encryption types so the KDC can pick one it also supports.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the request unencrypted?** Because at this point, the client *has not yet* proven its identity. The password-derived key $K_c$ is computed only on the client side; the server cannot encrypt to it. So the client must request *something* (a TGT) before any key-sharing can happen.

#### Step 2: AS → Client Response

The AS looks up the client in its database, retrieves $K_c$ (the long-term key derived from the user's password), and:

1. Generates a fresh random **session key** $K_{c,tgs}$ (typically **128 bits** if AES is used).
2. Constructs the **TGT** for the TGS:

$$ T_{c,tgs} = E(K_{tgs}, \, tgs \, \| \, c \, \| \, \text{addr} \, \| \, t_{valid} \, \| \, t_{until} \, \| \, K_{c,tgs} \, \| \, \text{flags}) $$

3. Encrypts $K_{c,tgs}$ and other fields under $K_c$ to create the "enc-part":

$$ E(K_c, \, K_{c,tgs} \, \| \, t_{valid} \, \| \, t_{until} \, \| \, N_1 \, \| \, \text{realm}_c \, \| \, c \, \| \, \text{flags}) $$

The full **AS-REP** is:

$$ \text{AS-REP} = (T_{c,tgs} \, \| \, E(K_c, \, K_{c,tgs} \, \| \, t_{valid} \, \| \, t_{until} \, \| \, N_1 \, \| \, \text{realm}_c \, \| \, c \, \| \, \text{flags})) $$

#### Step 3: Client-Side Decryption

The client receives AS-REP and:

1. Prompts the user for password (if not already cached).
2. Derives $K_c$ from the password using the *string-to-key* function:

$$ K_c = \text{string2key}(\text{password}, \, \text{salt}, \, \text{params}) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In AES-256:** the function is `PBKDF2 + SHA-1` (or in newer implementations, `PBKDF2 + SHA-256`) with **4096 iterations**. This is what makes a strong password *necessary* — a weak password can be brute-forced offline by an attacker who captures the AS-REP (this is the basis of **AS-REP Roasting** attacks).

3. Decrypts the enc-part using $K_c$ to recover $K_{c,tgs}$.
4. Verifies the nonce $N_1$ matches the one it sent.
5. Stores $K_{c,tgs}$ and the TGT in a **credentials cache** (e.g., `/tmp/krb5cc_1000` on Linux, or the `lsass.exe` process memory on Windows).

> [!TIP]
> **Credentials Cache:** On Windows, this is the **LSA (Local Security Authority) Subsystem**. On Linux/UNIX, it is the file `ccache` managed by the `kinit` command. The ticket remains valid until $t_{until}$ — this is **Single Sign-On (SSO)**.

### 3.1.2 PHASE 2: Ticket Granting Service Exchange (TGS-REQ / TGS-REP)

**Objective:** Exchange the TGT for a service-specific ticket, without re-authenticating to the KDC.

#### Step 1: Client → TGS Request

The client constructs a fresh **authenticator** to prove it is the legitimate owner of the TGT:

$$ A_{c,tgs} = E(K_{c,tgs}, \, c \, \| \, t_{now1}) $$

Then the **TGS-REQ** is:

$$ \text{TGS-REQ} = (T_{c,tgs} \, \| \, s \, \| \, \text{authz-data} \, \| \, N_2 \, \| \, \text{times} \, \| \, A_{c,tgs}) $$

**Why an authenticator is needed:**
- The TGT alone doesn't prove the *present* presence of the client. The TGT could have been stolen from disk.
- The authenticator, encrypted with $K_{c,tgs}$, is fresh and proves the client currently knows $K_{c,tgs}$ — and that the time is recent.

#### Step 2: TGS Decryption and Verification

The TGS:

1. Decrypts $T_{c,tgs}$ using its long-term $K_{tgs}$ → extracts $K_{c,tgs}$, $c$, and ticket parameters.
2. Decrypts $A_{c,tgs}$ using the extracted $K_{c,tgs}$.
3. Verifies: $c$ in $T_{c,tgs}$ matches $c$ in $A_{c,tgs}$, $t_{now1}$ is within the allowed clock skew (default **5 minutes**), and the ticket has not expired.
4. If all checks pass, generates a new session key $K_{c,s}$ and constructs the **service ticket**:

$$ T_{c,s} = E(K_s, \, s \, \| \, c \, \| \, \text{addr} \, \| \, t_{valid} \, \| \, t_{until} \, \| \, K_{c,s} \, \| \, \text{flags}) $$

5. Encrypts $K_{c,s}$ under $K_{c,tgs}$:

$$ E(K_{c,tgs}, \, K_{c,s} \, \| \, t_{valid} \, \| \, t_{until} \, \| \, N_2 \, \| \, \text{realm}_s \, \| \, s \, \| \, \text{flags}) $$

6. Sends the full **TGS-REP** to the client.

#### Step 3: Client Decryption

The client decrypts the enc-part with $K_{c,tgs}$, recovers $K_{c,s}$, and stores it alongside $T_{c,s}$. It then *destroys* $K_{c,tgs}$ (or retains it only if other TGS exchanges are pending).

### 3.1.3 PHASE 3: Client/Server Authentication (AP-REQ / AP-REP)

**Objective:** Authenticate the client to the service and (optionally) authenticate the service back to the client.

#### Step 1: Client → Service Request

The client sends:

$$ \text{AP-REQ} = (T_{c,s} \, \| \, A_{c,s}) $$

where the new authenticator is:

$$ A_{c,s} = E(K_{c,s}, \, c \, \| \, t_{now2}) $$

#### Step 2: Server Verification

The service:

1. Decrypts $T_{c,s}$ using its long-term $K_s$ → extracts $K_{c,s}$.
2. Decrypts $A_{c,s}$ using $K_{c,s}$.
3. Verifies: $c$ matches, $t_{now2}$ is recent, ticket not expired, ticket valid for this client (e.g., IP address in `addr` field matches).

#### Step 3: Mutual Authentication (AP-REP)

If the `mutual-required` flag is set in the ticket, the server replies:

$$ \text{AP-REP} = E(K_{c,s}, \, t_{now2} + 1) $$

The client decrypts, verifies that the timestamp is exactly $t_{now2} + 1$. This proves the server *knew* $K_{c,s}$ and is therefore the genuine service.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the +1 added?** It ensures the response is not a replay of a previous authenticator. By adding 1, the message is unique to this session and cannot be confused with any other valid timestamp.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Ticket Types in Kerberos

| Ticket Type | Issued By | Encrypting Key | Used For | Lifetime |
|-------------|-----------|----------------|----------|----------|
| **TGT** (Ticket Granting Ticket) | AS | $K_{tgs}$ (TGS long-term key) | Authenticating to the TGS | Typically **10–24 hours** |
| **Service Ticket (ST)** | TGS | $K_s$ (Service long-term key) | Authenticating to a specific service | Typically **1–8 hours** |
| **Renewable Ticket** | AS / TGS | Same as base ticket | Can be renewed before expiry | Up to **1 week** |
| **Forwardable Ticket** | AS / TGS | Same as base ticket | Allows delegation to another host | Same as base |
| **Postdated Ticket** | AS / TGS | Same as base ticket | Active only from a future time | Same as base |

## 3.3 Derivation of the Mutual Authentication Property

Let us formally prove that **only the legitimate server** can produce a valid $R_s$.

**Premise 1 (Ticket confidentiality):** $T_{c,s}$ is encrypted with $K_s$. The attacker $\mathcal{A}$ cannot decrypt $T_{c,s}$ to learn $K_{c,s}$.

**Premise 2 (Authenticator freshness):** $A_{c,s}$ contains $t_{now2}$, which the server validates within clock skew $\delta$:

$$ t_{now,server} - t_{now2} \le \delta $$

**Premise 3 (Server response uniqueness):** The server responds with $E(K_{c,s}, t_{now2} + 1)$. The attacker $\mathcal{A}$ cannot produce this without knowing $K_{c,s}$.

**Conclusion:** Since $K_{c,s}$ is known only to the legitimate client and the legitimate service (because $K_s$ is the service's long-term secret), only the genuine service can produce the response. Therefore, **the mutual authentication property holds**.

## 3.4 Failure & Error Cases — The K_Error Packet

Kerberos defines standardized **error codes** (krberr) returned in the `e-data` field:

| Error Code | Mnemonic | Meaning |
|------------|----------|---------|
| 0 | `KDC_ERR_NONE` | No error |
| 1 | `KDC_ERR_NAME_EXP` | Client principal unknown |
| 6 | `KDC_ERR_C_PRINCIPAL_UNKNOWN` | Client not in database |
| 14 | `KDC_ERR_ETYPE_NOSUPP` | Encryption type not supported |
| 18 | `KDC_ERR_CLIENT_REVOKED` | Client credentials revoked |
| 31 | `KRB_AP_ERR_SKEW` | Clock skew too great |
| 33 | `KRB_AP_ERR_BADVERSION` | Wrong Kerberos version |
| 36 | `KRB_AP_ERR_MODIFIED` | Ticket modified in transit |
| 37 | `KRB_AP_ERR_BADORDER` | Authenticator and ticket don't match |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Detail:** The `KRB_AP_ERR_MODIFIED` error is a critical defensive signal. It means the ticket was altered in transit (impossible without breaking the encryption), OR the client sent the ticket to the *wrong* server (whose key doesn't match). In the latter case, the wrong server tries to decrypt the ticket and fails, returning this error.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Complete Kerberos Authentication Sequence (Mermaid Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant C as Client (Alice)
    participant AS as Authentication Server
    participant TGS as Ticket Granting Server
    participant S as Application Server

    Note over C: Initial state: User enters password<br/>Client derives K_c from password

    C->>AS: AS-REQ = (c, tgs, realm_c, N1, times, etype-list)
    Note over AS: Looks up K_c, K_tgs in database<br/>Generates K_c,tgs (random 128-bit)

    AS-->>C: AS-REP = (T_c,tgs, E(K_c, {K_c,tgs, N1, t_until, ...}))
    Note over C: Derives K_c from password<br/>Decrypts enc-part<br/>Stores T_c,tgs and K_c,tgs in cache<br/>SSO session established

    Note over C,S: PHASE 2: Service Ticket Request

    C->>TGS: TGS-REQ = (T_c,tgs, s, N2, A_c,tgs)
    Note over TGS: Decrypts T_c,tgs with K_tgs<br/>Recovers K_c,tgs<br/>Decrypts A_c,tgs<br/>Verifies c, timestamp, lifetime<br/>Generates K_c,s

    TGS-->>C: TGS-REP = (T_c,s, E(K_c,tgs, {K_c,s, N2, t_until, ...}))
    Note over C: Decrypts with K_c,tgs<br/>Stores K_c,s and T_c,s

    Note over C,S: PHASE 3: Client/Server Authentication

    C->>S: AP-REQ = (T_c,s, A_c,s)
    Note over S: Decrypts T_c,s with K_s<br/>Recovers K_c,s<br/>Decrypts A_c,s<br/>Verifies c, timestamp, lifetime, addr

    alt mutual_required = true
        S-->>C: AP-REP = E(K_c,s, t_now2 + 1)
        Note over C: Decrypts, verifies +1<br/>Mutual authentication complete
    else mutual_required = false
        S-->>C: Service access granted
    end

    Note over C,S: Encrypted session using K_c,s
```

## 4.2 Kerberos KDC Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph KDC["KDC - Key Distribution Center"]
        direction TB
        subgraph DB["Kerberos Database (e.g., LDAP, DB2)"]
            P["Principal Database<br/>alice, bob, host/server1, HTTP/web"]
            K["Long-term Keys<br/>K_c, K_s, K_tgs (encrypted at rest)"]
            POL["Policies<br/>Password policy, ticket lifetime"]
        end

        subgraph AS["Authentication Server (AS)"]
            AS1["Validates client identity"]
            AS2["Generates K_c,tgs session key"]
            AS3["Issues TGT encrypted with K_tgs"]
        end

        subgraph TGS["Ticket Granting Server (TGS)"]
            TGS1["Validates TGT + Authenticator"]
            TGS2["Authorizes service access"]
            TGS3["Generates K_c,s session key"]
            TGS4["Issues Service Ticket encrypted with K_s"]
        end

        KDC_CLOCK["KDC Time Source<br/>NTP synchronized, skew < 5 min"]
    end

    CLIENT["Client Workstation<br/>(kinit / LSASS)"] -->|"AS-REQ (cleartext)"| AS
    AS -->|"AS-REP (encrypted with K_c)"| CLIENT
    CLIENT -->|"TGS-REQ (TGT + Authenticator)"| TGS
    TGS -->|"TGS-REP (Service Ticket)"| CLIENT
    CLIENT -->|"AP-REQ (Service Ticket + Authenticator)"| SERVICE["Application Server<br/>(sshd, httpd, smbd, ...)"]
    SERVICE -->|"AP-REP (optional)"| CLIENT
```

## 4.3 Ticket Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Requested: Client sends AS-REQ
    Requested --> Issued_AS: AS validates user, issues TGT
    Issued_AS --> Stored: Client decrypts, stores in ccache
    Stored --> TGS_Request: Client wants service access
    TGS_Request --> Issued_TGS: TGS validates, issues Service Ticket
    Issued_TGS --> Service_Auth: Client sends AP-REQ to service
    Service_Auth --> Mutual_Auth_Success: Server returns AP-REP (mutual)
    Service_Auth --> Auth_Success: Server grants access (no mutual)
    Mutual_Auth_Success --> Expired: After t_until
    Auth_Success --> Expired: After t_until
    Expired --> TGS_Request: If renewable, request renewal
    Expired --> [*]: Final expiry
    Stored --> Renewed: TGS issues renewal
    Renewed --> TGS_Request: Continue with new validity window
```

## 4.4 Attack Surface Map (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Attacker Capabilities"] --> B["Passive Eavesdropping"]
    A --> C["Active MITM"]
    A --> D["Credential Theft"]

    B --> B1["AS-REP Roasting<br/>Offline crack of K_c"]
    B1 --> B1A["Defended: Strong passwords,<br/>AES-256 etype"]

    C --> C1["Replay Attack"]
    C1 --> C1A["Defended: Timestamps,<br/>nonces, ticket lifetime"]

    C --> C2["MITM via forged AS-REP"]
    C2 --> C2A["Defended: Mutual auth,<br/>PKINIT for KDC verification"]

    D --> D1["Pass-the-Ticket (PtT)"]
    D1 --> D1A["Defended: TGT signing keys,<br/>credential guard, AES-256"]

    D --> D2["Kerberoasting"]
    D2 --> D2A["Defended: Strong service<br/>account passwords, AES"]

    D --> D3["Golden Ticket Attack"]
    D3 --> D3A["Defended: krbtgt password<br/>rotation every 6 months"]

    D --> D4["Silver Ticket Attack"]
    D4 --> D4A["Defended: Service account<br/>monitoring, PAC validation"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List and briefly explain the three main components of the Key Distribution Center (KDC) in Kerberos.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Key Distribution Center (KDC) is the trusted third party in Kerberos and consists of two logical sub-services backed by a database:

1. **Authentication Server (AS)** — (1 mark) The first point of contact. It verifies the client's identity during initial login and issues a **Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT)** encrypted with the TGS's long-term secret key. The client cannot read the TGT; it can only pass it back to the TGS.

2. **Ticket Granting Server (TGS)** — (1 mark) Accepts a valid TGT + authenticator from the client and issues **service-specific tickets** for the application server requested. The TGS performs the second-level authentication and authorization step.

3. **Kerberos Database** — (1 mark) A secure store (e.g., LDAP, DB2) containing all principal names and their long-term secret keys (hashed/encrypted at rest). Both the AS and TGS query this database. The KDC also uses a **clock synchronization service** (NTP) because Kerberos is timestamp-based.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention the **Database** as a third component. Examiners will deduct **1 mark** if the KDC is described as having only AS + TGS. Mention the database explicitly.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What is a Kerberos Realm? How is cross-realm authentication achieved?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Kerberos Realm** (1 mark) is an administrative domain that defines the boundary of authentication. It is conventionally written in **UPPERCASE** (e.g., `KTU.EDU`). Every realm has its own KDC, principal database, and policies. The realm name appears as the suffix of every principal (`alice@KTU.EDU`).

**Cross-realm authentication** (2 marks) is achieved by establishing **inter-realm keys**. A KDC in realm A shares a secret key with the KDC in realm B. To access a service in realm B, the client in realm A:

1. Requests a **cross-realm TGT** from its local TGS (for `krbtgt/B@A`).
2. Presents this cross-realm TGT to a TGS in realm B.
3. Receives a service ticket for the target service in realm B.

This forms a **chain of trust** and can be extended to multiple realms (transitive). It is the foundation of **federated authentication** in Kerberos.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, Q1a/b]**
**Explain the complete Kerberos authentication protocol with a neat sequence diagram. List the three phases and detail the cryptographic operations in each phase.**

#### Part (a) — Phases 1 & 2 of Kerberos Authentication (7 marks)

**Phase 1: Authentication Service Exchange (AS-REQ / AS-REP)** — (3.5 marks)

1. **Client → AS Request (AS-REQ):**
   The client sends its identity, the TGS principal name, a fresh nonce $N_1$, requested lifetime parameters, and a list of supported encryption types. This message is in **cleartext** because no shared secret yet exists.

2. **AS → Client Response (AS-REP):**
   The AS retrieves the client's key $K_c$ from the database, generates a new session key $K_{c,tgs}$, and constructs:
   - A **TGT** encrypted with $K_{tgs}$ (TGS's long-term key).
   - A copy of $K_{c,tgs}$ encrypted with $K_c$ (client's long-term key), along with $N_1$ and the lifetime.

3. **Client Processing:**
   The client derives $K_c$ from the user's password using a string-to-key function, decrypts the enc-part, verifies the nonce, and stores $K_{c,tgs}$ and the TGT in its credentials cache. **The password is never transmitted over the network.**

**Phase 2: Ticket Granting Service Exchange (TGS-REQ / TGS-REP)** — (3.5 marks)

1. **Client → TGS Request (TGS-REQ):**
   The client constructs an **authenticator** $A_{c,tgs} = E(K_{c,tgs}, c \, \| \, t_{now1})$ and sends: TGT, service principal name $s$, a new nonce $N_2$, and the authenticator.

2. **TGS → Client Response (TGS-REP):**
   The TGS decrypts the TGT with $K_{tgs}$ to recover $K_{c,tgs}$, decrypts the authenticator, verifies the client ID and timestamp. If valid, it generates a new session key $K_{c,s}$ and returns:
   - A **service ticket** $T_{c,s} = E(K_s, s \, \| \, c \, \| \, \text{addr} \, \| \, t_{until} \, \| \, K_{c,s})$.
   - $K_{c,s}$ encrypted under $K_{c,tgs}$.

3. The client decrypts and stores $K_{c,s}$ and $T_{c,s}$.

#### Part (b) — Phase 3, Mutual Authentication, and Defensive Properties (7 marks)

**Phase 3: Client/Server Authentication (AP-REQ / AP-REP)** — (3 marks)

1. **Client → Service (AP-REQ):**
   Sends $T_{c,s}$ and a new authenticator $A_{c,s} = E(K_{c,s}, c \, \| \, t_{now2})$.
2. **Service Verification:**
   The service decrypts $T_{c,s}$ with $K_s$, recovers $K_{c,s}$, decrypts the authenticator, and verifies identity + freshness.
3. **Mutual Authentication (optional but standard):**
   The service returns $E(K_{c,s}, t_{now2} + 1)$. The client verifies the $+1$ increment, proving the server knows $K_{c,s}$.

**Cryptographic Operations Summary** — (2 marks)

| Step | Plaintext | Cipher Key | Algorithm |
|------|-----------|------------|-----------|
| AS-REP enc-part | $K_{c,tgs}$ | $K_c$ | AES-256-CTS (typical) |
| TGT | $K_{c,tgs}$ | $K_{tgs}$ | AES-256-CTS |
| Authenticator | $c, t_{now}$ | $K_{c,tgs}$ or $K_{c,s}$ | AES-256-CTS |
| Service Ticket | $K_{c,s}$ | $K_s$ | AES-256-CTS |

**Why Kerberos is Secure — Defensive Properties** — (2 marks)

1. **Confidentiality:** Passwords never sent in cleartext.
2. **Replay Protection:** Timestamps + nonces + ticket lifetime.
3. **Mutual Authentication:** Server must prove knowledge of $K_{c,s}$.
4. **Single Sign-On:** One TGT, many service tickets.
5. **Forward Secrecy:** Each session uses a unique random $K_{c,s}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 1, Q2a/b]**
**Discuss the limitations and vulnerabilities of the Kerberos protocol. With a neat diagram, explain how Kerberoasting and Golden Ticket attacks work and the mitigations against them.**

#### Part (a) — Limitations and Vulnerabilities of Kerberos (7 marks)

**Architectural Limitations:** (3 marks)

1. **Single Point of Failure — The KDC:** If the KDC goes down, no new authentication can occur. Tickets already issued continue to work until expiry, but new ticket requests fail. Mitigation: Deploy **KDC slaves/replicas** with database replication (kprop).

2. **Clock Synchronization Dependency:** All parties must have synchronized clocks within a small skew (default **5 minutes**). Misconfigured time leads to `KRB_AP_ERR_SKEW` errors. Mitigation: NTP with authentication, monitor clock drift.

3. **Secret-Key Distribution Problem:** Kerberos uses symmetric keys, so each service must share a key with the KDC. Deploying thousands of services requires significant key management overhead.

4. **No Public-Key Cryptography (in classic v5):** Asymmetric operations are an extension (**PKINIT**), not native. Without PKINIT, password-derived keys are vulnerable to **offline dictionary attacks**.

**Cryptographic Vulnerabilities:** (2 marks)

1. **DES Weakness in v4:** Original Kerberos v4 used DES in **ECB mode**, which is broken. v5 supports modern AES but legacy deployments remain vulnerable.
2. **Replay Window:** Although timestamps limit replay, a clock skew of ±5 minutes creates a 10-minute window during which a captured authenticator can be replayed.

**Operational Vulnerabilities:** (2 marks)

1. **Offline Brute-Force (AS-REP Roasting):** An attacker can request an AS-REP for any account (especially those with **pre-authentication disabled**) and crack the password offline.
2. **Service Account Weakness (Kerberoasting):** Service account passwords are often weak and can be cracked offline from the TGS-REP.
3. **Stealth of Compromise:** Kerberos is silent — a stolen ticket is indistinguishable from a legitimate one.

#### Part (b) — Kerberoasting & Golden Ticket Attacks (7 marks)

**Attack 1: Kerberoasting** — (3.5 marks)

**Setup:** An attacker with a valid domain user account requests a TGS for any service account in the domain (e.g., `MSSQLSvc/db1`).

**Mechanism:**
1. Attacker has a valid TGT (low-privilege user).
2. Attacker requests a TGS for a service principal name (SPN). The TGS-REP contains a service ticket encrypted with the **service account's long-term key** ($K_s$).
3. Since service accounts often have weak passwords (10–14 chars, complex but predictable patterns), the attacker extracts the ticket and performs an **offline brute-force**:
   - Guess password $p$.
   - Derive $K_s^{guess} = \text{string2key}(p)$.
   - Try $D(K_s^{guess}, T_{c,s})$.
   - If decryption succeeds, the password is found.
4. With the cracked service account password, the attacker can impersonate that service and escalate privileges.

**Mitigation:** (built into the 3.5 marks)
- Use **long, random service account passwords** (≥ 25 characters).
- Set service accounts to use **AES-256** (RC4 tickets crack much faster).
- Use **Group Managed Service Accounts (gMSAs)** in Windows AD with auto-rotating 240-character passwords.

**Attack 2: Golden Ticket Attack** — (3.5 marks)

**Setup:** Attacker gains **Domain Admin** (or equivalent) privileges — e.g., by compromising the DC, dumping `NTDS.dit`, or extracting the `krbtgt` account hash.

**Mechanism:**
1. Attacker extracts the **NT hash of the `krbtgt` account** (the account used by the TGS itself).
2. The attacker forges a TGT with arbitrary claims: any user, any group membership, any lifetime (e.g., 10 years).
3. Because the forged TGT is encrypted with the real $K_{tgs}$ (= `krbtgt` hash), it is **cryptographically indistinguishable** from a legitimate one.
4. The attacker can then request TGS tickets for *any* service in *any* realm that trusts the compromised one — full domain (and forest) compromise.

**Diagram (Mermaid):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1["Attacker compromises DC<br/>Dumps NTDS.dit"] --> A2["Extract krbtgt NT hash"]
    A2 --> A3["Forge TGT for fake-admin@REALM<br/>Group: Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins<br/>Lifetime: 10 years"]
    A3 --> A4["Submit forged TGT to TGS<br/>(via Mimikatz 'kerberos::golden')"]
    A4 --> A5["TGS accepts forged TGT<br/>(signed with real krbtgt key)"]
    A5 --> A6["TGS issues service ticket<br/>for ANY service"]
    A6 --> A7["Attacker has full domain persistence<br/>+ ticket valid for years"]

    A2 -.->|"Defended by"| D1["krbtgt password rotation<br/>twice (with delay) every 6 months"]
    A3 -.->|"Defended by"| D2["Monitor anomalous TGS_REQ<br/>with impossible travel / unusual ticket flags"]
    A5 -.->|"Defended by"| D3["Credential Guard, Protected Users group<br/>Restrict DC logon, PAW"]
```

**Mitigation:**
- **Rotate the `krbtgt` account password twice** every 6 months (the double rotation ensures AES and RC4 keys are both reset).
- Use **Credential Guard** to prevent hash dumping on endpoints.
- Deploy **Privileged Access Workstations (PAWs)** for admins.
- Monitor for **anomalous ticket requests** (e.g., TGTs with 10-year lifetimes, unusual SPNs).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. When describing **Golden Ticket**, do not confuse with **Silver Ticket**:
>    - **Golden** = forging TGT (requires `krbtgt` hash, domain-wide impact).
>    - **Silver** = forging service ticket (requires service account hash, single-service impact).
> 2. Always mention **krbtgt rotation** as the *primary* mitigation for Golden Ticket — examiners specifically look for this.
> 3. For **Kerberoasting**, mentioning the term *"offline brute-force"* is mandatory; saying only "weak password" is worth only partial credit.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Viva & Exam:**

- [x] **Kerberos = trusted third-party authentication using symmetric keys.**
- [x] **Three heads of Cerberus** = Client, Server, KDC.
- [x] **Three phases:** AS Exchange, TGS Exchange, CS Exchange.
- [x] **TGT** = Ticket Granting Ticket (issued by AS, used to talk to TGS).
- [x] **Service Ticket** = issued by TGS, used to talk to a specific service.
- [x] **Authenticator** = client-generated, encrypted with session key, contains client ID + timestamp.
- [x] **Password is NEVER sent in cleartext** — only the initial key derivation happens locally.
- [x] **Mutual authentication** = server replies with $E(K_{c,s}, t_{now} + 1)$.
- [x] **Replay protection** = timestamps + nonces + ticket lifetime + clock skew.
- [x] **Single Sign-On** = one TGT, many service tickets.
- [x] **Clock skew** = default ±5 minutes (must be NTP-synchronized).
- [x] **Realm** = administrative domain; cross-realm needs inter-realm keys.
- [x] **Principal format:** `primary/instance@REALM` (e.g., `alice@KTU.EDU`).
- [x] **Kerberos v4** used **DES-ECB** (broken). **v5** uses AES-CTS (RFC 4120).
- [x] **AS-REP Roasting** = crack $K_c$ offline from AS-REP.
- [x] **Kerberoasting** = crack $K_s$ offline from service ticket.
- [x] **Golden Ticket** = forge TGT using stolen `krbtgt` hash; rotation is the defense.
- [x] **Silver Ticket** = forge service ticket using stolen service account hash.
- [x] **Windows AD** uses Kerberos v5 with **PAC (Privilege Attribute Certificate)** for authorization.
- [x] **PKINIT** = Public-Key extension using X.509 certificates for initial authentication.
- [x] **KDC = single point of failure** — deploy KDC slaves for HA.
- [x] **Defaults:** TGT lifetime ~10–24 hours, service ticket ~1–8 hours.
- [x] **Ticket flags** to know: RENEWABLE, FORWARDABLE, PROXIABLE, POSTDATED, INITIAL, PRE-AUTH, TRANSITED-POLICY-CHECKED.
- [x] **Encryption etypes (in order of preference):** `aes256-cts-hmac-sha384-192` > `aes128-cts-hmac-sha256-128` > `aes256-cts-hmac-sha1-96` > `aes128-cts-hmac-sha1-96` > `rc4-hmac` (legacy) > `des-*` (deprecated).
- [x] **Error codes to recognize:** `KDC_ERR_C_PRINCIPAL_UNKNOWN` (6), `KRB_AP_ERR_SKEW` (31), `KRB_AP_ERR_MODIFIED` (36), `KRB_AP_ERR_BADORDER` (37).
- [x] **GSSAPI** = Generic Security Service Application Program Interface — the API through which apps use Kerberos (used by SSH, SMTP, NFS, etc.).
- [x] **MIT Kerberos** = open-source reference implementation. **Heimdal** = BSD-licensed alternative. **MS Kerberos** = Windows variant with PAC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When drawing the Kerberos sequence diagram in the exam, **always label every message with its encryption key** (e.g., `E(K_c, …)`) and **explicitly name the three sessions keys** $K_{c,tgs}$ and $K_{c,s}$. Examiners allocate marks for these cryptographic details, not just the arrows. A diagram without cryptographic annotations loses up to **3 marks**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Secure Communication at the Data Link and Network Layers (IPSEC and IKE)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT633 - SECURE COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Secure Communication at the Data Link and Network Layers (IPSEC and IKE)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Secure Communication: A Layered Perspective

In the **TCP/IP reference model**, security can be applied at virtually every layer of the stack. Two layers that receive prominent industry attention for native cryptographic protection are:

- **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)** — Secures the hop-by-hop link between two adjacent nodes (e.g., a host and a router, a laptop and a wireless access point).
- **Network Layer (Layer 3)** — Secures the end-to-end logical path between source and destination, agnostic of the underlying physical medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** *PECPT633 Module 1* mandates the study of **IPSec (Internet Protocol Security)** for network-layer protection and **IKE (Internet Key Exchange)** for automated cryptographic key negotiation. The data-link layer is discussed in context (e.g., MACsec, L2TP, WPA2-Enterprise) to position IPSec in the broader security stack.

---

## 1.2 What is IPSec?

**Formal Definition:**
> **IPSec (Internet Protocol Security)** is a suite of protocols standardized by the IETF (primarily in **RFC 4301 – RFC 4309**, with later extensions) that operates at the **Network Layer (Layer 3)** of the OSI model to provide **confidentiality, data integrity, authentication of origin, and anti-replay protection** for IP datagrams.

IPSec is **end-to-end**: it protects packets between two IPSec-aware endpoints (hosts or security gateways), regardless of the intermediate routing infrastructure.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Tamper-Proof Sealed Envelope

Imagine you want to mail a confidential legal document from **Kochi to Trivandrum** through several courier hubs along the way.

- **Without security:** You write your letter on a postcard. Every sorting hub, every courier handler can read it, and a malicious person can alter the text with a pen.
- **With IPSec (Transport Mode):** You place the letter in a **tamper-evident sealed envelope** signed with a unique wax seal. The postman can see the *outer* envelope (original addressing) but cannot read the letter inside, and any tampering breaks the seal.
- **With IPSec (Tunnel Mode):** You place the letter into a sealed envelope, then place *that* envelope inside **another completely new addressed envelope** sent to a regional office in Trivandrum. The new outer envelope hides even the original sender/recipient identity from intermediate couriers. The Trivandrum office opens the outer envelope and forwards the inner sealed letter locally.

This dual-mode ability — protecting either the payload alone or the **entire original datagram** — is what makes IPSec the de facto standard for **VPNs (Virtual Private Networks)**.

---

## 1.3 What is IKE?

**Formal Definition:**
> **IKE (Internet Key Exchange)** is the key-management protocol used by IPSec to **dynamically negotiate, establish, refresh, and tear down Security Associations (SAs)** — the logical stateful connections over which IPSec traffic is protected. The current standard is **IKEv2 (RFC 7296)**.

Without IKE, an administrator would have to manually configure identical cryptographic keys, SAs, and lifetime values on every pair of IPSec peers. IKE automates this using **Diffie–Hellman key exchange** combined with strong peer authentication (pre-shared keys, digital certificates, or EAP).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Strangers, One-Secret" Handshake

Two strangers (Alice and Bob) meet in a coffee shop in Kochi. They have never shared a secret. To establish a *shared secret* in public:

1. They agree on a common paint color (e.g., yellow) — **public**.
2. Each picks a **private** color (Alice picks red, Bob picks blue).
3. They mix: Alice sends yellow + red = **orange** to Bob; Bob sends yellow + blue = **green** to Alice — both mixes are **public**.
4. Alice mixes Bob's green with her private red = **a dark mud color**. Bob mixes Alice's orange with his private blue = the **same dark mud color**.

A snooping eavesdropper, who only knows the public colors, **cannot reconstruct** the final shared secret. This is essentially the **Diffie–Hellman key exchange** at the heart of IKE. IKE wraps this mechanism inside an authenticated, structured negotiation protocol.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why IKE matters in KTU exams:** A very common question pattern is *"Differentiate IKEv1 Phase 1 and Phase 2"* or *"Explain how IPSec SAs are established."* Master the IKE state machine.

---

## 1.4 Secure Communication at the Data Link Layer — Quick Contextual Overview

Although the primary focus of Module 1 is IPSec/IKE, the syllabus requires brief contextual coverage of link-layer security to compare layered protection.

| Protocol / Standard | Layer / Scope | Primary Purpose | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PPP (Point-to-Point Protocol)** | Data Link | Encapsulation over point-to-point links | Historical baseline |
| **PPTP (Point-to-Point Tunneling Protocol)** | Data Link | Microsoft-era VPN tunneling | Mostly deprecated; exam reference only |
| **L2TP (Layer 2 Tunneling Protocol)** | Data Link | Tunneling (no native crypto) | Often paired with IPSec (L2TP/IPSec) |
| **WEP / WPA / WPA2 / WPA3** | Data Link (Wi-Fi) | Wireless link encryption | WPA2-Enterprise uses 802.1X + AES-CCMP |
| **MACsec (IEEE 802.1AE)** | Data Link | Hop-by-hop authentication + encryption on Ethernet | Used in carrier-grade networks |
| **802.1X (Port-Based NAC)** | Data Link | Authentication framework for port access | Backbone of enterprise WLAN/LAN security |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked *"Where does IPSec fit versus link-layer security?"* — answer that **link-layer protocols secure a single hop**, while **IPSec secures an entire end-to-end path** between IPSec endpoints. They are **complementary, not competing** — modern designs often use both (e.g., MACsec on the LAN + IPSec over the WAN).

---

## 1.5 Why Study IPSec & IKE Together?

IPSec without IKE is operationally infeasible for large networks; IKE without IPSec has no payload to protect. They are **two halves of the same architectural coin**:

- **IPSec** = the **traffic-protection engine** (defines *what* is encrypted/authenticated and *how*).
- **IKE** = the **negotiation engine** (decides *which keys, algorithms, lifetimes* are used and *authenticates* the peer).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of IPSec Architecture

IPSec is not a single protocol. It is a **framework** composed of three architectural pillars:

1. **Security Protocols:** AH (Authentication Header) and ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload).
2. **Key Management:** IKE (Internet Key Exchange), typically in version 2 (IKEv2).
3. **Databases (the "state"):** SPD, SAD, and PAD.

### 2.1.1 Pillar 1 — The Two Security Protocols

#### A. AH — Authentication Header (RFC 4302)

- **IP Protocol Number: 51**
- **Services provided:** Data origin authentication, data integrity, **anti-replay**.
- **Services NOT provided:** **Confidentiality** (no encryption). AH deliberately does not encrypt.
- **Coverage:** Protects the entire IP packet **except** mutable fields in transit (e.g., TTL, Header Checksum, DSCP/TOS that may be altered by routers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AH header format (in order):**
> Next Header (8) | Payload Length (8) | Reserved (16) | SPI (32) | Sequence Number (32) | ICV (variable, multiple of 32 bits)

- **ICV (Integrity Check Value):** A MAC (Message Authentication Code) computed over the packet, typically using **HMAC-SHA-1-96** or **HMAC-SHA-256**.

#### B. ESP — Encapsulating Security Payload (RFC 4303)

- **IP Protocol Number: 50**
- **Services provided:** **Confidentiality** (encryption), data origin authentication, data integrity, anti-replay.
- **Coverage:** Protects the **payload** (in transport mode) or the **entire original datagram** (in tunnel mode), but **does not protect the outer IP header** itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ESP packet format (in order):**
> [ESP Header: SPI (32) | Sequence Number (32)] [Payload Data (encrypted)] [ESP Trailer: Padding | Pad Length | Next Header] [ESP Auth Data: ICV]

- **Encryption algorithms typically used:** **AES-CBC (128/192/256-bit)**, **AES-GCM** (AEAD), **ChaCha20-Poly1305** (AEAD).
- **Authentication algorithms:** **HMAC-SHA-1-96**, **HMAC-SHA-256-128**, or **AES-GCM (built-in AEAD)**.

### 2.1.2 Pillar 2 — Security Association (SA)

> **A Security Association (SA) is a simplex (one-way) logical contract** that contains all the state and parameters agreed upon by two IPSec peers for protecting traffic flowing in a single direction.

Key properties of an SA:

- **Simplex:** If A and B want to protect bidirectional traffic, they need **two SAs** — one for A→B and one for B→A.
- **Identified uniquely** by a triple: **(SPI, Destination IP, IPSec Protocol)**. The **SPI (Security Parameter Index)** is a **32-bit** arbitrary value chosen by the receiving end.
- **Stateful:** Contains the cryptographic algorithms, keys, anti-replay sequence counter, SA lifetime, mode (transport/tunnel), and the path MTU.
- **Stored in the SAD (Security Association Database)** on each peer.

### 2.1.3 Pillar 3 — The Three Databases

Every IPSec endpoint maintains three databases:

| Database | Full Name | Purpose | Exam-Worthy Detail |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SPD** | Security Policy Database | Defines *what to do* with each packet: **PROTECT, BYPASS, or DISCARD** | Indexed by selectors (Src IP, Dst IP, Protocol, Port, User ID) |
| **SAD** | Security Association Database | Stores the *active* SAs — every parameter negotiated | Each entry indexed by (SPI, Dst IP, Protocol) |
| **PAD** | Peer Authentication Database | Holds authentication data (PSK, certs) used by IKE | Used only during IKE negotiation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Flow for an Outgoing Packet (KTU favorite question):**
> 1. The packet's selectors (Src IP, Dst IP, Protocol, Port) are matched against the **SPD**.
> 2. If the policy is **DISCARD** → drop the packet.
> 3. If the policy is **BYPASS** → forward the packet in cleartext.
> 4. If the policy is **PROTECT** → look up (or create via IKE) a matching **SA in the SAD**, then apply ESP or AH.

### 2.1.4 Two Operating Modes

| Mode | What is Protected | Header Layout | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Transport Mode** | The IP payload (e.g., TCP/UDP segment) | Original IP header + IPSec header + payload + IPSec trailer | Host-to-host communication between two end systems |
| **Tunnel Mode** | The **entire original IP datagram** (header + payload) | **NEW** IP header + IPSec header + **original full datagram** + IPSec trailer | Site-to-site VPN (gateway-to-gateway); host-to-gateway remote-access VPN |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In Tunnel Mode, the original IP header is encapsulated (hidden) inside the encrypted payload.** Intermediate routers see only the new outer IP header. This is why Tunnel Mode is mandatory for **VPN gateways** where the internal IP addressing scheme should not be exposed.

---

## 2.2 IKE — The Negotiation Engine (Deep Dive)

### 2.2.1 IKEv1 vs. IKEv2 (Comparison Table)

| Feature | IKEv1 (RFC 2409) | IKEv2 (RFC 7296) |
|---|---|---|
| Phases | Phase 1 (Main/Aggressive) + Phase 2 (Quick) | Single unified exchange (INIT + AUTH) |
| Messages for initial SA | 6 (Main Mode) or 3 (Aggressive Mode) | **4 messages** (2 pairs) |
| Authentication methods | Pre-shared key, Digital Signatures, Public Key Encryption | Pre-shared key, Digital Signatures, EAP |
| Built-in NAT traversal | No (extension) | **Yes (built-in)** |
| Built-in anti-DoS | No (cookie in Aggressive Mode only) | **Yes (cookies in IKE_SA_INIT response)** |
| PFS (Perfect Forward Secrecy) | Optional in Phase 2 | Easier to achieve, built into CREATE_CHILD_SA |
| Reliability | No native reliability for Phase 2 | **Built-in retransmission + sequence numbers** |
| Status in 2024 | Legacy / widely deployed | **Recommended** for new deployments |

### 2.2.2 IKEv1 — Two-Phase Architecture

#### Phase 1: Establish the ISAKMP SA (IKE SA)

Goal: Authenticate the peer and create a secure, authenticated channel for Phase 2. Produces the **ISAKMP SA** (also called the IKE SA), which is itself protected by encryption and authentication.

Two modes in Phase 1:
- **Main Mode (6 messages):** Identity protection — peer identities are encrypted. Mandatory for IKEv1 when using PFS or pre-shared keys with non-IP-address identities.
- **Aggressive Mode (3 messages):** Faster, but peer identities are exchanged in cleartext. Only needed for legacy interoperability.

**Phase 1 authentication methods (4):**
1. **Pre-Shared Secret (PSK):** A symmetric key manually configured on both peers. Vulnerable to off-line dictionary attack if weak.
2. **Digital Signatures (RSA or DSA):** Each peer signs the exchange using its private key. Most common in production.
3. **Public Key Encryption (rare):** Encrypts nonces with the peer's public key.
4. **Revised Public Key Encryption (rare):** Variant of above.

#### Phase 2: Establish the IPSec SAs (Quick Mode)

Goal: Negotiate the actual IPSec SAs (one or more) that will protect user data. Quick Mode is **always encrypted and authenticated** under the ISAKMP SA established in Phase 1.

Key points:
- Uses **nonces** (random numbers) for fresh key material.
- Optional **PFS (Perfect Forward Secrecy)** triggers a fresh **Diffie–Hellman** exchange per Phase 2.
- A single Phase 1 SA can be used to negotiate **many** Phase 2 SAs.

### 2.2.3 IKEv2 — Modern, Streamlined Exchange

IKEv2 collapses the two-phase structure into a **4-message initial exchange** (often called "IKE_SA_INIT" + "IKE_AUTH"):

| Msg # | Direction | Contents |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initiator → Responder | **IKE_SA_INIT:** SA proposal (encryption + integrity + PRF + DH + KE payload) |
| 2 | Responder → Initiator | Chosen suite + Responder's KE + **anti-DoS cookie** if requested |
| 3 | Initiator → Responder | **IKE_AUTH:** Identity + Authentication material (cert/sig/PSK) + first **CHILD_SA** (i.e., first IPSec SA) |
| 4 | Responder → Initiator | Identity proof + first CHILD_SA confirmed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key IKEv2 concepts to memorize for KTU:**
> - **IKE_SA** (the management channel) is **separate** from **CHILD_SA** (the data-protection SA). One IKE_SA can establish many CHILD_SAs.
> - **CREATE_CHILD_SA** exchange (2 messages) is used to create additional CHILD_SAs or rekey existing ones.
> - **INFORMATIONAL** exchange (2 messages) handles deletes, configuration, and notifications.

---

## 2.3 The Core Cryptographic Primitives

IPSec/IKE rely on a small set of well-known primitives. KTU frequently asks for the math behind these.

### 2.3.1 HMAC — The Workhorse of IPSec Authentication

**HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code)** is used by AH and the authentication component of ESP.

$$
\text{HMAC}(K, M) = H \left( (K \oplus \text{opad}) \,\|\, H \left( (K \oplus \text{ipad}) \,\|\, M \right) \right)
$$

Where:
- $H$ is a cryptographic hash function (e.g., SHA-1, SHA-256).
- $K$ is the secret key, padded/truncated to the hash block length.
- $\text{opad} = 0x5C$ repeated to block size (the "outer pad").
- $\text{ipad} = 0x36$ repeated to block size (the "inner pad").
- $\|$ denotes concatenation.
- $\oplus$ is the bitwise XOR operator.

### 2.3.2 Diffie–Hellman Key Exchange (The Heart of IKE)

The DH exchange allows two parties to derive a **shared secret** over an insecure channel, which is computationally infeasible to recover by an eavesdropper.

The mathematical setup uses a **multiplicative group modulo a large prime $p$** with a generator $g$. The shared secret derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Alice chooses private } a &\in \{2, \dots, p-2\} \\
\text{Bob chooses private } b &\in \{2, \dots, p-2\} \\
\text{Alice computes and sends } A &= g^{a} \bmod p \\
\text{Bob computes and sends } B &= g^{b} \bmod p \\
\text{Shared secret } S &= A^{b} \bmod p \;=\; B^{a} \bmod p \;=\; g^{ab} \bmod p
\end{aligned}
$$

The security relies on the **Computational Diffie–Hellman Problem (CDHP)**: given $g^a \bmod p$ and $g^b \bmod p$, computing $g^{ab} \bmod p$ is infeasible for large $p$ (~2048+ bits).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standardized DH Groups used in IKE (memorize at least these):**
> - **Group 1:** 768-bit MODP — **deprecated**.
> - **Group 2:** 1024-bit MODP — **legacy / not recommended**.
> - **Group 14:** 2048-bit MODP — **common**.
> - **Group 15:** 3072-bit MODP.
> - **Group 16:** 4096-bit MODP.
> - **Group 19/20/21:** NIST **Elliptic Curve Groups** (ECDH — ECP256/ECP384/ECP521). Preferred for new deployments due to smaller key size and equivalent security.

### 2.3.3 Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)

> **PFS** ensures that the compromise of a long-term private key does **not** compromise past session keys.

In IKEv1 Phase 2 / IKEv2 CREATE_CHILD_SA, this is achieved by performing a **fresh DH exchange** for every new CHILD_SA. Each session key is derived from an independent ephemeral DH pair, so past sessions remain secure even if the long-term authentication key is later leaked.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Value | Key Symbol | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SA Identification Triple** | $(\text{SPI}, \text{Dest IP}, \text{Protocol})$ | SPI is **32 bits** | Protocol = AH (51) or ESP (50) |
| **AH Protocol Number** | $\text{Num}_{\text{AH}} = 51$ | — | IANA assigned |
| **ESP Protocol Number** | $\text{Num}_{\text{ESP}} = 50$ | — | IANA assigned |
| **HMAC Computation** | $\text{HMAC}(K,M) = H((K \oplus \text{opad}) \mid\mid H((K \oplus \text{ipad}) \mid\mid M))$ | $\text{opad}=0x5C^n$, $\text{ipad}=0x36^n$ | $n$ = hash block length in bytes |
| **Diffie–Hellman Shared Secret** | $S = g^{ab} \bmod p$ | $g$ generator, $p$ prime, $a,b$ private | Both peers compute the same $S$ |
| **Diffie–Hellman Public Values** | $A = g^{a} \bmod p$, $B = g^{b} \bmod p$ | — | Exchanged in clear over the wire |
| **AH ICV Length** | Truncated MAC (e.g., **96 bits** for HMAC-SHA-1-96) | — | Prevents packet bloat |
| **ESP IV Length (AES-CBC)** | **128 bits** | — | Must be unique per packet for security |
| **AES Block Size** | **128 bits** | — | For AES-CBC mode in ESP |
| **MODP Group Strengths** | Group 14 = 2048-bit ≈ RSA 2048-bit | — | Group 19/20 = 256/384-bit ECDH |
| **IPsec Anti-Replay Window** | Sliding window, default size **32 or 64** packets | — | Uses 32-bit or 64-bit ESN |
| **PFS Condition** | Fresh DH per CHILD_SA | — | Optional but strongly recommended |
| **MTU Overhead (Tunnel + ESP+AES-CBC+HMAC-SHA256)** | $\sim 50\text{–}81$ bytes per packet | — | Drives need for Path MTU Discovery |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This layered, modular architecture is what makes IPSec deployable in everything from tiny IoT devices (using ECDH groups for low CPU) to carrier-scale MPLS backbones (using AES-GCM AEAD for line-rate throughput). Real-world performance trade-offs — **AES-CBC + HMAC vs. AES-GCM AEAD** — are frequent subjects of KTU design questions.
