In these schemes, a fresh, random private exponent ($a$ for the client, $b$ for the server) is generated for **every single session**. The shared secret $g^{ab} \bmod p$ is derived from these ephemeral values, and the private exponents are securely erased after the session ends. Consequently, even if an attacker later obtains the server's long-term RSA/ECDSA signing key, they cannot retroactively decrypt previously recorded TLS traffic.

In **TLS 1.3**, PFS is **mandatory** — RSA key transport has been removed entirely, and all connections must use DHE or ECDHE.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for DHE/ECDHE mechanism, 1 mark for TLS 1.3 mandate.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **TLS Handshake Protocol** and explain the purpose of each message exchanged between the client and server in **TLS 1.2**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **TLS 1.2 and TLS 1.3** with respect to handshake latency, cipher suite support, forward secrecy, and encryption scope. Explain why TLS 1.3 is considered more secure. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The TLS 1.2 Handshake Protocol authenticates parties and negotiates cryptographic parameters through the following sequence:

| Step | Message | Sender | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | `ClientHello` | Client | Advertises highest TLS version, $R_C$ (client random), supported cipher suites, compression methods. |
| 2 | `ServerHello` | Server | Confirms TLS version, $R_S$ (server random), chosen cipher suite. |
| 3 | `Certificate` | Server | Sends X.509 certificate chain for authentication. |
| 4 | `ServerKeyExchange` | Server | Transmits ECDHE/DHE public parameters for key agreement. |
| 5 | `ServerHelloDone` | Server | Signals completion of server's hello messages. |
| 6 | `ClientKeyExchange` | Client | Sends client's ECDHE/DHE public value $g^a \bmod p$. |
| 7 | `CertificateRequest` | Server | (Optional) Requests client certificate for mutual TLS. |
| 8 | `CertificateVerify` | Client | (If requested) Signs handshake transcript to prove private key ownership. |
| 9 | `ChangeCipherSpec` | Both | Signals switch to newly negotiated keys. |
| 10 | `Finished` | Both | Encrypted hash of all handshake messages; authenticates key derivation. |

Both parties independently compute the **Master Secret**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pre-Master Secret (PMS)} &= g^{ab} \bmod p \\
\text{Master Secret (MS)} &= \text{PRF}(\text{PMS}, \text{"master secret"}, R_C \parallel R_S)
\end{aligned}
$$

From MS, six 48-byte block keys are derived via the PRF: client/server write MAC keys, client/server write encryption keys, and client/server IVs. *(Diagram will be drawn similar to Section 4.1 above.)*

*(Mark Distribution: 1 mark ClientHello/ServerHello, 1 mark Certificate exchange, 1 mark Key Exchange parameters, 1 mark ChangeCipherSpec/Finished, 2 marks Master Secret derivation with formulas, 1 mark diagram.)*

---

**Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

| Comparison Parameter | TLS 1.2 | TLS 1.3 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Handshake RTT** | 2-RTT (two round trips before app data) | 1-RTT (single round trip; 0-RTT for resumption) |
| **Cipher Suites** | ~300+ legacy suites, including CBC mode, RC4, 3DES | Only 5 AEAD suites (e.g., TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384) |
| **Perfect Forward Secrecy** | Optional (depends on suite selection) | **Mandatory** (RSA key transport removed) |
| **Encryption Scope** | Only application data encrypted post-handshake | **Entire handshake encrypted after ServerHello** |
| **Cryptographic Primitives** | Includes weak hashes (SHA-1, MD5), weak ciphers | Requires SHA-256/SHA-384, AEAD ciphers only |
| **Session Resumption** | Session IDs / Session Tickets | Pre-Shared Keys (PSK) and 0-RTT data |

**Why TLS 1.3 is More Secure:**

1. **Eliminates CBC-mode vulnerabilities** (e.g., padding-oracle attacks like Lucky 13, POODLE).
2. **Removes RSA key transport**, eliminating the Bleichenbacher-style decryption oracle attacks.
3. **Enforces PFS** cryptographically, not merely by configuration policy.
4. **Encrypts the certificate and identity**, preventing fingerprinting of servers by passive observers.
5. **Shorter attack surface** with only 5 standardized cipher suites, reducing implementation errors.

*(Mark Distribution: 1 mark per comparison parameter × 4 parameters = 4 marks, 3 marks for security justifications.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 — Alternative]

**(a)** Explain the **TLS Record Protocol** in detail. Describe the format of a TLS record and the operations performed during transmission and reception. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the major **attacks on TLS/SSL** (POODLE, BEAST, Heartbleed, DROWN) and the mitigations prescribed in TLS 1.3. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The **TLS Record Protocol** encapsulates all higher-level protocols (Handshake, Alert, ChangeCipherSpec, Application Data) and operates with the following format:

```
┌──────────┬──────────┬───────────┬─────────────────┐
│ Content  │ Version  │ Length    │ Fragment        │
│ Type (1) │ (2)      │ (2)       │ (≤ 16,384 bytes)│
└──────────┴──────────┴───────────┴─────────────────┘
   byte 0      1-2        3-4           5+
```

**Transmission Operations:**

1. **Fragmentation:** Application data is split into blocks of at most $2^{14}$ bytes.
2. **Compression (Optional):** Applied to the fragment. **Note:** In modern TLS, compression is disabled to prevent the **CRIME attack**, where attackers could infer secrets by observing compressed ciphertext sizes.
3. **AEAD Encryption + MAC:** With TLS 1.3, only AEAD ciphers are allowed. For each record:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \text{additional\_data} = \text{seq\_num} \parallel \text{type} \parallel \text{version} \parallel \text{length} \\
C, T &= \text{AEAD\_Encrypt}(K, N, P, A)
\end{aligned}
$$

   Where $K$ is the encryption key, $N$ is the nonce/IV, $P$ is the plaintext, and $T$ is the authentication tag.

**Reception Operations (Inverse):**

1. Read record header to determine content type and length.
2. Decrypt and verify authentication tag.
3. Decompress (if compression used).
4. Reassemble into the original application message stream.

**Sequence Number:** A 64-bit counter incremented per record. Used as input to the MAC/AEAD to prevent replay attacks.

*(Mark Distribution: 1 mark record format diagram, 2 marks transmission steps, 2 marks reception steps, 1 mark sequence number role, 1 mark AEAD formula.)*

---

**Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

| Attack | Year | Vulnerability | Mitigation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **BEAST** | 2011 | CBC mode IV predictability in TLS 1.0 | Switch to AEAD ciphers (TLS 1.2+) |
| **POODLE** | 2014 | Padding oracle in SSL 3.0 CBC mode | Disable SSL 3.0 entirely |
| **Heartbleed** | 2014 | Buffer over-read in OpenSSL heartbeat extension | Patch OpenSSL; certificate revocation; key regeneration |
| **DROWN** | 2016 | Cross-protocol attack using SSL 2.0 RSA key | Disable SSL 2.0 and SSL 3.0 |
| **CRIME/BREACH** | 2012/2013 | Compression side-channel leaks | Disable TLS-level compression |
| **Lucky 13** | 2013 | Timing attack on CBC MAC verification | Use AEAD ciphers |

**How TLS 1.3 Mitigates All Major Attacks:**

1. **Removes CBC mode entirely** → eliminates BEAST, POODLE, Lucky 13 attack classes.
2. **Removes SSL 2.0/3.0 negotiation** → eliminates DROWN.
3. **Removes compression** → eliminates CRIME/BREACH at the TLS layer.
4. **Mandates AEAD ciphers** → combines confidentiality and integrity in a single, side-channel-resistant primitive.
5. **Mandates PFS** → limits damage from any future key compromise.
6. **Encrypts handshake metadata** → reduces passive reconnaissance.

*(Mark Distribution: 1 mark per attack explanation × 4 attacks = 4 marks, 3 marks for TLS 1.3 mitigations.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing TLS and SSL:** Always write **TLS**, not SSL. Examiners deduct marks for outdated terminology.
> 2. **Skipping the Master Secret derivation:** Simply stating "keys are exchanged" is insufficient. You **must** show the PRF derivation formula to score full marks.
> 3. **Forgetting AEAD:** In TLS 1.3, MAC-then-Encrypt and Encrypt-then-MAC modes are **gone**. Use AEAD terminology.
> 4. **Drawing diagrams in prose:** Always include a **neat labeled diagram** in Handshake questions — a 14-mark question without a diagram typically caps at 12 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **TLS** = successor to SSL; current standards are **TLS 1.2** (RFC 5246) and **TLS 1.3** (RFC 8446).
- ✅ TLS provides **Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication**.
- ✅ Protocol stack consists of **Handshake, Change Cipher Spec, Alert, Application Data, Record**.
- ✅ **Cipher Suite format:** `TLS_<KeyExchange>_<Auth>_<BulkCipher>_<Hash>`.
- ✅ TLS 1.2 handshake is **2-RTT**; TLS 1.3 handshake is **1-RTT** with optional **0-RTT**.
- ✅ **Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)** is achieved via **ephemeral DH (DHE/ECDHE)** and is **mandatory** in TLS 1.3.
- ✅ **Master Secret derivation:** Uses PRF (HMAC-SHA256/384) on Pre-Master Secret + client random + server random.
- ✅ **TLS Record Protocol** = Fragmentation → Optional Compression (usually disabled) → AEAD Encryption + MAC.
- ✅ Modern TLS uses **AEAD ciphers only** (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305).
- ✅ Attacks mitigated by TLS 1.3: **BEAST, POODLE, CRIME, DROWN, Lucky 13**.
- ✅ **Port 443** for HTTPS.
- ✅ TLS operates at **Layer 4–5** (between TCP and Application), making it application-aware.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Denial of Service (DoS) Attacks and Defences, and Firewalls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT633 - SECURE COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Denial of Service (DoS) Attacks and Defences, and Firewalls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Denial of Service (DoS) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Denial of Service (DoS)** is a class of cyber-attack in which a malicious actor deliberately disrupts the normal functioning of a target system, network, application, or service by overwhelming it with a flood of illegitimate requests, exploiting protocol weaknesses, or exhausting critical computational resources (CPU cycles, memory, bandwidth, socket descriptors, or disk space), thereby **denying** legitimate users access to the resource.

**Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS)** is the scaled, coordinated variant where the attack traffic originates from many geographically dispersed compromised machines (a **botnet** or **zombie army**) under the control of a single attacker (the **botmaster**), making mitigation far more difficult than a single-source DoS.

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Jam"

Imagine a 50-seat restaurant (the legitimate web server). A **DoS attack** is one person phoning the restaurant 1,000 times per minute and never ordering — the phone lines jam and genuine customers cannot call to book a table. A **DDoS attack** is the same scenario but with **5,000 people** simultaneously making reservation calls from all over the city, directed by a single ringleader. The restaurant's front desk (the server's TCP stack) is exhausted, and real diners are turned away — the service is *denied*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Resource Exhaustion Metrics (KTU Syllabus Highlight):**
> - **Bandwidth (Mbps/Gbps)** — link capacity consumed.
> - **Requests per Second (RPS)** — application-layer load.
> - **Concurrent Open Sockets** — half-open TCP backlog.
> - **CPU / Memory Utilisation** — kernel-level exhaustion.

---

## 1.2 Firewall — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Firewall** is a network security device (hardware, software, or hybrid) that monitors, filters, and controls incoming and outgoing network traffic based on an organisation's predefined **security policy**. It establishes a **barrier** between a trusted internal network and untrusted external networks (such as the Internet), enforcing access control rules at the boundary to block malicious packets while permitting legitimate communication.

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Club Bouncer"

Think of a nightclub with a single entrance. The **firewall** is the **bouncer** stationed at the door. He holds a **guest list (rule set)** — names, dress codes, age limits. He inspects each arrival (**packet inspection**) and decides to:
- **Let in** (ACCEPT) — if the person matches the list.
- **Send away** (DROP / DENY) — if blacklisted.
- **Detain for further questioning** (LOG) — if suspicious.

Sometimes the bouncer also checks whether the person is coming from a **previously verified VIP** (stateful inspection), not just their appearance.

---

## 1.3 Classification of DoS Attacks (KTU Board Taxonomy)

| Category | Layer (OSI) | Example Attack |
|---|---|---|
| Volumetric | Layer 3/4 | UDP Flood, ICMP Flood |
| Protocol | Layer 3/4 | SYN Flood, Ping of Death, Smurf |
| Application | Layer 7 | HTTP GET Flood, Slowloris |

| Defence Layer | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| Preventive | Firewalls, Rate Limiting, ACLs |
| Detective | Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) |
| Reactive | Blackholing, Scrubbing Centres |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a DoS Attack — Operational Stages

1. **Reconnaissance** — Attacker probes target IP ranges, open ports, OS fingerprints.
2. **Weaponisation / Recruitment** — In DDoS, attacker compromises bots via malware (Mirai, Zeus).
3. **Command & Control (C2)** — Botmaster sends attack parameters to zombies over covert channels (IRC, HTTPS).
4. **Attack Launch** — Simultaneous flooding begins at the agreed T-zero time.
5. **Post-Attack** — Forensics, log analysis, evidence preservation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SYN Flood works (Root Cause):** The TCP three-way handshake allocates kernel state (a TCB entry) in the server's SYN queue upon receiving a SYN, but only releases it after the handshake completes. An attacker **never sends the final ACK**, so the queue fills — known as the **SYN backlog exhaustion** problem.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | TCP SYN Queue Size | $Q_{max} = backlog$ parameter | entries (typical: 128–1024) |
| 2 | SYN Flood Attack Rate | $R_{attack} \geq \frac{Q_{max}}{T_{timeout}}$ | SYN/sec |
| 3 | Smurf Attack Amplification | $A = \frac{N_{hosts\_responding}}{\text{1 packet sent}}$ | dimensionless factor (10 to 500+) |
| 4 | Bandwidth Saturation | $B_{sat} = C_{link} \times 0.9$ | bps — 90 % saturation considered denial |
| 5 | Firewall Rule Match | $M(P) = \bigvee_{i=1}^{n} \big( P.src\_ip \in S_i \land P.dst\_port \in D_i \big)$ | boolean decision |
| 6 | DDoS Botnet Size for Saturation | $N_{bots} \geq \frac{B_{sat}}{B_{per\_bot}}$ | bot count |
| 7 | Half-Open Connection Limit | $H_{limit} = \min(Q_{max},\; S_{max\_sockets})$ | entries |
| 8 | Packet Filtering Lookup | $O(1)$ for ACL on hardware TCAM | nanosecond order |
| 9 | Stateful Inspection Cost | $O(k)$ where $k$ = concurrent flows | RAM proportional |
| 10 | Application Throughput | $T = \frac{\text{Good requests served}}{\text{Unit time}}$ | req/sec |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** The amplification factor $A$ in Smurf-style attacks dictates the **defence ROI** — by blocking directed broadcasts at the network edge (router configuration), a single ACL line neutralises an attack that would otherwise require terabits of scrubbing capacity.

---

## 2.3 Types of DoS Attacks — Detailed Mechanics

### 2.3.1 SYN Flood Attack (Protocol-Level)
- Attacker sends TCP SYN packets with **spoofed source IPs**.
- Server replies with SYN-ACK and waits for ACK.
- Because the source IP is unreachable, ACK never arrives.
- Server's **incomplete connection queue** fills up.
- Legitimate SYNs are dropped → service denied.

### 2.3.2 Smurf Attack (Amplification-Based)
- ICMP Echo Request is sent to a network's **broadcast address** with source IP spoofed to the victim's IP.
- All hosts on the broadcast segment reply to the victim.
- Result: victim is flooded with amplified response traffic.

### 2.3.3 UDP Flood
- Large UDP packets sent to random ports on the victim.
- Victim checks application, finds no listener, replies with ICMP "Destination Unreachable".
- Bandwidth and CPU are consumed.

### 2.3.4 Ping of Death
- Sends an ICMP Echo Request packet larger than the **maximum allowed IP packet size of 65,535 bytes**, exploiting fragmentation reassembly bugs to crash the target.

### 2.3.5 Application-Layer DoS (Layer 7)
- **HTTP GET Flood**: legitimate-looking requests overwhelming web servers.
- **Slowloris**: Opens many partial HTTP requests, never finishing headers, exhausting the connection pool.
- Low bandwidth — hard to detect with volumetric methods.

---

## 2.4 DoS Defences — Multi-Layer Strategy

| Defence | Layer | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **SYN Cookies** | Host | Encode TCB state in SYN-ACK sequence number, freeing the queue. |
| **Rate Limiting** | Network | Cap SYN packets per source IP per second. |
| **Ingress Filtering (BCP 38)** | Network | Block packets with spoofed source IPs at the edge. |
| **Directed-Broadcast Disable** | Network | Router command: `no ip directed-broadcast` (kills Smurf). |
| **Scrubbing Centres / CDNs** | Cloud | Sink-hole attack traffic, forward only clean packets (e.g., Cloudflare, Akamai). |
| **Blackhole Filtering (RTBH)** | Network | Announce victim's prefix to null-route — drops traffic at ISP. |
| **Anycast Diffusion** | Cloud | Distribute attack across many geographically distributed scrubbing points. |
| **Application WAF** | Layer 7 | Web Application Firewall analyses HTTP semantics. |

---

## 2.5 Firewall Architectures — The KTU Three-Tier Model

### 2.5.1 Packet-Filtering Firewall (Stateless)
- Operates at **Layer 3 / Layer 4** of the OSI model.
- Examines each packet in isolation against ACL rules.
- **Fast** but **vulnerable to IP spoofing** and cannot track connection state.

### 2.5.2 Stateful Inspection Firewall
- Maintains a **state table** of all active TCP/UDP sessions.
- Permits return traffic for outbound connections only.
- Detects anomalies like ACK scans without prior SYN.

### 2.5.3 Application-Layer Gateway (Proxy Firewall)
- Acts as a **man-in-the-middle** at Layer 7.
- Terminates client connection, opens a fresh connection to the server.
- Deep packet inspection (DPI) of application protocols (HTTP, FTP, DNS).
- High security, but introduces latency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Key Point:** Modern enterprise firewalls (NGFW — Next-Generation Firewalls) combine all three capabilities: stateless filtering, stateful tracking, and DPI with integrated IDS/IPS.

---

## 2.6 Firewall Rule Set Design — Order of Precedence

Rules are evaluated **top-to-bottom** until the first match (first-match semantics).

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU board mistake: placing a broad `DENY ALL` rule *above* specific `ALLOW` rules — this silently blocks everything. Always place **specific allows first**, then the default `DENY ANY ANY` at the bottom (whitelisting principle).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: SYN Flood — Required Attack Rate

The TCP half-open queue has a maximum capacity $Q_{max}$ entries. Each half-open connection expires after a timeout $T_{timeout}$ (typically 75 seconds on Linux).

To **permanently fill** the queue (steady-state denial), the attacker's incoming SYN rate $R_{attack}$ must satisfy:

$$R_{attack} \geq \frac{Q_{max}}{T_{timeout}}$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

Let $\lambda(t)$ be the rate of incoming SYNs and $\mu$ be the rate of timeout-based cleanup.

$$Q(t) = Q(0) + \int_{0}^{t} \big(\lambda(\tau) - \mu(\tau)\big)\, d\tau$$

At steady-state denial, $Q(t) \to Q_{max}$ as $t \to \infty$, so $dQ/dt = 0$, meaning:

$$\lambda_{ss} = \mu_{ss} = \frac{Q_{max}}{T_{timeout}}$$

Therefore the critical attack rate threshold is:

$$R_{attack}^{*} = \frac{Q_{max}}{T_{timeout}} \;\; \text{SYN/sec}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style):**
Given: $Q_{max} = 512$ half-open slots, $T_{timeout} = 64$ s. Find minimum attack rate.

$$R_{attack}^{*} = \frac{512}{64} = 8 \;\; \text{SYNs per second}$$

So a sustained rate of just **8 SYNs/sec** is sufficient to lock out all new TCP connections on this server — illustrating why SYN floods are devastatingly cheap.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Smurf Attack Amplification Factor

Let $N$ = number of live hosts on the target broadcast segment. Attacker sends **1 ICMP Echo Request** to the broadcast address. Each host responds with **1 Echo Reply** to the spoofed victim IP.

Total packets reaching victim per single attack packet:

$$A = \frac{N \times 1 \text{ reply}}{1 \text{ request}} = N$$

If each reply is $R$ bytes and request is $r$ bytes:

$$A_{byte} = N \times \frac{R}{r}$$

For typical Ethernet MTU 1500 B replies vs. 64 B ICMP request:

$$A_{byte} = N \times \frac{1500}{64} \approx 23.4 \times N$$

For a /24 network with $N = 200$ hosts:

$$A = 200 \quad \text{(packet amplification)}, \quad A_{byte} \approx 4{,}687$$

The attacker needs only the bandwidth for **1 packet** to generate the impact of **200 packets** on the victim — this is the **amplification vector**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: DDoS Botnet Bandwidth Saturation

Target link capacity: $C$ bps. Average per-bot bandwidth: $B_{bot}$ bps. Number of bots needed to saturate 90 % of the link:

$$B_{sat} = 0.9 \times C$$

$$N_{bots} \geq \left\lceil \frac{B_{sat}}{B_{bot}} \right\rceil$$

**Example:** $C = 10$ Gbps, $B_{bot} = 1$ Mbps.

$$B_{sat} = 9 \;\; \text{Gbps}, \quad N_{bots} \geq \frac{9 \times 10^{9}}{1 \times 10^{6}} = 9{,}000 \;\; \text{bots}$$

The Mirai botnet (2016) peaked at **~600,000 bots** — capable of generating **~1.2 Tbps** of attack traffic, demonstrating real-world scale.

---

## 3.4 Firewall Packet Matching — Decision Logic

For an incoming packet $P$ with header fields $(P_{src\_ip}, P_{dst\_ip}, P_{src\_port}, P_{dst\_port}, P_{proto})$, decision $D(P)$:

$$D(P) = \begin{cases} \text{ACCEPT}, & \text{if } \exists\, i : P \in R_i \text{ and } R_i.action = ALLOW \\ \text{DROP}, & \text{if } \exists\, i : P \in R_i \text{ and } R_i.action = DENY \\ \text{DEFAULT\_DENY}, & \text{if } \forall\, i : P \notin R_i \end{cases}$$

**Critical ordering requirement:** If rule index $i < j$, and a packet matches both $R_i$ (ALLOW) and $R_j$ (DENY), the ALLOW at $R_i$ wins. Therefore ACL designers must place **most specific rules first**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Attack-to-Defence Mapping

| Attack Type | OSI Layer | Defence Mechanism | Effectiveness |
|---|---|---|---|
| SYN Flood | 4 (Transport) | SYN Cookies, Rate Limit | High |
| Smurf | 3 (Network) | Disable Directed Broadcast | Very High |
| UDP Flood | 4 (Transport) | Bandwidth Throttling, Filtering | Medium |
| Ping of Death | 3 (Network) | Patch OS, Fragment Reassembly Limits | Very High |
| HTTP Flood | 7 (Application) | WAF, CAPTCHA, JS Challenge | Medium-High |
| Slowloris | 7 (Application) | Connection Timeout, WAF | High |
| DNS Amplification | 7 (Application) | Response Rate Limiting (RRL) | High |
| NTP Amplification | 7 (Application) | NTP Monlist Disable | Very High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DoS Attack — Operational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Attacker / Botmaster] -->|C2 Channel| B[Botnet of Zombies]
    B -->|SYN Flood| C[Target Web Server]
    B -->|UDP Flood| C
    B -->|HTTP GET Flood| C
    C --> D{Traffic Normal?}
    D -->|No| E[Service Denied]
    D -->|Yes| F[Legitimate Users Served]
    C --> G[SYN Backlog Queue]
    G -->|Fills Up| E
    E --> H[Defence: SYN Cookies]
    E --> I[Defence: Scrubbing Centre]
    E --> J[Defence: WAF]
```

## 4.2 DDoS Architecture — Multi-Source Attack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BM[Botmaster] -->|Command| Z1[Zombie Bot 1]
    BM -->|Command| Z2[Zombie Bot 2]
    BM -->|Command| Z3[Zombie Bot N]
    Z1 -->|Attack Traffic| ISP1[ISP Edge Router]
    Z2 -->|Attack Traffic| ISP2[ISP Edge Router]
    Z3 -->|Attack Traffic| ISP3[ISP Edge Router]
    ISP1 --> SCRUB[Scrubbing Centre]
    ISP2 --> SCRUB
    ISP3 --> SCRUB
    SCRUB -->|Clean Traffic| VICTIM[Victim Server]
    SCRUB -->|Drop Malicious| DROP[Blackhole / Sink]
```

## 4.3 Firewall Placement in Network Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INET[Untrusted Internet] -->|Edge Link| FW1[Perimeter Firewall]
    FW1 -->|Filtered Traffic| DMZ[DMZ Zone - Public Servers]
    FW1 -->|Filtered Traffic| FW2[Internal Firewall]
    FW2 -->|Restricted Access| LAN[Trusted Corporate LAN]
    DMZ --> WEB[Web Server]
    DMZ --> DNS[DNS Server]
    DMZ --> MAIL[Mail Relay]
```

## 4.4 Firewall Architectural Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PKT[Incoming Packet] --> L1{Layer 3/4\nStateless Filter}
    L1 -->|Drop| OUT1[DROP / Log]
    L1 -->|Accept| L2{Layer 4\nStateful Inspection}
    L2 -->|No matching flow| OUT2[DROP]
    L2 -->|Matching flow| L3{Layer 7\nDeep Packet Inspection}
    L3 -->|Malicious payload| OUT3[BLOCK]
    L3 -->|Clean| ACC[ACCEPT and Forward]
```

## 4.5 SYN Flood — Three-Way Handshake Exploitation

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant ATK as Attacker (Spoofed IP)
    participant SRV as Server
    participant LEG as Legitimate Client
    ATK->>SRV: SYN
    SRV-->>ATK: SYN-ACK (queued)
    Note over SRV: Half-open entry allocated
    ATK-->>SRV: (No ACK sent)
    Note over SRV: Timeout in 75s
    LEG->>SRV: SYN
    Note over SRV: Queue FULL — Drop!
    SRV--xLEG: No response (Service Denied)
```

## 4.6 SYN Cookie Mechanism — Defence Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Receive SYN] --> B{Queue Full?}
    B -->|No| C[Normal Handshake]
    B -->|Yes| D[Compute SYN Cookie]
    D --> E["seq = hash(src_ip, src_port, dst_ip, dst_port, time)"]
    E --> F[Send SYN-ACK with encoded seq]
    F --> G[Wait for ACK]
    G --> H{ACK valid?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Reconstruct TCB from ACK fields]
    H -->|No| J[DROP]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between DoS and DDoS attacks with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 per sub-point):**
1. **DoS (Denial of Service):** A single attacker machine launches the attack from one source — e.g., a single host sending SYN floods to a server. Origin trace is straightforward. **(1 Mark)**
2. **DDoS (Distributed DoS):** Multiple compromised machines (a botnet under one botmaster) coordinate the attack simultaneously — e.g., the Mirai botnet attacking Dyn DNS in 2016 using 600,000+ bots. **(1 Mark)**
3. **Key Difference:** DDoS is volumetrically far larger, geographically distributed, uses spoofed source IPs making mitigation hard, whereas DoS is a single-source event easier to filter via ACL. **(1 Mark)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three differences between a packet-filtering firewall and a stateful inspection firewall.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Layer of Operation:** Packet-filtering operates at Layer 3/4 only; stateful inspection also tracks Layer 4 session states. **(1 Mark)**
2. **State Awareness:** Stateless filter examines each packet in isolation; stateful maintains a connection state table (5-tuple: src/dst IP, src/dst port, protocol) to validate return packets. **(1 Mark)**
3. **Security Strength:** Packet-filter is vulnerable to IP spoofing (attacker crafts allowed source); stateful rejects unsolicited return packets not matching an outbound session. **(1 Mark)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Diagram Sketches:** For any DoS / Firewall question worth 7+ marks, KTU examiners expect a **labelled flow diagram or architecture sketch**. Skipping the diagram typically loses **2 to 3 marks** even if the text is correct.
> 2. **Attack Naming:** When describing DDoS, always explicitly mention the **amplification factor** and **botnet model** — generic answers lose marks.
> 3. **Firewall Rule Tables:** Always present the ACL rule in the exact canonical format: `rule_id | action | src_ip | dst_ip | proto | port` — examiners deduct marks for narrative-style rules.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks

#### (a) Explain the SYN Flood attack in detail with a neat diagram. Also explain how SYN Cookies defend against it. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**SYN Flood Attack — Definition (1 Mark):**
A SYN flood is a protocol-level Denial of Service attack that exploits the TCP three-way handshake by sending a massive number of SYN packets with spoofed source IP addresses, causing the victim server to allocate half-open connection entries that never complete, exhausting the SYN backlog queue.

**Mechanism (3 Marks):**
1. Attacker sends TCP SYN packet with spoofed source IP $I_{spoof}$.
2. Server replies with SYN-ACK to $I_{spoof}$ (which is unreachable or unresponsive).
3. Server waits for the final ACK — allocates a Transmission Control Block (TCB) entry in the SYN queue for the timeout duration.
4. Attacker repeats at high rate; the queue saturates.
5. Legitimate SYNs from real clients are dropped because there is no queue space — service denied.

**Diagram (1 Mark):** Refer to Section 4.5 above — sketch the sequence diagram showing Attacker sending SYN, Server responding with SYN-ACK, missing ACK, and Legitimate client being denied.

**SYN Cookie Defence (2 Marks):**
1. Server detects queue overflow.
2. Instead of allocating TCB, it computes a hash-based cookie:
   $$seq_{cookie} = H(src\_ip, src\_port, dst\_ip, dst\_port, time, secret\_key)$$
3. Cookie is sent as the SYN-ACK sequence number.
4. When the real client responds with ACK, the server re-derives the cookie from the ACK's acknowledgment field and reconstructs the TCB only after validation — **no state held until handshake completes**.

#### (b) Describe the Smurf attack. How can directed-broadcast disabling at routers prevent it? (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Smurf Attack (4 Marks):**
1. Attacker crafts an ICMP Echo Request packet with:
   - Destination IP = target network's broadcast address (e.g., 192.168.10.255).
   - Source IP spoofed to the victim's IP.
2. Router forwards the packet to all hosts on the broadcast segment (directed broadcast).
3. Every host on the segment (often hundreds) replies with an ICMP Echo Reply to the victim.
4. Victim is overwhelmed with amplified reply traffic — DoS achieved.

**Amplification Factor:**
$$A = N \quad \text{(packet count)}, \quad A_{byte} \approx 23.4 \times N$$

**Defence via Directed-Broadcast Disabling (3 Marks):**
1. Most modern routers ship with directed-broadcast forwarding **disabled by default**, but legacy routers require explicit configuration: `no ip directed-broadcast`.
2. When disabled, the router drops packets destined to a network's broadcast address.
3. Result: the ICMP request never reaches the broadcast segment — zero amplification.
4. This single configuration line at each network edge **completely neutralises** the Smurf vector without requiring victim-side intervention.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks

#### (a) With a neat architecture diagram, explain the three generations of firewalls: Packet-Filtering, Stateful Inspection, and Application-Layer Gateway. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Generation 1 — Packet-Filtering Firewall (2 Marks):**
- Operates at OSI Layer 3/4.
- Examines each packet's header fields (source/destination IP, ports, protocol).
- Uses a static Access Control List (ACL); first-match wins.
- **Limitation:** No session awareness; spoofed packets with allowed IPs pass through.

**Generation 2 — Stateful Inspection Firewall (2 Marks):**
- Tracks active TCP/UDP sessions in a state table.
- Allows inbound packets **only if** they belong to an established outbound session.
- Detects and rejects anomalous packets (e.g., SYN without prior LISTEN state).
- **Advantage:** Defeats blind ACK scans and most spoofed-packet attacks.

**Generation 3 — Application-Layer Gateway (ALG / Proxy) (2 Marks):**
- Acts as an intermediary at Layer 7.
- Terminates client connection, inspects application payload, opens new connection to the server.
- Performs deep packet inspection (DPI) for HTTP headers, SQL syntax, malware signatures.
- **Trade-off:** Highest security but introduces latency and scalability cost.

**Architecture Diagram (1 Mark):** Refer to Section 4.4 — show three-layer flow from Stateless → Stateful → DPI.

#### (b) A corporate network has a web server in the DMZ. Design a two-firewall architecture (screened subnet) and explain the rule sets for each firewall. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture (3 Marks):** Refer to Section 4.3 diagram.
- **External Firewall** (between Internet and DMZ): allows HTTP/HTTPS from Internet to web server, blocks all direct access to internal LAN.
- **Internal Firewall** (between DMZ and LAN): allows only LAN-initiated connections to DMZ for administrative purposes (SSH, monitoring); blocks Internet-initiated connections to LAN.

**External Firewall Rules (2 Marks):**

| Rule ID | Action | Source | Destination | Protocol | Port |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | ALLOW | ANY | Web_Server_IP | TCP | 80, 443 |
| R2 | ALLOW | LAN_Subnet | ANY | TCP | 80, 443 (outbound) |
| R3 | DENY | ANY | LAN_Subnet | ANY | ANY |
| R4 | DENY | ANY | ANY | ANY | ANY (default) |

**Internal Firewall Rules (2 Marks):**

| Rule ID | Action | Source | Destination | Protocol | Port |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | ALLOW | LAN_Subnet | Web_Server_IP | TCP | 22, 161 (mgmt) |
| R2 | DENY | DMZ_Subnet | LAN_Subnet | ANY | ANY |
| R3 | DENY | ANY | ANY | ANY | ANY (default) |

**Defence-in-Depth Justification (Bonus 1 Mark):** Even if the external firewall is breached and the web server is compromised, the internal firewall prevents lateral movement into the corporate LAN — this is the **screened subnet / DMZ** design principle.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3**
> - **DoS vs DDoS:** DoS = single source; DDoS = botnet-coordinated multi-source.
> - **SYN Flood:** Exploits TCP handshake — half-open queue exhaustion. Defence: **SYN Cookies**, rate limiting.
> - **Smurf Attack:** ICMP to broadcast + spoofed source = amplification. Defence: **Disable directed broadcast** on routers.
> - **UDP Flood:** Random-port UDP packets → ICMP unreachable storms. Defence: rate-limit + filtering.
> - **Application DoS:** HTTP flood, Slowloris. Defence: **WAF, CAPTCHA, JS challenges**.
> - **Firewall Generations:** (1) Packet Filter (L3/4 stateless), (2) Stateful Inspection (session table), (3) ALG/Proxy (L7 DPI).
> - **DMZ Architecture:** Two-firewall screened subnet — external FW protects DMZ from Internet; internal FW protects LAN from compromised DMZ.
> - **ACL Rule Order:** Specific rules first, default `DENY ANY ANY` last. First-match semantics.
> - **NGFW:** Next-Generation Firewalls integrate all three generations + IDS/IPS + DPI.
> - **Critical Formula:** $R_{attack}^{*} = \dfrac{Q_{max}}{T_{timeout}}$ — minimum SYN rate for queue saturation.
> - **Amplification Formula:** $A = N$ for Smurf; $A_{byte} = 23.4 \times N$ for full-MTU Echo Replies.
> - **Always draw diagrams** in 7+ mark questions — KTU examiners expect at least one labelled schematic per long-answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IP Spoofing Prevention, Routing Protocols Security, and Router Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT633 - SECURE COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** IP Spoofing Prevention, Routing Protocols Security, and Router Security # Module 3 — IP Spoofing Prevention, Routing Protocols Security, and Router Security ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 IP Spoofing — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **IP Spoofing** is a network-layer impersonation attack in which a malicious source host forges the **Source IP Address** field in the IPv4/IPv6 header of outgoing packets so that they appear to originate from a trusted, legitimate, or unrelated host. According to **RFC 4942 (IPv6 Transition/Co-existence Security)** and **RFC 2827 (Network Ingress Filtering)**, IP spoofing is classified as a **Layer 3 Identity Subversion Attack**. It is the foundational primitive behind reflection attacks, session hijacking, and TCP SYN flooding. **Key technical taxonomy of IP spoofing:** | Spoofing Variant | Target Protocol | Primary Goal | |---|---|---| | Blind Spoofing | TCP (3-way handshake) | Session hijack without seeing replies | | Non-Blind Spoofing | TCP/UDP | Packet injection on same subnet | | Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) | ARP/DHCP | Bidirectional traffic interception | | Reflection/Amplification | UDP (DNS, NTP, Memcached) | Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) | | Source-Routing Spoofing | IP (Loose/Strict) | Bypass ACLs by dictating return path | ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Forged Return Address" Metaphor > [!NOTE] > **Real-world analogy:** Imagine posting a letter to a company with a fake return address on the envelope. The recipient (the *target server*) sends a confidential reply to the *fake address* (which is actually the attacker's victim). The attacker now has a powerful "relay" that redirects responses — and the company believes the letter came from a *trusted* sender. This is precisely how IP spoofing works: the **return path is forged**, and the **identity is falsified**. A **traceback** becomes extremely difficult because the *true* source IP in the packet header is fabricated; the attacker intentionally avoids the IP that would map back to their real machine. ### 1.3 Routing Protocol Security — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Routing Protocol Security** refers to the set of cryptographic, architectural, and procedural mechanisms that protect the **control plane** of network routing — particularly protocols such as **BGP (Border Gateway Protocol)**, **OSPF (Open Shortest Path First)**, **RIP (Routing Information Protocol)**, and **IS-IS** — against unauthorized route injection, route hijacking, route leaks, and session hijacking. Routing is the *brain* of the internet. If routing is compromised, **every packet** can be misdirected, regardless of how strong the application-layer encryption is. The motto is: *"If you can't trust the path, you can't trust the packet."* ### 1.4 Router Security — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Router Security** is the discipline of hardening the **Management Plane**, **Control Plane**, and **Data Plane** of network routers to prevent unauthorized access, configuration tampering, route manipulation, and denial-of-service. It encompasses AAA (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting), secure management protocols (SSH, SNMPv3, HTTPS), and access control mechanisms. **The Three Planes of a Router (critical concept for KTU):** | Plane | Function | Security Concern | Defense Mechanism | |---|---|---|---| | **Management Plane** | Configuration & monitoring | Unauthorized CLI/GUI access | SSHv2, AAA, TACACS+, RADIUS | | **Control Plane** | Routing protocol operation | Route hijacking, session theft | MD5/SHA authentication, BGPSEC | | **Data Plane (Forwarding Plane)** | Packet forwarding | Spoofed traffic, DoS | uRPF, ACLs, CoPP, BCP 38 | ### 1.5 Why These Three Topics Are Bundled in KTU Module 3 > [!NOTE] > KTU groups these topics because they form a **layered defense chain**: > 1. **IP Spoofing Prevention** → Stops attackers from *crossing* the network border. > 2. **Routing Protocol Security** → Stops attackers from *redirecting* traffic. > 3. **Router Security** → Stops attackers from *owning* the device that makes decisions. > > Compromising any one of these three collapses the entire security model. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 IP Spoofing — Attack Lifecycle The attack follows a deterministic four-phase lifecycle: 1. **Reconnaissance Phase** — Attacker enumerates topology using tools like `traceroute`, `ping sweeps`, and BGP looking glasses to find trusted IP ranges and ACL gaps. 2. **Forge Phase** — Using raw sockets (e.g., `scapy`, `hping3`), the attacker crafts packets with a *forged* source IP in the IP header. 3. **Injection Phase** — Forged packets are injected toward the target. The target's reply is sent to the **forged** (victim) address. 4. **Post-Exploitation** — Either a DoS against the victim (reflection), session hijack, or trust-bypass is executed. > [!IMPORTANT] > The attacker's own machine **never receives** the replies. This makes traceback non-trivial and is why *preventive* (not detective) controls are mandatory. ## 2.2 IP Spoofing Prevention — The Defensive Arsenal ### 2.2.1 Ingress Filtering (BCP 38 / RFC 2827) > [!IMPORTANT] > **RFC 2827 — "Network Ingress Filtering: Defeating Denial-of-Service Attacks which employ IP Source Address Spoofing"** (Best Current Practice 38, May 2000). The principle is simple yet powerful: **"Drop packets whose source address is not part of the announced/allocated prefix of the incoming network."** **Example:** If a router receives a packet on interface `ge0/0` with a source IP of `203.0.113.50` (a prefix not assigned to that customer link), the packet is **dropped silently** at the ISP edge. ### 2.2.2 Unicast Reverse Path Forwarding (uRPF) **uRPF** verifies that a packet's source address is *reachable* via the interface on which it arrived. Three modes exist: | Mode | Behavior | Use Case | |---|---|---| | **Strict uRPF** | Packet dropped if src-IP not in FIB for incoming interface | Symmetric paths (datacenter core) | | **Loose uRPF (uRPF Loose Check)** | Packet dropped only if src-IP is *unroutable* in FIB | Asymmetric paths (ISP edge) | | **ACL-Exempt uRPF** | Strict check but excludes traffic matching a specific ACL | Networks using NAT/PAT | **Operational Rule (Strict uRPF):** $$ \text{Action} = \begin{cases} \text{FORWARD} & \text{if } \text{FIB.lookup(src\_ip).out\_interface} == \text{in\_interface} \\ \text{DROP} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ ### 2.2.3 Access Control Lists (ACLs) & Source Guard - **IP Source Guard (IPSG):** Dynamically binds a Layer-2 port to a permitted source IP using DHCP snooping binding tables. - **Port ACLs / Transit ACLs:** Filter known-bad source prefixes at network borders. ### 2.2.4 Cryptographic Approaches (Hop-by-Hop / End-to-End) - **TCP MD5 Signature Option (RFC 2385):** Each TCP segment (SYN, data, FIN) carries an MD5 digest computed over a shared secret + TCP pseudo-header. Prevents blind TCP spoofing. - **IPsec AH (Authentication Header):** Provides *integrity* and *authentication* for the entire IP packet (including source IP). - **TCP-AO (Authentication Option, RFC 5925):** Successor to RFC 2385 MD5; uses stronger algorithms (HMAC-SHA-1, AES-CMAC). ## 2.3 Routing Protocol Security ### 2.3.1 BGP Security — The Critical Internet Protocol > [!IMPORTANT] > **BGP (Border Gateway Protocol, RFC 4271)** is the de facto inter-domain routing protocol of the Internet. It is a **path-vector** protocol where each Autonomous System (AS) advertises IP prefixes it can reach. **BGP has no built-in security** — it inherently trusts any UPDATE message from a configured peer. **Threats against BGP:** | Threat | Description | Real-World Example | |---|---|---| | **BGP Hijack** | Attacker announces a prefix they don't own | Pakistan Telecom hijacking YouTube (2008) | | **BGP Route Leak** | Misconfigured AS forwards routes beyond scope | AWS Route 53 leak (2018) | | **Sub-Prefix Hijack** | Announcing a more-specific /24 of a /8 | Indosat/Spain Telecom (2014) | | **BGP Session Hijack** | Forging BGP TCP session to inject routes | Various nation-state actors | ### 2.3.2 BGP Security Mechanisms | Mechanism | Layer | Function | |---|---|---| | **TCP MD5 Signature (RFC 2385)** | Transport | Authenticates BGP session packets | | **GTSM (Generalized TTL Security Mechanism, RFC 5082)** | Network | Rejects BGP packets with TTL < 254 (defeats off-path attackers) | | **BGPsec (RFC 8205)** | Path | Cryptographically signs AS_PATH so each hop can be verified | | **RPKI (Resource Public Key Infrastructure, RFC 6480)** | Origin | Cryptographically certifies the AS authorized to originate a prefix | | **ROA (Route Origin Authorization)** | RPKI object | Maps a prefix to the authorized originating AS | | **RPKI Route Origin Validation (ROV)** | RPKI process | Router drops/permits BGP routes based on ROA match | **RPKI Validation States:** $$ \text{ROV Result} = \begin{cases} \text{VALID} & \text{if ROA exists and matches (prefix, max\_len, AS)} \\ \text{INVALID} & \text{if ROA exists but prefix-length or AS mismatch} \\ \text{UNKNOWN} & \text{if no ROA exists in cache} \end{cases} $$ ### 2.3.3 OSPF and EIGRP Security - **OSPFv2 Authentication:** Two types — *plaintext* (deprecated) and **MD5** (per RFC 2328). OSPFv3 (RFC 5340) mandates IPsec ESP for authentication. - **EIGRP MD5 Authentication:** Cisco proprietary; HMAC-MD5 keyed hash on every EIGRP packet. - **Key Rollover:** Best practice is to configure two keys (current + next) to allow seamless key rotation. ### 2.3.4 RIP, IS-IS, and General Routing Authentication All modern routing protocols support message authentication via **HMAC**. The fundamental formula is: $$ \text{MAC} = \text{HMAC}_{K}\left(\text{Message} \, \Vert \, \text{SeqNum} \, \Vert \, \text{Timestamp}\right) $$ Where **K** is the shared secret (key), and the message digest authenticates both *integrity* and *source*. ## 2.4 Router Security ### 2.4.1 Router Hardening Checklist (KTU High-Yield) 1. **Disable unused services** — HTTP, Telnet, Finger, BOOTP, PAD. 2. **Use SSHv2 only** for management; disable SSHv1 (vulnerable to MITM). 3. **Enable AAA** with TACACS+ (preferred) or RADIUS for centralized auth. 4. **Set banners and legal warnings** for compliance with jurisdictional law. 5. **Restrict VTY lines** with `access-class` and `transport input ssh`. 6. **Enable `service password-encryption`** and use `enable secret` (MD5-crypt hash), never `enable password`. 7. **Implement role-based CLI** (parser views) for privilege separation. 8. **Use Control Plane Policing (CoPP)** to rate-limit traffic destined to the router's CPU (defeats control-plane DoS). 9. **Configure `login block-for`** to defeat brute-force attacks on VTY/console. 10. **Disable CDP/LLDP** on external interfaces to prevent topology disclosure. ### 2.4.2 SNMP Security | Version | Security | KTU Note | |---|---|---| | **SNMPv1** | Community string (plaintext) | **Insecure — never use** | | **SNMPv2c** | Community string (plaintext) | **Insecure — discouraged** | | **SNMPv3** | Auth (MD5/SHA) + Priv (DES/AES) | **Mandatory in KTU 2024 syllabus** | ### 2.4.3 Secure Management Protocols - **SSHv2** — Replaces Telnet. Diffie-Hellman key exchange, AES-256 CTR encryption, HMAC-SHA-2 integrity. - **HTTPS / NETCONF over SSH** — Replaces HTTP-based management. - **SCP / SFTP** — Secure file transfer (replaces TFTP and FTP). - **NTP with Authentication** — Prevents time-shift attacks on logging and certificate validation. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates the most-tested mathematical and algorithmic formulations for the KTU ESE. **No vertical bars (|) appear in table cells** to preserve rendering integrity. | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | HMAC Computation | $\text{HMAC}_{K}(M) = H\!\left[\left(K \oplus opad\right) \,\Vert\, H\!\left[\left(K \oplus ipad\right) \,\Vert\, M\right]\right]$ | $opad = 0x5C5C\ldots$, $ipad = 0x3636\ldots$ | | MD5 Block | $512\text{ bits} = 16 \times 32\text{-bit words}$ | Output = 128-bit digest | | SHA-1 Block | $512\text{ bits}$ | Output = 160-bit digest | | SHA-256 Block | $512\text{ bits}$ | Output = 256-bit digest | | GTSM TTL Threshold | $\text{TTL}_{\min} = 255 - \text{Hops}_{\max}$ | Typically 254 enforced for eBGP | | Strict uRPF Lookup | $\text{FIB}(\text{src\_ip}).\text{out\_iface} = \text{in\_iface}$? | Drop on mismatch | | RPKI ROV States | $\{VALID, INVALID, UNKNOWN\}$ | RPKI cache consulted | | OSPFv3 Crypto | $\text{ESP\_SPI} = \text{Hash}(\text{Key}, \text{Instance ID}, \text{Interface})$ | 32-bit Security Parameter Index | | BGP Path Vector Length | $\text{Length} = \vert \text{AS\_PATH} \vert$ (AS hops) | Used in route selection | | Brute-Force Lockout | $\text{Block Duration} = 30 \text{ to } 600 \text{ seconds}$ | After N failed attempts (N=3–5) | | SSHv2 Key Exchange | $\text{Shared Secret} = g^{ab} \bmod p$ | Diffie-Hellman, $\vert p \vert \ge 2048\text{ bits}$ | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of HMAC-MD5 for TCP Segment Authentication (RFC 2385) > [!NOTE] > This derivation shows how a router computes the MD5 digest that protects a **BGP TCP segment** from spoofing. This is the most-tested analytical question in KTU for this module. **Given:** A BGP TCP segment with the following fields: - TCP Source Port = $S_P$ - TCP Destination Port = $D_P$ - TCP Sequence Number = $\text{SEQ}$ - TCP Payload = $P$ - Shared secret key (pre-configured) = $K$ **Goal:** Compute the 16-byte MD5 digest that authenticates the segment. **Step 1 — Construct the TCP pseudo-header (used as part of the MD5 input):** The pseudo-header binds the digest to the *specific* 4-tuple of the TCP session, preventing replay across sessions. $$ \text{PseudoHdr} = \text{SrcIP} \,\Vert\, \text{DstIP} \,\Vert\, \text{ZeroByte} \,\Vert\, \text{Proto}=6 \,\Vert\, \text{TCPLen} $$ **Step 2 — Construct the full MD5 input message:** $$ M = \text{PseudoHdr} \,\Vert\, \text{TCPHeader} \,\Vert\, P $$ Where $\text{TCPHeader}$ includes source port, dest port, sequence, ack, data offset, flags, window, checksum (set to zero for computation), urgent pointer, and TCP options. **Step 3 — Append the shared secret $K$ (16 bytes max per RFC 2385):** $$ M' = M \,\Vert\, K $$ **Step 4 — Compute the MD5 digest using the HMAC construction:** $$ \text{Digest} = \text{HMAC}_{K}(M) = H\!\left[(K \oplus opad) \,\Vert\, H\!\left[(K \oplus ipad) \,\Vert\, M\right]\right] $$ Where the inner and outer padding constants are: $$ ipad = \underbrace{0x36 \,\Vert\, 0x36 \,\Vert\, \ldots \,\Vert\, 0x36}_{64 \text{ bytes}} \quad\quad opad = \underbrace{0x5C \,\Vert\, 0x5C \,\Vert\, \ldots \,\Vert\, 0x5C}_{64 \text{ bytes}} $$ **Step 5 — Place the 16-byte digest in the TCP header's MD5 Signature Option (Kind = 19, Length = 18).** The receiving router recomputes the digest over the same $M$ using the same $K$. If the digests match, the segment is **authentic** and unaltered. **Step 6 — Verification of forgery resistance:** An off-path attacker who does not know $K$ cannot produce a valid digest. The probability of a random 128-bit digest matching the legitimate digest is: $$ P_{\text{collision}} = \frac{1}{2^{128}} \approx 2.94 \times 10^{-39} $$ Thus forgery is computationally infeasible. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters for KTU:** This construction directly defeats the *blind spoofing* attack — the attacker cannot inject forged BGP TCP segments because they lack $K$. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Defense-in-Depth for IP Spoofing The following matrix maps **attack vectors** to **defensive controls** and **residual risk**: | Attack Vector | Primary Defense | Secondary Defense | Residual Risk | KTU Marks Weight | |---|---|---|---|---| | Source IP forgery at ISP edge | BCP 38 Ingress Filtering | Loose uRPF | Low if universally deployed | High (7 marks) | | Blind TCP session hijack | TCP MD5 / TCP-AO | IPsec AH | Low with strong keys | High (7 marks) | | Reflection DDoS (DNS/NTP) | Source validation at reflector | Rate limiting | Medium | Medium (5 marks) | | DHCP starvation + spoofing | DHCP Snooping + IPSG | Port security | Low on managed switches | Medium (5 marks) | | ARP spoofing (LAN) | Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) | Static ARP entries | Low | Medium (4 marks) | | BGP session hijack (off-path) | GTSM (TTL=255) | TCP-AO | Very Low | High (7 marks) | | BGP prefix hijack | RPKI / ROA + ROV | BGPSEC (path validation) | Medium (RPKI still deploying) | High (8 marks) | | Router credential brute force | `login block-for` + AAA | Banner warnings + IP allowlist | Very Low | Medium (4 marks) | ## 3.3 Worked Example — RPKI Route Origin Validation **Scenario:** Router R1 receives a BGP UPDATE advertising prefix `198.51.100.0/24` originated by AS 65001. **Step 1 — Router queries the RPKI cache (typically a local validator like Routinator or RPKI Validator 3).** **Step 2 — Validator checks the ROA database for `198.51.100.0/24`.** **Step 3 — Three possible outcomes:** $$ \text{RPKI State} = \begin{cases} \text{VALID} & \text{if ROA: } (198.51.100.0/24, \text{AS}=65001, \text{max\_len}=24) \text{ exists} \\ \text{INVALID} & \text{if ROA: } (198.51.100.0/24, \text{AS}=65002, \text{max\_len}=24) \text{ exists} \quad \text{[wrong AS]} \\ \text{INVALID} & \text{if ROA: } (198.51.100.0/22, \text{AS}=65001, \text{max\_len}=24) \text{ exists and announced}/24 \text{ is more specific} \\ \text{UNKNOWN} & \text{if no ROA found in any cache} \end{cases} $$ **Step 4 — Policy decision (configurable per operator):** - **Strict policy:** Drop `INVALID`, prefer `VALID` over `UNKNOWN`. - **Permissive policy:** Log `INVALID`, prefer `VALID`, allow `UNKNOWN`. - **Reject-all:** Drop both `INVALID` and `UNKNOWN`. **Conclusion:** RPKI shifts BGP from a *trust-based* model to a *cryptographically verifiable* model — the first major architectural change to BGP security in its 30+ year history. ## 3.4 The GTSM (TTL Hack) — Why TTL=254 Works **Derivation of GTSM protection:** $$ \text{TTL}_{\text{received}} = 255 - \text{Hops}_{\text{legitimate}} - 1 \quad \text{(decremented by each router)} $$ If the legitimate BGP peer is **1 hop away** (directly connected), the received TTL should be **254**. An off-path attacker would have to inject packets with a TTL of **255** (which routers would then decrement to 254) — but they cannot *spoof the source IP* AND *control TTL* AND *forge the TCP MD5* simultaneously. **Defense threshold:** $$ \text{Drop if } \text{TTL}_{\text{received}} < 254 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Off-path attacks fail} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > GTSM is **not a cryptographic** defense — it is a *lightweight topological* defense. It should be combined with TCP-AO or MD5 for true cryptographic session authentication. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — IP Spoofing Attack & Defense Flow The following diagram illustrates how a spoofed packet traverses the network and where each defense layer intercepts it. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Attacker Machine
Real IP 10.10.10.5] --> B[Forge Packet
Spoofed Src IP 203.0.113.50] B --> C[ISP Edge Router
Ingress Filtering Check] C -->|Src IP not in
customer prefix| D[Packet DROPPED
BCP 38 Violation] C -->|Src IP valid| E[Core Router
uRPF Verification] E -->|FIB mismatch| F[Packet DROPPED
uRPF Strict Check] E -->|FIB match| G[Target Server
Process TCP Segment] G --> H[Reply sent to
Spoofed IP 203.0.113.50] H --> I[Victim Host
Receives unsolicited reply] I --> J[Reflection DoS
or Session Hijack Attempt] A -.Forges.-> B ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — BGP Session Security Stack This architecture flow shows the layered cryptographic and topological defenses protecting a single BGP session between two Autonomous Systems. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph "eBGP Session Security Layers" L1[Layer 1 GTSM
TTL >= 254
Defeats off path attackers] --> L2 L2[Layer 2 TCP MD5 or TCP AO
Per segment HMAC authentication
Defeats blind spoofing] --> L3 L3[Layer 3 BGPSEC
Cryptographic AS PATH signature
Defeats route path forgery] --> L4 L4[Layer 4 RPKI Origin Validation
Cryptographic prefix ownership
Defeats prefix hijack] end AS65001[AS 65001 Router A] -->|BGP UPDATE
signed and validated| AS65002[AS 65002 Router B] L1 -.protects.-> AS65001 L4 -.protects.-> AS65002 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Router Three-Plane Security Architecture The canonical block-level functional architecture of router security, showing isolation of the three operational planes. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph MGMT[Management Plane] M1[SSHv2 CLI Access] M2[HTTPS Web Interface] M3[SNMPv3 Monitoring] M4[NETCONF YANG over SSH] end subgraph CTRL[Control Plane] C1[BGP with GTSM and TCP AO] C2[OSPFv3 with IPsec ESP] C3[IS IS with HMAC SHA] C4[Control Plane Policing CoPP] end subgraph DATA[Data Plane Forwarding Plane] D1[Unicast RPF Check] D2[Ingress ACL Filtering] D3[IP Source Guard on Access Ports] D4[DHCP Snooping Binding Table] end AAA[TACACS Plus AAA Server
Centralized Auth Audit Acct] --> MGMT RPKI[RPKI Validator Cache
Local ROA Store] --> CTRL MGMT -->|Secured by SSH AAA| ROUTER[Router Core CPU] CTRL -->|Secured by CoPP and crypto| ROUTER DATA -->|Secured by uRPF and ACL| ROUTER ROUTER --> FW[Forwarding Engine TCAM] ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — RPKI Validation Decision Flow The complete decision flow for Route Origin Validation as performed by a router consulting its local RPKI cache. ```mermaid flowchart TD P[BGP UPDATE Received
Prefix 198.51.100.0/24
Origin AS 65001] --> Q[Query RPKI Cache
for matching ROA] Q --> R{ROA found?} R -->|No| S[State UNKNOWN
Apply local policy
Accept or log] R -->|Yes| T{AS and prefix length
match ROA exactly?} T -->|Yes| U[State VALID
Prefer over UNKNOWN
Install in RIB] T -->|No| V[State INVALID
Apply local policy
Drop or depreference] S --> POL[Operator Policy Engine] U --> POL V --> POL POL --> RIB[Routing Information Base] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid compilation note:** All node identifiers above use alphanumeric prefixes (e.g., `node1` would be `A`, `B`, `C`) to comply with the KTU renderer safeguard. No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as node names. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define IP Spoofing. List any four prevention techniques. `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** IP Spoofing is the act of forging the **Source IP Address** field in an IP packet header to make the packet appear to originate from a trusted or different host, thereby bypassing IP-based authentication and enabling reflection, session hijack, or DoS attacks. **Four prevention techniques (2 marks — 0.5 each):** 1. **BCP 38 / RFC 2827 Ingress Filtering** — ISPs drop packets whose source address is not from the announced prefix of the incoming link. 2. **Unicast Reverse Path Forwarding (uRPF)** — Router verifies that the source IP is reachable via the incoming interface. 3. **TCP MD5 Signature Option (RFC 2385)** / **TCP-AO (RFC 5925)** — Authenticates each TCP segment cryptographically. 4. **IP Source Guard (IPSG)** — Dynamically binds Layer-2 ports to permitted source IPs via DHCP snooping. --- ### Question 2: What is RPKI? Explain the three validation states. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **RPKI Definition (1 mark):** **Resource Public Key Infrastructure (RPKI)** is a cryptographic framework defined in **RFC 6480** that issues **digital certificates** to Regional Internet Registries (RIRs), National Internet Registries (NIRs), and ISPs. These certificates authorize the holder to originate specific IP prefixes, enabling **Route Origin Validation (ROV)**. **Three Validation States (2 marks):** | State | Condition | Operator Action | |---|---|---| | **VALID** | ROA exists and prefix, max-length, and AS match the BGP announcement | Accept — install in RIB | | **INVALID** | ROA exists but announcement does not match (wrong AS or too specific) | Drop or depreference | | **UNKNOWN** | No ROA found in any RPKI cache for the prefix | Apply local policy (accept/log) | --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern) ### Question A (14 Marks) #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **With neat diagrams, explain IP Spoofing and the various techniques used to prevent it. Discuss the role of ingress filtering and uRPF in detail. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** **Model Solution:** **(i) Definition of IP Spoofing (1.5 marks):** IP Spoofing is the creation of IP packets with a **forged source address** to conceal the sender's identity or impersonate another computing system. The attacker exploits the fact that **routers forward packets based on destination address only** and typically do not validate the source. **Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the *Block Diagram — IP Spoofing Attack & Defense Flow* in Section 4.1. **(ii) Attack Lifecycle (1.5 marks):** Reconnaissance → Forge → Injection → Post-Exploitation (Reflection/Session Hijack). The attacker's IP is never seen by the target; the victim (whose IP was forged) receives the replies. **(iii) Prevention Techniques (1.5 marks):** - BCP 38 Ingress Filtering - uRPF (Strict and Loose) - IP Source Guard with DHCP Snooping - TCP MD5 / TCP-AO for session-level authentication - IPsec AH for end-to-end packet integrity **(iv) Detailed Role of Ingress Filtering and uRPF (1.5 marks):** **Ingress Filtering (BCP 38):** Operates at ISP edge routers. A packet arriving from a customer link must have a source IP belonging to that customer's allocated prefix. If not, it is dropped. This is the **first line of defense** and prevents spoofed traffic from leaving the attacker's network. **uRPF:** Operates at any router in the path. In **strict mode**, the router consults its FIB to determine the *expected* incoming interface for the source IP. If the packet arrived on a different interface, it is dropped. This catches packets that *somehow* bypassed the ISP's ingress filter. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Explain the security mechanisms available for BGP. Discuss BGPSEC and RPKI in detail. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** **Model Solution:** **(i) BGP Security Threat Landscape (1.5 marks):** BGP is a **path-vector** protocol with no native authentication of UPDATE messages. Attackers can hijack prefixes, leak routes, or forge sessions. The 2008 YouTube hijack by Pakistan Telecom is the canonical example. **(ii) Defense Layers (2 marks):** 1. **GTSM (RFC 5082):** Enforces TTL ≥ 254 on BGP packets to defeat off-path attackers. 2. **TCP MD5 / TCP-AO:** Authenticates the BGP TCP session segment-by-segment. 3. **BGPsec (RFC 8205):** Signs each AS hop in the AS_PATH cryptographically. 4. **RPKI / ROA / ROV:** Validates that the originating AS is authorized to announce the prefix. **(iii) BGPSEC in Detail (1.5 marks):** BGPsec uses **ECDSA signatures (P-256)** to sign each hop of the AS_PATH. When AS 65001 receives a route from AS 65002, AS 65001 adds its own signature covering the next-hop field. Downstream ASes can verify that no AS was inserted or removed in the path. **(iv) RPKI in Detail (2 marks):** RPKI provides **X.509 certificates** issued by RIRs (APNIC, ARIN, RIPE, AFRINIC, LACNIC) to organizations, binding them to their allocated IP prefixes. A **Route Origin Authorization (ROA)** is a signed object that authorizes a specific AS to originate a specific prefix (with optional max-length). Routers run **Route Origin Validation (ROV)** by consulting a local RPKI cache (e.g., Routinator) over the **RPKI-to-Router (RTR) protocol (RFC 8210)**. **Conclusion:** RPKI + BGPSEC together provide **origin authentication** and **path authentication** — the two missing pillars of BGP security for 30 years. --- ### Question B — Alternative 14-Mark Question #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Discuss Router Security in detail. Explain the three planes of a router and the security mechanisms applicable to each. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** **Model Solution:** **(i) Introduction (1 mark):** Router security is a multi-layered discipline targeting the management, control, and data planes of network devices. A single compromised router can subvert an entire network. **(ii) The Three Planes (3 marks — 1 mark each):** - **Management Plane:** Handles configuration, monitoring, and software management. Compromised management = full device takeover. Defenses: SSHv2, HTTPS, SNMPv3, AAA (TACACS+/RADIUS), role-based CLI, banners, login block-for. - **Control Plane:** Handles routing protocol operation (BGP, OSPF, IS-IS). Compromised control = route hijacking. Defenses: GTSM, TCP MD5/TCP-AO, OSPFv3 IPsec, CoPP to defeat control-plane DoS. - **Data Plane:** Handles per-packet forwarding. Compromised data = traffic misdirection, DoS. Defenses: uRPF, ACLs, IP Source Guard, DHCP Snooping, DAI. **(iii) Router Hardening Checklist (2 marks):** Disable unused services, use SSHv2, set `enable secret`, restrict VTY with `access-class`, enable `service password-encryption`, configure CoPP, enable `login block-for`, disable CDP on external interfaces, use NTP authentication, enable logging with syslog over TLS. **(iv) SNMPv3 vs SNMPv2c (1 mark):** SNMPv2c uses plaintext community strings and is vulnerable to sniffing and replay. SNMPv3 provides **authentication (HMAC-MD5/SHA)** and **privacy (DES/AES-128/192/256)** and is mandatory in modern deployments. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Explain GTSM and TCP MD5 Signature in detail. How do they protect the BGP session? `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** **Model Solution:** **(i) GTSM — Generalized TTL Security Mechanism (RFC 5082) (3 marks):** - GTSM is a *lightweight* defense that leverages the **IP TTL field** to verify that BGP packets originated from a directly connected peer. - The legitimate BGP peer is **1 hop away**, so the received TTL should be **254** (255 - 1 hop). - The router is configured to **drop any BGP packet with TTL < 254**. - An off-path attacker trying to inject BGP packets from beyond 1 hop would have a TTL ≤ 253, and the packet would be discarded. - **Limitation:** GTSM is *topological*, not cryptographic. An attacker *on the same subnet* could still inject packets. Hence, GTSM must be combined with TCP-AO for full protection. **(ii) TCP MD5 Signature (RFC 2385) (3 marks):** - Each BGP TCP segment carries an **MD5 digest** in a TCP option (Kind=19, Length=18). - The digest is computed over: **pseudo-header + TCP header + payload + shared secret key**. - The receiving router recomputes the digest using the same key and compares. - An off-path attacker who doesn't know the key cannot produce a valid digest. - **Limitation:** MD5 is cryptographically weakened; modern deployments use **TCP-AO (RFC 5925)** with **HMAC-SHA-1-96** or **AES-CMAC**. **(iii) Combined Effect (1 mark):** GTSM filters by topology; TCP MD5/AO filters by cryptographic key. Together they defeat both off-path and on-path session hijack attempts, making the BGP session highly resistant to spoofing. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing Ingress Filtering with uRPF:** Ingress filtering checks *announced prefix*; uRPF checks *FIB reachability*. Examiners frequently give partial credit for conflating them. (Penalty: -1.5 marks) > 2. **Stating MD5 is "secure":** MD5 is **cryptographically broken** (collision attacks demonstrated). State "deprecated; use TCP-AO with HMAC-SHA-1 or stronger." (Penalty: -1 mark) > 3. **Forgetting the three RPKI states:** Many students write only VALID and INVALID, missing **UNKNOWN**. (Penalty: -1 mark) > 4. **Skipping diagrams in Part B:** The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly requires *neat labeled diagrams* for full 14-mark questions. (Penalty: -1 to -2 marks) > 5. **Confusing RPKI with BGPSEC:** RPKI = **origin** validation. BGPSEC = **path** validation. They are *complementary*, not interchangeable. (Penalty: -1 mark) > 6. **Missing plane identification:** When asked about router security, always explicitly name the **Management / Control / Data** plane each defense protects. (Penalty: -1 mark) --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:** - ☐ **IP Spoofing** is forging the Source IP in the IP header — the attacker's real IP is *never seen* by the target. - ☐ **BCP 38 / RFC 2827** is the foundational ingress filtering standard — drop packets with src-IPs not in the announced prefix. - ☐ **uRPF Strict** requires FIB(in)=in_interface; **uRPF Loose** only drops if src-IP is *unroutable*; useful for asymmetric paths. - ☐ **IP Source Guard** uses DHCP Snooping binding table to enforce per-port source IP. - ☐ **BGP** is a path-vector protocol with **no native security** — every defense is *added on top*. - ☐ **GTSM (RFC 5082)** uses TTL ≥ 254 to defeat off-path attackers; it is *topological*, not cryptographic. - ☐ **TCP MD5 (RFC 2385)** and **TCP-AO (RFC 5925)** authenticate individual TCP segments; MD5 is **deprecated**, prefer TCP-AO. - ☐ **RPKI (RFC 6480)** binds AS numbers to prefixes via X.509 certificates. - ☐ **ROA** = signed object that authorizes (prefix, max-length, AS) tuple. - ☐ **RPKI ROV States** = `{VALID, INVALID, UNKNOWN}` — operators choose policy for each. - ☐ **BGPSEC (RFC 8205)** uses ECDSA-P256 signatures to bind each AS hop; defends path integrity. - ☐ **RPKI-to-Router (RTR) protocol (RFC 8210)** is how routers fetch validated ROAs from a local cache. - ☐ **OSPFv2** supports MD5 authentication; **OSPFv3** mandates **IPsec ESP** for authentication. - ☐ **SNMPv3** is mandatory; v1/v2c use plaintext community strings and are **insecure**. - ☐ **Router Three Planes:** Management (SSH/AAA), Control (routing crypto + CoPP), Data (uRPF/ACL/IPSG). - ☐ **AAA** = Authentication, Authorization, Accounting — TACACS+ preferred for device admin; RADIUS for network access. - ☐ **`login block-for`** with N failed attempts and 30–600s lockout defeats brute-force. - ☐ **CoPP (Control Plane Policing)** rate-limits traffic destined to the router CPU to defeat control-plane DoS. - ☐ **NTP authentication** prevents time-shift attacks on logs and certificate validation. - ☐ **Enable secret** uses MD5-crypt hash; **enable password** uses reversible Vigenère and is **insecure** — never use it. - ☐ The **KAM** (Key Authentication Material) for BGP, OSPF, EIGRP should be **rotated periodically** using a **two-key overlap** strategy to avoid session drops. - ☐ The **2008 YouTube hijack** by Pakistan Telecom is the canonical real-world case study for BGP insecurity — quote it in 14-mark answers. - ☐ **RPKI deployment** is ongoing — as of 2024, **~50% of advertised IPv4 space** has ROA coverage; reference this in long-form answers. - ☐ The defense-in-depth ordering is: **BCP 38 (ISP edge) → uRPF (core) → TCP-AO (session) → RPKI (origin) → BGPSEC (path)** — remember the chain.

topics.title: Domain Name Server (DNS) Security and Traffic Monitoring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT633 - SECURE COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Domain Name Server (DNS) Security and Traffic Monitoring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition: DNS Security

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DNS Security** is the discipline of protecting the Domain Name System — the Internet's distributed hierarchical naming database — against unauthorized access, data tampering, spoofing, cache poisoning, denial-of-service, and information leakage, employing cryptographic authentication (DNSSEC), encrypted transport (DoT/DoH), and continuous traffic telemetry.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, **Domain Name System (DNS) Security** encompasses three orthogonal protection layers:

1. **Data-origin authentication** — ensuring the DNS response truly originated from the authoritative server (achieved via **DNSSEC**, RFC 4033–4035).
2. **Channel confidentiality and integrity** — preventing eavesdropping and tampering of DNS queries in transit (achieved via **DNS-over-TLS (DoT)** — RFC 7858 and **DNS-over-HTTPS (DoH)** — RFC 8484).
3. **Availability and rate-limiting protection** — defending authoritative and recursive resolvers against reflection and amplification floods (mitigated via **Response Rate Limiting (RRL)**, BCP 199 / **RFC 5210** derivatives).

### 1.2 Formal Definition: DNS Traffic Monitoring

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DNS Traffic Monitoring** is the systematic capture, parsing, statistical analysis, and correlation of DNS query/response packets traversing a network to detect anomalies, malicious domains, command-and-control (C2) beaconing, data exfiltration, and policy violations, typically performed via passive taps on port **UDP/53** or **TCP/53**, full-packet captures, or passive DNS replication feeds.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Internet's Phone Book & Security Guard"**
> Imagine DNS as the world's largest, decentralized telephone directory. When you type `www.ktu.ac.in`, your computer is asking a "librarian" (the recursive resolver) to fetch the actual phone number (IP address `103.x.x.x`) of that name. **DNS Security** is like installing tamper-proof seals on every page of that directory so nobody can slip in a fake number — and putting the librarian behind bullet-proof glass so eavesdroppers can't overhear your request. **DNS Traffic Monitoring**, meanwhile, is the closed-circuit TV (CCTV) and logbook in the library — it records every lookup, every spelling, every repeat visitor, so the librarian can later spot someone suspicious asking for 10,000 fraudulent bank domains in 5 minutes.

### 1.4 Standardized Constants & Reference Metrics

- **Default DNS port:** **UDP/53** (queries), **TCP/53** (zone transfers, large responses, DNSSEC).
- **Encrypted DNS ports:** **TCP/853** (DoT), **TCP/443** (DoH over HTTPS).
- **Maximum DNS UDP message size:** **512 bytes** (RFC 1035); extended to **1232 bytes** via **EDNS0** (RFC 6891) to accommodate DNSSEC chains.
- **DNS header size:** fixed **12 bytes**; question/answer RRs are variable.
- **Root zone signing key rollover cadence:** currently **30-day** ZSK, **multi-year** KSK.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 expects the student to differentiate between **authoritative DNS**, **recursive DNS**, and **stub resolvers**, and to map each attack class (cache poisoning, NXDOMAIN attack, tunneling, random-subdomain) to the corresponding detection signal in monitoring tools.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Threat Model of DNS

The DNS attack surface is decomposed into four logically separated planes; every defensive control maps to exactly one plane:

1. **Resolver Plane (Client ↔ Recursive Resolver)**
   - Threats: on-path eavesdropping, query injection, ISP-level censorship.
   - Controls: DoT (RFC 7858), DoH (RFC 8484), DNSCrypt (legacy), QNAME minimization (RFC 9156).

2. **Authoritative Plane (Resolver ↔ Authoritative Server)**
   - Threats: cache poisoning, Kaminsky-style birthday forgery, RRset tampering.
   - Controls: **DNSSEC** validation chain, source-port randomization (16-bit entropy), transaction-ID (16-bit) randomization, 0x20-bit encoding entropy.

3. **Infrastructure Plane (Anycast/EDNS)**
   - Threats: DNS amplification DDoS (UDP reflection), NXDOMAIN flood, random-subdomain attacks.
   - Controls: **RRL (Response Rate Limiting)**, BCP 38 ingress filtering, anycast distribution, ACLs.

4. **Data Plane (Log/Telemetry)**
   - Threats: tunneling (data exfil via TXT/A-record), DGAs (Domain Generation Algorithms), Fast-Flux.
   - Controls: passive DNS sensors, statistical baseline, SIEM correlation, ML anomaly scoring.

### 2.2 DNSSEC Chain of Trust — Operational Logic

DNSSEC achieves authentication via an asymmetric signature chain anchored at the **Root Zone KSK** (Key Signing Key):

1. The **Root Zone** signs the **DS (Delegation Signer)** record of each TLD (`.in`, `.com`, …) using its **KSK**.
2. The TLD signs the **DS record** of each second-level domain using its own **KSK**.
3. The second-level domain signs its full **RRset** (A, AAAA, MX, …) using its **ZSK (Zone Signing Key)**.
4. A validating resolver follows the chain upward, verifying each signature against the parent's published DS record until the trust anchor (root KSK) is reached.
5. If any signature fails cryptographic verification, the response is marked **SERVFAIL** or **Bogus**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two key types?** The KSK is short and used only to sign the DS RRset; it is rotated rarely (multi-year) so that the parent-registered DS digest remains stable. The ZSK is rotated frequently (monthly) to limit exposure of an active signing key. This is a textbook example of **key-separation** in PKI.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Expression | Standard Value / Unit | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| DNS Amplification Factor | $AF = \dfrac{S_{\text{resp}}}{S_{\text{query}}}$ | typically **28× – 54×** for DNSSEC ANY queries | Compute reflected bandwidth |
| Birthday Collision Probability | $P_{\text{coll}} \approx 1 - e^{-n^{2}/(2N)}$ | dimensionless, $0 \le P \le 1$ | Cache poisoning risk estimation |
| Validating Resolver Latency | $T_{\text{val}} = T_{\text{RTT}} + T_{\text{crypto}}$ | milliseconds | DNSSEC performance budgeting |
| Source-Port Entropy | $H_{\text{port}} = \log_{2}(2^{16}) = 16$ bits | bits | Anti-spoofing design |
| Transaction-ID Entropy | $H_{\text{TXID}} = \log_{2}(2^{16}) = 16$ bits | bits | Combined with port for 32-bit guessing space |
| NXDOMAIN Rate Threshold | $R_{\text{nx}} > \mu + k\sigma$ | queries/sec | Anomaly detection trigger |
| Passive DNS Replication Window | $W_{\text{obs}}$ | typically **7 – 30 days** | Indicator-of-Compromise lookups |

### 2.4 DNS-over-TLS vs DNS-over-HTTPS — Engineering Trade-Offs

| Property | DNS over UDP/53 (Plain) | DNS over TLS (DoT) | DNS over HTTPS (DoH) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Confidentiality | None | TLS 1.2/1.3 | TLS 1.2/1.3 inside HTTP/2 |
| Wire port | UDP **53** | TCP **853** | TCP **443** |
| Latency overhead | Baseline | +1 RTT (TLS handshake) | +1 RTT + HTTP framing |
| Indistinguishable from HTTPS | No | No (SNI exposes) | **Yes** (multiplexed on 443) |
| Centralization risk | Low (per-ISP) | Low | **High** (single provider) |
| Standard | RFC 1035 | **RFC 7858** | **RFC 8484** |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In production environments (enterprise SOCs, ISP NOCs, cloud providers like Cloudflare `1.1.1.1`, Google `8.8.8.8`, Quad9 `9.9.9.9`), DNS security and monitoring underpin:
- **Threat Intelligence Feeds** (passive DNS databases such as Farsight DNSDB, RiskIQ).
- **C2 Detection** — beaconing domains follow periodic timing with low-variance TTLs.
- **Brand Protection** — typosquatting detection via Levenshtein-distance monitoring of newly registered domains (NRDs).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: DNS Cache Poisoning Probability (Birthday Bound)

The original Kaminsky-style cache-poisoning attack treats the resolver's cache as a 16-bit TXID × 16-bit source-port space. An attacker forges responses hoping to win the race before the legitimate one. The probability that at least one forged response matches the live query across $n$ trials is governed by the **birthday problem**.

**Step 1 — Define the problem parameters.**
