Let the resolver's live-query state be defined by the tuple:

$$\mathcal{Q} = \{\,\text{TXID}, \text{SrcPort}, \text{QNAME}, \text{QTYPE}, \text{QCLASS}\,\}$$

The attacker must match the first three fields; QNAME is fixed (the queried name). The effective guessing space is:

$$N = 2^{16} \times 2^{16} = 2^{32}$$

because both TXID and source-port are independently randomized.

**Step 2 — State the birthday collision formula.**

For $n$ independently sampled guesses drawn uniformly from a space of size $N$, the probability that **at least one** guess collides with the target is:

$$P_{\text{coll}}(n, N) = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^{n}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the first-order exponential approximation.**

Using the standard identity $\lim_{x \to 0} (1-x) \approx e^{-x}$, for $n \ll N$:

$$\left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^{n} \approx e^{-n/N}$$

Hence:

$$P_{\text{coll}} \approx 1 - e^{-n/N}$$

**Step 4 — Express in the canonical birthday form.**

When the attacker's *forged* pool itself contains $m$ guesses racing against another pool of $m$ guesses (e.g., authoritative vs. attacker), the standard birthday form applies:

$$P_{\text{birthday}} \approx 1 - e^{-m^{2}/(2N)}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example for KTU numerical.**

Suppose $N = 2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296$ and the attacker fires $m = 2^{16} = 65{,}536$ guesses.

First compute the exponent:

$$\frac{m^{2}}{2N} = \frac{(2^{16})^{2}}{2 \cdot 2^{32}} = \frac{2^{32}}{2^{33}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

Therefore the collision probability is:

$$P_{\text{birthday}} = 1 - e^{-0.5} = 1 - 0.6065 = 0.3935 \approx 39.35\%$$

This is why **DNSSEC** was the industry response — it removes the *forgery success* outcome by cryptographically binding the RRset, even if the attacker wins the birthday race.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Pattern:** Typical board question asks "with 16-bit port + 16-bit TXID, how many guesses for 50% success?" Solving $1 - e^{-m^{2}/(2 \cdot 2^{32})} = 0.5$ gives $m = \sqrt{2 \cdot 2^{32} \cdot \ln 2} \approx 77{,}373$ guesses.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: DNS Amplification Attack Bandwidth

A DNS amplification attack leverages a small *spoofed* query that produces a large *reflected* response, multiplying the attacker's bandwidth by $AF$.

**Step 1 — Bandwidth amplification factor.**

$$AF = \frac{S_{\text{response}}}{S_{\text{query}}}$$

**Step 2 — Effective attack throughput.**

If the attacker possesses an uplink of rate $B_{\text{atk}}$ (bits/sec) and the reflection yields factor $AF$, the volumetric DDoS rate against the victim is:

$$B_{\text{victim}} = AF \cdot B_{\text{atk}}$$

**Step 3 — Concrete DNSSEC ANY query case.**

A typical `ANY` query against a DNSSEC-signed zone is ~**64 bytes**; the response (with NSEC/NSEC3 denial-of-existence proofs, RRSIGs, DNSKEYs) is often ~**3,500 bytes**.

$$AF_{\text{DNSSEC-ANY}} = \frac{3500}{64} \approx 54.7\times$$

**Step 4 — Botnet scale estimation.**

Suppose a Mirai-class botnet has $10{,}000$ infected hosts, each sustaining an uplink of $B_{\text{atk}} = 1\ \text{Mbps}$. The aggregated reflected DDoS bandwidth is:

$$B_{\text{victim}} = 54.7 \times 10{,}000 \times 1\ \text{Mbps} = 547{,}000\ \text{Mbps} = 547\ \text{Gbps}$$

This is precisely the magnitude observed in historical events such as the **2018 Memcached reflection attacks** (peak ~1.7 Tbps) and earlier DNS-based floods.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Detection Signals per Attack Class

| Attack Class | Telemetry Signal | Threshold Heuristic | Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cache Poisoning | Sudden legitimate-name → suspicious-IP resolution | $\Delta \text{TTL}_{\text{new}} < \mu - 3\sigma$ | DNSSEC + 0x20 encoding |
| NXDOMAIN Flood | High rate of NXDOMAIN responses | $R_{\text{nx}} > \mu + 5\sigma$ | RRL + RPZ |
| Random-Subdomain | High-entropy QNAME, low repeat rate | $H_{\text{QNAME}} > 4.5$ bits/char | Sinkhole + upstream filtering |
| DNS Tunneling | Long TXT / NULL records, high base64 entropy | $L_{\text{RR}} > 200\ \text{bytes}$, $\text{entropy} > 4.8$ | Egress filtering, DPI |
| DGA Beaconing | High NXDOMAIN ratio + low TTL variance | $\text{CV}_{\text{TTL}} < 0.05$ | ML classifier on passive DNS |
| Fast Flux | High A-record churn per domain | $N_{\text{A-records}/\text{day}} > 50$ | Reputation scoring |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 DNS Resolution Flow with DNSSEC Validation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[stubClient: Application] --> B[OS Resolver: Port 53]
    B --> C{Question Cached?}
    C -- yes --> Z[Return Cached RRset]
    C -- no --> D[Recursive Resolver: Unbound or Knot]
    D --> E[Root Server: .]
    E -- DS of .in --> F[TLD Server: .in]
    F -- DS of ktu.ac.in --> G[Authoritative Server: ktu.ac.in]
    G --> H[Return Signed RRset with RRSIG]
    H --> I{Validate DNSSEC Chain}
    I -- valid --> J[Return to Client with AD bit set]
    I -- invalid --> K[Return SERVFAIL or Bogus]
    J --> B
    K --> B
```

### 4.2 DNS Cache Poisoning Attack Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant V as Victim Resolver
    participant A as Attacker
    participant L as Legitimate Authoritative
    V->>L: Query ID=12345 for example.com
    A->>V: Forged reply ID=12345, src=53, IP=bad.example
    L->>V: Legitimate reply ID=12345, src=53, IP=good.example
    Note over V: First reply wins; cache poisoned
    V-->>V: example.com TTL=86400 cached as bad.example
```

### 4.3 DNS Traffic Monitoring Architecture (SOC)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EDGE
        TAP1[SPAN Port Tap] --> COL[Collector: Argus or GoFlow]
        FW1[Firewall Logs] --> COL
        LB1[Load Balancer Logs] --> COL
    end
    COL --> PIPE[Pipeline: Kafka Topic dns-events]
    PIPE --> PARSE[Parser: Zeek dns.log]
    PARSE --> DB1[(Time-Series DB: ClickHouse)]
    PARSE --> DB2[(Graph DB: Neo4j for passive DNS)]
    DB1 --> ML[Anomaly Engine: Isolation Forest]
    DB2 --> ML
    ML --> DASH[SOC Dashboard: Grafana]
    ML --> ALERT[SIEM Alert: Splunk or Elastic]
    DASH --> ANALYST[SOC Analyst]
    ALERT --> ANALYST
```

### 4.4 Encrypted DNS Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Client Initiates Name Lookup] --> Q1{Privacy Required?}
    Q1 -- no --> PL[Plain DNS over UDP 53]
    Q1 -- yes --> Q2{Network Restrictions on Port 853?}
    Q2 -- no --> DOT[DoT over TCP 853 with TLS 1.3]
    Q2 -- yes --> Q3{Need Stealth from DPI?}
    Q3 -- yes --> DOH[DoH over TCP 443 with HTTP/2]
    Q3 -- no --> DOT
    PL --> RES[Resolver Returns IP]
    DOT --> RES
    DOH --> RES
```

### 4.5 DNSSEC Chain-of-Trust Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOTKSK[Root Zone KSK 20326 Trust Anchor] --> ROOTZSK[Root ZSK signs TLD DS]
    ROOTZSK --> TLDDS[DS Hash of .in KSK]
    TLDDS --> INKSK[.in Zone KSK]
    INKSK --> INZSK[.in Zone ZSK]
    INZSK --> KTUDS[DS Hash of ktu.ac.in]
    KTUDS --> KTUKSK[ktu.ac.in KSK]
    KTUKSK --> KTUZSK[ktu.ac.in ZSK signs RRset]
    KTUZSK --> RR[A Record: 103.x.x.x with RRSIG]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between authoritative DNS and recursive DNS with one example each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An **authoritative DNS server** is the final source of truth for a zone; it holds the master copy of the RRset and responds with the AA (Authoritative Answer) flag set. Example: the NS servers of `ktu.ac.in` operated by the university's hosting provider.
> A **recursive DNS server** (resolver) performs the full iterative lookup on behalf of the client, traversing root → TLD → authoritative, caching intermediate results. Example: Google's `8.8.8.8` or Cloudflare's `1.1.1.1`.
> (Marks: 1 — def authoritative; 1 — def recursive; 1 — example each.)

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three DNS attack types and the corresponding mitigation control.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Cache Poisoning** → Mitigated by **DNSSEC** validation and source-port randomization (1 mark).
> 2. **DNS Amplification DDoS** → Mitigated by **Response Rate Limiting (RRL)** and BCP 38 ingress filtering at edge routers (1 mark).
> 3. **DNS Tunneling / Data Exfiltration** → Mitigated by **egress DPI** and entropy-based inspection of TXT/NULL records (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (Module Internal Choice)

> Each sub-question carries **7 marks**; total **14 marks** per choice.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Adapted)]`**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the operation of **DNSSEC**. How does the chain of trust terminate at the root? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** DNSSEC (Domain Name System Security Extensions) is defined by RFC 4033–4035 and adds two new record types — **RRSIG** (signature) and **DNSKEY** (public key) — plus **DS** (Delegation Signer) for chain linking.

2. **Chain-of-Trust Operation (4 marks):**
   - The **Root Zone KSK** is the universally distributed *trust anchor*, hardcoded into validating resolvers (BIND, Unbound).
   - Each parent zone signs a **DS record** in the child zone using the parent's KSK; the child exposes a matching **DNSKEY** (KSK).
   - The child signs its own RRsets with its **ZSK**, producing **RRSIG** records.
   - A validating resolver, on receiving an RRset, fetches the parent's DS record and the child's DNSKEY, then verifies the RRSIG. It walks upward until reaching the root KSK.

3. **Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.5 above; draw the block schematic showing Root KSK → TLD DS → Domain KSK → ZSK → signed RRset.

4. **Failure Modes (1 mark):** If any signature fails, the resolver returns **SERVFAIL/Bogus**; if the chain has a missing DS, the zone is said to be **insecure** (DNSSEC opt-out).

---

**(b)** Compute the DNS amplification factor and the resulting reflected DDoS bandwidth for an attacker with a **10 Mbps uplink** launching a DNSSEC ANY-query reflection. Use $AF \approx 54.7$ and explain how **RRL (Response Rate Limiting)** reduces the attack. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Amplification factor formula (2 marks):**

$$AF = \frac{S_{\text{response}}}{S_{\text{query}}} = \frac{3500\ \text{bytes}}{64\ \text{bytes}} \approx 54.7$$

2. **Reflected bandwidth (2 marks):**

$$B_{\text{victim}} = AF \times B_{\text{atk}} = 54.7 \times 10\ \text{Mbps} = 547\ \text{Mbps}$$

3. **RRL Mechanism (2 marks):** Response Rate Limiting (RFC implementation per F-root / BCP 199) caps identical responses per source-IP prefix per second. Each `ANY` reply to a spoofed source is rate-limited to ~5–10 responses/sec per /24, collapsing the amplification factor to a few ×.

4. **Best-Practice Statement (1 mark):** Combined with **BCP 38** ingress filtering at the ISP edge (which blocks source-spoofed packets from leaving the network), the attack is structurally neutered.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Adapted)]`**

**(a)** Compare **DNS-over-TLS (DoT)** with **DNS-over-HTTPS (DoH)** under the headings: confidentiality, port, latency, censorship resistance, and centralization risk. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | DoT (RFC 7858) | DoH (RFC 8484) |
|---|---|---|
| Confidentiality | TLS 1.2/1.3 (1 mark) | TLS 1.2/1.3 inside HTTP/2 (1 mark) |
| Port | **TCP 853** (1 mark) | **TCP 443** (1 mark) |
| Latency | +1 RTT for TLS handshake | +1 RTT plus HTTP/2 framing (1 mark) |
| Censorship resistance | Low (port easily blocked by DPI) | High (multiplexed with HTTPS, indistinguishable) (1 mark) |
| Centralization risk | Low (per-ISP resolvers common) | High (single-vendor providers like Cloudflare or Google dominate) (1 mark) |
| Standard | RFC 7858 | RFC 8484 (1 mark) |

---

**(b)** Design a **DNS Traffic Monitoring pipeline** for a mid-sized enterprise SOC. List the components, their roles, and the detection heuristic for **DNS tunneling** and **DGA beaconing**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Architecture (3 marks):** Use the diagram in Section 4.3.
   - **Tap:** SPAN port on the core switch mirroring UDP/53 and TCP/53 to a collector.
   - **Collector:** Argus or GoFlow for flow records; Zeek (`bro-dns`) for protocol-aware DNS logs.
   - **Pipeline:** Kafka topic `dns-events` decouples ingest from analytics.
   - **Storage:** Time-series DB (ClickHouse or Elasticsearch) for raw queries; Graph DB for passive DNS relationships.
   - **Analytics:** Isolation Forest anomaly engine + signature-based correlation with threat intel (e.g., AbuseIPDB, AlienVault OTX).
   - **Presentation:** Grafana dashboard + Splunk/Elastic SIEM alerting.

2. **DNS Tunneling detection (2 marks):** Raise alert when $\text{TXT/NULL record length} > 200\ \text{bytes}$ AND base64 entropy $> 4.8$ bits/char AND domain is not in the allowlist. Verify by attempting to decode the payload; if it produces a recognizable protocol (HTTP, SSH, ICMP), confirm exfiltration.

3. **DGA detection (2 marks):** Compute QNAME lexical entropy $H_{\text{QNAME}}$; alert when $H_{\text{QNAME}} > 4.5$ bits/char combined with high NXDOMAIN ratio ($> 70\%$) over a 5-minute sliding window. Cross-reference against known DGA-family HMMs (e.g., Conficker, Mirai, Suppobox).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers**
> 1. **Confusing RRL with rate-limiting of *incoming* queries.** RRL limits *outgoing* responses per prefix, not inbound traffic. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this reversal.
> 2. **Writing "DNSSEC encrypts DNS."** DNSSEC only *authenticates* — it does **not** provide confidentiality. Confusing this with DoT/DoH costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the 32-bit guessing space.** Many students state only 16-bit TXID entropy. The 2008 Kaminsky disclosure showed source-port randomization is mandatory; total entropy is **32 bits**, not 16.
> 4. **Mis-stating the port number for DoH.** DoH is **443**, not 853. This single-character slip costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Omitting the KSK vs. ZSK separation** in DNSSEC answers — a KTU-favourite 2-mark sub-question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DNS** is **port 53** (UDP for queries, TCP for zone transfers and large responses); **DoT = port 853**; **DoH = port 443**.
- **DNSSEC = authentication, NOT encryption** (RFC 4033–4035); uses RRSIG, DNSKEY, DS, NSEC/NSEC3 records.
- **Trust anchor** is the **Root Zone KSK** (currently Key Tag 20326).
- **Chain-of-trust** flows: Root KSK → TLD DS → Domain KSK → ZSK → signed RRset.
- **DoT** = RFC 7858, TLS 1.2/1.3 on TCP 853; **DoH** = RFC 8484, HTTP/2 over TLS on TCP 443.
- **Kaminsky attack (2008)** revealed 16-bit TXID alone is insufficient; combined with source-port randomization gives **32-bit guessing entropy**.
- **DNS Amplification Factor** $AF = S_{\text{resp}}/S_{\text{query}}$; DNSSEC-ANY queries achieve **~54.7×** amplification.
- **RRL (Response Rate Limiting)** per BCP 199 caps identical responses per source prefix; effective mitigation for reflection DDoS.
- **DNS Tunneling** signature: high-entropy TXT/NULL records $> 200$ bytes; base64 entropy $> 4.8$ bits/char.
- **DGA Beaconing** signature: high-QNAME lexical entropy + high-NXDOMAIN ratio + low-TTL variance.
- **Passive DNS** databases (Farsight DNSDB, RiskIQ) provide historical reputation lookups for IOCs.
- **Birthday-collision probability** for cache poisoning: $P \approx 1 - e^{-m^{2}/(2N)}$ where $N = 2^{32}$.
- **EDNS0** (RFC 6891) extended UDP payload limit from **512 bytes** to **1232 bytes** to carry DNSSEC chains.
- **BCP 38** ingress filtering at ISP edge prevents source-spoofed reflection packets from leaving the network.
- **Stub resolver** runs on the client OS; **recursive resolver** is operated by ISP or public provider (e.g., Cloudflare `1.1.1.1`).
- **QNAME minimization** (RFC 9156) reduces privacy leakage by sending the *minimum* labels needed per step.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intrusion Detection and Honeypots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT633 - SECURE COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Intrusion Detection and Honeypots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🛡️ 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Intrusion Detection — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intrusion Detection** is the process of **monitoring, analyzing, and identifying** unauthorized or malicious activities (intrusions) occurring within a computer system or network, typically achieved by deploying specialized software/hardware appliances known as **Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS)**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, intrusion detection forms the **second line of defense** in the *Defense-in-Depth* security model — the first line being perimeter devices (firewalls) and the third line being post-incident recovery.

## 1.2 The "Burglar Alarm" Analogy 🔔

Imagine your house has three layers of security:

| Layer | Real-World Analogue | Cyber Equivalent |
|:------|:--------------------|:-----------------|
| Outer Fence | Walls / Locked doors | **Firewall** |
| Alarm System | Motion sensors / sirens | **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** |
| Police Response | On-call security patrol | **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)** |

An **IDS is like a burglar alarm** — it does *not* stop the intruder physically, but it **detects**, **logs**, and **raises an alert** the moment something suspicious occurs. A firewall decides *who gets in*, an IDS decides *what is happening once they are in*.

## 1.3 Honeypots — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> A **Honeypot** is a *decoy system* or *network resource* intentionally deployed to be probed, attacked, or compromised. Its primary purpose is to **collect intelligence** on attacker techniques, tools, and motivations without exposing production assets.

## 1.4 Honeypot Analogy 🍯

Think of a **honeypot as a fake jewelry store** placed next to a real one:

- **Lure** — Thieves are attracted to the visible, unprotected storefront.
- **Trap** — While thieves spend time cracking the fake safe, security cameras record every move.
- **Distraction** — Real assets remain untouched.
- **Intelligence** — Police learn *modus operandi*, *tools*, and *identities*.

In cyber-space, a honeypot is the **digital jewelry store**: a vulnerable-looking server that wastes attacker time, captures attack signatures, and diverts threats away from real systems.

## 1.5 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Meaning |
|:-----|:--------|
| **True Positive (TP)** | Intrusion correctly flagged as intrusion |
| **True Negative (TN)** | Legitimate activity correctly allowed |
| **False Positive (FP)** | Legitimate activity wrongly flagged — *false alarm* |
| **False Negative (FN)** | Intrusion wrongly allowed — *missed attack* |
| **Detection Rate (DR)** | $\text{DR} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ |
| **False Alarm Rate (FAR)** | $\text{FAR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Intrusion Detection Systems

### A. Based on **Data Source** (Where the IDS looks)

1. **Network-Based IDS (NIDS)**
   - Monitors **entire network segments** by sniffing packets on a SPAN/mirror port.
   - Example: **Snort**, **Suricata**, **Zeek (Bro)**.
   - Pros: Covers many hosts, single deployment point.
   - Cons: Encrypted traffic (TLS) blinds the sensor; high-speed links drop packets.

2. **Host-Based IDS (HIDS)**
   - Resides on an **individual host**, analyzing OS logs, syscalls, file integrity.
   - Example: **OSSEC**, **Tripwire**, **AIDE**.
   - Pros: Visibility into encrypted payloads (post-decryption), detects local privilege escalation.
   - Cons: Host overhead, must be deployed on every critical endpoint.

3. **Hybrid / Distributed IDS**
   - Combines NIDS + HIDS with a **central correlation engine** (SIEM).
   - Example: **Prelude SIEM**, **OSSIM (AlienVault)**.

### B. Based on **Detection Methodology**

| Method | Principle | Strength | Weakness |
|:-------|:----------|:---------|:---------|
| **Signature-Based (Misuse)** | Pattern-matches traffic against a database of known attack signatures (snort rules) | Very low FP, fast | Cannot detect **Zero-Day** attacks |
| **Anomaly-Based** | Builds a baseline of "normal" behavior; flags deviations | Detects unknown attacks | High FP during training, vulnerable to *slow poisoning* |
| **Stateful Protocol Analysis** | Tracks protocol state machines (e.g., TCP handshake legitimacy) | Catches malformed/malicious state transitions | Stateful, complex to maintain |
| **Specification-Based** | Uses manually defined "correct" behavior specs | Low FP, detects novel attacks | Hard to scale, manual spec creation |

## 2.2 The Base Rate Fallacy ⚠️

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU **favourite** concept. Even with a 99% accurate IDS, when intrusions are *rare* (say 1 in 10,000 events), the vast majority of alarms will be **false positives**. This is the **Base Rate Fallacy**.

$$
P(\text{Intrusion} \mid \text{Alert}) = \frac{P(\text{Alert} \mid \text{Intrusion}) \cdot P(\text{Intrusion})}{P(\text{Alert})}
$$

Bayes' theorem underpins IDS alert correlation and is **critical for KTU problems**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Meaning / Unit |
|:--------|:-----------------|:---------------|
| Detection Rate (Sensitivity / Recall) | $\text{DR} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Fraction of real attacks detected — *higher is better* |
| False Positive Rate | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | Fraction of normal traffic wrongly flagged |
| Precision | $\text{P} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Trustworthiness of an alert |
| Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Overall correctness |
| F1-Score | $\text{F1} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of Precision & Recall |
| ROC–AUC | Area under $TPR$ vs $FPR$ curve | Overall classifier quality — max value **1.0** |
| Bayes Posterior | $P(I \mid A) = \dfrac{P(A \mid I)\,P(I)}{P(A \mid I)\,P(I) + P(A \mid \bar I)\,P(\bar I)}$ | Probability the alert is genuine |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas use dimensionless ratios bounded in $[0,1]$. **Bayes' theorem** is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU board papers.

## 2.4 Architecture of a Typical IDS Pipeline

1. **Sensor / Tap** — Captures traffic (SPAN port, NIC in promiscuous mode, log forwarder).
2. **Preprocessor** — Decodes protocols, reassembles fragments, normalizes.
3. **Detection Engine** — Applies signature/anomaly rules.
4. **Alert Generation** — Produces structured events (e.g., Snort unified2, IDMEF).
5. **Logging & Correlation** — SIEM correlates events across sensors.
6. **Response (optional)** — Active response via IPS, TCP reset, quarantine.

## 2.5 Honeypot Classification

| Dimension | Type | Description |
|:----------|:-----|:------------|
| **Interaction Level** | **Low-Interaction** | Emulates only the *service surface* (e.g., ports that respond like Telnet but go nowhere). Safe, low information. Examples: **Honeyd**, **Cowrie (low-mode)**, **Kippo** |
| | **High-Interaction** | Real OS/services with no restrictions; riskier but rich intel. Examples: **Sebek**, **HoneyWall** (roo) |
| **Purpose** | **Production Honeypot** | Placed inside corporate network to *divert and detect* real attackers |
| | **Research Honeypot** | Used by academia/government to study malware, botnets (e.g., The Honeynet Project) |
| **Deployment** | **Honeynet** | A *network* of honeypots behind a *data control* gateway (HoneyWall) |
| | **Honeypot Farms** | Distributed honeypots coordinated globally |
| **Physicality** | **Physical Honeypot** | Real hardware with a real OS |
| | **Virtual Honeypot** | Emulated via VM/container — cheaper, scalable |

## 2.6 IDS vs IPS vs Honeypot — Core Distinction

| Feature | IDS | IPS | Honeypot |
|:--------|:---|:----|:---------|
| Action on detection | **Alert** only | **Block / Drop** | **Engage & Log** attacker |
| Inline placement | No (passive tap) | Yes (inline bridge) | Standalone decoy |
| Risk to production | None | Low (false drops) | None to prod (only decoy) |
| Purpose | Visibility | Prevention | Intelligence / Deception |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔍 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bayes' Theorem Applied to IDS Alerts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is **high-yield for KTU Part-B (14-mark) questions**. Practice until fluent.

**Given:**
- $P(I)$ = Prior probability of an intrusion (base rate) = $0.001$ (1 in 1000 packets)
- $P(\bar I) = 1 - P(I) = 0.999$
- $P(A \mid I)$ = Sensitivity = 0.99 (IDS catches 99% of intrusions)
- $P(A \mid \bar I)$ = False Positive Rate = 0.01 (1% of legitimate packets trigger alert)

**Find:** $P(I \mid A)$ — probability that an alert is a *real intrusion*.

### Step 1 — Write the standard Bayes' formula

$$
P(I \mid A) = \frac{P(A \mid I) \cdot P(I)}{P(A)}
$$

### Step 2 — Expand the denominator using the Law of Total Probability

$$
P(A) = P(A \mid I) \cdot P(I) + P(A \mid \bar I) \cdot P(\bar I)
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute numerical values

$$
P(A) = (0.99)(0.001) + (0.01)(0.999)
$$

$$
P(A) = 0.00099 + 0.00999
$$

$$
P(A) = 0.01098
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the numerator

$$
P(A \mid I) \cdot P(I) = (0.99)(0.001) = 0.00099
$$

### Step 5 — Final substitution

$$
P(I \mid A) = \frac{0.00099}{0.01098}
$$

$$
P(I \mid A) \approx 0.0902 \;\;\; \text{or} \;\;\; 9.02\%
$$

### Step 6 — Interpretation

> Even with a 99%-accurate IDS, when an alert fires, there is only a **~9% chance** it is a true intrusion. **90.98% are false positives.** This dramatic result is the **Base Rate Fallacy**, and it explains why SOC analysts suffer from *alert fatigue*.

### Extension — How Sensitivity Improves the Outcome

If $P(A \mid I)$ rises to $0.999$:

$$
P(A) = (0.999)(0.001) + (0.01)(0.999) = 0.000999 + 0.00999 = 0.010989
$$

$$
P(I \mid A) = \frac{0.000999}{0.010989} \approx 0.0909 \;\;\; (\text{Negligible improvement})
$$

### Better Fix — Lower the False Positive Rate

If we keep $P(A \mid I) = 0.99$ but reduce $P(A \mid \bar I)$ to $0.001$:

$$
P(A) = (0.99)(0.001) + (0.001)(0.999) = 0.00099 + 0.000999 = 0.001989
$$

$$
P(I \mid A) = \frac{0.00099}{0.001989} \approx 0.4978 \;\;\; (\approx 50\%)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Reducing the False Positive Rate is *far more impactful* than raising sensitivity — a key KTU insight.

## 3.2 Derivation: ROC Curve Construction Logic

Given detections across thresholds:

| Threshold $\tau$ | $TPR(\tau)$ | $FPR(\tau)$ |
|:----------------:|:-----------:|:-----------:|
| 0.1 | 0.99 | 0.90 |
| 0.3 | 0.92 | 0.40 |
| 0.5 | 0.85 | 0.15 |
| 0.7 | 0.70 | 0.05 |
| 0.9 | 0.40 | 0.01 |

The **AUC** is computed as the area under this staircase:

$$
\text{AUC} = \int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR^{-1}(x)) \, dx
$$

Discretely:

$$
\text{AUC} = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \frac{(FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i)(TPR_i + TPR_{i+1})}{2}
$$

Plugging values from the table:

$$
\text{AUC} \approx \frac{(0.40-0.90)(0.99+0.92)}{2} + \frac{(0.15-0.40)(0.92+0.85)}{2} + \ldots
$$

After full summation (left to student), we obtain $\text{AUC} \approx 0.91$, indicating a strong classifier.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Detection Methods

| Criterion | Signature | Anomaly | Stateful | Specification |
|:----------|:---------:|:-------:|:--------:|:-------------:|
| Zero-Day Detection | ❌ | ✅ | ⚠️ | ✅ |
| False Positives | Low | **High** | Medium | Low |
| Speed | Very Fast | Slow (training) | Medium | Medium |
| Resource Cost | Low | High | Medium | High |
| Maintenance | Rule updates | Baseline drift | Protocol updates | Manual specs |
| Example Tool | Snort VRT | Zeek ML | Suricata | Bro scripts |

## 3.4 Worked Example — Honeypot Risk Computation

A high-interaction honeypot is compromised with probability $P(C) = 0.05$ and pivots to production with probability $P(L \mid C) = 0.2$ given compromise.

$$
P(\text{Breach}) = P(L \mid C) \cdot P(C) = 0.2 \times 0.05 = 0.01
$$

If **data control** (e.g., HoneyWall) reduces $P(L \mid C)$ from $0.2$ to $0.02$:

$$
P(\text{Breach}_{\text{controlled}}) = 0.02 \times 0.05 = 0.001
$$

A **10× risk reduction** — justifying the cost of a HoneyWall in production honeypot deployments.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 🧩 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IDS Architecture — Functional Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Network Tap / SPAN Port] --> B[Packet Decoder]
    B --> C[Protocol Normalizer]
    C --> D{Detection Engine}
    D -- Signature Match --> E[Alert: Misuse]
    D -- Statistical Deviation --> F[Alert: Anomaly]
    D -- Protocol Violation --> G[Alert: Stateful]
    E --> H[Correlation / SIEM]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[SOC Dashboard]
    H --> J[Active Response via IPS]
```

## 4.2 Honeypot Deployment Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Internet((Internet / Attacker)) --> FW[Corporate Firewall]
    FW --> DMZ[DMZ Switch]
    DMZ --> WebServer[Production Web Server]
    DMZ --> Honeypot1[Low-Interaction Honeypot - Honeyd]
    DMZ --> Honeypot2[High-Interaction Honeypot VM]
    Honeypot2 --> HoneyWall[HoneyWall - Data Control Gateway]
    HoneyWall --> Logger[Centralized Logger]
    Logger --> SIEM[SIEM / SOC Analyst]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Snort Rule Flow (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Packet Arrives] --> Decode{Protocol Decode OK?}
    Decode -- No --> Drop[Drop & Log Decode Error]
    Decode -- Yes --> Match{Signature in Rule DB?}
    Match -- Yes --> Severity{Severity ≥ Threshold?}
    Match -- No --> AnomalyCheck{Anomaly Score > Baseline?}
    AnomalyCheck -- Yes --> Severity
    AnomalyCheck -- No --> Pass[Forward Silently]
    Severity -- Yes --> Alert[Generate Alert + Log]
    Severity -- No --> Watch[Watch / Tag]
```

## 4.4 IDS vs IPS vs Honeypot — Interaction Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Attacker((Attacker)) --> Perimeter[Firewall / Perimeter]
    Perimeter --> IDS[IDS - Passive Sensor]
    IDS -.Alert.-> SOC[SOC Team]
    Perimeter --> IPS[IPS - Inline]
    IPS -- Block --> Attacker
    Honeypot[Decoy Honeypot] <--> Attacker
    Honeypot --> Logger[Intelligence Logger]
```

```mermaid
    subgraph Internet_Zone

    end

        B1[Honeypot A - SSH]

    end

        HW[HoneyWall - Sebek + iptables]
        LG[Log Collector - Kippo / Cowrie]
    end
    A --> B1
    A --> B2
    A --> B3
    B1 --> HW
    B2 --> HW
    B3 --> HW
    HW --> LG
    LG --> Analyst((Analyst / SIEM))
```
