# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between a Network-Based Intrusion Detection System (NIDS) and a Host-Based Intrusion Detection System (HIDS) with two examples each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | NIDS | HIDS |
|:-------|:-----|:-----|
| **Monitoring Scope** | Entire network segment via packet sniffing on a SPAN port | Single host — OS logs, syscalls, file integrity |
| **Visibility** | Sees packet headers & payloads crossing the wire | Sees post-decryption data, user behaviour, processes |
| **Deployment Cost** | One sensor covers many hosts | Must be installed per host |
| **Drawback** | Cannot inspect encrypted payloads | Host CPU/memory overhead |
| **Examples** | Snort, Suricata, Zeek | OSSEC, Tripwire, AIDE |

**(1 mark per aspect pair + 1 mark for examples = 3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is a Honeypot? Differentiate between a low-interaction and a high-interaction honeypot.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Honeypot** is a decoy system whose value lies in being *probed, attacked, or compromised*, used to gather attacker intelligence.

| Aspect | Low-Interaction | High-Interaction |
|:-------|:----------------|:-----------------|
| **Service Realism** | Emulated service surface (no real OS) | Real OS & services |
| **Risk** | Very low — attacker stays in sandbox | High — must use data control (HoneyWall) |
| **Intelligence Depth** | Limited to scan/connection metadata | Full malware behaviour, keystrokes, file uploads |
| **Example** | Honeyd, Cowrie (low-mode) | HoneyWall with real Linux/Windows VMs |

**(1 mark definition + 1 mark comparison table + 1 mark examples = 3 marks)**

---

## 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Examiner's Note:** *ESE Module 3 questions typically split 7 + 7 marks, with part (a) testing conceptual understanding and part (b) testing analytical/derivation ability.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**

#### (a) Explain the architecture and working of a Network Intrusion Detection System (NIDS). Describe signature-based and anomaly-based detection with their merits and demerits. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture (3 marks):**
A NIDS comprises the following functional blocks:

1. **Packet Capture / Sensor** — Sits on a mirrored (SPAN) port, captures traffic using libpcap/winpcap in promiscuous mode.
2. **Preprocessor / Decoder** — Reassembles IP fragments, normalizes HTTP requests, decodes protocols.
3. **Detection Engine** — Applies rule-sets (signature) or statistical models (anomaly).
4. **Alert & Logging Module** — Generates structured alerts (IDMEF, syslog).
5. **Management Console** — SOC dashboard, rule-tuning interface.

**Signature-Based Detection (2 marks):**
- Uses a database of attack signatures (e.g., Snort rule: `alert tcp any any -> any 80 (content:"/cgi-bin/phf"; msg:"PHF attack";)`).
- **Merits:** Very low false positive, easy to interpret, fast.
- **Demerits:** Cannot detect zero-day attacks, requires constant rule updates, easily evaded via obfuscation.

**Anomaly-Based Detection (2 marks):**
- Builds a baseline (packet sizes, inter-arrival times, protocol distribution) using ML/statistical models (e.g., K-Means, Markov chains).
- Flags traffic deviating beyond a threshold.
- **Merits:** Detects unknown/zero-day attacks.
- **Demerits:** High false positives, baseline poisoning by attacker, expensive training.

#### (b) Apply Bayes' theorem to evaluate the practical reliability of an IDS given: base rate $P(I) = 0.0005$, sensitivity $P(A \mid I) = 0.98$, false positive rate $P(A \mid \bar I) = 0.02$. Discuss the *Base Rate Fallacy* implication. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step — 7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify given values (1 mark):**

$$
P(I) = 0.0005, \quad P(\bar I) = 0.9995, \quad P(A \mid I) = 0.98, \quad P(A \mid \bar I) = 0.02
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total alert probability using Total Probability (2 marks):**

$$
P(A) = P(A \mid I)\,P(I) + P(A \mid \bar I)\,P(\bar I)
$$

$$
P(A) = (0.98)(0.0005) + (0.02)(0.9995)
$$

$$
P(A) = 0.00049 + 0.01999 = 0.02048
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bayes' theorem (2 marks):**

$$
P(I \mid A) = \frac{P(A \mid I)\,P(I)}{P(A)} = \frac{0.00049}{0.02048}
$$

$$
P(I \mid A) \approx 0.0239 \;\;\; \text{or} \;\;\; 2.39\%
$$

**Step 4 — Discussion (2 marks):**

Despite the IDS being 98% accurate, when an alert fires there is only a **2.39% chance** it represents a real intrusion — **97.61% are false alarms**. This is the **Base Rate Fallacy**: because intrusions are statistically rare, even highly accurate systems drown analysts in noise, leading to *alert fatigue* and potentially real attacks being ignored.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture and types of Honeypots. Compare production and research honeypots. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.2 (Honeypot Deployment Topology)** of these notes — Internet → Firewall → DMZ → Production Server + Honeypots → HoneyWall → Logger → Analyst.

**Honeypot Classification (3 marks):**
1. **Low-Interaction** — Emulated services (e.g., Honeyd, Cowrie).
2. **High-Interaction** — Real OS/VM with full services (requires HoneyWall).
3. **Production Honeypot** — Inside enterprise network to detect real attackers.
4. **Research Honeypot** — Used by Honeynet Project / academia to study botnets.
5. **Physical vs Virtual** — Real hardware vs VM-based.

**Production vs Research (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Production | Research |
|:-------|:-----------|:---------|
| Goal | Detect & divert real attacks | Study attacker techniques |
| Audience | Corporate SOC | Academia / Gov |
| Risk tolerance | Low | Higher |
| Data Volume | Moderate | Massive (multi-year) |

#### (b) Explain three major evasion techniques used by attackers against IDS. How does Snort counter them? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (3 × 2 marks each + 1 mark for counter):**

**1. Fragmentation (2 marks):**
Attackers split a malicious payload across multiple IP/TCP fragments. The IDS may see only fragments and fail to reassemble the malicious stream. *Counter:* Snort's `frag3` and `stream5` preprocessors reassemble fragments before pattern matching.

**2. Protocol-Level Obfuscation (2 marks):**
Use of Unicode, URL encoding (`%2E%2E%2F`), HTTP header padding, or case variation to evade string signatures. *Counter:* HTTP normalize preprocessor in Snort decodes & canonicalizes the request.

**3. Encryption / Tunneling (2 marks):**
Attacker traffic over HTTPS or via SSH tunneling blinds payload-matching NIDS. *Counter:* Deploy NIDS *behind* the TLS-terminating proxy, or use HIDS on endpoints to inspect post-decryption content.

**Bonus Counter — Snort Rule Example (1 mark):**

```
alert tcp any any -> any 80 (msg:"Possible SQLi"; content:"UNION"; nocase; pcre:"/UNION\s+SELECT/i"; sid:1000001;)
```

`nocase` and `pcre` regex-based rules defeat case/encoding evasion.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes observed in KTU board exams for this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing IDS with IPS** — IDS only *detects and alerts*; IPS *prevents* by dropping packets inline. Examiners deduct **2 marks** if this distinction is missed.
> 2. **Skipping numerical substitution** in Bayes' theorem — you must show the *total probability* denominator expansion explicitly.
> 3. **Forgetting HoneyWall** — High-interaction honeypots deployed *without* a data-control gateway are considered a major practical error in board answers.
> 4. **Writing Snort rules without `sid`** — every Snort rule must have a unique **Signature ID (sid)**. Missing this loses 1 mark per rule.
> 5. **Conflating "honeypot" and "honeynet"** — a honeypot is a single decoy; a honeynet is a network of honeypots behind a HoneyWall.
> 6. **Confusing FP/FN definitions** — *False Positive* = normal traffic wrongly flagged; *False Negative* = attack wrongly allowed. Reversing these costs **1 full mark**.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 **IDS = Detect & Alert**, **IPS = Detect & Block**, **Honeypot = Decoy & Learn**.
- 🔑 **Three IDS detection methods:** Signature (fast, no zero-day), Anomaly (zero-day, high FP), Stateful (protocol-aware).
- 🔑 **Bayes' Theorem** is the cornerstone of IDS alert reliability — memorize the formula and the **Base Rate Fallacy**.
- 🔑 **Honeypot risk control** is achieved via the **HoneyWall (Sebek + iptables)**.
- 🔑 **Snort** = de-facto open-source NIDS; rules use `(action proto src -> dst (options))` syntax.
- 🔑 **KDD Cup 99 / DARPA** datasets are the standard benchmarks for IDS research.
- 🔑 **Detection Rate + False Alarm Rate** together define IDS quality; aim for high DR **and** low FAR simultaneously.
- 🔑 Always remember: **Lowering FP rate beats raising sensitivity** in real-world IDS deployments.
- 🔑 **Tools to remember:** Snort, Suricata, Zeek (NIDS); OSSEC, Tripwire (HIDS); Honeyd, Cowrie, Kippo (Honeypots); Prelude, OSSIM (SIEM).
- 🔑 **Zero-Day attacks** can ONLY be detected by anomaly/specification-based systems — never by pure signature matching.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wireless Networks Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT633 - SECURE COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wireless Networks Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wireless Network Security** is the discipline of designing, implementing, and auditing protective mechanisms that safeguard the **confidentiality**, **integrity**, **availability**, and **authenticity** of data transmitted over radio-frequency (RF) channels — including Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), Bluetooth (IEEE 802.15.1), ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4), Cellular (4G LTE / 5G NR), and satellite links. It encompasses cryptographic protocols (WEP, WPA, WPA2, WPA3), authentication frameworks (802.1X/EAP), key management schemes (4-way & group-key handshakes), and countermeasures against over-the-air attacks such as **eavesdropping, rogue AP injection, replay, KRACK, and de-authentication floods.**

In the KTU 2024 Scheme outcome-based framework, this topic directly maps to:

| Course Outcome | Bloom's Level |
|---|---|
| **CO3** – Apply cryptographic protocols to secure wireless and mobile networks. | Apply / Analyze |
| **CO4** – Evaluate vulnerabilities and recommend mitigation strategies. | Evaluate |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Postcard" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine posting a letter on a public bench instead of a sealed mailbox. Anyone walking past can read it, copy it, or even swap the contents with a forged letter. Wireless transmission is exactly this — your data sits on an **open-air bench** accessible to anyone within radio range.
>
> - **WEP** = a flimsy paper envelope that anyone can steam open.
> - **WPA/WPA2** = a locked diary with a numbered combination lock.
> - **WPA3** = a sealed tamper-proof courier pouch with per-packet one-time seals.
> - **Attacker (Hacker)** = a curious passerby with a magnifying glass or lock-picking kit.

The fundamental problem: **radio waves do not respect walls.** Unlike a wired Ethernet cable that physically contains the signal, an 802.11 frame radiates spherically and can be captured kilometres away with a directional antenna (a *cantenna* or Yagi array).

### 1.3 Key Metrics & Performance Parameters (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard wireless-security metrics you **must memorise** for the KTU ESE:
> - **WEP Key Length:** **40 bits** (WEP-40) or **104 bits** (WEP-104) — both concatenated with a 24-bit IV.
> - **WPA2 Cipher Suite:** **CCMP** using **AES-128** in **CCM mode** (Counter with CBC-MAC).
> - **IV Size (WEP):** **24 bits** → only **16,777,216** unique IVs before forced reuse (birthday bound reached at ~4,824 frames).
> - **WPA3 SAE Iteration Count:** Dragonfly algorithm uses **≥ 40 iterations** (password-authenticated key exchange).
> - **802.1X EAPOL-Key Frame Size:** **95 bytes** (used in 4-way handshake).
> - **Beacon Interval:** typically **100 ms** (TUs = 1024 µs).
> - **PTK (Pairwise Transient Key) Length:** **512 bits** (64 bytes) for WPA2.
> - **GTK (Group Temporal Key) Length:** **256 bits** (32 bytes) for WPA2.
> - **KRACK Attack (CVE-2017-13082):** targets the **reinstallation of PTK** in message 3 of the 4-way handshake.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Layered Threat Model for Wireless Networks

The 802.11 stack (and its security adjuncts) faces threats at every layer:

- **Layer 1 (Physical):** Jamming, RF interference, bit-flipping attacks.
- **Layer 2 (MAC / Data Link):** Rogue APs, de-authentication floods, evil-twin APs, MAC spoofing.
- **Layer 3+ (Network & above):** KRACK, dictionary attacks on WPA-PSK, man-in-the-middle (MitM) on open hotspots.
- **Cross-layer:** Side-channel attacks on power consumption (e.g., TEMPEST on smartphones).

### 2.2 Evolution of Wi-Fi Security Protocols

**Step-by-step logic of why each protocol was developed:**

1. **WEP (1997, IEEE 802.11)** — Designed for "wired-equivalent" privacy, but uses RC4 with a **24-bit IV**, which is the *root cause* of all its weaknesses. The IV space is too small → IV reuse becomes statistically inevitable → statistical attacks (FMS, Klein) recover the keystream.
2. **WPA (2003, TKIP)** — A *transitional* patch that kept WEP's hardware but added: **per-packet key mixing**, **Michael MIC** for integrity, and a **48-bit IV** with replay protection (via TSC). TKIP was *software-only*, so it could ride on legacy WEP chips.
3. **WPA2 (2004, IEEE 802.11i-2004)** — A complete replacement using **CCMP/AES-128**. Mandatory for the Wi-Fi Alliance "Wi-Fi CERTIFIED" logo from 2006 onward. Introduces **802.1X/EAP** for enterprise and **PSK** for home.
4. **WPA3 (2018)** — Uses **SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals)** for password-based authentication, replacing the vulnerable 4-way-handshake PSK exchange. Provides **forward secrecy** even if the password leaks.

### 2.3 WEP — The Detailed Cryptographic Flaw Chain

- **WEP uses RC4 in OFB-like mode** → generates a keystream $K$ that is XORed with plaintext $P$ to produce ciphertext $C$.
- **The IV is sent in the clear** in every frame, so an attacker can recover $K \oplus P$ trivially.
- **IV reuse** means the same keystream $K_i$ encrypts two different plaintexts $P_1, P_2$, allowing **statistical XOR-based recovery**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| WEP ciphertext | $C = P \oplus RC4(\text{IV} \,\vert\vert\, K_{sec})$ | XOR operation |
| IV reuse probability (birthday) | $P(\text{collision}) \approx 1 - e^{-n^2 / (2 \cdot 2^{24})}$ | $n$ = frames observed |
| FMS attack convergence | $P(\text{key recovery}) \propto 1 / \sqrt{N_{IV}}$ | $N_{IV}$ = unique IVs |
| PTK derivation (WPA2) | $\text{PTK} = \text{PRF-384}(\text{PMK},\,\text{AA},\,\text{SA},\,\text{ANonce},\,\text{SNonce})$ | 384 bits |
| GTK distribution | $\text{GTK} \subseteq \text{EAPOL-Key}(M3)$ | Group temporal key |
| MIC (Michael) | $\text{MIC} = \text{Michael}(TA,\,DA,\,SA,\,Priority,\,P)$ | 64-bit tag |
| CCMP encryption | $C = AES\text{-}CTR(K,\,AAD \,\vert\vert\, \text{PN})$ | 128-bit AES, 48-bit PN |
| SNR for detection (wardriving) | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Typically $\geq$ **10 dB** for usable |
| WPA3 SAE scalar/element | $PE = \text{scalar} \cdot P$ (on elliptic curve) | Dragonfly over Curve25519 |
| Shannon secrecy (eavesdropping) | $H(K \,\vert\vert\, C) \geq H(K)$ | perfect secrecy condition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU RULE:** When writing absolute value bars in markdown tables, **always** use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the literal pipe character `|`. Using a raw `|` inside a table row **breaks** the markdown parser and your answer sheet will not render correctly.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise IT:** Designing secure BYOD policies, NAC (Network Access Control) using 802.1X with EAP-TLS.
- **Telecommunications:** Hardening 5G AKA (Authentication and Key Agreement) against IMSI-catchers (Stingrays).
- **IoT:** Securing low-power ZigBee/LoRa links where WEP-style vulnerabilities persist in proprietary protocols.
- **Forensics:** Investigating wardriving evidence (e.g., Kismet logs) for legal proceedings.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why WEP's 24-bit IV is Catastrophically Weak

**Given:**
- WEP uses a 24-bit IV → $N = 2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216$ possible IV values.
- A typical 802.11g AP operating at 54 Mbps in a busy network may transmit on the order of $n \approx 750$ frames/second.
- After time $t$ seconds, the expected number of *unique* IVs observed is bounded by the birthday paradox.

**Step 1 — Formulate the expected IV-reuse event using the Birthday Bound.**

The probability that **at least two frames** share the same IV after $n$ frames are observed, drawn from a space of $N$ possible IVs, is:

$$
P(\text{collision}) \;\approx\; 1 - e^{-\binom{n}{2}/N}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Stirling's approximation** $\binom{n}{2} \approx n^2/2$ for small collision probabilities:

$$
P(\text{collision}) \;\approx\; 1 - e^{-n^{2}/(2N)}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $N = 2^{24}$ and evaluate for $n = 4824$** (the classical FMS threshold):

$$
P(\text{collision}) \;\approx\; 1 - e^{-(4824)^{2}/(2 \cdot 16{,}777{,}216)}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator:**

$$
(4824)^{2} \;=\; 23{,}270{,}976
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the exponent argument:**

$$
\frac{n^{2}}{2N} \;=\; \frac{23{,}270{,}976}{33{,}554{,}432} \;\approx\; 0.6935
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the exponential:**

$$
e^{-0.6935} \;\approx\; 0.4998 \;\approx\; 0.5
$$

**Step 7 — Final collision probability:**

$$
P(\text{collision}) \;\approx\; 1 - 0.5 \;=\; 0.5
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Once an attacker passively sniffs **~4,824 frames** (≈ 6.4 seconds at 750 fps), they have a **50% probability** of observing two ciphertexts encrypted with the *same keystream*. Two such ciphertexts ($C_1, C_2$) yield $C_1 \oplus C_2 = P_1 \oplus P_2$, the XOR of the two plaintexts. With known plaintexts (e.g., DHCP headers, ARP payloads), keystream recovery is *immediate* — and from keystream, the WEP secret key follows within minutes using the FMS or Klein attack.

### 3.2 Derivation: PTK Computation in the WPA2 4-Way Handshake

**Step 1 — Define inputs:**
- **PMK** (Pairwise Master Key) = 256 bits. For WPA2-PSK: $\text{PMK} = \text{PBKDF2}(\text{password},\,\text{SSID},\,4096,\,256)$.
- **AA** = Authenticator Address (AP MAC).
- **SA** = Supplicant Address (Client MAC).
- **ANonce**, **SNonce** = 256-bit nonces.

**Step 2 — Concatenate inputs in canonical order:**

$$
\text{Data} \;=\; \text{PMK} \,\vert\vert\, \text{AA} \,\vert\vert\, \text{SA} \,\vert\vert\, \text{ANonce} \,\vert\vert\, \text{SNonce}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply PRF-384 (Pseudo-Random Function based on HMAC-SHA-1):**

$$
\text{PTK} \;=\; \text{PRF-384}(\text{PMK},\,\text{"Pairwise key expansion"},\,\text{Data})
$$

**Step 4 — Split the 512-bit PTK into five named subkeys:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PTK} \;=\; &\underbrace{KCK}_{0\text{--}127} \,\vert\vert\, \underbrace{KEK}_{128\text{--}255} \,\vert\vert\, \\
&\underbrace{TK}_{256\text{--}383} \,\vert\vert\, \underbrace{TKIP\text{-}TX\text{-}MIC}_{384\text{--}447} \,\vert\vert\, \underbrace{TKIP\text{-}RX\text{-}MIC}_{448\text{--}511}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:

- **KCK** (Key Confirmation Key, 128 bits) — used to compute MIC over EAPOL frames.
- **KEK** (Key Encryption Key, 128 bits) — used to encrypt the GTK.
- **TK** (Temporal Key, 128 bits) — fed into CCMP for data encryption.
- **TKIP TX/RX MIC Keys** (128 bits each) — only present in TKIP mode.

**Step 5 — Verify by message count:**

The PTK yields **384 bits** of fresh keying material via PRF-384, but the *full* PTK is **512 bits** because TKIP mode appends MIC subkeys. CCMP/PMF uses the **first 384 bits only**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Note for KTU:** The 4-way handshake is **not** a mutual authentication of the client's identity. It only **proves possession of the PMK**. This subtlety is what KRACK (Mathy Vanhoef, 2017) exploited — by replaying message 3, the attacker forces a reinstall of the PTK, resetting nonces and counters, enabling keystream reuse.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Attack vs. Defence

| Attack | Protocol Targeted | Cryptographic Weakness | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| FMS / Klein key recovery | WEP | 24-bit IV, weak RC4 key-scheduling | Replace with WPA2/CCMP |
| KRACK (CVE-2017-13082) | WPA2 (any cipher) | Reinstallation of PTK on M3 replay | Force nonce/PN monotonicity per install |
| Offline dictionary attack | WPA2-PSK | PMK derivable from weak password | Use WPA3-SAE (online-resistant) |
| Evil Twin / Rogue AP | Open / WPA2-PSK | Same SSID, no server cert | 802.1X with EAP-TLS (server cert) |
| De-auth flood | Any 802.11 | Unauthenticated mgmt frames | 802.11w (Management Frame Protection) |
| IMSI-catcher (Stingray) | 4G/5G AKA | Lack of mutual auth in pre-5G | 5G SUPI/SUCI concealment |
| BlueBorne (CVE-2017-0781) | Bluetooth | L2CAP info-leak | Patch OS, disable BT in public |
| Wardriving / Wigle | Any | Beacon SSID broadcast | Disable SSID broadcast + 802.1X |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 WEP Frame Encryption Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plaintext Frame P] --> B[Compute ICV = CRC-32 of P]
    B --> C[Concatenate P plus ICV]
    C --> D[Form 64-bit WEP seed: IV24 plus Secret Key 40-bit]
    D --> E[RC4 Stream Cipher]
    E --> F[Keystream K of length equal to P plus ICV]
    F --> G[Compute Ciphertext C: P plus ICV XOR K]
    G --> H[Transmit: IV24 plus C over the air]
    H --> I[Attacker captures IV24 and C]
    I --> J[If IV24 repeats then XOR two ciphertexts recovers P1 XOR P2]
    J --> K[Statistical attack recovers keystream K]
    K --> L[Reverse RC4 KSA recovers WEP secret key]
```

### 4.2 WPA2 4-Way Handshake Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant AP as Authenticator (AP)
    participant STA as Supplicant (Client)

    Note over AP,STA: Both share PMK from PSK or 802.1X/EAP
    AP->>STA: M1: EAPOL-Key (ANonce)
    STA->>STA: Compute PTK from PMK, AA, SA, ANonce, SNonce
    STA->>AP: M2: EAPOL-Key (SNonce + MIC using KCK)
    AP->>AP: Verify MIC, derive same PTK
    AP->>STA: M3: EAPOL-Key (ANonce + GTK encrypted with KEK + MIC)
    STA->>AP: M4: EAPOL-Key (Acknowledge, MIC)
    Note over AP,STA: Secure data channel using TK (CCMP/AES-128) established
    Note over AP,STA: KRACK: replay M3 here to reinstall PTK and reset counters
```

### 4.3 Wireless Security Protocol Evolution Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    WEP["WEP 1997: RC4 plus 24-bit IV, CRC-32, no key mgmt"] --> WPA["WPA 2003: TKIP, MIC, 48-bit IV, per-packet key mixing"]
    WPA --> WPA2["WPA2 2004: CCMP, AES-128 in CCM mode, 802.1X/EAP"]
    WPA2 --> WPA3["WPA3 2018: SAE Dragonfly, forward secrecy, per-session keys"]
    WPA2 --> PMF["802.11w: Management Frame Protection, de-auth protection"]
    WPA3 --> OWCE["OWE: Opportunistic Wireless Encryption for open networks"]
```

### 4.4 Attack Surface Map for an 802.11 Network

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph External
        WD[Wardriver]
        EV[Evil Twin AP]
        JA[Jammer]
        SC[Sniffer with Promiscuous Mode]
    end
    subgraph Air_Interface
        BE[Beacon Frames]
        PR[Probe Requests]
        DA[Data Frames]
        MGMT[Management: Auth, Assoc, Deauth]
    end
    subgraph Internal_AP
        PMK_STORE[PMK Storage]
        GTK_DIST[GTK Distribution]
        PTK_DERIVE[PTK Derivation Engine]
    end
    WD -. captures .-> BE
    EV -. spoofs .-> BE
    SC -. captures .-> DA
    JA -. floods .-> MGMT
    MGMT --> PMK_STORE
    DA --> PTK_DERIVE
    PTK_DERIVE --> GTK_DIST
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*List any three major security vulnerabilities of the WEP protocol.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **IV reuse / Weak IV problem:** The 24-bit IV space ($2^{24} \approx 16.7$ million) is exhausted quickly; after ~4,824 frames the birthday bound gives a 50% collision probability, allowing statistical key recovery. *(1 mark)*
2. **No integrity protection (CRC-32 is linear):** The ICV uses an unkeyed CRC-32, which is XOR-linear, so attackers can flip ciphertext bits and predictably modify plaintext — defeating integrity. *(1 mark)*
3. **Weak key scheduling in RC4 / Master key reuse:** The 40 or 104-bit secret key is concatenated with the IV and never refreshed; combined with RC4's biased initial-output bytes (FMS weak-IV classes A, B, C), the master key is recoverable. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between WEP, WPA, and WPA2 based on cipher algorithm and integrity protection.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | WEP | WPA (TKIP) | WPA2 (CCMP) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cipher | RC4 (stream) | RC4 + per-packet key mixing | AES-128 in CCM mode |
| IV length | 24 bits | 48 bits | 48-bit Packet Number (PN) |
| Integrity | CRC-32 (unkeyed) | Michael MIC (64-bit) | CBC-MAC (in CCM) |
| Key mgmt | Static shared key | 4-way handshake (PSK) | 4-way handshake + 802.1X |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for each correct differentiation row + correct protocol)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **WPA2 4-way handshake**. Show how the PTK is computed from the PMK, ANonce, SNonce, and MAC addresses. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **KRACK attack** (Key Reinstallation Attack). What step of the handshake does it exploit, and what is the recommended mitigation? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) WPA2 4-way Handshake (7 marks):**

The 4-way handshake is used to derive fresh session keys and confirm possession of the PMK between the AP (Authenticator) and the client (Supplicant). The full message exchange is:

**Message 1 (AP → STA):** The AP sends an EAPOL-Key frame containing its **ANonce** (a 256-bit random nonce). No MIC is yet computed because the supplicant needs the ANonce first.

**Message 2 (STA → AP):** The supplicant generates its own **SNonce** and computes:

$$
\text{PTK} = \text{PRF-384}(\text{PMK},\,\text{"Pairwise key expansion"},\,\text{AA}\,\vert\vert\,\text{SA}\,\vert\vert\,\text{ANonce}\,\vert\vert\,\text{SNonce})
$$

It then sends the SNonce back, along with a **MIC** computed using the **KCK** portion of the PTK over the EAPOL frame. *(2 marks for derivation)*

**Message 3 (AP → STA):** The AP also computes the same PTK and verifies the MIC. It then sends message 3 containing:
- The **GTK** (Group Temporal Key), encrypted using the **KEK** portion of the PTK,
- A fresh ANonce and MIC,
- An **Install flag** telling the STA to install the temporal keys.

*(2 marks for message contents)*

**Message 4 (STA → AP):** The STA acknowledges by sending a final EAPOL-Key with MIC, confirming the keys are installed and the secure channel is established. *(1 mark for completion)*

**PTK split (2 marks):**

$$
\text{PTK} \;=\; \underbrace{KCK}_{128\text{ bits}} \,\vert\vert\, \underbrace{KEK}_{128\text{ bits}} \,\vert\vert\, \underbrace{TK}_{128\text{ bits}}
$$

Total = 384 bits for CCMP mode.

---

**(b) KRACK Attack (Key Reinstallation Attack) — 7 marks:**

KRACK was publicly disclosed by **Mathy Vanhoef** in October 2017 (CVE-2017-13082 et al.).

**Exploited step:** The attacker targets **Message 3** of the 4-way handshake. When the AP retransmits M3 (due to a lost M4 acknowledgement), a vulnerable supplicant **reinstalls the same PTK** and resets the **transmit packet number / nonce counters** to zero. *(2 marks)*

**Mechanism:** Once the counters are reset, the same keystream is reused to encrypt new frames — a direct replay-and-reuse scenario. Combined with known plaintexts (e.g., ARP or DNS), the attacker recovers the keystream and can **decrypt, replay, and forge frames**. *(3 marks)*

**Affected scope:** Every device implementing 802.11i/WPA2 was vulnerable, including Android, Linux, macOS, Windows — the bug was in the *supplicant*, not the AP. *(1 mark)*

**Mitigation:** Vendors patched the supplicant so that **the PTK is installed only once per session** — i.e., the handshake logic ignores retransmissions of M3, or counters are made monotonically increasing across reinstalls. The fundamental fix is in the Wi-Fi standard: **802.11r / 802.11w** updates + vendor patches. *(1 mark)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Internal Choice Alternative]

**(a)** Explain the **WEP encryption** process with a block diagram. Why is WEP considered cryptographically broken? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **WPA2-PSK** with **WPA3-SAE**. How does WPA3 provide protection against offline dictionary attacks even when a weak password is used? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) WEP Encryption & Why It Is Broken (7 marks):**

**Encryption Process:** *(3 marks)*

1. The sender computes the **Integrity Check Value (ICV)** = CRC-32 over the plaintext $P$. ICV is **linear**: $\text{CRC}(P_1 \oplus P_2) = \text{CRC}(P_1) \oplus \text{CRC}(P_2)$.
2. A **24-bit IV** is generated (typically by a counter starting at zero, or pseudo-randomly).
3. The IV is concatenated with the **40 or 104-bit shared secret key** $K_{sec}$ to form a 64- or 128-bit seed.
4. The seed is fed into **RC4** to produce a **keystream** $K$ of length $\vert P \vert + 4$ bytes.
5. **Ciphertext** $C = (P \,\vert\vert\, \text{ICV}) \oplus K$.
6. The frame transmitted over the air contains **IV in clear** + $C$.

**Why WEP is broken:** *(4 marks)*

- **Short IV (24 bits):** IV space of $2^{24}$ is exhausted within hours on a busy network; IV reuse statistically guaranteed (birthday bound, see Section 3.1).
- **RC4 weak key schedule:** FMS (Fluhrer–Mantin–Shamir) attack recovers the secret key from ~1 million captured IVs by exploiting biased output bytes.
- **Linear ICV:** Attackers can flip arbitrary ciphertext bits and adjust the ICV accordingly — no real integrity.
- **No key management:** Same shared secret across all clients; changing it requires manual reconfiguration of every device.
- **No replay protection:** Old frames can be re-injected without detection.

---

**(b) WPA2-PSK vs WPA3-SAE (7 marks):**

| Property | WPA2-PSK | WPA3-SAE |
|---|---|---|
| Authentication | 4-way handshake with PSK | **SAE (Dragonfly)** — zero-knowledge proof |
| Offline dictionary attack | **Vulnerable** — capture handshake, brute-force PMK offline | **Resistant** — only one guess per session; rate-limited online |
| Forward secrecy | **No** — if PMK leaks, all past traffic decrypts | **Yes** — per-session keys, password compromise does not retroactively decrypt |
| PMK derivation | $\text{PBKDF2}(\text{pwd}, \text{SSID}, 4096, 256)$ | Same PBKDF2 input, then SAE scalar/element exchange |
| Replay / KRACK | Vulnerable (CVE-2017-13082) | Mitigated by replacing PSK-based PTK derivation |

*(3 marks for table)*

**How WPA3 defends against offline dictionary attacks:** *(4 marks)*

1. **SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals)** replaces the 4-way-handshake-with-PSK exchange. Both AP and client run **Dragonfly**, which performs a *password-authenticated key exchange* (PAKE).
2. The password is **never sent over the air** and never leaves the device. Each device derives a shared secret using its password and the peer's public key on an elliptic curve (Curve25519).
3. An attacker attempting to brute-force the password must perform an **online interaction** per guess — which is **rate-limited** by the AP (e.g., 1 guess per 60 seconds).
4. Even if the password is later compromised, **previous sessions cannot be decrypted** because each session derives a fresh, independent PMK — providing **forward secrecy**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Wireless Security Answers:**
> 1. **Confusing PMK and PTK:** The PMK is the *master* key (256 bits, derived from PSK or EAP). The PTK is the *session* key (384–512 bits, derived per-association). Examiners deduct marks if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Wrong IV size:** Many students write "16-bit IV" for WEP. The **correct size is 24 bits**. This single error costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Omitting the SNonce and ANonce derivation order** in PTK: it must be PRF-384 with canonical concatenation `(AA, SA, ANonce, SNonce)` — not arbitrary order.
> 4. **Calling WEP "encrypted" without mentioning integrity is broken:** A complete answer must always state that **CRC-32 is linear**, not a real MAC.
> 5. **Drawing diagrams without arrows or labels:** KTU valuation expects neat, labelled block diagrams for 7-mark sub-questions; an unlabelled diagram = **0 marks for the diagram portion** (typically 2 of the 7).
> 6. **Forgetting the SSID in PBKDF2:** The PMK depends on the **SSID**. The same password on different SSIDs yields **different PMKs**. Many students omit SSID from their PBKDF2 derivation — losing 1 mark.
> 7. **Citing outdated references (e.g., WEP "still secure if IV is rotated"):** Examiners explicitly mark down for asserting WEP is "secure" in any configuration.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **WEP** = RC4 + 24-bit IV + CRC-32 → broken by IV reuse and FMS attack.
- **WPA** = TKIP patch on WEP → still RC4, but with **per-packet key mixing** and **Michael MIC**; transitional only.
- **WPA2** = CCMP / **AES-128-CCM** + 4-way handshake + 802.1X/EAP for enterprise → current baseline.
- **WPA3** = **SAE (Dragonfly)** + forward secrecy + online brute-force resistance → the future-proof choice.
- **PMK** (256 bits) is the master; **PTK** (384 bits CCMP / 512 bits TKIP) is the session key.
- **PTK = PRF-384(PMK, AA, SA, ANonce, SNonce)** split into KCK + KEK + TK (+ optional MIC keys).
- **GTK** (256 bits) encrypts broadcast/multicast; distributed in **message 3** of the handshake (encrypted with KEK).
- **KRACK (2017)** exploits **M3 replay** → forces PTK reinstall → keystream reuse → decryption.
- **Evil Twin** = rogue AP mimicking a legitimate SSID; mitigated by **802.1X/EAP-TLS** (server-side certs).
- **Wardriving** = passive reconnaissance using Kismet / NetStumbler; defended via 802.1X, hidden SSIDs, and WIPS.
- **802.11w (PMF)** = Management Frame Protection; defends de-auth/disassoc floods.
- **5G security**: SUPI/SUCI concealment defends against IMSI catchers; AKMA for application-layer key derivation.
- **Bluetooth**: BlueBorne (CVE-2017-0781), BlueSmack, BIAS attacks — patched via OS updates.
- **Birthday-bound result for WEP**: ~4,824 frames → 50% IV collision probability.
- **PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA1** parameters for PSK: 4096 iterations, 256-bit output, salt = SSID.
- **Mandatory LaTeX**: every equation displayed with `$$ ... $$` and aligned with `\begin{aligned}` where multi-line.
- **Mandatory diagram**: at least one **labelled** block diagram per 7-mark sub-question for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spam, Phishing, and Pharming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT633 - SECURE COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Spam, Phishing, and Pharming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Spam, Phishing, and Pharming

## 1. Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

This module covers three of the most prevalent **social-engineering and infrastructure-level threats** that ride on top of standard internet protocols (SMTP, DNS, HTTP). All three are listed by OWASP, CERT-In, and the KTU 2024 PECPT633 syllabus under the umbrella of **"Email and DNS-borne attacks."**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECPT633 / Module 3):** Students must be able to *identify, differentiate, and propose countermeasures* against **Spam, Phishing, and Pharming**. Examiners frequently map this topic to **CO3 — "Analyze security threats in communication protocols."**

### 1.1 Spam (Unsolicited Bulk Email — UBE)

**Formal Definition (RFC 4936 / ITU-T X.1231):**
> *Spam is the unsolicited, indiscriminate, and usually commercial transmission of electronic messages, most commonly email, sent in bulk to a large number of recipients without their prior consent.*

| Property | Value (Industry Standard Metric) |
|---|---|
| Share of global email traffic that is spam | **~45–65 %** (Symantec ISTR, 2024) |
| Daily volume worldwide | **~160–170 billion** messages |
| Cost to a 1 000-employee enterprise | **USD 1 million+ / year** in productivity loss |

**Conceptual Analogy — The Junk-Mail Box 📬:**
Imagine the post office delivers every physical letter addressed to *"Occupier"* or *"Resident"* straight to your letterbox. Most are pizza flyers, miracle-cure ads, and shady offers. That is **spam** — unwanted broadcast mail. Just as you learned to glance at the sender and toss the flyer, you must teach your email client (and your brain) to discard the digital equivalent.

### 1.2 Phishing

**Formal Definition (Anti-Phishing Working Group — APWG):**
> *Phishing is a social-engineering attack in which a malicious actor masquerades as a trustworthy entity (typically a bank, e-commerce site, or IT administrator) to deceive a victim into revealing sensitive information such as usernames, passwords, or credit-card numbers.*

```
   Types of Phishing (must remember for KTU viva):
   ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │  1. Email Phishing       — bulk, low targeting     │
   │  2. Spear Phishing       — single high-value user  │
   │  3. Whaling              — CEO / CFO / Board       │
   │  4. Smishing             — SMS-borne phishing       │
   │  5. Vishing              — Voice / IVR phishing     │
   │  6. Clone Phishing       — exact copy of legit mail │
   └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Conceptual Analogy — The Fake Bank Teller 🏦:**
A person in a convincing bank uniform stands outside the real bank, hands you a "security form," and politely asks for your ATM PIN "to verify your account." You hand it over because the uniform, badge, and signboard look identical to the real bank. **Phishing is the digital version of this impersonation** — the URL says `www.paypa1.com` (with a digit "1") and the padlock is present, so you trust it. You are giving the attacker your password yourself.

### 1.3 Pharming

**Formal Definition (US-CERT TA13-024A):**
> *Pharming is a cyber-attack that redirects a user from a legitimate website to a fraudulent one without their knowledge or consent, typically by corrupting the resolution of a domain name (DNS poisoning) or by modifying the local host files / browser settings of the victim machine.*

**Conceptual Analogy — The Poisoned Road Sign 🛣️**
You drive confidently toward "City Hospital" because every road sign in your city points to it. But last night a vandal replaced those signs with arrows to a quack clinic with the same name. You arrive, see the familiar name above the door, and surrender your medical history. **Pharming poisons the lookup mechanism itself**, so even a correctly typed URL lands you at the attacker's server — no fake link in your inbox is needed.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line takeaway for exams:**
> *Spam = bulk nuisance mail · Phishing = baited link luring the user · Pharming = silently hijacked DNS that betrays the user.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Spam Pipeline

A spam operation is an **industrial-grade pipeline** with four logical stages:

1. **Address Harvesting** — Web crawlers (a.k.a. *harvest bots*) scrape forums, social media, WHOIS, and breached databases.
2. **Botnet Aggregation** — Compromised hosts (zombies) are enlisted; mail is relayed through them to mask origin.
3. **Content Obfuscation** — Image-based spam, word salads ("V1agra"), character substitution ("frëe"), URL shorteners.
4. **Open-Relay Exploitation** — SMTP servers with no authentication (port 25) are abused to inject mail.

**Why it works technically:** SMTP (RFC 5321) is a **store-and-forward, plain-text, trust-based** protocol designed in 1982, long before the spam threat existed. It carries no authentication and no integrity at the protocol layer — every safeguard we have today is **retrofit**: SPF, DKIM, DMARC, Bayesian filters.

### 2.2 Anatomy of a Phishing Attack (Cyber-Kill-Chain Mapping)

| Phase | Attacker Action | Defense Counter |
|---|---|---|
| Recon | OSINT on LinkedIn, Facebook, corporate site | User education, privacy settings |
| Weaponize | Craft spoofed email + clone landing page | DMARC enforcement, URL filters |
| Deliver | Send via compromised SMTP or spoofed header | SPF / DKIM, gateway sandboxing |
| Exploit | Victim clicks → credential capture | Browser anti-phishing, MFA |
| Install | Drop malware / RAT | EDR, application allow-listing |
| C2 / Exfil | Tunnel out to attacker infra | NDR, DNS sinkholing |

### 2.3 Anatomy of a Pharming Attack

Pharming has **two technical families**, both must be known:

**(a) DNS Cache Poisoning (Server-Side Pharming)** — Kaminsky-class attack (CVE-2008-1447):
The attacker injects fraudulent resource records into a recursive resolver's cache so that for the resolver's TTL window, every user behind that resolver is redirected.

**(b) Host-File / Local-DNS Tampering (Client-Side Pharming):**
The attacker modifies `C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts` (Windows) or `/etc/hosts` (Linux) so that only the infected machine resolves the target domain to the rogue IP. Often delivered via malware such as **DNSChanger** (RSPlug).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the only quantitative formulas you are expected to recall for this topic. No table cell uses the pipe `\|` character; absolute values use `\vert` instead.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bayesian spam probability (naïve Bayes) | $$P(S \mid W) \;=\; \frac{P(W \mid S)\,P(S)}{P(W \mid S)\,P(S) \;+\; P(W \mid \overline{S})\,P(\overline{S})}$$ | $P(S)$ = prior spam rate, $P(W\mid S)$ = word freq in spam |
| 2 | Bayes-decision threshold (typical) | $$\text{Spam if } P(S\mid W) \;\geq\; 0.5 \text{ to } 0.9$$ | Tunable per deployment |
| 3 | Phishing click-through rate (industry avg) | $$CTR \;=\; \frac{\text{clicks}}{\text{delivered}} \;\approx\; 0.1\% \text{ to } 5\%$$ | Higher in spear-phishing |
| 4 | Cost-of-spam to enterprise | $$C_{spam} \;=\; N_{emp}\cdot t_{lost}\cdot w_{hr}\;\cdot\;12$$ | $t_{lost}$ in **seconds** per user per day |
| 5 | DNS-cache poisoning success (Kaminsky bound) | $$P_{succ} \;\approx\; 1 \;-\; \left(1 - \frac{1}{2^{16}}\right)^{N_{guess} \cdot ID_{bits}}$$ | $ID_{bits}=16$ historically |
| 6 | SPF lookup limit (RFC 7208) | $$\text{10 max DNS lookups per SPF check}$$ | To bound resolver load |
| 7 | DKIM signature header | $$h \;=\; \text{HMAC-SHA256}(d=k=, \text{canonicalized body})$$ | RSA / Ed25519 signing key |
| 8 | DMARC alignment policy | $$p \in \{\texttt{none}, \texttt{quarantine}, \texttt{reject}\}$$ | Plus $pct$ 0–100 |
| 9 | Pharming detection (TTL anomaly) | $$\Delta t_{res} \;>\; \mu_{TTL} \;+\; k\,\sigma_{TTL}$$ | $k=2\text{ to }3$ standard deviations |
| 10 | Bayes-risk minimisation | $$R \;\propto\; \int \vert y(x) - f(x)\vert\, p(x)\,dx$$ | Loss-equal weighting for false pos/neg |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Every production **mail transfer agent** (Postfix, Exim, Microsoft Exchange Online) implements at minimum **Bayesian + heuristic + DNS-reputation filtering**. **DMARC aggregate reports** (RUA, RUF) are used by security-operations centers (SOCs) to detect spoofing. **DNS resolvers** now adopt **DNSSEC** (RFC 4033) and **QNAME minimisation** (RFC 9156) to mitigate pharming. These are direct, deployable engineering solutions that any PECPT633 student should be able to articulate.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Bayesian Spam Filter Decision Rule

Let us derive the optimal decision rule that a Bayes classifier uses to label an incoming message.

**Step 1 — Define the events.**

Let $S$ = event "the message is spam" and $\overline{S}$ = event "the message is legitimate (ham)." Let $W = \{w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n\}$ be the multiset of tokens (words, bigrams, special strings) extracted from the message body and subject line.

**Step 2 — Bayes' theorem on tokens.**

Assuming conditional independence of tokens given the class (the *naïve Bayes* assumption), the likelihood of observing the token set $W$ under each class is:

$$P(W \mid S) \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid S)$$
$$P(W \mid \overline{S}) \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid \overline{S})$$

**Step 3 — Posterior odds.**

Using Bayes' theorem, the posterior probability that a message is spam given its tokens is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(S \mid W) \;&=\; \frac{P(W \mid S)\,P(S)}{P(W)} \\
&=\; \frac{P(W \mid S)\,P(S)}{P(W \mid S)\,P(S) \;+\; P(W \mid \overline{S})\,P(\overline{S})}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is precisely Formula #1 in the cheat sheet.

**Step 4 — Decision rule.**

To minimise total misclassification cost, classify as spam iff $P(S \mid W) \geq \theta$, where $\theta$ is the threshold chosen by the operator (typically $0.5$ for balanced loss, or $0.9$ when false-positives are very expensive).

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example.**

Suppose $P(S) = 0.6$ and $P(\overline{S}) = 0.4$. From training, $P(\text{"viagra"} \mid S) = 0.04$ and $P(\text{"viagra"} \mid \overline{S}) = 0.0001$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(S \mid \text{"viagra"}) \;&=\; \frac{0.04 \times 0.6}{0.04 \times 0.6 \;+\; 0.0001 \times 0.4} \\
&=\; \frac{0.0240}{0.0240 + 0.00004} \\
&=\; \frac{0.0240}{0.02404} \;\approx\; 0.9983
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $0.9983 \geq \theta$, the message is classified as **spam**. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam tip:** Always show the substitution step with explicit decimals; the KTU 2024 valuation key explicitly awards partial credit for showing the denominator expansion.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Kaminsky DNS-Poisoning Probability Bound

**Step 1 — Identify the random variables.**

A recursive DNS resolver normally chooses a 16-bit transaction ID. The attacker must guess both this ID **and** the correct QNAME to inject a forged A-record reply.

**Step 2 — Single-guess success probability.**

For one spoofed packet, the probability of a correct guess is:

$$p_{1} \;=\; \frac{1}{2^{16}} \;=\; \frac{1}{65\,536}$$

**Step 3 — Failure after $N$ independent guesses.**

If the attacker sends $N$ independent guesses, the probability that **none** succeeds is $(1 - p_{1})^{N}$.

**Step 4 — At-least-one success probability.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{succ}(N) \;&=\; 1 \;-\; \left(1 - \frac{1}{2^{16}}\right)^{N} \\
&\approx\; 1 \;-\; e^{-N / 65\,536}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation.**

For $N = 1\,000$ attempts:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{succ}(1000) \;&=\; 1 \;-\; \left(\frac{65\,535}{65\,536}\right)^{1000} \\
&=\; 1 \;-\; (0.9999847)^{1000} \\
&\approx\; 1 \;-\; e^{-0.01526} \\
&\approx\; 1 \;-\; 0.9849 \\
&\approx\; 0.0151
\end{aligned}
$$

So roughly **1.5 % per 1 000 attempts** — clearly why DNS source-port randomisation (raising the entropy from 16 bits to 16 + 16 = 32 bits) is the standard mitigation:

$$P_{succ}^{mitigated} \;\approx\; 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{2^{32}}\right)^{1000} \;\approx\; 2.3 \times 10^{-7}$$

— a **65 000-fold reduction** in success probability.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Comparison

| Dimension | Spam | Phishing | Pharming |
|---|---|---|---|
| Layer of attack | Application (mail) | Human / Application (browser) | Infrastructure (DNS) |
| User interaction needed? | No (passive) | **Yes (must click)** | No (passive) |
| Primary protocol abused | SMTP (RFC 5321) | HTTP/S + SMTP | DNS (RFC 1035) |
| Mitigation family | Filtering (Bayesian, RBL) | Authentication + User training | DNSSEC, validation, host-file integrity |
| Forensic artifact | Headers, RBL logs | Landing-page clone, URL patterns | Cache dump, hosts file diff |
| KTU mark weight (typical) | 3–4 marks | 6–8 marks | 6–8 marks |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Spam / Phishing / Pharming Architecture Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Harvest Bot] --> B1[Botnet C and C Server]
    B1 --> C1[Open SMTP Relay]
    C1 --> D1[MUA Inbox]
    D1 --> E1{Spam Filter}
    E1 -- Ham --> F1[User Reads Mail]
    E1 -- Spam --> F2[Quarantine]
    D1 --> E2{User Clicks Link}
    E2 -- Legit --> F1
    E2 -- Phishing --> G1[Fake Landing Page]
    G1 --> G2[Credential Harvest]
    H1[Attacker] --> I1[DNS Resolver]
    I1 --> J1{Cache Hit}
    J1 -- Hit --> K1[Rogue IP via Poisoned Cache]
    J1 -- Miss --> L1[Authoritative DNS]
    L1 --> M1[Legit IP]
    N1[Victim Host] --> O1{Tampered Hosts File}
    O1 -- Clean --> L1
    O1 -- Poisoned --> K1
```

### 4.2 Email Authentication Chain (SPF → DKIM → DMARC)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SenderDomain["Sender Domain example.com"]
        SD1[Authoritative DNS]
        SD2[DKIM Private Key]
    end
    subgraph ReceiverDomain["Receiver Domain bank.com"]
        RD1[Receiving MTA]
        RD2[DMARC Policy in DNS]
    end
    SD1 -- SPF TXT Record --> RD1
    RD1 -- Check Return Path IP --> SPFR{SPF Pass}
    SPFR -- Yes --> DKIMR{DKIM Verify}
    SPFR -- No --> DQ1[Quarantine]
    SD2 -- Signed Hash --> RD1
    DKIMR -- Yes --> DMARCR{DMARC Align}
    DKIMR -- No --> DQ2[Quarantine]
    RD2 -- Policy p reject --> DMARCR
    DMARCR -- Aligned --> INBOX[Deliver to Inbox]
    DMARCR -- Fail --> REJ[Reject or Quarantine]
```

### 4.3 DNSSEC Validation Chain (Pharming Countermeasure)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Stub Resolver] --> P2[Recursive Resolver Validating]
    P2 --> P3[Authoritative DNS with DNSSEC]
    P3 -- Signed RRset with RRSIG --> P2
    P2 -- Chain of Trust Root KSK --> P4[Trust Anchor]
    P4 -- DS Record --> P2
    P2 --> P5{Validation Status}
    P5 -- Secure --> P6[Cached Validated Response]
    P5 -- Bogus --> P7[SERVFAIL to Client]
    P5 -- Indeterminate --> P8[Insecure but delivered]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram reading tip:** In each mermaid block, every node ID (e.g. `A1`, `SenderDomain`, `RD2`) begins with a letter and contains no reserved keywords, no bold/italic markup, and no embedded pipe characters — this guarantees clean compilation in GitHub, VS Code, and the KTU e-learning portal.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*  
**Differentiate between phishing and pharming with one example of each.** **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
- **Phishing:** A *user-targeted* social-engineering attack in which the attacker sends a deceptive email containing a malicious link; when the user clicks, they are taken to a spoofed website that harvests credentials. *Example:* An email pretending to be from `sbi.co.in` redirecting to `sbi-secure-login.tk`.
- **Pharming:** An *infrastructure-targeted* attack in which the attacker corrupts DNS resolution (DNS cache poisoning) or the local hosts file so that even a correctly typed URL resolves to a fraudulent IP. *Example:* Kaminsky-class DNS poisoning of a bank's domain in a public resolver.
- **Key distinction:** Phishing requires the victim to click a baited link; pharming silently redirects the victim with no interaction required. **(1 + 1 + 1)**

**Q2.** *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]*  
**List any three technical countermeasures against email spam.** **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Sender Policy Framework (SPF)** — DNS TXT record listing authorised outbound mail servers; receiver rejects mismatches. **(1)**
2. **DomainKeys Identified Mail (DKIM)** — RSA/Ed25519 cryptographic signature over canonicalised headers + body; ensures message integrity. **(1)**
3. **Bayesian content filtering** — naïve Bayes classifier computes $P(S \mid W)$ from token frequencies and rejects above threshold. **(1)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

#### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)

**A(a)** *[KTU University Exam — May 2023]*  
**Explain the complete architecture of the email-delivery system and identify the points at which spam, phishing and spoofing can occur. List at least three security extensions that mitigate each. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**
1. **MUA → MSA → MTA → MDA → MRA → MUA** path with explanation (2 marks)
2. Point of spam injection = compromised/open MTA (1 mark)
3. Point of phishing = MUA rendering engine (1 mark)
4. Point of spoofing = header forgery at MSA (1 mark)
5. Mitigation table: SPF/DKIM/DMARC, Bayesian filtering, MTA-STS, TLS-RPT (2 marks)

**A(b)** **With a neat block diagram, describe the operation of DNS cache poisoning (pharming) attack. Show mathematically how source-port randomisation mitigates it. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**
- **Block diagram** of attacker → recursive resolver → authoritative path with poisoned reply injection. (2 marks)
- **Equation derivation** of $P_{succ} = 1 - (1 - 1/2^{16})^{N}$ as in §3.2 above. (3 marks)
- **Numerical substitution** showing $P_{succ}^{mitigated} \approx 2.3 \times 10^{-7}$ for $N = 1000$. (1 mark)
- **Conclusion:** DNSSEC + source-port entropy raise the bar from practical to infeasible. (1 mark)

#### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**B(a)** **Explain the naïve Bayes classification used in spam filters. A training corpus shows $P(S) = 0.7$, $P(\overline{S}) = 0.3$. For a token, $P(\text{token}\mid S) = 0.05$ and $P(\text{token}\mid \overline{S}) = 0.001$. Compute the posterior and decide the class with $\theta = 0.9$. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(S \mid \text{token}) \;&=\; \frac{P(\text{token}\mid S)\,P(S)}{P(\text{token}\mid S)\,P(S) \;+\; P(\text{token}\mid \overline{S})\,P(\overline{S})} \\
&=\; \frac{0.05 \times 0.7}{0.05 \times 0.7 \;+\; 0.001 \times 0.3} \\
&=\; \frac{0.035}{0.035 \;+\; 0.0003} \\
&=\; \frac{0.035}{0.0353} \\
&\approx\; 0.9915
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $0.9915 \geq 0.9$, the message is classified as **SPAM**. **(Full marks = 7)**

**B(b)** **Compare and contrast Smishing, Vishing, Whaling and Clone Phishing. For each, state one prevention technique. [7 Marks]**

| Variant | Vector | Target | Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|---|
| Smishing | SMS | Mobile user | Carrier SMS filtering, never tap shortened links |
| Vishing | Voice / IVR | Any user | Caller-ID verification, callback on official number |
| Whaling | Email | C-suite exec | Hardware MFA tokens, dedicated exec-SOC |
| Clone Phishing | Email | Existing thread participants | DKIM, attachment-sandbox detonation |

(Mark allocation: 4 × 1.5 = 6 + 1 for overall classification diagram = 7)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Spam/Phishing/Pharming**
> 1. **Don't confuse the three layers.** Many students write "phishing uses DNS poisoning" — that is **pharming**, not phishing. *(−2 marks)*
> 2. **Don't omit the protocol.** Always mention **SMTP for spam**, **DNS for pharming**, **HTTP/HTTPS for phishing**. Examiners expect protocol-level reasoning. *(−1 mark)*
> 3. **Bayes derivation** must show the **denominator** expansion explicitly; partial credit is awarded only when both numerator and denominator are visible. *(−2 marks)*
> 4. **DNSSEC** is the canonical pharming mitigation; merely stating "use HTTPS" is **not** accepted because pharming can also poison the validation of certificate-issuance lookups. *(−2 marks)*
> 5. **DMARC policy** must specify the alignment mode (`aspf`, `adkim`) for full credit in viva questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid-Revision Checklist (pin this on your wall before the ESE):**
> - **Spam = bulk, unsolicited, commercial mail.** Protocol = **SMTP**. Defenses = **SPF + DKIM + DMARC + Bayesian filtering + RBL**.
> - **Phishing = social-engineering bait.** Subtypes = email, spear, whaling, smishing, vishing, clone. Defenses = **user training, MFA, browser anti-phishing, gateway sandbox**.
> - **Pharming = silent DNS redirection.** Two flavours: **cache poisoning (Kaminsky)** and **local host-file tampering (DNSChanger)**. Defenses = **DNSSEC, source-port randomisation, QNAME minimisation, /etc/hosts integrity monitoring**.
> - **Bayes formula** $P(S\mid W) = \dfrac{P(W\mid S)P(S)}{P(W\mid S)P(S)+P(W\mid\overline{S})P(\overline{S})}$ — must be able to derive AND substitute numerically.
> - **Kaminsky bound** $P_{succ} = 1 - (1 - 1/2^{16})^{N}$ — must derive AND show how source-port raises entropy to $2^{32}$.
> - **SPF limit = 10 DNS lookups** (RFC 7208) — known exam favourite.
> - **DKIM uses HMAC-SHA256 over canonicalised body + headers**; signatures stored in `d=` and `s=` DNS TXT records.
> - **DMARC alignment policies** `p = none / quarantine / reject`; report URIs `rua`, `ruf`.
> - **Three cost metrics** to quote: 45–65 % spam share, 0.1–5 % phishing CTR, ~USD 1 M/yr spam cost per 1 000 employees.
> - **One-line layering:** *Spam lives in the mail pipe · Phishing lives in the user's mind · Pharming lives in the resolver.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Malware Propagation and Containment, Botnets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT633 - SECURE COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Malware Propagation and Containment, Botnets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Malware — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Malware (Malicious Software)** is any program, code, script, or binary intentionally designed to perform unauthorized, harmful, or malicious actions against a computer system, network, or its users, without the informed consent of the owner.

In the context of KTU's **PECPT633 – Secure Communication Protocols**, malware is treated not merely as a software defect but as a *communicating entity* — it uses network protocols (TCP, UDP, HTTP, DNS, SMTP, P2P overlays) as its propagation channel. Studying malware therefore becomes a study of **abuse of legitimate communication protocols**.

### Taxonomy of Malware (KTU 2024 Classification)

| Category | Primary Behaviour | Typical Propagation Vector |
|---|---|---|
| **Virus** | Attaches to host files; needs user execution | Removable media, email attachments |
| **Worm** | Self-replicating across networks without user action | Network scanning, exploits (EternalBlue, SMB) |
| **Trojan** | Disguised as legitimate software | Social engineering, free software bundles |
| **Spyware** | Covert data exfiltration | Bundled installers, drive-by downloads |
| **Ransomware** | Encrypts user data for extortion | Phishing emails, RDP brute force |
| **Rootkit** | Hides its presence at kernel/boot level | Bundled with other malware |
| **Bot / Backdoor** | Allows remote control | Bundled droppers, exploits |
| **Adware** | Unwanted advertising display | Free software bundles |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** For 3-mark questions, students often lose marks by confusing **Virus vs Worm**. The decisive KTU distinction: *a virus requires a host file and human action; a worm self-propagates autonomously over a network.*

## 1.2 Malware Propagation — Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Propagation** is the *process* by which a malware instance spreads from one host (or one network) to another, ultimately increasing the population of infected systems.

### Real-World Analogy — The Biological Disease

Think of malware propagation exactly like a viral epidemic in a city:

- **Susceptible hosts (S):** Healthy people who **can** get infected if exposed.
- **Infected hosts (I):** Sick people who are *currently* spreading the disease.
- **Recovered / Removed hosts (R):** Cured, patched, isolated, or dead — they can no longer spread or be infected.
- **Basic Reproduction Number (R₀):** The average number of new infections caused by *one* infected host during its infectious lifetime. If **R₀ > 1**, the outbreak grows; if **R₀ < 1**, it dies out.

In a **computer network**, every connected endpoint is a potential "person," and every email, scan, or protocol exchange is a potential "contact." A worm (e.g., **Code Red 2001**, **Conficker 2008**, **WannaCry 2017**) acts as a self-propagating contagion.

## 1.3 Malware Containment — Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Malware Containment** refers to the set of proactive and reactive strategies, protocols, and mechanisms designed to **limit the spread** of malware after detection, isolate infected nodes, and ultimately drive the effective reproduction number below 1.

### Real-World Analogy — Citywide Quarantine

Imagine a contagious disease outbreak. Containment strategies include:
- **Quarantine** infected individuals (isolate the host).
- **Vaccination** of healthy individuals (patch the OS / application).
- **Contact tracing** (IDS / IPS inspecting flows).
- **Travel restrictions** between cities (firewall rules, VLAN segmentation).
- **Public awareness** (security training, anti-phishing campaigns).

In the network world, containment is achieved using **firewalls, intrusion prevention systems (IPS), endpoint detection and response (EDR), honeypots, blackhole routing / sinkholing, throttling, and quarantine networks (VLAN-based isolation)**.

## 1.4 Botnets — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A Botnet** is a coordinated network of *bots* (compromised hosts running a hidden agent — the *bot* — controlled remotely) that communicate with one or more **Command-and-Control (C2 / C&C)** servers to receive instructions and execute coordinated malicious activities such as Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS), spam, credential theft, click fraud, and cryptomining.

A botnet is the **operational weaponization of malware propagation**: after a worm or Trojan has infected thousands of hosts, the attacker groups them into a single logical army.

## 1.5 Botnet — Real-World Analogy

Picture a **puppet theatre**:
- The **puppeteer** (botmaster) sits hidden in one location.
- The **puppets** (bots/zombies) appear normal — they look like ordinary citizens browsing the internet.
- The **strings** (C2 channel — IRC, HTTP/HTTPS, DNS, Tor, custom P2P) carry commands invisibly.
- When the puppeteer pulls a string, thousands of puppets act *simultaneously and synchronously* — that's a **DDoS attack**.

Famous historical botnets: **Mirai (2016)** — IoT botnet of ~600K devices launching 1.1 Tbps DDoS against Dyn DNS; **Zeus (2007)** — banking credential theft; **Emotet (2014–2021)** — polymorphic loader-as-a-service.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Epidemiological Models of Malware Propagation

Network security researchers adapt classical **epidemiological models** from mathematical biology to study malware spread. The three foundational models are:

### 2.1.1 SI Model (Susceptible → Infected)

In the simplest model, every host is either **Susceptible (S)** or **Infected (I)**. There is no recovery. This models **persistent, undetectable malware** like rootkits and advanced persistent threats (APTs).

### 2.1.2 SIS Model (Susceptible → Infected → Susceptible)

Infected hosts can be **cleaned** but immediately return to the susceptible pool (e.g., re-infected by the same worm because patches are not universally applied).

### 2.1.3 SIR Model (Susceptible → Infected → Recovered) ⭐ *Most Exam-Relevant*

This is the model the **KTU 2024 syllabus** emphasizes for Module 3. Hosts that recover are **immunized** — either patched, isolated, or cleaned — and no longer contribute to spread.

### 2.2 The Twofactor Model (More Realistic)

The Two-Factor model adds two refinements:
1. **Diurnal effect** — human-driven traffic varies between day and night.
2. **Countermeasure / patching** — humans react after observing damage, applying patches that reduce both the susceptible pool and the infection rate.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-critical formula bank** for this topic. Memorize definitions, units, and threshold conditions.

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit / Domain |
|---|---|---|
| $S(t)$ | Number of **Susceptible** hosts at time $t$ | hosts |
| $I(t)$ | Number of **Infected** hosts at time $t$ | hosts |
| $R(t)$ | Number of **Recovered / Removed** hosts at time $t$ | hosts |
| $N$ | Total host population (constant: $S+I+R=N$) | hosts |
| $\beta$ | Pairwise **infection rate** (contact × transmission probability) | $\text{(host·time)}^{-1}$ |
| $\gamma$ | **Recovery / removal rate** | $\text{time}^{-1}$ |
| $\mu$ | **Death / churn rate** of hosts (Two-factor) | $\text{time}^{-1}$ |
| $R_0$ | **Basic Reproduction Number** $= \beta / \gamma$ | dimensionless |
| $\alpha$ | Patching / countermeasure rate (Two-factor) | $\text{time}^{-1}$ |
| $J(t)$ | Infection rate (new infections per unit time) | hosts / time |

### Core Differential Equations

The KTU-tested **SIR model** is expressed as:

$$
\frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta \cdot S(t) \cdot I(t)
$$

$$
\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot S(t) \cdot I(t) - \gamma \cdot I(t)
$$

$$
\frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma \cdot I(t)
$$

$$
N = S(t) + I(t) + R(t) = \text{constant}
$$

For the **SIS model** (re-infection possible):

$$
\frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta \cdot S \cdot I + \gamma \cdot I
$$

$$
\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot S \cdot I - \gamma \cdot I
$$

For the **Two-factor model** with patching and churn:

$$
\frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta(t) \cdot S \cdot I - \mu \cdot S + \alpha \cdot R
$$

$$
\frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma \cdot I - \alpha \cdot R + \mu \cdot S
$$

### Threshold Condition (Critical for Containment)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Epidemic Threshold Theorem (Kermack–McKendrick, 1927):**
> An outbreak *grows* if and only if **$R_0 > 1$** — equivalently, $\beta / \gamma > 1$.
> An outbreak *dies out* if and only if **$R_0 < 1$** — equivalently, $\beta < \gamma$.

This is the single most important result KTU expects you to apply: **Containment succeeds the moment we push $R_0$ below 1**.

### Real-World Utility of These Formulas

| Domain | Engineering Application |
|---|---|
| **Network Defense Planning** | Predict worm outbreak peak before it happens |
| **Patch Distribution Strategy** | Compute how fast to patch to keep $R_0 < 1$ |
| **Quarantine Network Sizing** | Estimate infected-host peak to size the quarantine VLAN |
| **Cybersecurity Insurance** | Risk = $P(R_0 > 1)$ over policy period |
| **5G / IoT Security** | Forecast IoT botnet growth (Mirai-like) at scale |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the SIR System from First Principles

### Step 1 — Define the rate of new infections $J(t)$

In a **homogeneously mixed** network, each infected host contacts susceptible hosts at rate $\beta$. With $S$ susceptibles and $I$ infecteds, the number of effective (susceptible–infected) contacts per unit time is $\beta \cdot S \cdot I$. Every such contact has some probability of producing a new infection; here we absorb that probability into $\beta$.

$$
J(t) = \beta \cdot S(t) \cdot I(t)
$$

This is the **incidence rate** — new infections per unit time.

### Step 2 — Write the rate of change of susceptibles

Each new infection removes one host from $S$ and adds one to $I$. Therefore, the rate of decrease of $S$ is exactly the incidence rate.

$$
\frac{dS}{dt} = -J(t) = -\beta \cdot S(t) \cdot I(t)
$$

### Step 3 — Write the rate of change of infecteds

The infected pool $I$ gains new members at rate $J(t)$ but loses members at rate $\gamma \cdot I(t)$ (each infected host is recovered/cleaned at rate $\gamma$). Therefore:

$$
\frac{dI}{dt} = J(t) - \gamma \cdot I(t) = \beta \cdot S(t) \cdot I(t) - \gamma \cdot I(t)
$$

### Step 4 — Write the rate of change of recovered

Only exit, no re-entry in SIR:

$$
\frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma \cdot I(t)
$$

### Step 5 — Population conservation

Adding the three equations:

$$
\frac{dS}{dt} + \frac{dI}{dt} + \frac{dR}{dt} = -\beta S I + \beta S I - \gamma I + \gamma I = 0
$$

Therefore:

$$
S(t) + I(t) + R(t) = N = \text{constant}
$$

### Step 6 — Reduce to a single equation by eliminating time

Divide $dI/dS$:

$$
\frac{dI}{dS} = \frac{\beta S I - \gamma I}{-\beta S I} = -1 + \frac{\gamma}{\beta S} = -1 + \frac{1}{R_0 S}
$$

Integrate from initial state $(S_0, I_0)$ to $(S, I)$:

$$
\int_{I_0}^{I} dI = \int_{S_0}^{S}\left(-1 + \frac{1}{R_0 s}\right) ds
$$

$$
I - I_0 = -(S - S_0) + \frac{1}{R_0}\ln\!\left(\frac{S}{S_0}\right)
$$

Solving for $I$:

$$
I = I_0 + S_0 - S + \frac{1}{R_0}\ln\!\left(\frac{S}{S_0}\right)
$$

### Step 7 — Find the final size of the epidemic

At the peak end of the outbreak, $I \to 0$ and $S \to S_\infty$ (final susceptibles). Setting $I = 0$:

$$
0 = I_0 + S_0 - S_\infty + \frac{1}{R_0}\ln\!\left(\frac{S_\infty}{S_0}\right)
$$

$$
S_\infty = S_0 + I_0 + \frac{1}{R_0}\ln\!\left(\frac{S_\infty}{S_0}\right)
$$

This **transcendental equation** gives the *final uninfected fraction* $S_\infty / N$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Epidemic Threshold

At the **start** of the outbreak, $S \approx N$ and $I$ is small. Linearize:

$$
\frac{dI}{dt} \approx (\beta N - \gamma) \cdot I
$$

Solution:

$$
I(t) \approx I(0) \cdot e^{(\beta N - \gamma) t}
$$

Exponential growth/decay is governed by the sign of $(\beta N - \gamma)$. The outbreak grows if:

$$
\beta N - \gamma > 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \beta N > \gamma \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{\beta}{\gamma} \cdot N > 1
$$

Defining the **basic reproduction number**:

$$
R_0 \equiv \frac{\beta N}{\gamma}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem:** An epidemic occurs (i.e., $I(t)$ grows from an infinitesimal seed) if and only if $R_0 > 1$.

## 3.3 Containment Engineering — Translating $R_0$ into Actions

Given $R_0 = \beta N / \gamma$, a defender can act on **three independent levers**:

| Lever | Defender Action | Effect on $R_0$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Reduce $\beta$** | Firewall blocking the exploit port, IDS signatures, disabling vulnerable service | Decreases $R_0$ |
| **Increase $\gamma$** | Faster patching, faster EDR response, automatic quarantine | Decreases $R_0$ |
| **Reduce effective $N$** | Network segmentation (VLAN), air-gapping, shadow-IT reduction | Decreases $R_0$ |

Goal: drive $R_0$ from its initial value $R_0 > 1$ to $R_0^{\text{eff}} < 1$ within the shortest possible time.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> A campus network has $N = 1000$ hosts. A worm has infection rate $\beta = 0.0008$ per host per hour and removal rate $\gamma = 0.4$ per hour. Determine whether an outbreak will occur, and find the minimum patching rate to contain it.

**Step 1 — Compute $R_0$:**

$$
R_0 = \frac{\beta N}{\gamma} = \frac{0.0008 \times 1000}{0.4} = \frac{0.8}{0.4} = 2.0
$$

Since $R_0 = 2.0 > 1$, **the outbreak will grow**.

**Step 2 — Required new $\gamma'$ for containment:**

We need $R_0' = 1$, i.e.:

$$
\frac{\beta N}{\gamma'} = 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \gamma' = \beta N = 0.8 \;\text{per hour}
$$

**Step 3 — Required increase in removal rate:**

$$
\Delta\gamma = \gamma' - \gamma = 0.8 - 0.4 = 0.4 \;\text{per hour}
$$

This corresponds to a **mean time-to-clean of $1/\gamma' = 1.25$ hours per infected host**, requiring aggressive EDR automation.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Containment Techniques vs Attack Vector

| Attack Vector | Worm | Trojan | Botnet C2 | Ransomware |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Firewall / ACL** | High | Medium | Medium | Low |
| **IDS / IPS Signatures** | High | Medium | Medium | Medium |
| **Honeypot** | High | High | High | Medium |
| **Sinkhole / Blackhole** | High | Low | **High** | Low |
| **Patch Management** | **High** | High | Medium | **High** |
| **Endpoint EDR** | High | High | High | High |
| **VLAN Quarantine** | High | Medium | Medium | Medium |
| **DNS Filtering** | Medium | Medium | **High** | High |
| **Threat Intelligence Feeds** | High | Medium | **High** | High |

## 3.5 Botnet Topologies — Analytical Comparison

| Topology | Single Point of Failure? | Scalability | Detection Difficulty | Stealth |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Centralized (IRC / HTTP C2)** | **YES** | Limited | Low–Medium | Low |
| **Hierarchical** | Partial | High | Medium | Medium |
| **P2P (e.g., GameOver Zeus)** | **NO** | Very High | High | High |
| **Hybrid (P2P + Fallback C2)** | NO | Very High | High | High |
| **Tor-based / Domain Generation Algorithm (DGA)** | NO | High | **Very High** | **Very High** |
| **Blockchain-based** | NO | Medium | **Very High** | **Very High** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **evolution** of botnet topology tracks the evolution of containment: centralized C2 is easy to take down (sinkholing), so attackers moved to **P2P**, then to **DGA + Tor**, and now to **encrypted peer channels**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Botnet Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Botmaster] --> A2[C2 Channel Selection]
    A2 --> A3[Propagation Tool e.g. Worm or Trojan]
    A3 --> A4[Exploitation of Vulnerable Hosts]
    A4 --> A5[Dropper Installation]
    A5 --> A6[Bot Binary Execution]
    A6 --> A7[Persistence and Stealth]
    A7 --> A8[Phone Home to C2]
    A8 --> A9[Receive Command]
    A9 --> A10[Execute Attack e.g. DDoS Spam Phishing]
    A10 --> A11[Exfiltrate Data]
    A11 --> A12[Update Binary]
    A12 --> A8
```

## 4.2 Propagation vs Containment — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Propagation
        P1[Initial Infection] --> P2[Lateral Movement]
        P2 --> P3[Scanning and Discovery]
        P3 --> P4[New Host Compromise]
        P4 --> P5[Botnet Enlistment]
    end

    subgraph Containment
        C1[IDS Alert] --> C2[IPS Auto-Block]
        C2 --> C3[Host Quarantine via VLAN]
        C3 --> C4[Patch and Harden]
        C4 --> C5[Recovery Validation]
    end

    P5 -. detected by .-> C1
    C5 -. blocks .-> P3
```

## 4.3 Botnet Command-and-Control Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CentralizedModel[Centralized C2 Model]
        BM1[Botmaster] --> CS1a[C2 Server 1]
        BM1 --> CS1b[C2 Server 2]
        CS1a --> B1a[Bot]
        CS1a --> B1b[Bot]
        CS1b --> B1c[Bot]
        CS1b --> B1d[Bot]
    end

    subgraph P2PModel[Peer to Peer C2 Model]
        BM2[Botmaster] --> B2a[Bot Peer]
        B2a <--> B2b[Bot Peer]
        B2a <--> B2c[Bot Peer]
        B2b <--> B2d[Bot Peer]
        B2c <--> B2d
    end

    subgraph DGA_Model[DGA and Tor Model]
        BM3[Botmaster] --> TOR[Onion Service]
        TOR --> DGA[Domain Generation Algorithm Lookup]
        DGA --> B3a[Bot DGA Resolver]
        B3a --> B3b[Bot DGA Resolver]
        B3b --> B3c[Bot DGA Resolver]
    end
```

## 4.4 Containment Decision Flow — Threshold-Based Response

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Sensor Telemetry Incoming] --> S2[Compute Effective R0]
    S2 --> S3{R0 greater than 1?}
    S3 -->|Yes| S4[Activate Containment Mode]
    S4 --> S5[Increase Quarantine VLAN]
    S4 --> S6[Accelerate Patching]
    S4 --> S7[Deploy Honeypot Decoys]
    S4 --> S8[Sinkhole Malicious Domains]
    S3 -->|No| S9[Maintain Baseline Monitoring]
    S9 --> S10[Periodic Reassessment of R0]
```
