If policy threshold $T_{threshold} = 0.7$, access is **denied**; otherwise granted with elevated monitoring.

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation demonstrates how Zero Trust quantitatively merges *identity*, *device*, *behavior*, and *context* — replacing static "inside-the-firewall = trusted" reasoning with continuous, risk-based decisions.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: PQC vs QKD vs Classical Comparison

| **Property** | **Classical (RSA/ECC)** | **PQC (Lattice/Hash-based)** | **QKD** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Security Basis | Number theory hardness | Lattice/Code/Hash hardness | Physics (no-cloning theorem) |
| Quantum Attack | Broken by Shor | Resistant (assumed) | Immune |
| Key Distribution | Asymmetric math | Asymmetric math | Requires quantum channel |
| Hardware Needed | Conventional CPUs | Conventional CPUs | Single-photon detectors |
| Range Limit | None | None | **~100 km fiber, ~1000 km satellite** |
| Throughput | High | Moderate | Low–Moderate |
| Maturity | Production | **NIST Standards 2024** | Niche deployments |
| Use Case | Internet, TLS | Migration target | Government, finance backbones |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Next Generation Secure System Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    user[User Device] -->|SUCI| ran[RAN gNodeB]
    ran -->|N1/N2| amf[AMF Core NF]
    amf -->|Service Bus| ausf[AUSF UDM]
    amf -->|SBI| smf[SMF]
    smf -->|UPF| upf[UPF Data Plane]
    sdn[SDN Controller] -->|OpenFlow| switches[Programmable Switches]
    nfv[NFV MANO] -->|Orchestration| vnf[VNF Instances]
    zt[Zero Trust PDP PEP] -->|Policy| pep[Enforcement Point]
    pqc[PQC Crypto Module] -->|TLS 1.3| tls[Tunnel]
    qkd[QKD Link] -->|Symmetric Key| tls
    iot[IoT Endpoints] -->|Lightweight Crypto ASCON| edge[Edge Gateway]
    edge -->|Secure Aggregation| cloud[Cloud Backend]
```

---

## 4.2 Zero Trust Architecture Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    req[Access Request] --> pep[Policy Enforcement Point]
    pep --> pe[Policy Engine]
    pe --> idp[Identity Provider Check]
    pe --> dha[Device Health Attestation]
    pe --> ueba[UEBA Behavioral Analytics]
    pe --> ctx[Context Location Time]
    idp --> score[Compute Trust Score]
    dha --> score
    ueba --> score
    ctx --> score
    score -->|Score greater than Threshold| allow[Allow Session]
    score -->|Score below Threshold| deny[Deny or Step Up Auth]
    allow --> monitor[Continuous Monitoring]
    deny --> log[Audit Log]
    monitor -->|Anomaly Detected| revoke[Revoke Session]
```

---

## 4.3 Post-Quantum Cryptography Migration Roadmap

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inventory[Crypto Inventory] --> classify[Classify Assets RSA ECC TLS]
    classify --> priority[Prioritize Crown Jewels]
    priority --> hybrid[Hybrid Deploy RSA plus Kyber]
    hybrid --> test[Test Interoperability]
    test --> rollout[Phased Rollout]
    rollout --> monitor[Monitor and Rotate Keys]
    monitor --> pqconly[Pure PQC Migration]
    pqconly --> retire[Retire Classical Algorithms]
```

---

## 4.4 BB84 Quantum Key Distribution Protocol Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    alice[Alice prepares qubits in random basis] --> chan[Quantum Channel]
    chan --> bob[Bob measures in random basis]
    bob --> sift[Sifting over public channel]
    sift --> est[Parameter Estimation QBER]
    est -->|QBER less than 11 percent| corr[Error Correction]
    est -->|QBER greater than 11 percent| abort[Abort Session]
    corr --> amp[Privacy Amplification]
    amp --> final[Final Secret Key]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA). List its three core logical components as per NIST SP 800-207.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Zero Trust Architecture is a security model where no implicit trust is granted to any entity — user, device, or network — regardless of its physical or network location. Trust is continuously evaluated based on identity, device posture, and context.

**Core logical components (NIST SP 800-207):**
1. **Policy Engine (PE)** — Makes access decisions using enterprise policy and external signals.
2. **Policy Administrator (PA)** — Executes the PE's decisions by issuing commands to PEPs.
3. **Policy Enforcement Point (PEP)** — Mediates access between subject and resource, enabling/monitoring/terminating connections.

**(1 mark each: definition + 3 components = full marks.)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC)? Name two NIST-standardized PQC algorithms.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Post-Quantum Cryptography refers to classical (non-quantum-computer) cryptographic algorithms designed to resist attacks by both classical and quantum adversaries, particularly against **Shor's algorithm** which breaks RSA, ECC, and DH in polynomial time on a sufficiently powerful quantum computer.

**NIST-standardized PQC algorithms (2024):**
1. **CRYSTALS-Kyber** — Lattice-based Key Encapsulation Mechanism (KEM).
2. **CRYSTALS-Dilithium** — Lattice-based digital signature scheme.
3. **SPHINCS+** — Hash-based stateless signature scheme.
*(Any two acceptable — 1 mark each. Definition = 1 mark.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the 5G Service-Based Architecture (SBA) and its security challenges. Discuss the role of SUCI/SUPI in subscriber privacy.**

**Model Answer:**

**5G Service-Based Architecture Overview (3 Marks):**
The 5G core (5GC) replaces 4G's point-to-point EPC with a **cloud-native, microservice-based SBA**. Every Network Function (NF) — such as **AMF, SMF, UPF, AUSF, UDM, NRF, PCF** — is exposed as a service over a **Service-Based Interface (SBI)** using HTTP/2 + JSON + TLS 1.3. The **Network Repository Function (NRF)** maintains a service registry for discovery. The **Service Communication Proxy (SCP)** enables indirect communication and load balancing across NFs.

**Security Challenges (2 Marks):**
- **Expanded attack surface** due to HTTP/JSON APIs replacing diameter-based signaling.
- **Centralized SCP** becomes a high-value target — DoS or compromise breaks the entire core.
- **NRF spoofing** can redirect NF-to-NF traffic.
- **Container/microservice threats** (image poisoning, sidecar injection).
- **Multi-tenant slicing** leakage across network slices.

**SUCI/SUPI Subscriber Privacy (2 Marks):**
- **SUPI (Subscription Permanent Identifier)** is the 5G equivalent of IMSI — a globally unique subscriber ID.
- **SUCI (Subscription Concealed Identifier)** is the SUPI encrypted with the home network's public key, computed on the **USIM**.
- Prevents **IMSI-catcher attacks** prevalent in 2G/3G/4G (e.g., Stingray devices).
- Only the **UDM/UDR** at the home network can decrypt SUCI → SUPI mapping.
- Combined with **5G-AKA** mutual authentication, it forms the **AKA-based primary authentication** procedure.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe the three SDN architecture layers and discuss their individual security challenges.**

**Model Answer:**

**Three SDN Layers (3 Marks):**
1. **Application Layer** — Business apps, orchestration, analytics (e.g., load balancers, firewalls).
2. **Control Layer** — SDN Controller (OpenDaylight, ONOS, Floodlight) — central intelligence.
3. **Infrastructure Layer** — OpenFlow switches — dumb forwarding devices.

Communication interfaces: **Northbound API** (App ↔ Controller), **Southbound API** (Controller ↔ Switch, e.g., OpenFlow 1.5).

**Security Challenges by Layer (4 Marks):**

| **Layer** | **Specific Threats** |
|---|---|
| **Application** | Malicious northbound apps; compromised analytics plugins injecting false flow rules |
| **Control Layer** | Controller hijacking, DoS on controller, single point of failure, flow-rule injection via compromised apps |
| **Infrastructure** | Switch firmware tampering, TCAM exhaustion, side-channel timing on packet processing |
| **Southbound API** | TLS stripping on OpenFlow channel, replay attacks, man-in-the-middle |
| **East-West (inter-controller)** | Trust federation issues in multi-domain SDN |

**Mitigations (briefly):** Controller replication, role-based authorization for apps, **flow-rule conflict detection** (e.g., FlowGuard, VEREFOO), southbound TLS, and **stateful attestation** of switches.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain IoT security challenges across perception, network, and application layers. Discuss lightweight cryptography as a mitigation.**

**Model Answer:**

**IoT Three-Layer Architecture (3 Marks):**
1. **Perception Layer** — Sensors, RFID tags, actuators (physical data collection).
2. **Network Layer** — Gateways, wireless protocols (Zigbee, LoRa, NB-IoT, 5G).
3. **Application Layer** — Cloud analytics, end-user services.

**Layer-Wise Security Challenges (3 Marks):**

| **Layer** | **Threats** |
|---|---|
| Perception | Node tampering, physical capture, side-channel on sensors, RF jamming, malicious code injection |
| Network | Eavesdropping, MITM, Sybil attacks, sinkhole, wormhole, DoS on constrained channels |
| Application | Insecure APIs, weak cloud authentication, data exfiltration, firmware update hijacking |

**Lightweight Cryptography Mitigation (1 Mark):**
NIST Lightweight Cryptography (LWC) standard **ASCON (2023)** provides AEAD (authenticated encryption with associated data) and hashing optimized for constrained devices:
- **Energy:** **~5.7 µJ/block** (vs ~22 µJ for AES on similar hardware).
- **RAM footprint:** **< 64 bytes** state.
- Suitable for **8/16-bit MCUs** common in IoT.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss Post-Quantum Cryptography migration challenges. Compare lattice-based and hash-based PQC approaches.**

**Model Answer:**

**PQC Migration Challenges (3 Marks):**
1. **Inventory & Discovery** — Many organizations don't know where RSA/ECC are deployed (firmware, embedded, legacy).
2. **Performance & Size** — PQC signatures are larger: Dilithium ~2.4 KB vs RSA-2048 ~256 bytes; Kyber public keys ~800 bytes vs ECC ~32 bytes — increases TLS handshake size.
3. **Hybrid Deployment** — Cannot switch overnight; must run classical + PQC side-by-side (e.g., **Chrome's X25519+Kyber-768 hybrid KEM**).
4. **Standards Convergence** — TLS 1.3, IKEv2, S/MIME, and CMS all need updated PQC profiles.
5. **Compliance** — NIST CNSA 2.0 mandates PQC by **2033** for US national security.
6. **Cryptographic Agility** — Systems must support algorithm switching without re-architecting.

**Lattice-based vs Hash-based (4 Marks):**

| **Property** | **Lattice-based (Kyber / Dilithium)** | **Hash-based (SPHINCS+)** |
|---|---|---|
| Hard Problem | LWE / Module-LWE | Collision/preimage resistance |
| Signature Size | **~2.4 KB** (Dilithium-3) | **~8 KB** (SPHINCS+-128s) |
| Speed | Fast (NTT-based) | Slower (many hash calls) |
| Key Size | Small public keys | Very small public keys |
| Security Confidence | Moderate (relatively newer) | **Very high** (only relies on hash functions) |
| Stateful? | No | Stateless variant (s) / Stateful (XMSS) |
| Use Case | TLS KEM + signatures | Long-term document signatures, firmware |

**Conclusion:** Lattice-based schemes offer the best performance for **online protocols** (TLS, IKE), while hash-based schemes offer the **highest security confidence** for long-archive signatures where quantum-proofing for decades is critical.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Grover's vs Shor's** — Students often write "quantum computers halve RSA key length" — *wrong*. RSA is broken **completely**, not halved. Only symmetric ciphers face the half-strength effect. **(Lose 1–2 marks)**
> 2. **Writing "Zero Trust = no firewall"** — Incorrect. ZTA uses firewalls but adds per-request authentication, microsegmentation, and continuous verification. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 3. **Missing SUCI in 5G answers** — Most students discuss 5G AKA but forget the **SUCI privacy mechanism** which is a 2024-scheme high-weight topic. **(Lose 2 marks)**
> 4. **Confusing PQC with QKD** — PQC runs on classical computers with new math. QKD requires quantum channels and physical hardware. Do not interchange them. **(Lose 1–2 marks)**
> 5. **Omitting NIST PQC year** — Always cite **2022 selection, 2024 standardization (FIPS 203/204/205)** for Kyber, Dilithium, SPHINCS+.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Next Generation System Designs and Challenges:**

- **5G SBA**: Service-based, HTTP/2 + TLS 1.3 between NFs. NRF = registry, SCP = proxy. AKA uses **SUCI** (privacy) and **SUPI** (identity).
- **6G Vision (2030+)**: Sub-THz, AI-native, integrated sensing & communication (ISAC), ubiquitous intelligence, zero-energy devices.
- **SDN Layers**: App → Controller → Switch. Northbound + Southbound APIs. Main threat = **controller compromise**.
- **NFV**: Decouples network functions from hardware. Threat = **VNF escape**, **hypervisor compromise**, MANO hijacking.
- **Zero Trust Principles**: Verify explicitly, least privilege, assume breach. **PE + PA + PEP** per NIST SP 800-207. Trust score is *continuous*, not one-time.
- **Post-Quantum Cryptography**:
  - **Shor's algorithm** breaks RSA/ECC/DH → polynomial-time factoring & discrete log.
  - **Grover's algorithm** halves symmetric key strength → use AES-256 (not 128).
  - NIST PQC winners: **Kyber (KEM)**, **Dilithium (Sig)**, **SPHINCS+ (Sig)**.
  - Crypto agility + hybrid migration are mandatory.
- **QKD**: Information-theoretic security. BB84 threshold = **11% QBER**. Use cases: high-value government/finance backbones.
- **IoT Security**: Three-layer model (Perception/Network/Application). Lightweight standard = **ASCON** (NIST 2023).
- **Edge/Fog Security**: Trust attestation at edge, secure aggregation, **TEE (Trusted Execution Environment)** based root-of-trust.
- **AI/ML in Security**: Both defensive (anomaly detection, UEBA) and offensive (adversarial ML, deepfakes). Concept drift is a major challenge.
- **Blockchain for Secure Communication**: Provides **tamper-evident audit trails** and **decentralized PKI** but suffers from **scalability (Layer-2 needed)** and **quantum vulnerability of ECDSA signatures**.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Next Generation System Designs and Challenges**

*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme — PECPT633 Secure Communication Protocols*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cyber-Physical System Overview and Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT633 - SECURE COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cyber-Physical System Overview and Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is an engineered, integrated computational-physical system whose operations are monitored, coordinated, controlled, and integrated by a computing and communication core. It tightly couples **cyber components** (computation, communication, control algorithms) with **physical processes** (sensors, actuators, mechanical/biological entities) through a continuous feedback loop.

$$ CPS = \{ Computational\,Core \} \cup \{ Physical\,Process \} \cup \{ Feedback\,Loop \} $$

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Interpretation):**
> A CPS is a system in which physical and software components are deeply intertwined, operating at different spatial and temporal scales, interacting in ways that change with context, and exhibiting emergent behavior not predictable from either subsystem alone.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Smart Human Body"

Think of a CPS as a **human body**:
- **Sensors** (nerves) gather data: temperature, pressure, pain.
- **Communication network** (spinal cord + nervous system) transmits signals.
- **Cyber core** (brain) processes and decides.
- **Actuators** (muscles) execute actions.
- **Feedback loop** ensures continuous adjustment (homeostasis).

If an attacker cuts the spinal cord (denial of communication) or poisons the brain's signals (false data injection), the **entire body collapses** — even though every organ is physically intact. This is exactly what happened in the **Stuxnet attack (2010)** on Iranian centrifuges.

## 1.3 CPS vs Related Paradigms

| Feature | CPS | IoT | Embedded System | SCADA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Core Focus** | Bidirectional control | Data collection | Single-task function | Industrial monitoring |
| **Feedback Loop** | **Tight, real-time** | Mostly sensing → cloud | Often open-loop | Slow supervisory |
| **Physical Actuation** | **Mandatory** | Optional | Sometimes | Yes |
| **Time-Criticality** | **Hard real-time** | Soft real-time | Depends | Soft real-time |
| **Scale** | Medium–Large | Massive | Single device | Plant-level |

> [!NOTE]
> **CPS is NOT just IoT + Actuators.** CPS emphasizes *closed-loop, model-based, real-time control* where the cyber layer physically alters the world — not just observes it.

## 1.4 Core Characteristics (KTU Board Favourites)

1. **Heterogeneity** — Mix of hardware, software, networking, and physical dynamics.
2. **Real-time constraints** — Deterministic deadlines (e.g., airbag deployment ≤ 5 ms).
3. **Safety-Criticality** — Failures cause loss of life, environment, or capital.
4. **Continuous operation** — Often **24×7×365** availability required.
5. **Distributed & networked** — Geographic spread with multi-hop communication.
6. **Adaptive / Learning behaviour** — Often integrates ML/AI for control.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Canonical CPS Architecture — The 5C Model

The **5C Architecture** (Academic Citation: Prof. Jay Lee, Univ. of Maryland — widely adopted in KTU textbooks) decomposes a CPS into five progressive layers:

| Layer | Full Name | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **C1** | Smart Connection Level | Acquires raw data from sensors/actuators | CAN bus, Modbus, MQTT |
| **C2** | Data-to-Information Level | Converts raw signals to meaningful features | Filtering, FFT, feature extraction |
| **C3** | Cyber Level | Network-level analytics, ML, edge/cloud compute | Anomaly detection, MPC controllers |
| **C4** | Cognition Level | Human-machine interface, decision support | Dashboards, alerts, recommendations |
| **C5** | Configuration Level | Feedback loop — reconfigures the system itself | Adaptive tuning, auto-calibration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **C5 → C1 feedback arrow** is what makes a system a *CPS* and not merely a *monitoring system*.

## 2.2 Mathematical State-Space Formulation

A discrete-time CPS is canonically modelled as:

$$ x_{k+1} = f(x_k, u_k, w_k) $$
$$ y_k = g(x_k, v_k) $$

Where:
- $x_k \in \mathbb{R}^n$ — physical state vector at step $k$ (e.g., position, velocity, temperature).
- $u_k \in \mathbb{R}^m$ — control input from the cyber layer.
- $y_k$ — sensor measurement.
- $w_k, v_k$ — process and measurement noise.
- $f, g$ — (possibly nonlinear) transition and observation functions.

**Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) special case (highly testable):**

$$ x_{k+1} = A x_k + B u_k + w_k $$
$$ y_k = C x_k + v_k $$

## 2.3 Security Triad in CPS Context

| Traditional CIA | CPS-Specific Extension |
|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | May be deprioritized (e.g., public sensor data) |
| **Integrity** | **Critical** — false data can destroy physical plant |
| **Availability** | **Critical** — downtime = physical harm |

> [!NOTE]
> **CPS introduces two extra pillars: Safety & Real-time-ness.** A CPS that is "secure" but "misses its deadline" is **unsafe**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Risk Severity Score** | $R = T \times V \times I$ | $T$ = Threat probability, $V$ = Vulnerability (0–1), $I$ = Impact (asset value) |
| **Attack Surface Size** | $S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i \cdot e_i$ | $a_i$ = entry-point count, $e_i$ = exposure weight |
| **Mean Time to Failure (MTTF)** | $MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate (per hour) |
| **System Availability** | $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures |
| **Stealthy Attack Residual** | $\Vert y_k - \hat{y}_k \Vert_2 \le \tau$ | If residual $\le \tau$, attack passes detector |
| **Deadline Miss Probability** | $P_{miss} = P(T_{exec} > D)$ | $T_{exec}$ = task execution time, $D$ = deadline |
| **Control Energy (LQR)** | $J = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} (x_k^T Q x_k + u_k^T R u_k)$ | $Q, R$ are weighting matrices |
| **Kalman Innovation (Detection)** | $\nu_k = y_k - C\hat{x}_{k \mid k-1}$ | Trigger alarm if $\Vert \nu_k \Vert > \tau_{th}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT use `|` (vertical bar) in KTU answer tables** for absolute value or set notation — examiners often use markdown parsers. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\vert x \vert$ inside LaTeX math mode.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of CPS

- **Smart Grids** — Automated load balancing, fault isolation.
- **Autonomous Vehicles** — Lane keeping, collision avoidance.
- **Medical Devices** — Pacemakers, insulin pumps.
- **Industrial Robotics** — Collaborative manufacturing (cobots).
- **Aerospace & Defense** — Drones, missile guidance, satellite control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Risk Score for a CPS Asset

We derive the standard $R = T \times V \times I$ expression used in NIST SP 800-30 (referenced qualitatively in KTU).

### Step 1 — Define Threat Probability

Let $T$ be the conditional probability that an adversary attempts exploitation within a given period $\Delta t$:

$$ T = P(\text{attack launched} \,\vert\, \text{system exposed}) $$

### Step 2 — Define Vulnerability

A vulnerability is a flaw that, if exercised, allows the attack to succeed. Quantify via Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS) normalized to $[0, 1]$:

$$ V = \frac{CVSS\_Base}{10} $$

### Step 3 — Define Impact

Impact is a function of the physical damage potential. Let asset value $A_v$ be in monetary or criticality units:

$$ I = f(A_v, SafetyFactor, RecoveryTime) $$

For simplicity, KTU uses:

$$ I = \alpha \cdot A_v + \beta \cdot S_{cost} $$

where $S_{cost}$ is the human-safety cost factor and $\alpha, \beta$ are weights with $\alpha + \beta = 1$.

### Step 4 — Combine (Multiplicative Model)

The risk score is multiplicative because all three factors must coexist for damage:

$$ R = T \cdot V \cdot I $$

This product is then mapped to a qualitative level:

| Risk Range | Level | Action |
|---|---|---|
| $0 \le R \le 0.2$ | Low | Accept / monitor |
| $0.2 < R \le 0.5$ | Medium | Mitigate within 90 days |
| $0.5 < R \le 0.8$ | High | Mitigate within 30 days |
| $0.8 < R \le 1.0$ | Critical | Immediate shutdown/fix |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the **de-facto KTU 14-mark answer template** for "Discuss CPS risk assessment."

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Stealthy False Data Injection (FDI) Detector

### Setup

Plant (LTI): $x_{k+1} = A x_k + B u_k + w_k$, with $w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$.

Sensor: $y_k = C x_k + v_k$, with $v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$.

### Attack Model

Attacker injects $a_k$ into sensor:

$$ \tilde{y}_k = C x_k + v_k + a_k $$

### Kalman Filter Prediction

$$ \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} = A \hat{x}_{k-1 \mid k-1} + B u_{k-1} $$

### Innovation Sequence

$$ \nu_k = \tilde{y}_k - C \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} $$

Substituting:

$$ \nu_k = C(x_k - \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1}) + v_k + a_k $$

### Statistical Distribution Under No Attack

If $a_k = 0$, then $\nu_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, S_k)$ where:

$$ S_k = C P_{k \mid k-1} C^T + R $$

where $P_{k \mid k-1}$ is the predicted state covariance.

### Chi-Squared Detector (KTU exam favourite)

Define the normalized squared innovation:

$$ \chi_k^2 = \nu_k^T S_k^{-1} \nu_k $$

Then $\chi_k^2 \sim \chi^2_m$ (Chi-squared with $m = \dim(y)$ degrees of freedom) under no attack.

### Decision Rule

$$ \text{Alarm} \iff \chi_k^2 > \tau_{th} $$

where $\tau_{th}$ is chosen from $\chi^2$ table for a desired false-alarm rate $\alpha$:

$$ P(\chi^2_m > \tau_{th}) = \alpha $$

### Why This Matters in CPS

> An attacker who knows the model $(A, C)$ can craft $a_k$ such that $\chi_k^2 \le \tau_{th}$, **bypassing the detector while still corrupting the state estimate**. This is the basis of the seminal Liu–Ning–Reiter attack (2009) on smart grids.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Question:** A CPS asset has threat probability $T = 0.3$, CVSS base score = 7.5, asset value $A_v = 100$, safety factor $S_{cost} = 80$, weights $\alpha = 0.6, \beta = 0.4$. Compute the risk.

**Solution:**

$$ V = \frac{7.5}{10} = 0.75 $$

$$ I = (0.6)(100) + (0.4)(80) = 60 + 32 = 92 $$

$$ R = T \cdot V \cdot I = 0.3 \times 0.75 \times 92 = 20.7 $$

Normalize by maximum possible $R_{max} = 1 \cdot 1 \cdot A_{v,max}$. Assuming $A_{v,max} = 200$:

$$ R_{norm} = \frac{20.7}{200} = 0.1035 $$

**Verdict: LOW risk** — Accept with monitoring.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CPS Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Plant\nMachines, Grid, Vehicle] -->|Sensors| B[Pre-Processing\nFiltering, ADC]
    B --> C[Communication Layer\nCAN, Wireless, 5G]
    C --> D[Cyber Core\nController, ML, Edge]
    D -->|Control Commands| E[Actuator Interface]
    E -->|Actuation| A
    D --> F[Dashboard / HMI\nOperator]
    D --> G[Cloud / Analytics]
    G --> D
    style A fill:#ffd5b3
    style D fill:#b3d9ff
    style C fill:#d5b3ff
```

> [!NOTE]
> The closed loop **A → B → C → D → E → A** is the defining signature of a CPS. Breaking any segment (e.g., jamming C) compromises physical safety.

---

## 4.2 CPS Attack Surface Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[CPS Attack Surface] --> P1[Physical Layer\nSensor tampering, actuator hijack]
    P --> P2[Network Layer\nMITM, replay, DoS, protocol exploits]
    P --> P3[Cyber Layer\nMalware, insider, code injection]
    P --> P4[Application Layer\nAPI abuse, weak auth, privilege escalation]
    P --> P5[Supply Chain\nFirmware backdoors, HW trojans]
    P1 --> X[Impact: Physical damage, Safety loss]
    P2 --> X
    P3 --> X
    P4 --> X
    P5 --> X
    style P fill:#ff9999
    style X fill:#ff4444,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.3 CPS Security Defense-in-Depth Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Physical Asset] --> L1[Layer 1: Physical Security\nTamper-proofing, enclosures]
    L1 --> L2[Layer 2: Device Security\nSecure boot, TPM, attestation]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Network Security\nEncryption, IDS/IPS, segmentation]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Application Security\nAuthN/AuthZ, code signing]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: Data Security\nIntegrity checks, audit logs]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6: Monitoring & Response\nSIEM, anomaly detection, IRP]
    style L0 fill:#e0e0e0
    style L6 fill:#90ee90
```

---

## 4.4 CIA + S + R Pentagon for CPS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C[Confidentiality] --> Core(CPS Security Goals)
    I[Integrity] --> Core
    A[Availability] --> Core
    S[Safety] --> Core
    R[Real-timeness] --> Core
    style Core fill:#ffe066
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) and the Internet of Things (IoT).**

**Model Answer (3 marks — allocate 1 mark per contrast point):**

| Aspect | CPS | IoT |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Goal** | Real-time closed-loop control of physical processes | Data collection and remote monitoring |
| **Feedback** | Bidirectional, control-driven | Mostly unidirectional (sensor → cloud) |
| **Actuation** | Integral; alters physical state | Optional / external |
| **Time-Criticality** | Hard real-time (ms deadlines) | Soft / best-effort |
| **Example** | Autonomous braking system | Smart fitness band |

> *Conclusion (1 mark):* CPS focuses on *control and safety*, while IoT focuses on *connectivity and data*. CPS ⊋ IoT (i.e., a CPS may use IoT, but not all IoT systems are CPS).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain any three security challenges specific to Cyber-Physical Systems.**

**Model Answer (1 mark per challenge):**

1. **Real-time constraints vs. cryptographic overhead** — Heavy encryption can exceed control deadlines (e.g., AES-256 may add latency unacceptable in airbag deployment).
2. **Legacy & heterogeneity** — CPS often mix decades-old industrial protocols (Modbus, DNP3) with no built-in security, coexisting with modern IP networks.
3. **Safety–Security trade-off** — A secure shutdown may itself cause physical harm (e.g., emergency stop in a moving train). Security responses must be context-aware.
4. **(Bonus) Physical consequences** — Unlike IT systems, CPS attacks can cause loss of life, environmental damage, or capital destruction.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]*

**Q3 (a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **5C architecture of a Cyber-Physical System**. Explain how the C5 (Configuration) level enables self-adaptation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **5C architecture** integrates data, analytics, and control into five progressive layers:

1. **C1 — Smart Connection (2 marks):**
   - Bidirectional data flow between physical assets and the cyber layer.
   - Uses industrial protocols (OPC-UA, MQTT, Modbus, CAN).
   - Handles sensor calibration, timestamping, noise filtering.

2. **C2 — Data-to-Information Conversion (1 mark):**
   - Raw signals → meaningful indicators (e.g., vibration FFT → bearing health index).
   - Edge analytics to reduce bandwidth.

3. **C3 — Cyber Level (1 mark):**
   - Network-level analytics, ML, model predictive control (MPC).
   - Often deployed on edge or fog nodes.

4. **C4 — Cognition (1 mark):**
   - Operator-facing dashboards, alerts, prescriptive analytics.
   - Bridges human decision-making with automated loops.

5. **C5 — Configuration (2 marks):**
   - Closes the loop back to C1.
   - Dynamically reconfigures thresholds, control parameters, and machine settings.
   - Example: A turbine auto-adjusts blade pitch when wind speed changes — closing the cyber-physical loop.

> *Self-adaptation* (1 mark): C5 uses feedback from C3/C4 to **rewrite its own operational parameters** without human intervention — fulfilling the "cyber-physical" co-evolution.

---

**(b)** A CPS node in a smart grid has the following parameters: Threat probability $T = 0.4$, CVSS base score = 6.0, asset value $A_v = 500$ units, safety cost factor $S_{cost} = 200$, weights $\alpha = 0.7, \beta = 0.3$. Compute the normalized risk and state your mitigation decision. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute vulnerability.

$$ V = \frac{CVSS}{10} = \frac{6.0}{10} = 0.6 $$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute impact.

$$ I = \alpha \cdot A_v + \beta \cdot S_{cost} = (0.7)(500) + (0.3)(200) = 350 + 60 = 410 $$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute raw risk and normalize. Assume max possible asset $A_{v,max} = 1000$.

$$ R = T \cdot V \cdot I = 0.4 \times 0.6 \times 410 = 98.4 $$

$$ R_{norm} = \frac{98.4}{1000} = 0.0984 $$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Decision — $0.0984 \in [0, 0.2]$ → **LOW risk → Accept with routine monitoring**.

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q4 (a)** Explain the **attack surface of a Cyber-Physical System** with examples. Why is a CPS more vulnerable than a traditional IT system? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The CPS attack surface is the union of all physical, cyber, and network entry points an adversary can exploit:

1. **Sensors / Physical Layer (2 marks):**
   - Examples: Spoofing GPS in a UAV; replacing a temperature sensor in a nuclear plant.
   - Stuxnet altered centrifuge speed sensors to read "normal" while motors spun out of control.

2. **Communication / Network Layer (2 marks):**
   - MITM on CAN bus; replay attacks on wireless sensor networks; DoS on industrial Wi-Fi.
   - Protocols like Modbus lack authentication by default.

3. **Controllers / Cyber Layer (1 mark):**
   - Buffer overflows in PLC firmware; malware injection (e.g., Triton/TRISIS targeting Schneider Triconex).

4. **Supply Chain (1 mark):**
   - Hardware Trojans, malicious firmware updates, counterfeit components.

5. **Human / Insider (1 mark):**
   - Social engineering, privilege abuse, USB-based attacks (Stuxnet's NITRO Zeus vector).

**Why CPS is more vulnerable than IT (1 mark):**
- Mix of legacy (no-auth) + modern (IP-connected) systems.
- Attacks cause **physical** (not just data) damage.
- Patching is hard — downtime for a CPS patch can cost millions or risk safety.
- Safety-critical constraints prevent standard IT security responses (e.g., shutting down a hospital infusion pump).

---

**(b)** Describe the **$\chi^2$ (Chi-squared) detector** for False Data Injection attacks in a CPS. State the limitation of this detector. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Setup (2 marks):**
- Linear CPS: $x_{k+1} = A x_k + B u_k + w_k$, $y_k = C x_k + v_k$.
- Attack: $\tilde{y}_k = C x_k + v_k + a_k$.

**Innovation (2 marks):**

$$ \nu_k = \tilde{y}_k - C \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} $$

Under $H_0$ (no attack), $\nu_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, S_k)$ where $S_k = C P_{k \mid k-1} C^T + R$.

**Test Statistic (2 marks):**

$$ \chi_k^2 = \nu_k^T S_k^{-1} \nu_k \sim \chi^2_m $$

where $m = \dim(y)$.

**Decision Rule (1 mark):**

$$ \text{Alarm} \iff \chi_k^2 > \tau_{th} $$

where $P(\chi^2_m > \tau_{th}) = \alpha$ for chosen false-alarm rate.

> *Conclusion:* Threshold violations trigger an alert to the SCADA operator.

**Limitation (bonus, 0.5 each):**
1. **Vulnerable to stealthy attacks** — A model-aware attacker can choose $a_k$ such that $\nu_k^T S_k^{-1} \nu_k \le \tau_{th}$ while still corrupting the state.
2. **Assumes linearity & Gaussianity** — Real CPS often nonlinear and non-Gaussian; performance degrades.
3. **Static threshold** — Does not adapt to operating-mode changes (e.g., start-up vs. steady-state).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Senior Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Confusing CPS with IoT** — 2-mark penalty if you call every CPS an "IoT device." Always emphasize *closed-loop control*.
> 2. **Skipping the safety–security link** — KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests "CPS Security = Safety + Cybersecurity." Omit this and lose 3–5 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting units / normalizing risk** — In numericals, $R$ is **dimensionless** after normalization. Leaving $R = 98.4$ unnormalized is a common 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Writing `|x|` in tables** — Markdown parsers may break. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ in LaTeX.
> 5. **Not drawing the feedback loop** — A 14-mark question without a labelled block diagram (showing sensor → controller → actuator → plant → sensor) is capped at ~10 marks.
> 6. **Generic "use firewall" answers** — Examiners expect **CPS-specific** mitigations: secure boot, PLC whitelisting, network segmentation (Purdue model), and **physical-layer** protections.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> **Things to Remember (Print-Friendly Bullet Sheet):**

- **CPS Definition** = *Cyber* (computation) + *Physical* (process) + *Bidirectional Feedback Loop*.
- **CPS ≠ IoT** — CPS mandates *real-time control + actuation*.
- **5C Architecture** — Connection → Conversion → Cyber → Cognition → **Configuration** (loops back).
- **State-Space** — $x_{k+1} = A x_k + B u_k + w_k$, $y_k = C x_k + v_k$.
- **Security Pillars** — CIA + **Safety** + **Real-timeness** (5 vertices).
- **Risk Formula** — $R = T \cdot V \cdot I$ (multiplicative; all three required).
- **FDI Detection** — $\chi^2_k = \nu_k^T S_k^{-1} \nu_k$, alarm if $> \tau_{th}$.
- **Stealthy attacks** can bypass $\chi^2$ if adversary knows $(A, C)$ → defense-in-depth is mandatory.
- **Attack Surface Layers** — Physical, Network, Cyber, Application, Supply Chain, Human.
- **CPS-Specific Defenses** — Secure boot, TPM, code signing, Purdue model segmentation, **time-aware** crypto, anomaly detection tuned for physical dynamics.
- **Famous CPS Attack** — Stuxnet (2010) — first known cyber-physical weapon, altered centrifuge speeds while spoofing sensor readings.
- **Remember** — In CPS, **safety failure = security failure**. Always treat them jointly in 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internet of Things and Smart Grid Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT633 - SECURE COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Internet of Things and Smart Grid Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Internet of Things (IoT) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> The **Internet of Things (IoT)** is a cyber-physical ecosystem of uniquely identifiable physical objects ("things") embedded with sensors, actuators, software, and network connectivity that enables these objects to collect, exchange, and act upon data over existing network infrastructure (typically **IP-based**), achieving seamless integration between the physical world and computational systems.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, IoT is treated as a **three-layer convergence model**: *Perception (Sensing) Layer → Network (Transport) Layer → Application (Service) Layer*, governed by the ITU-T Y.2060 reference model.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The PECPT633 course expects you to know IoT **only through the lens of security** — i.e., IoT as an *attack surface*, an *identity-management challenge*, and a *lightweight cryptography application domain*. Do NOT drift into pure IoT application design.

## 1.2 Smart Grid — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (NIST Smart Grid Framework, adopted by KTU):**
> A **Smart Grid** is an electricity supply network that integrates **Information and Communication Technology (ICT)** with the traditional power grid to enable two-way flow of electricity **and** information between utilities and consumers, supporting real-time monitoring, automated control, demand response, and self-healing capabilities.

The KTU 2024 module treats Smart Grid as a **critical infrastructure** whose security directly impacts *national security*. The canonical reference architecture follows the **NIST Smart Grid Conceptual Model (NIST SP 1108r3)** comprising seven logical domains: Bulk Generation, Transmission, Distribution, Customers, Operations, Markets, and Service Providers.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogies (Intuition Layer)

### Analogy 1 — IoT as a "Nervous System for the World"

Imagine your **human body**:
- **Sensors** (skin, eyes, ears) gather data about the world → these are the IoT *Perception Layer* devices.
- **Nerves** carry signals to the **brain** → these are the IoT *Network Layer* protocols (Wi-Fi, 6LoWPAN, LoRaWAN).
- The **brain** decides what to do and sends commands to **muscles** → these are the IoT *Application Layer* services that trigger *actuators*.

Now imagine the body has **no skin protection, no pain receptors for cyber-pain, and no immune system**. That's precisely the IoT security predicament: billions of tiny, low-power, often physically exposed "nerves" with **no built-in immunity**.

### Analogy 2 — Smart Grid as an "Air Traffic Control System for Electricity"

The traditional grid was a **one-way highway**: power plants → transmission lines → your home. No feedback, no rerouting.

The smart grid is like **air traffic control**:
- Sensors on every line report load, temperature, voltage → **SCADA + PMUs (Phasor Measurement Units)**.
- A central command (control center) decides which "plane" (electron flow) goes where.
- Consumers become **prosumers** (producers + consumers) — solar panels push power back up the "runway".

If a hacker spoofs a sensor signal saying *"all clear"* when a transformer is overheating, it's the equivalent of an air traffic controller staring at a fabricated radar — catastrophe follows.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world incident to anchor memory:** The **2015 Ukraine Power Grid Attack** (BlackEnergy malware) left 225,000 people without electricity. This is the canonical KTU case study for smart grid security failure — expect it in exam answers.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters for KTU Examinations

| Reason | KTU Significance |
|---|---|
| **Interdisciplinary weightage** | Appears in both core Security courses and Elective clusters |
| **Industry 4.0 alignment** | Directly tied to NEP 2020 skill outcomes |
| **High Bloom's Level** | Module 3 targets *Apply / Analyze / Evaluate* cognitive levels |
| **Question variety** | Tests diagrams, layered architectures, and protocol mapping |

The key performance metric in smart grid communications is the **latency budget**: protection commands (e.g., GOOSE messages in IEC 61850) require **less than 4 ms** end-to-end. This brutal timing constraint is what makes smart grid security *fundamentally different* from generic enterprise security.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 IoT Security — Layered Threat Architecture

The KTU 2024 module expects a layered (defense-in-depth) treatment of IoT threats. Each layer has distinct attack vectors:

### 2.1.1 Perception Layer Threats (Hardware/Sensor Tier)

The Perception Layer contains resource-constrained devices: **8-bit MCUs**, sub-1 GHz radios, passive RFID tags, MEMS sensors. Typical memory budgets: **< 32 KB RAM, < 256 KB Flash**.

| Threat | Mechanism | Example Attack |
|---|---|---|
| **Node Tampering** | Physical access to hardware | Replacing a smart meter chip with rogue firmware |
| **Side-Channel Attack** | Power/EM emanation analysis | DPA on AES in smart card |
| **Eavesdropping** | Wireless sniffing on ISM bands | Intercepting ZigBee traffic with SDR |
| **RF Jamming** | Denial via noise flooding | Disabling a sensor cluster |
| **Node Cloning** | Duplicating identity | Replay of stolen X.509 cert |

### 2.1.2 Network Layer Threats

The Network Layer uses heterogeneous protocols: **6LoWPAN, RPL (IPv6 Routing Protocol for Low-Power Lossy Networks), CoAP, MQTT, DTLS**.

| Threat | Mechanism | Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|
| **Sinkhole Attack** | Rogue node advertises attractive routes | RPL rank authentication |
| **Sybil Attack** | Forged multiple identities | Resource testing, chain of trust |
| **Wormhole Attack** | Out-of-band fast tunnel | SECTOR / packet leash protocols |
| **Man-in-the-Middle** | Intercept + relay + modify | Mutual DTLS handshake |
| **Routing Table Poisoning** | Inject fake DIO/DAO messages | RPL secure mode (RFC 6550) |

### 2.1.3 Application Layer Threats

| Threat | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **Data Exfiltration** | CoAP/MQTT channel misuse |
| **Malicious Firmware OTA** | Compromised update server |
| **Privilege Escalation** | Exploiting broken ACLs on cloud broker |
| **API Abuse** | Replaying REST calls to backend |

## 2.2 IoT Lightweight Cryptography — The KTU High-Yield Core

Because IoT nodes cannot run **AES-256 in hardware** comfortably, the KTU syllabus emphasizes **lightweight primitives**. The standard reference is the **NIST Lightweight Cryptography (LWC) Standardization Project (2023)** which finalized **Ascon** as the standard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ascon Family (NIST LWC Winner):**
> - **Ascon-128 / Ascon-128a** — Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data (AEAD)
> - **Ascon-Hash / Ascon-Hash256** — Hash functions
> - **Ascon-Xof / Ascon-Xofa** — Extendable Output Functions
> - Designed for **8-bit, 32-bit, 64-bit** platforms
> - Energy footprint: **~5× lower than AES-GCM** on constrained devices

### Key Design Constraints of Lightweight Cryptography

1. **Gate Equivalent (GE) Budget** — must fit in < 2,000 GE for ultra-low-cost tags.
2. **Throughput-Ratio** — bits/sec normalized to area.
3. **Energy per Bit (pJ/bit)** — measured at 0.9 V on 65nm CMOS.
4. **Side-Channel Resistance** — first-order masking must be cheap.

## 2.3 Smart Grid Security Framework

### 2.3.1 NIST Cybersecurity Framework Mapping for Smart Grid

| Function | Smart Grid Implementation |
|---|---|
| **Identify** | Asset inventory of PMUs, RTUs, IEDs |
| **Protect** | IEC 62351-3 TLS for transport, role-based access |
| **Detect** | Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) on SCADA networks |
| **Respond** | Automated isolation of compromised IEDs |
| **Recover** | Black start procedures, firmware re-flashing |

### 2.3.2 IEC 62351 Security Standards (KTU High-Yield)

This is the **single most important standard** for smart grid security in KTU exams:

| Part | Scope | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **IEC 62351-3** | TCP/IP profiles (IEC 61850) | **TLS 1.2+** with mutual auth |
| **IEC 62351-4** | MMS (Manufacturing Message Spec) | TLS + X.509 |
| **IEC 62351-6** | IEC 61850 GOOSE / Sampled Values | **Multicast group security** |
| **IEC 62351-8** | Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) | Attribute certificates |
| **IEC 62351-9** | Key management | Symmetric key distribution |
| **IEC 62351-11** | XML security | XML signatures & encryption |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** GOOSE messages (Generic Object Oriented Substation Event) operate at **Layer 2** directly over Ethernet — they CANNOT use TLS. Therefore IEC 62351-6 uses a custom **multicast authentication** scheme with sequence-number anti-replay. Students who write "GOOSE uses TLS" automatically lose marks.

### 2.3.3 Smart Grid Communication Architecture

The smart grid has a **hierarchical communication model**:

| Tier | Latency | Protocol | Security |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tier 1 — Field Area** | 3-10 ms | GOOSE, SV (IEC 61850) | Multicast MAC auth |
| **Tier 2 — Process Bus** | 10-100 ms | MMS, DNP3 | IEC 62351 TLS |
| **Tier 3 — Station Bus** | 100 ms – 1 s | IEC 61850, ICCP | TLS + RBAC |
| **Tier 4 — Control Center** | 1 s – 1 min | ICCP, TASE.2 | TLS + firewalls |
| **Tier 5 — Enterprise** | Minutes-Hours | Web services, REST | OAuth 2.0, SAML |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_b = I \cdot V \cdot t_{op}$ | Energy per bit for crypto operation | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $T_{pkt} = \frac{L_{pkt}}{R_{bit}}$ | Packet transmission time | seconds (s) |
| 3 | $C_{tot} = C_{agg} + N \cdot C_{sec}$ | Total cost = aggregation + per-node security | monetary |
| 4 | $P_{col} = 1 - (1-p)^N$ | Collision probability with N nodes | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\eta_{sec} = \frac{T_{useful}}{T_{useful}+T_{crypto}}$ | Security efficiency ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Shannon entropy of key source | bits |
| 7 | $\text{Latency}_{E2E} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} (t_{proc,i} + t_{queue,i} + t_{tx,i})$ | End-to-end latency budget | ms |
| 8 | $K_{req} \geq H(X) \cdot 1.44$ | Required key bits (NIST SP 800-57) | bits |
| 9 | $DR = \frac{N_{detected}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Detection rate of smart grid IDS | percent |
| 10 | $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP+TN} \times 100\%$ | False positive rate of IDS | percent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical substitution rule for exam:** When asked to compute *energy per encrypted byte* for an IoT device, use $E_b = \frac{V_{DD} \cdot I_{active} \cdot N_{cycles}}{f_{clk} \cdot 8}$. Replace `|` symbols with `\vert` if absolute value is needed.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

This knowledge is directly applied in:
- **Smart meter firmware** development (energy utilities)
- **Substation automation** engineering (POWERGRID, KSEB projects in Kerala)
- **IoT penetration testing** (consulting roles)
- **Critical Infrastructure Protection (CIP)** under India's **IT Act 2000, Section 70** (declares smart grid as "Critical Information Infrastructure")
- **NERC-CIP** compliance in North American deployments

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Energy for AES-128 Encryption on an IoT Node

> **Problem (KTU standard):** An IoT sensor node operates at $V_{DD} = 1.8$ V, draws $I_{active} = 12$ mA during AES-128 encryption, and the AES engine requires $N_{cycles} = 1032$ clock cycles. The clock is $f_{clk} = 16$ MHz. Compute the **energy per encrypted byte** in microjoules.

### Step-by-Step Exhaustive Solution

**Step 1 — Time taken for one AES-128 encryption**

$$t_{enc} = \frac{N_{cycles}}{f_{clk}}$$

$$t_{enc} = \frac{1032}{16 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$t_{enc} = 64.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 64.5 \; \mu s$$

**Step 2 — Power drawn during encryption**

$$P_{active} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{active}$$

$$P_{active} = 1.8 \times 12 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$P_{active} = 21.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W} = 21.6 \text{ mW}$$

**Step 3 — Energy consumed per single AES-128 block (16 bytes plaintext)**

$$E_{block} = P_{active} \cdot t_{enc}$$

$$E_{block} = 21.6 \times 10^{-3} \times 64.5 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$E_{block} = 1393.2 \times 10^{-9} \text{ J}$$

$$E_{block} = 1.3932 \; \mu J \text{ per 16-byte block}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to energy per encrypted byte**

$$E_{byte} = \frac{E_{block}}{16}$$

$$E_{byte} = \frac{1.3932 \; \mu J}{16}$$

$$E_{byte} = 0.087 \; \mu J / \text{byte} \approx 87 \; nJ / \text{byte}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation for IoT battery life**

A typical IoT coin cell (CR2032) stores ~ **2200 mAh at 3 V** → total energy:

$$E_{cell} = 3 \times 2200 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 23.76 \; kJ$$

Maximum encrypted bytes possible if *all* energy went to AES:

$$N_{bytes} = \frac{E_{cell}}{E_{byte}} = \frac{23760}{0.087 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 2.73 \times 10^{11} \text{ bytes}$$

That's ~ **273 GB** — confirming AES-128 is feasible for IoT in *encryption energy*, but actual battery life is dominated by **radio transmission**, which consumes 10-100× more energy.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Collision Probability in Slotted ALOHA for Smart Meter Reporting

> **Problem:** A neighborhood has $N = 200$ smart meters reporting to a data concentrator using **slotted ALOHA**. Each meter transmits with probability $p$ in any given slot. Find the optimal $p$ and the resulting maximum throughput.

### Exhaustive Solution

**Step 1 — Probability that a *specific* meter succeeds**

In slotted ALOHA, a successful transmission occurs when exactly **one** node transmits in a slot.

$$P_{one} = \binom{N-1}{1} \cdot p \cdot (1-p)^{N-1}$$

**Step 2 — Probability of *any* successful transmission (channel throughput)**

By linearity of expectation over $N$ nodes:

$$S = N \cdot p \cdot (1-p)^{N-1}$$

**Step 3 — Find optimal $p$**

Take derivative and equate to zero:

$$\frac{dS}{dp} = N \left[ (1-p)^{N-1} - p \cdot (N-1)(1-p)^{N-2} \right] = 0$$

$$(1-p)^{N-2} \left[ (1-p) - p(N-1) \right] = 0$$

Since $(1-p)^{N-2} \neq 0$:

$$1 - p - p(N-1) = 0$$

$$1 - pN = 0$$

$$\boxed{p_{opt} = \frac{1}{N}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into $S$ for maximum throughput**

$$S_{max} = N \cdot \frac{1}{N} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^{N-1}$$

$$S_{max} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^{N-1}$$

For $N = 200$:

$$S_{max} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{200}\right)^{199}$$

$$S_{max} = (0.995)^{199}$$

$$S_{max} \approx e^{-199/200} \approx e^{-0.995} \approx 0.3697$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

Maximum throughput = **36.97 %** (classic slotted ALOHA bound). At $p_{opt} = 1/200 = 0.005$, only ~ 0.5 % of meters transmit per slot on average.

> [!NOTE]
> **Security implication:** A smart grid IDS must monitor for abnormal *burst* retransmissions (when $p \gg p_{opt}$), which is a signature of a **jamming or flooding DoS attack**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Required Key Entropy for Smart Grid Session

> **Problem:** A smart grid substation uses a nonces from a hardware RNG whose output distribution has probabilities $p = \{0.5, 0.25, 0.125, 0.125\}$. Compute the **Shannon entropy** and the **minimum required key length** under NIST SP 800-57 (factor 1.44).

### Exhaustive Solution

**Step 1 — Apply Shannon's formula**

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{4} p_i \log_2 p_i$$

$$H(X) = -\left[ 0.5 \log_2 0.5 + 0.25 \log_2 0.25 + 0.125 \log_2 0.125 + 0.125 \log_2 0.125 \right]$$

Compute each term:
- $\log_2 0.5 = -1$ → $-0.5 \times (-1) = 0.5$
- $\log_2 0.25 = -2$ → $-0.25 \times (-2) = 0.5$
- $\log_2 0.125 = -3$ → $-0.125 \times (-3) = 0.375$
- $\log_2 0.125 = -3$ → $-0.125 \times (-3) = 0.375$

Sum:
$$H(X) = 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.375 + 0.375 = 1.75 \text{ bits per symbol}$$

**Step 2 — Minimum key length with NIST margin**

$$K_{req} = 1.44 \times H(X)$$

For a target of 128-bit security equivalent:

$$N_{symbols} = \frac{128}{1.75} \approx 73.14 \approx 74 \text{ symbols}$$

**Step 3 — Total bit length to capture**

$$L_{total} = 74 \times 1.75 = 129.5 \approx 130 \text{ bits captured}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering conclusion**

A 128-bit AES key requires at least **74 consecutive RNG samples** from this biased source to reach cryptographic-grade entropy. In practice, NIST recommends running an **entropy extractor (e.g., Hash_DRBG)** to condition the raw output.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Lightweight Ciphers

| Cipher | Block Size (bits) | Key Size (bits) | GE (≈) | Throughput @ 100 kHz (Mbps) | Energy (nJ/bit) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AES-128** | 128 | 128 | 2,400 | 12.8 | 4.7 |
| **Ascon-128** | 128 | 128 | 2,890 | 5.6 | 2.1 |
| **Speck 128/128** | 128 | 128 | 1,240 | 12.0 | 1.5 |
| **Simon 128/128** | 128 | 128 | 1,560 | 12.0 | 1.8 |
| **PRESENT-80** | 64 | 80 | 1,570 | 0.20 | 3.7 |

**Reading the table:** Speck is best for *throughput-per-area*, AES for *ubiquitous trust*, Ascon for *AEAD + side-channel resistance*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IoT Three-Layer Security Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Perception Layer\nSensors Actuators RFID] --> B[Network Layer\n6LoWPAN RPL MQTT CoAP]
    B --> C[Application Layer\nCloud Analytics Dashboards]

    A1[Node Tampering Attack] -.threat.-> A
    A2[RF Jamming Attack] -.threat.-> A
    A3[Side Channel Attack] -.threat.-> A

    B1[Sinkhole Attack] -.threat.-> B
    B2[Sybil Attack] -.threat.-> B
    B3[MITM Attack] -.threat.-> B
    B4[Wormhole Attack] -.threat.-> B

    C1[Data Exfiltration] -.threat.-> C
    C2[Malicious OTA Firmware] -.threat.-> C
    C3[API Abuse] -.threat.-> C

    A --> Asec[Lightweight Cryptography\nAscon Speck Hardware TPM]
    B --> Bsec[DTLS TLS 1.3\nIPSec PKI]
    C --> Csec[OAuth 2.0 RBAC\nEnd to End Encryption]
```

## 4.2 Smart Grid NIST Reference Model with Security Overlays

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GEN[Bulk Generation Domain]
        GEN1[Power Plants]
        GEN2[Renewable Farms]
    end

    subgraph TRANS[Transmission Domain]
        TRANS1[HV Substations]
        TRANS2[PMUs RTUs]
    end

    subgraph DIST[Distribution Domain]
        DIST1[Distribution Substations]
        DIST2[Smart Meters]
    end

    subgraph CUST[Customer Domain]
        CUST1[Home Area Network]
        CUST2[EV Charging]
        CUST3[Solar Prosumers]
    end

    subgraph OPS[Operations Domain]
        OPS1[Control Center SCADA]
        OPS2[EMS DMS Software]
    end

    subgraph MKT[Markets Domain]
        MKT1[Energy Trading Systems]
        MKT2[Billing Platforms]
    end

    subgraph SVC[Service Provider Domain]
        SVC1[Cloud Analytics]
        SVC2[Demand Response]
    end

    GEN --> TRANS
    TRANS --> DIST
    DIST --> CUST

    OPS -.monitors.-> GEN
    OPS -.monitors.-> TRANS
    OPS -.monitors.-> DIST
    MKT -.settlement.-> CUST
    SVC -.services.-> CUST
    SVC -.analytics.-> OPS

    SEC1[IEC 62351 TLS 1.2] -.secures.-> TRANS
    SEC2[Multicast Authentication] -.secures.-> DIST
    SEC3[OAuth 2.0 RBAC] -.secures.-> OPS
    SEC4[End to End Encryption] -.secures.-> CUST
```

## 4.3 DTLS Handshake in CoAP-Based IoT (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant IoT as IoT Sensor Client
    participant GW as Gateway Server
    participant CA as Certificate Authority

    Note over IoT CA: Phase 1 Pre Provisioning
    IoT ->> CA: Obtain X.509 Certificate
    CA -->> IoT: Signed Certificate Issued

    Note over IoT GW: Phase 2 DTLS 1.2 Handshake
    IoT ->> GW: ClientHello with PSK Identity Hint
    GW ->> IoT: ServerHello ServerKeyExchange ServerHelloDone
    IoT ->> GW: ClientKeyExchange ChangeCipherSpec Finished
    GW ->> IoT: ChangeCipherSpec Finished

    Note over IoT GW: Phase 3 Secure CoAP Exchange
    IoT ->> GW: CoAP POST encrypted Application Data
    GW ->> IoT: CoAP ACK encrypted Acknowledgment

    Note over IoT GW: Phase 4 Session Termination
    IoT ->> GW: Alert Close Notify
    GW -->> IoT: Alert Close Notify Ack
```

## 4.4 Smart Grid Attack Surface Map (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ATTACKER[Threat Actor Profile]
        ATK1[Nation State APT]
        ATK2[Insider Employee]
        ATK3[Terrorist Group]
        ATK4[Hacktivist]
    end

    subgraph SURFACE[Attack Surface Components]
        SF1[Smart Meter Firmware]
        SF2[Substation IEDs]
        SF3[Control Center HMI]
        SF4[Wireless Sensor Links]
        SF5[Cloud Analytics APIs]
    end

    subgraph IMPACT[Consequence Severity]
        IMP1[Power Outage]
        IMP2[Equipment Damage]
        IMP3[Economic Loss]
        IMP4[Public Safety Risk]
    end

    ATK1 --> SF1
    ATK1 --> SF3
    ATK2 --> SF2
    ATK2 --> SF5
    ATK3 --> SF4
    ATK4 --> SF3
    ATK4 --> SF5

    SF1 --> IMP1
    SF2 --> IMP2
    SF3 --> IMP1
    SF3 --> IMP3
    SF4 --> IMP1
    SF5 --> IMP3
    SF5 --> IMP4
```

## 4.5 Lightweight Cryptography Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[New IoT Product Design] --> Q1{Resource Budget?}
    Q1 -->|Greater than 32 KB RAM| AES[AES 128 GCM via Hardware]
    Q1 -->|8 to 32 KB RAM| ASCON[Ascon 128 AEAD]
    Q1 -->|Less than 8 KB RAM| SPE[Speck or Simon Block Cipher]

    AES --> Q2{Need Authenticated Encryption?}
    ASCON --> Q2
    SPE --> Q2

    Q2 -->|Yes| AEAD[Use AEAD Mode Only]
    Q2 -->|No| ENC[Encrypt then MAC Pattern]

    AEAD --> OUT1[Deploy]
    ENC --> OUT1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between the Perception Layer and Network Layer of an IoT architecture. Give one security threat specific to each layer.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Perception Layer | Network Layer |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Data acquisition via sensors/RFID | Data routing and transport |
| **Components** | Sensors, actuators, edge nodes | Routers, gateways, 6LoWPAN |
| **Protocols** | ZigBee, BLE, LoRa | IPv6, RPL, CoAP |
| **Security Threat** | Node Tampering / RF Jamming | Sinkhole Attack / MITM |
| **Countermeasure** | Tamper-proof enclosures, lightweight crypto (Ascon) | RPL secure mode, DTLS |

*(1 mark — correct identification, 1 mark — distinction, 1 mark — specific threat with example)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Why is GOOSE messaging in IEC 61850 protected differently from MMS messaging in smart grid communications? Mention the corresponding IEC 62351 parts.**

**Model Answer:**

- **MMS messaging** runs over **TCP/IP** with latency tolerance of ~100 ms, so it is secured by **IEC 62351-3** using **TLS 1.2+** with mutual X.509 authentication.
- **GOOSE messaging** runs directly over **Ethernet Layer 2** (bypassing TCP/IP) with **ultra-strict latency budgets < 4 ms** for protection commands, so TLS is impossible due to handshake overhead.
- GOOSE is therefore secured by **IEC 62351-6** using a **custom multicast authentication scheme** based on HMAC and **sequence-number-based anti-replay** (rejecting stale GOOSE frames).
- **IEC 62351-9** handles the symmetric key distribution supporting both.

*(1 mark per correct point × 3 points)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**Q (a)** Explain the **three-layer IoT security architecture** in detail. For each layer, identify **two specific attacks** and propose **one suitable countermeasure** with technical justification. **(7 Marks)**

**Q (b)** Discuss the **security requirements and challenges in Smart Grid communications** as per the **NIST Smart Grid Conceptual Model**. Explain how **IEC 62351** addresses the **CIA triad** in this context. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q(a) — IoT Three-Layer Security Architecture

**Layer 1 — Perception Layer (Sensing Tier)**

- **Function:** Physical signal capture via MEMS sensors, RFID tags, BLE beacons. Devices have constrained memory (often < 32 KB RAM) and battery power.
- **Attack 1 — Node Tampering:** Attacker physically opens a sensor enclosure and replaces firmware. **Countermeasure:** Use **tamper-evident hardware enclosures**, hardware security modules (HSMs), and Physically Unclonable Functions (PUFs) for device identity binding.
- **Attack 2 — Side-Channel Attack:** Differential Power Analysis (DPA) recovers AES keys from current traces. **Countermeasure:** Deploy **masked AES implementations** with first-order Boolean masking and constant-time operations.

**Layer 2 — Network Layer (Transport Tier)**

- **Function:** Routing and data transport via 6LoWPAN, RPL, CoAP, MQTT, and DTLS.
- **Attack 3 — Sinkhole Attack:** A rogue RPL node advertises high "rank" attractiveness, luring traffic through itself. **Countermeasure:** Use **RPL secure mode (RFC 6550)** with authenticated DIO/DAO messages and rank validation via watchdog monitoring.
- **Attack 4 — Man-in-the-Middle (MITM):** Attacker intercepts CoAP handshake. **Countermeasure:** Mandatory **mutual DTLS 1.3** with **Pre-Shared Keys (PSK) or Raw Public Keys** to avoid full PKI overhead.

**Layer 3 — Application Layer (Service Tier)**

- **Function:** Cloud aggregation, analytics, dashboards, OTA firmware management.
- **Attack 5 — Malicious OTA Update:** Compromised firmware pushed to fleet. **Countermeasure:** **Code signing with X.509 certificates**, secure boot chain of trust from hardware root-of-trust.
- **Attack 6 — API Token Theft:** OAuth tokens stolen from cloud. **Countermeasure:** **Short-lived JWT tokens**, mutual TLS, role-based access control with least-privilege.

**Conclusion:** A defense-in-depth approach requires *cross-layer trust anchors* and *lightweight cryptographic primitives* (e.g., Ascon) to maintain battery life while providing CIA guarantees.

#### Model Solution for Q(b) — Smart Grid Security Requirements

**NIST Smart Grid Conceptual Model (NIST SP 1108r3)** has **7 logical domains** connected via **bidirectional flows of electricity and information**.

**CIA Triad in Smart Grid Context:**

| CIA Pillar | Smart Grid Implication | IEC 62351 Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Customer consumption data is PII; pricing data is business-critical | TLS 1.2 (Part 3), XML encryption (Part 11) |
| **Integrity** | Tampered control commands can cause blackouts | HMAC for GOOSE (Part 6), digital signatures |
| **Availability** | Substation commands must arrive in < 4 ms | Multicast authentication, anti-replay sequence numbers |

**Critical Security Requirements:**

1. **Real-time authentication:** Substation IEDs must authenticate messages in < 1 ms.
2. **Non-repudiation:** Billing transactions (MMS) must be cryptographically signed for regulatory compliance.
3. **Scalability:** Millions of smart meters must be securely keyed and rekeyed periodically.
4. **Inter-domain trust:** Operations, Markets, and Service Provider domains need federated PKI.
5. **Legacy coexistence:** Many deployed devices use DNP3 without security; retrofit via gateways.

**How IEC 62351 Addresses These:**

- **IEC 62351-3:** Mandates TLS for TCP/IP-based protocols (MMS, ICCP). Mutual authentication prevents rogue control centers.
- **IEC 62351-6:** Custom multicast security for GOOSE/SV — sequence number + HMAC tag.
- **IEC 62351-8:** Role-Based Access Control with attribute certificates (X.509 extensions).
- **IEC 62351-9:** Defines key management lifecycle including rekeying intervals.
- **IEC 62351-11:** XML security for IEC 61970 CIM (Common Information Model).

**Challenges:**

- **Key distribution complexity** at million-meter scale.
- **Latency-vs-security tradeoff** at the field bus.
- **Insider threats** from privileged operators.
- **Supply chain risk** from foreign-manufactured IEDs (Chinese firmware implants).

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**Q (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Smart Grid Communication Architecture** showing all five tiers. Compare the **security mechanisms** used in the **field tier** vs **control center tier**. **(7 Marks)**

**Q (b)** A smart meter operating at $V_{DD} = 3.3$ V draws $I = 8$ mA during AES-128 encryption taking $N = 1100$ clock cycles at $f_{clk} = 8$ MHz. Calculate **(i)** the time per encryption, **(ii)** the energy per encryption block, **(iii)** the energy per encrypted byte, and **(iv)** how many encryptions a **2400 mAh @ 3 V** battery can support if 100% energy were available for crypto. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q(a) — Smart Grid Communication Architecture

*(See Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram for visual reference.)*

**Five Tiers of Smart Grid Communication:**

| Tier | Latency Budget | Protocols | Security Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **T1 — Field Area Network (FAN)** | 3-10 ms | GOOSE, Sampled Values | IEC 62351-6 multicast HMAC |
| **T2 — Process Bus / Bay Level** | 10-100 ms | MMS, DNP3, Modbus | IEC 62351-3 TLS |
| **T3 — Station Bus** | 100 ms – 1 s | IEC 61850 MMS, ICCP | TLS + RBAC (62351-8) |
| **T4 — Control Center** | 1 s – 1 min | ICCP/TASE.2, OPC UA | TLS + firewalls + IDS |
| **T5 — Enterprise / Cloud** | Minutes-hours | REST APIs, IEC 61970 CIM | OAuth 2.0, SAML |

**Comparison: Field Tier vs Control Center Tier**

- **Field Tier Security:**
  - Latency budget of **< 4 ms** rules out TLS handshake overhead.
  - Uses **symmetric HMAC-SHA-256** with **pre-shared group keys**.
  - Anti-replay via **monotonically increasing sequence numbers**.
  - Authentication tags are typically 32-64 bytes appended to GOOSE frames.

- **Control Center Tier Security:**
  - Latency budget of seconds allows **full TLS 1.2/1.3 handshake**.
  - Uses **mutual X.509 authentication** between utility control centers.
  - **RBAC** ensures operators can only access authorized IEDs.
  - **Network segmentation** via DMZ, firewalls, and intrusion detection systems.

**Conclusion:** Security design is fundamentally different across tiers because the **latency-vs-cryptographic-strength tradeoff** dominates the architecture.

#### Model Solution for Q(b) — Numerical on IoT Energy

**Given:**
- $V_{DD} = 3.3$ V
- $I = 8$ mA $= 8 \times 10^{-3}$ A
- $N_{cycles} = 1100$
- $f_{clk} = 8$ MHz $= 8 \times 10^{6}$ Hz
- AES block size = **16 bytes**
- Battery: 2400 mAh @ 3 V

**(i) Time per encryption:**

$$t_{enc} = \frac{N_{cycles}}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1100}{8 \times 10^{6}} = 137.5 \; \mu s$$

**(ii) Power drawn:**

$$P = V_{DD} \cdot I = 3.3 \times 8 \times 10^{-3} = 26.4 \; mW$$

**Energy per AES block (16 bytes):**

$$E_{block} = P \cdot t_{enc} = 26.4 \times 10^{-3} \times 137.5 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$E_{block} = 3630 \times 10^{-9} \; J = 3.63 \; \mu J$$

**(iii) Energy per encrypted byte:**

$$E_{byte} = \frac{E_{block}}{16} = \frac{3.63}{16} \approx 0.2269 \; \mu J / \text{byte} \approx 226.9 \; nJ / \text{byte}$$

**(iv) Total battery energy:**

$$E_{cell} = V \times Q = 3 \times 2400 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 25{,}920 \; J$$

**Total encrypted bytes (if 100% used for crypto):**

$$N_{bytes} = \frac{E_{cell}}{E_{byte}} = \frac{25{,}920}{0.2269 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 1.143 \times 10^{11} \text{ bytes} \approx 114 \; GB$$

**Total AES blocks:**

$$N_{blocks} = \frac{N_{bytes}}{16} \approx 7.14 \times 10^{9} \text{ AES blocks} = 7.14 \; \text{billion encryptions}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversions** — Marks are deducted if you forget to convert mA to A, MHz to Hz, or $\mu$J to J. Always state units explicitly.
> 2. **Battery energy formula** — Students often forget the **3600** seconds/hour multiplier. The correct formula is $E = V \times I \times t$ where $t$ must be in seconds.
> 3. **AES block size = 16 bytes** — Never compute per-byte energy using AES key size (16 bytes = 128 bits). Many students incorrectly divide by 32 (256-bit AES) and lose 1 mark.
> 4. **GOOSE does NOT use TLS** — A repeated mistake costing up to 3 marks.
> 5. **Three layers, three names** — Always write "Perception, Network, Application" — not "Physical, MAC, Transport" (that's the OSI confusion trap).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3:**

- **IoT Architecture:** Three layers — **Perception → Network → Application**. Each layer has a dedicated protocol stack and unique threat profile.
- **IoT Threat Triad:** **Node Tampering** (perception), **Sinkhole/Sybil** (network), **API/OTA** (application).
- **Lightweight Crypto Standard:** **Ascon** family (NIST LWC 2023 winner). Preferred over PRESENT for new designs.
- **AES Feasibility:** AES-128 is feasible on IoT nodes but should be **hardware-accelerated** for battery life.
- **Smart Grid = 7 NIST Domains:** Bulk Generation, Transmission, Distribution, Customers, Operations, Markets, Service Provider.
- **IEC 62351 Mastery:** Parts **3, 4, 6, 8, 9, 11** are KTU-essential. Part 6 governs multicast GOOSE security.
- **GOOSE Timing:** < **4 ms** end-to-end → TLS impossible → uses **HMAC + sequence number**.
- **MMS Timing:** ~100 ms → uses **TLS 1.2+** with mutual X.509.
- **Smart Grid Attack Surfaces:** Smart meter firmware, IEDs, control center HMI, wireless sensor links, cloud APIs.
- **NIST SP 800-57:** Use factor **1.44** for converting entropy bits to required raw key material bits.
- **Slotted ALOHA Max Throughput:** $\frac{1}{e} \approx 36.8$ % at $p_{opt} = 1/N$.
- **Entropy Formula:** $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$. Always in **bits/symbol**.
- **Critical Real-World Incidents:** **2015 Ukraine BlackEnergy** attack is the canonical KTU case study.
- **India Context:** Smart Grid declared **Critical Information Infrastructure** under IT Act 2000, Section 70.
- **Defense-in-Depth:** Always advocate layered security — no single protocol is sufficient for IoT or Smart Grid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, structure your answer as:
> 1. **Diagram** (3 marks)
> 2. **Layer-by-layer explanation** (4 marks)
> 3. **Threat analysis with countermeasure** (4 marks)
> 4. **Real-world example or case study** (3 marks)
>
> Skipping the diagram is the **single biggest mark-loss** behavior reported by KTU examiners.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data and Infrastructure Security in Cloud/Edge Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT633 - SECURE COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data and Infrastructure Security in Cloud/Edge Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition: Cloud & Edge Computing Security

**Cloud Computing Security (Cloud Security)** refers to the comprehensive set of policies, controls, procedures, and technologies that work together to protect cloud-based systems, data, and infrastructure from internal and external threats. As defined by NIST SP 800-144, it encompasses the protection of *confidentiality, integrity, and availability* of information systems and data residing in public, private, hybrid, or community cloud environments.

**Edge Computing Security** extends this paradigm to distributed computing architectures where data processing occurs at the *network edge*—closer to the data source (IoT devices, gateways, local servers). It addresses the unique vulnerabilities introduced by decentralised topologies, constrained device capabilities, and heterogeneous network connections.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECPT633 – Module 3):**
> Data security in transit (TLS 1.3, IPsec, MACsec) and at rest (AES-256, envelope encryption) must be studied in conjunction with infrastructure security controls—hypervisor hardening, container isolation, IAM, and zero-trust network access (ZTNA). Students must be able to map each control to the **CIA Triad**.

---

### 1.2 The CIA Triad — The Foundation of All Cloud/Edge Security

Every security control in cloud and edge environments ultimately serves one (or more) of three foundational objectives:

| Pillar | Symbol | Meaning | Cloud/Edge Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | $C$ | Ensuring data is accessible only to authorised entities | AES-256 encryption of S3 buckets, field-level tokenisation |
| **Integrity** | $I$ | Guaranteeing data is unaltered in transit or at rest | SHA-256 checksums, HMAC-SHA-256, digital signatures |
| **Availability** | $A$ | Ensuring systems are accessible when required | Multi-AZ deployment, DDoS mitigation, redundant edge nodes |

---

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: "The Bank Vault in the Sky vs. the ATM Network"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Cloud is the Bank, Edge is the ATM"**
>
> Imagine a national bank (the **Cloud Provider**) that owns a giant vault with money. Customers (tenants) rent lockers inside. The bank provides *physical* security—guards, walls, surveillance cameras—but **each customer must lock their own locker** and **control who has the key**.
>
> Now consider the **ATM network (Edge)**. Each ATM is a small vault placed in remote locations. The ATM must be tamper-proof, communicate securely with the central bank over potentially hostile networks, and survive partial network outages.
>
> - **Bank Security** = Cloud Security (infrastructure-level)
> - **Locker Security** = Data Security (tenant responsibility)
> - **ATM Security** = Edge Security (distributed, resource-constrained)
> - **Shared Locks** = Shared Responsibility Model

This single analogy underpins the **Shared Responsibility Model** that AWS, Azure, and GCP all publish.

---

### 1.4 Shared Responsibility Model — KTU High-Yield Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shared Responsibility Model (NIST SP 800-144 aligned):**
> The cloud provider is responsible for **security *of* the cloud** (the underlying infrastructure, physical hosts, hypervisor, networking fabric). The customer is responsible for **security *in* the cloud** (data, OS, application logic, IAM, network configuration).

| Layer | Cloud Provider (CSP) | Customer (Tenant) |
|---|---|---|
| Physical Data Centre | ✅ | ❌ |
| Network & Hypervisor | ✅ | ❌ |
| Host OS / Virtualisation | ✅ | ❌ |
| Guest OS | ❌ | ✅ |
| Application Software | ❌ | ✅ |
| Data & Identity | ❌ | ✅ |

> [!NOTE]
> In **IaaS**, the CSP owns less; in **SaaS**, the CSP owns more. **Edge** typically behaves like IaaS+—the edge operator owns physical security of the device, but the workload owner owns configuration.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Layered Threat Model for Cloud/Edge Environments

Cloud and edge computing introduce a **multi-tenant, distributed attack surface**. Security must be applied at every layer:

#### **Layer 1 — Data Security**
Data exists in three states: *in transit*, *at rest*, and *in use*.

- **Data in Transit:** Encrypted using **TLS 1.3** (cloud APIs), **IPsec ESP** (site-to-site VPN), or **MACsec** (LAN-level, IEEE 802.1AE).
- **Data at Rest:** Encrypted using **AES-256-GCM** with keys managed via **AWS KMS / Azure Key Vault / GCP KMS**. Envelope encryption is the gold standard: a *Key Encryption Key (KEK)* encrypts the *Data Encryption Key (DEK)*.
- **Data in Use:** Protected via **Confidential Computing** using Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs) such as Intel SGX, AMD SEV-SNP, or AWS Nitro Enclaves.

#### **Layer 2 — Identity & Access Management (IAM)**
- **Authentication:** MFA, FIDO2/WebAuthn, SAML 2.0, OAuth 2.0 with OIDC.
- **Authorisation:** RBAC (Role-Based), ABAC (Attribute-Based), PBAC (Policy-Based).
- **Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP):** Grant only the *minimum* permissions required.
- **Privileged Access Management (PAM):** Just-in-time elevation for admin tasks.

#### **Layer 3 — Infrastructure Security**
- **Hypervisor Hardening:** Disable unnecessary services, patch CVEs (e.g., Venom, Cloudburst).
- **Container Security:** Signed images (Docker Content Trust, Sigstore/Cosign), runtime sandboxing (gVisor, Kata Containers), CIS Benchmark compliance.
- **Software-Defined Networking (SDN) Micro-segmentation:** Each workload gets its own security policy—zero lateral movement.

#### **Layer 4 — Edge-Specific Concerns**
- **Device Tamper Resistance:** TPM 2.0, secure boot chains.
- **Intermittent Connectivity:** Local caching of security policies, offline-tolerant auth (e.g., cached OAuth tokens).
- **Lightweight Crypto:** ECDSA (P-256), ChaCha20-Poly1305, Ed25519 (preferred over RSA for constrained devices).

---

### 2.2 Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA) — NIST SP 800-207

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zero Trust (NIST Definition):**
> "Zero Trust is a security model, a set of system design principles, and a coordinated cybersecurity and system management strategy based on an acknowledgement that threats exist both inside and outside traditional network perimeters."

The core maxim is: **"Never trust, always verify."** The three foundational principles are:

1. **Verify Explicitly** — Authenticate and authorise *every* request using identity, device posture, location, and data sensitivity.
2. **Least Privilege Access** — Time-bound, just-in-time (JIT) access using tools like Azure AD PIM, AWS IAM Identity Center.
3. **Assume Breach** — Design as if the attacker is *already inside*. Use micro-segmentation and continuous monitoring.

#### Mathematical Pillars of Zero Trust
Zero Trust can be modelled as a **continuous adaptive trust decision function**:

$$T_{access}(R, t) = f\big(I_{identity}, D_{posture}, L_{location}, S_{sensitivity}, H_{history}\big)$$

Where:
- $T_{access}$ = Trust score for request $R$ at time $t$
- $I_{identity}$ = Identity assurance (e.g., MFA strength score)
- $D_{posture}$ = Device security posture (patch level, EDR status)
- $L_{location}$ = Geo-location and network context risk
- $S_{sensitivity}$ = Data sensitivity classification
- $H_{history}$ = Behavioural anomaly score

A request is granted iff $T_{access} \geq T_{threshold}$.

---

### 2.3 Envelope Encryption — The Industry Standard for Cloud Data Protection

In envelope encryption, a randomly generated **DEK** encrypts the data, while a separate **KEK** (often HSM-protected) encrypts the DEK. The KEK never leaves the HSM/KMS.

**Workflow:**

1. Generate random DEK locally.
2. Encrypt plaintext $P$ with DEK: $C = E_{DEK}(P)$.
3. Send DEK to KMS → receive encrypted DEK: $E_{KEK}(DEK)$.
4. Store $(C,\ E_{KEK}(DEK))$ together.
