5. To decrypt: KMS decrypts DEK → DEK decrypts $C$ → recover $P$.

This allows **cryptographic erasure**—deleting the DEK makes all associated ciphertext permanently unreadable, even if retained for compliance.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas for PECPT633 Module 3**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Description | Unit / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CIA Triad Completeness | $S = C \cap I \cap A$ | Security = intersection of all three | Boolean |
| 2 | AES Block Size | $B_{AES} = 128$ | Fixed block size | bits |
| 3 | AES-256 Key Schedule Rounds | $N_{r} = 14$ | For AES-256 | rounds |
| 4 | AES Effective Key Bits | $K = \{128, 192, 256\}$ | NIST FIPS-197 allowed | bits |
| 5 | RSA Modulus (n) | $n = p \cdot q$ | Product of two large primes | $\geq 2048$ bits |
| 6 | RSA Encryption | $C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$ | Public-key encryption | — |
| 7 | RSA Decryption | $M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$ | Private-key decryption | — |
| 8 | RSA Key Size Min (2024+) | $\vert n \vert \geq 2048$ | NIST SP 800-131A | bits |
| 9 | ECDSA Key Strength (equiv.) | $256\text{-bit EC} \approx 3072\text{-bit RSA}$ | NIST equivalence | bits |
| 10 | SHA-256 Output | $H(M) = 256\text{ bits}$ | Fixed hash size | bits |
| 11 | HMAC Construction | $HMAC(K,M) = H((K \oplus opad) \Vert H((K \oplus ipad) \Vert M))$ | RFC 2104 | bits |
| 12 | Shannon Entropy | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Information content | bits/symbol |
| 13 | TLS 1.3 Cipher Suites | $TLS\_AES\_256\_GCM\_SHA384$, etc. | AEAD mandatory | — |
| 14 | Envelope Encryption | $(C, E_{KEK}(DEK))$ | Combined ciphertext + wrapped key | tuple |
| 15 | Zero Trust Threshold | $\text{Allow} \iff T_{access} \geq T_{threshold}$ | Adaptive decision | — |
| 16 | Diffie-Hellman Shared Secret | $K_{AB} = g^{ab} \pmod{p}$ | Forward-secret key agreement | scalar |
| 17 | Cloud Storage Redundancy | $Durability = 1 - (1 - R)^{n}$ | $R$=reliability of one node | probability |
| 18 | Availability SLA | $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | System availability | ratio (0–1) |
| 19 | RTO / RPO | $RTO$, $RPO$ | Recovery Time / Point Objective | seconds |
| 20 | MACsec Encryption | $IEEE\ 802.1AE$, hop-by-hop | Layer 2 confidentiality | — |

> [!TIP]
> Use `\vert` (not `|`) when writing absolute values inside LaTeX tables to prevent markdown table breakage.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of These Concepts

- **AWS S3 + KMS** uses envelope encryption with AES-256-GCM for server-side encryption.
- **Azure Confidential VMs** use AMD SEV-SNP to encrypt VM memory, protecting "data in use."
- **Cloudflare Workers** (edge) authenticate every request via signed JWTs and propagate Zero Trust policies to 300+ edge PoPs.
- **Apple Private Relay** uses Oblivious DNS over HTTPS (ODoH) — a Zero-Trust pattern for privacy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Why AES-256 Provides 256-bit Security

The Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) is a **substitution–permutation network** operating on a 128-bit state matrix. The number of effective key bits $K_{eff}$ is bounded below by the **work factor** of the best known attack (related-key, biclique, etc.) relative to brute force.

For AES-256:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Work Factor}_{\text{brute force}} &= 2^{256} \\
\text{Work Factor}_{\text{biclique attack}} &\approx 2^{254.4} \\
\text{Effective Security} &= \log_2(\text{Work Factor}_{\text{best known}}) \\
&= 254.4\ \text{bits}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore AES-256 provides at minimum **256-bit effective security**, which exceeds the practical cryptanalytic reach for the foreseeable future. NIST approves it for **TOP SECRET** data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 256 and not 128?** AES-256 provides a larger margin against future cryptanalytic breakthroughs (especially quantum Grover's algorithm, which halves effective key strength to ~128 bits—still secure).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: RSA Encryption/Decryption Mathematical Correctness

Given $n = pq$ (product of two large primes), Euler's totient function is:

$$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$$

Choose $e$ such that $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$. The private exponent $d$ is computed as:

$$d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$$

Equivalently:

$$e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$$

This implies:

$$\exists k \in \mathbb{Z} : e \cdot d = 1 + k \cdot \phi(n)$$

**Encryption of message $M$ (where $0 < M < n$):**

$$C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$$

**Decryption of ciphertext $C$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C^{d} &\equiv (M^{e})^{d} \pmod{n} \\
      &\equiv M^{e \cdot d} \pmod{n} \\
      &\equiv M^{1 + k \cdot \phi(n)} \pmod{n} \\
      &\equiv M^{1} \cdot \left(M^{\phi(n)}\right)^{k} \pmod{n}
\end{aligned}$$

By **Euler's Theorem**, $\gcd(M, n) = 1 \Rightarrow M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$. Hence:

$$C^{d} \equiv M \cdot 1^{k} \equiv M \pmod{n}$$

✅ **Decryption recovers the original message $M$.**

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Cloud Security Controls

| Control | Purpose | Cloud Native? | Edge Compatible? | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AES-256-GCM | Symmetric encryption | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Fast, hardware-accelerated (AES-NI) | Key management complexity |
| RSA-2048 | Key exchange / signing | ✅ Yes | ⚠️ Slow | Mature, widely supported | 1000× slower than EC |
| ECDSA P-256 | Signing | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Compact, fast | Implementation pitfalls |
| ChaCha20-Poly1305 | Symmetric AEAD | ✅ Yes | ✅✅ Best | Constant-time, no AES hardware needed | Slightly newer adoption |
| TLS 1.3 | Transport security | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | 1-RTT handshake, FS by default | Requires modern stack |
| MACsec (802.1AE) | LAN security | ⚠️ Rare | ✅ LAN only | Hop-by-hop, low latency | Limited scope |
| TPM 2.0 | Device identity | ⚠️ Server-side | ✅✅ Ideal | Hardware root of trust | Cost, supply chain |
| Confidential VMs (SEV-SNP) | Memory encryption | ✅ Yes | ⚠️ Limited | Protects "data in use" | Performance overhead |
| Zero Trust Network Access | Identity-centric perimeter | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Replaces VPN | Initial deployment cost |
| CASB | Cloud service monitoring | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Shadow IT discovery | Agent dependency |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Envelope Encryption with AWS KMS

**Scenario:** A fintech tenant wants to encrypt a 500 MB JSON dataset stored in S3.

**Step-by-Step:**

1. **Generate DEK locally** (client-side, 256-bit random).
2. **Encrypt payload** with AES-256-GCM:
   $C = AES\text{-}256\text{-}GCM(DEK, IV, P, AAD)$
3. **Call KMS GenerateDataKey**:
   - KMS returns plaintext DEK + encrypted DEK (encrypted by CMK).
4. **Discard plaintext DEK from memory** after encryption completes.
5. **Store in S3 metadata**: $C$ as object body + $E_{KEK}(DEK)$ as object metadata.
6. **For decryption**:
   - Authorised client calls KMS Decrypt → obtains plaintext DEK.
   - DEK decrypts $C$ → recovers $P$.

**Security benefit:** Even if an attacker exfiltrates S3 objects, without KMS authorisation they cannot recover the plaintext. KMS access is logged to **AWS CloudTrail** for audit.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange (Forward Secrecy Basis)

Public parameters: prime $p$, generator $g$ of $\mathbb{Z}_p^{\ast}$.

1. Alice picks secret $a$, computes $A = g^{a} \pmod{p}$, sends $A$.
2. Bob picks secret $b$, computes $B = g^{b} \pmod{p}$, sends $B$.
3. Alice computes $K = B^{a} \pmod{p}$.
4. Bob computes $K = A^{b} \pmod{p}$.

**Correctness:**

$$\begin{aligned}
K_{\text{Alice}} &= B^{a} = (g^{b})^{a} = g^{ab} \pmod{p} \\
K_{\text{Bob}} &= A^{b} = (g^{a})^{b} = g^{ab} \pmod{p} \\
\therefore\ K_{\text{Alice}} &= K_{\text{Bob}} = g^{ab} \pmod{p}
\end{aligned}$$

An eavesdropper seeing $(A, B)$ must solve the **Computational Diffie-Hellman Problem (CDHP)** to recover $g^{ab}$, which is hard under the **Decisional Diffie-Hellman Assumption (DDHA)** for sufficiently large $p$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TLS 1.3 mandates ephemeral (EC)DHE** in every handshake → every session has a unique key → **forward secrecy**. Compromising the long-term private key does NOT decrypt past sessions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

#### **Question 1: Define the CIA Triad and Map Each Pillar to a Cloud Data Security Control.**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECPT633 Module 3]**

**Model Answer:**

The **CIA Triad** is the foundational model of information security comprising three pillars:

1. **Confidentiality** — Ensuring data is disclosed only to authorised parties.
   *Cloud control:* AES-256 encryption of S3 buckets + KMS-managed keys + IAM policies.

2. **Integrity** — Ensuring data is not modified or tampered with, accidentally or maliciously.
   *Cloud control:* SHA-256 checksums, HMAC-SHA-256 message authentication, S3 Object Lock with WORM compliance.

3. **Availability** — Ensuring systems and data remain accessible when needed.
   *Cloud control:* Multi-AZ deployment, AWS Shield for DDoS mitigation, Amazon S3 99.999999999% (11 nines) durability SLA.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip (3 marks):** Allot 1 mark for each pillar's *definition* and 1 mark *collectively* for the mapped control examples. Keep answers crisp — examiners reward precision, not verbosity.

---

#### **Question 2: What is Envelope Encryption? Why is it Preferred for Cloud Data at Rest?**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECPT633 Module 3]**

**Model Answer:**

**Envelope Encryption** is a cryptographic pattern in which the *Data Encryption Key (DEK)* — used to encrypt bulk data — is itself encrypted by a *Key Encryption Key (KEK)* held inside a Hardware Security Module (HSM) or cloud KMS.

**Why preferred:**

- **Performance:** Bulk encryption uses symmetric AES (fast); only the small DEK is asymmetrically wrapped.
- **Separation of duties:** The DEK can be generated client-side; the KEK never leaves the HSM.
- **Cryptographic erasure:** Deleting the (wrapped) DEK renders all ciphertext unreadable — a clean deletion primitive for compliance (GDPR right-to-erasure).
- **Auditability:** Every DEK decrypt call is logged to CloudTrail → non-repudiation.
- **Granularity:** Each object can have a unique DEK without overwhelming the KMS API quota.

**Cloud examples:** AWS S3 SSE-KMS, Azure Storage Customer-Managed Keys, GCP CMEK.

> [!TIP]
> Draw a small diagram with *Plaintext → DEK → Ciphertext* and *DEK → KEK → Wrapped DEK* to score full marks — visual aids are rewarded.

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Module questions carry 14 marks and offer an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Both Question A and Question B below are fully solved model answers.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive — Data Security in Cloud Computing**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the data lifecycle in cloud computing and the security controls applied at each stage.

**Model Answer:**

The **cloud data lifecycle** has six stages. Security controls must be applied at each:

| Stage | Description | Security Controls |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Create** | Data is generated by user/app | Data classification labelling (Public/Internal/Confidential/Restricted), DLP policies at source |
| 2. **Store** | Data is persisted | AES-256 encryption at rest, KMS-managed keys, immutable backups, WORM storage |
| 3. **Use** | Data is actively processed | Confidential computing (Intel SGX, AMD SEV-SNP), memory encryption, just-in-time decryption |
| 4. **Share** | Data is exchanged between services/tenants | IAM policies, signed URLs, scoped OAuth tokens, mTLS |
| 5. **Archive** | Data is moved to long-term cold storage | Glacier vault lock, compliance mode, retention policies |
| 6. **Destroy** | Data is deleted | Cryptographic erasure, NIST 800-88 media sanitisation, audit logs of deletion |

**Marks distribution:** ~1 mark per stage description + 1 mark for the consolidated control table.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Compare symmetric and asymmetric encryption for cloud data protection. Recommend a hybrid approach with justification.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Symmetric (AES) | Asymmetric (RSA / ECC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Key Size** | 128 / 192 / 256 bits | RSA ≥ 2048 bits; EC ≥ 256 bits |
| **Speed** | Very fast (GB/s with AES-NI) | ~1000× slower |
| **Key Distribution** | Hard — must share secret | Easy — public key is public |
| **Use Case** | Bulk data encryption | Key wrapping, signatures, key exchange |
| **Quantum Risk** | Grover halves effective bits | Shor breaks it entirely |

**Recommended Hybrid Approach — Envelope Encryption + ECDHE for Transit:**

- **In Transit:** Use **TLS 1.3** with **X25519 ECDHE** for key exchange → forward secrecy, fast handshake.
- **At Rest:** Use **AES-256-GCM** with envelope encryption. Wrap DEK using **RSA-2048 / RSA-3072 / ECDSA P-256** as the KEK.
- **Authentication:** Use **Ed25519** signatures for API requests (compact, fast).
- **Key Storage:** HSM or cloud KMS (FIPS 140-2 Level 3).
- **Compliance:** NIST SP 800-57, ISO 27001, PCI-DSS 4.0, SOC 2 Type II.

**Justification:** This hybrid leverages the *speed* of symmetric crypto for bulk operations and the *convenience* of asymmetric crypto for key management — the same pattern used by **TLS 1.3, AWS S3 SSE-KMS, and Azure Blob Storage**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Comprehensive — Infrastructure Security & Edge Computing**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the NIST Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA) and its core components with reference to cloud/edge environments.

**Model Answer:**

**NIST SP 800-207** defines Zero Trust as: *"a security model … based on an acknowledgement that threats exist both inside and outside traditional network perimeters."*

**Core Logical Components of ZTA:**

1. **Policy Engine (PE)** — Decides whether to grant access based on policies, identity, device, context.
2. **Policy Administrator (PA)** — Executes the PE's decisions — establishes/terminates sessions.
3. **Policy Enforcement Point (PEP)** — The gateway that allows/blocks traffic (e.g., Zscaler ZIA, Cloudflare Access, AWS Verified Access).

**Key Pillars:**

| Pillar | Cloud Application | Edge Application |
|---|---|---|
| Verify Explicitly | OIDC + MFA via Auth0 | mTLS device certificates via TPM |
| Least Privilege | JIT access via Azure AD PIM | Scoped API tokens per IoT device |
| Assume Breach | Microsegmentation via VPC | Network slicing in 5G + secure boot |
| Continuous Verification | SIEM-driven UEBA | Local anomaly detection on gateway |

**ZTNA replaces traditional VPN** by enforcing per-application, identity-aware access — eliminating the lateral movement that VPNs allow once breached.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Discuss security challenges unique to Edge Computing and propose mitigations.

**Model Answer:**

**Unique Challenges of Edge:**

1. **Physical Tampering** — Edge devices are deployed in unsecured locations (cell towers, factory floors, vehicles).
   *Mitigation:* TPM 2.0, tamper-evident enclosures, secure boot chain, hardware attestation.

2. **Resource Constraints** — Limited CPU, memory, battery.
   *Mitigation:* Lightweight crypto (ChaCha20-Poly1305, Ed25519, ECDSA P-256 over RSA), optimised TLS 1.3 stacks (wolfSSL, mbedTLS).

3. **Intermittent Connectivity** — Cannot rely on constant cloud access.
   *Mitigation:* Cached OAuth tokens, offline-tolerant policy decisions, store-and-forward with local encryption.

4. **Heterogeneous Networks** — Mix of Wi-Fi, 5G, LoRaWAN, satellite.
   *Mitigation:* MACsec (LAN), IPsec (WAN), DTLS 1.3 (UDP-based), application-layer mTLS.

5. **Distributed Attack Surface** — Each edge node is an entry point.
   *Mitigation:* Zero Trust Network Access (ZTNA), per-device certificates, software attestation (Sigstore).

6. **Data Sovereignty** — Data crosses jurisdictional boundaries.
   *Mitigation:* Geo-fencing, region-pinned KMS keys, GDPR/CCPA-aligned data residency controls.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> Students frequently **lose 2–3 marks** by:
> 1. **Confusing ZTA with ZTNA.** ZTA is the *philosophy/model*; ZTNA is the *product category* (Cloudflare Access, Zscaler Private Access). Do not use them interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the Shared Responsibility Model.** Always clarify *who does what* when answering cloud security questions — this is a recurring KTU board favourite.
> 3. **Writing "AES is unbreakable."** Examiners deduct marks for absolute claims. State: *"AES-256 is computationally infeasible to break with current technology under known cryptanalytic attacks."*
> 4. **Ignoring Forward Secrecy.** Always mention ECDHE / DHE when describing TLS — RSA key exchange alone is no longer accepted in TLS 1.3.
> 5. **Skipping cryptographic erasure.** When asked about "secure deletion in cloud," *cryptographic erasure via envelope encryption* is the gold-standard answer.

---

### **TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER**

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for PECPT633 – Module 3**

- 🔐 **CIA Triad:** Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — every cloud/edge control maps here.
- ☁️ **Shared Responsibility Model:** CSP = security *of* the cloud; Customer = security *in* the cloud.
- 🔑 **Envelope Encryption:** AES-256-GCM (DEK) + KMS-managed KEK → industry standard for data at rest.
- 🌐 **TLS 1.3:** Mandatory for data in transit; uses ECDHE for forward secrecy; AEAD cipher suites (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305).
- 🛡️ **Zero Trust (NIST 800-207):** Never trust, always verify; verify explicitly, least privilege, assume breach.
- 🏗️ **ZTA Components:** Policy Engine (PE) + Policy Administrator (PA) + Policy Enforcement Point (PEP).
- 💻 **Confidential Computing:** Protects *data in use* via TEEs (Intel SGX, AMD SEV-SNP, AWS Nitro Enclaves).
- 📦 **Container Security:** Signed images (Cosign), CIS Benchmarks, runtime sandboxing (gVisor, Kata).
- 🌐 **Edge Crypto:** Prefer ECDSA P-256 / Ed25519 / ChaCha20-Poly1305 over RSA for constrained devices.
- 🏭 **MACsec (802.1AE):** Layer 2 hop-by-hop encryption for LAN/edge; complements IPsec/TLS at higher layers.
- 📋 **Compliance:** NIST SP 800-144 (cloud), NIST SP 800-207 (zero trust), NIST SP 800-131A (crypto standards), ISO 27001, SOC 2, PCI-DSS 4.0.
- 🔢 **Key Length Equivalence:** 128-bit AES ≈ 3072-bit RSA ≈ 256-bit EC (NIST-aligned).
- 🚫 **Cryptographic Erasure:** Deleting the wrapped DEK = permanent deletion of associated ciphertext.
- 📊 **Availability Math:** $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$; cloud SLAs typically promise ≥ 99.9% (three nines) up to 99.999% (five nines).
- ⚡ **Post-Quantum:** Plan migration to **CRYSTALS-Kyber** (KEM) and **CRYSTALS-Dilithium** (signatures) per NIST PQC standards (FIPS 203/204).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: BLOCKCHAIN, DECENTRALIZED APPLICATIONS, AND SMART CONTRACTS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Blockchain?

A **Blockchain** is a distributed, immutable, append-only data structure consisting of cryptographically linked blocks, replicated and maintained across a peer-to-peer (P2P) network of nodes, governed by a consensus protocol rather than a central authority.

Formally, a blockchain can be defined as an ordered sequence of blocks:

$$ \mathcal{B} = \{ B_0,\ B_{1},\ B_{2},\ \ldots,\ B_{n} \} $$

where each block $B_i$ contains:
- A block header (metadata)
- A list of validated transactions $T = \{ t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_k \}$
- A cryptographic hash pointer to the previous block: $H_{i-1}$
- Its own block hash: $H_i = \text{SHA-256}(\text{Header}(B_i))$

> [!NOTE] **KTU Syllabus Highlight – Block Linking Equation**
> The cryptographic linkage between consecutive blocks is the **security anchor** of the entire blockchain. Tampering with block $B_{i-1}$ changes its hash $H_{i-1}$, which invalidates $B_i$, which in turn invalidates $B_{i+1}$, cascading the failure all the way to the genesis block. This is the **tamper-evidence** property.

**Intuition (Real-World Analogy):**
Imagine a **public notice board** in a small town where every citizen can post announcements. Once a notice is pinned, the town clerk takes a photo of the entire board, includes a unique fingerprint (hash) of the previous day's photo, and staples them together chronologically. The photo book is photocopied and distributed to every household. To alter even a single word, you would need to forge the photo of that day, change every subsequent photo (because each contains the previous fingerprint), AND convince the majority of households that your forged version is the real one. This is essentially how blockchain works — except the "households" are **nodes** and the "photo fingerprint" is a **cryptographic hash**.

---

### 1.2 Decentralized Applications (DApps)

A **Decentralized Application (DApp)** is a software application whose backend logic runs on a decentralized peer-to-peer network (typically a blockchain) rather than on a centralized server. It combines a frontend UI with smart contracts running on-chain.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Formal KTU Definition**
> A DApp is a tuple $\mathcal{D} = (\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{F}, \mathcal{N}, \mathcal{T})$ where:
> - $\mathcal{S}$ = Set of on-chain smart contracts
> - $\mathcal{F}$ = Frontend interface (off-chain UI)
> - $\mathcal{N}$ = Decentralized network of nodes
> - $\mathcal{T}$ = Token/cryptoeconomic incentive layer

**Properties of DApps (vs. traditional apps):**
| Property | Traditional App | DApp |
|---|---|---|
| Backend Control | Centralized server | Distributed P2P nodes |
| Data Storage | Centralized DB | Blockchain ledger |
| Trust Model | Trust the operator | Trustless (cryptographic) |
| Downtime | Possible | Near-zero (no SPOF) |
| Censorship | Operator can block | Resistant |
| Transparency | Opaque | Open-source & verifiable |

**Intuition:** Think of a traditional banking app (e.g., PayPal) — the company controls every transaction, can freeze your account, and can change rules unilaterally. A DApp is like a **vending machine with no owner** — the rules are baked into the code (smart contract), anyone can verify them, and the machine operates as long as the network is alive.

---

### 1.3 HashCash

**HashCash** is a proof-of-work (PoW) system originally proposed by **Adam Back in 1997**, designed to limit email spam and denial-of-service attacks. It requires the sender to compute a partial hash collision before the recipient will process a request.

The sender must find a string (called a *stamp* or *token*) such that when hashed (typically using SHA-1 or SHA-256), the resulting digest has a specified number of leading zero bits.

> [!NOTE] **KTU Syllabus Highlight – HashCash Stamp Format**
> A HashCash stamp is a tuple: `ver:N, date:string, resource:string, ext:hex, rand:hex, counter:hex`
> Valid only if: $\text{Hash}(ver, date, resource, ext, rand, counter)$ has the required number of leading zero bits (the *bit difficulty*).

**Intuition:** Imagine a postal worker who refuses to deliver your letter unless the envelope has a special stamp that you can only get by solving a small math puzzle. Spammers sending millions of junk emails cannot afford to solve the puzzle for each one, but a legitimate sender sending 5 emails a day can. This is HashCash.

---

### 1.4 Consensus Protocols

A **Consensus Protocol** is a fault-tolerant algorithm by which a distributed network of nodes agrees on a single, consistent state of the ledger, even in the presence of malicious or faulty participants (Byzantine nodes).

> [!IMPORTANT] **The Byzantine Generals Problem**
> Consensus protocols solve the classic Byzantine Generals Problem: how do distributed parties agree on a single strategy when some participants may be traitors broadcasting conflicting information? Lamport, Shostak, and Pease (1982) proved that consensus is achievable only if the number of traitors $T$ satisfies: $N \geq 3T + 1$ where $N$ is the total number of participants.

**Major consensus families (KTU syllabus-relevant):**
1. **Proof of Work (PoW)** — HashCash-style mining (e.g., Bitcoin)
2. **Proof of Stake (PoS)** — Stake-based validator selection (e.g., Ethereum 2.0)
3. **Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT)** — Voting-based (e.g., Hyperledger)
4. **Delegated PoS (DPoS)** — Stake-weighted voting for delegates
5. **Proof of Authority (PoA)** — Identity-based (e.g., VeChain)
6. **Raft/Paxos** — Crash-fault tolerant (non-Byzantine)

---

### 1.5 Smart Contracts

A **Smart Contract** is a self-executing computer program deployed on a blockchain that automatically enforces the terms of an agreement when predefined conditions are met. The code is immutable once deployed, and its execution is verified by every node in the network.

> [!NOTE] **KTU Definition – Smart Contract (Szabo, 1997)**
> *"A smart contract is a computerized transaction protocol that executes the terms of a contract. The general objectives are to satisfy common contractual conditions, minimize exceptions both malicious and accidental, and minimize the need for trusted intermediaries."* — Nick Szabo (1997, predates Bitcoin)

**Intuition:** A smart contract is a **vending machine contract**. You insert coins (cryptocurrency), the machine checks the conditions (price ≥ item cost), and if satisfied, dispenses the item (executes the transfer). No shopkeeper, no lawyer, no trust needed — just code and cryptography.

---

### 1.6 Blockchain Security

**Blockchain Security** refers to the cryptanalytic, protocol-level, and system-level defenses that protect the confidentiality, integrity, availability, and immutability of a blockchain network against attacks such as **51% attacks, Sybil attacks, double-spending, eclipse attacks, and smart contract exploits**.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Core Security Properties (CIA + I)**
> - **Confidentiality**: Pseudonymity via public key addresses (transactions are public, identities are not directly tied)
> - **Integrity**: Cryptographic hash chains (Merkle roots)
> - **Availability**: Distributed P2P (no single point of failure)
> - **Immutability**: One-way hash functions + consensus
> - **Authentication**: Digital signatures (ECDSA, Schnorr)
> - **Non-Repudiation**: Signed transactions are publicly verifiable
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Block

Each block $B_i$ in the blockchain has two components: a **header** and a **body**.

**Block Header Structure (Bitcoin-style):**

| Field | Size (bytes) | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Version | 4 | Protocol version number |
| Previous Block Hash | 32 | SHA-256 hash of previous block header |
| Merkle Root | 32 | Root of Merkle tree of all transactions |
| Timestamp | 4 | Unix epoch time of block creation |
| Difficulty Target (nBits) | 4 | Encoded PoW difficulty |
| Nonce | 4 | Counter incremented during mining |

**Block Body:** Contains the list of transactions $T = \{t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_k\}$.

---

## 2.2 Cryptographic Hash Functions (The Backbone)

A **cryptographic hash function** $H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ must satisfy:

1. **Determinism**: Same input $\Rightarrow$ same output
2. **Pre-image resistance**: Given $y$, finding $x$ such that $H(x) = y$ is computationally infeasible
3. **Second pre-image resistance**: Given $x_1$, finding $x_2 \neq x_1$ with $H(x_1) = H(x_2)$ is infeasible
4. **Collision resistance**: Finding any $x_1 \neq x_2$ with $H(x_1) = H(x_2)$ is infeasible
5. **Avalanche effect**: A 1-bit change in input changes $\approx 50\%$ of output bits
6. **Puzzle-friendliness**: For a target set $T \subseteq \{0,1\}^n$, finding $x$ with $H(x) \in T$ requires $\approx \frac{2^n}{\vert T \vert}$ trials

**Bitcoin uses double SHA-256:** $H(x) = \text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256}(x))$

---

## 2.3 Merkle Tree Construction

A **Merkle tree** is a binary tree of hash values that allows efficient and secure verification of large data structures.

**Construction algorithm:**
1. Hash each transaction: $h_i = H(t_i)$
2. Pair adjacent hashes and hash the concatenation: $h_{ij} = H(h_i \Vert h_j)$
3. Repeat until a single root hash remains

For $n$ transactions, the Merkle tree has depth $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ and the root is a single 256-bit hash.

**Merkle Proof (SPV):** A Simplified Payment Verification (SPV) client only needs $\log_2 n$ sibling hashes to verify a transaction's inclusion — not the entire block.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> Merkle trees are used in **Git version control**, **distributed databases (Cassandra, DynamoDB)**, **certificate transparency logs (Google)**, and **IPFS content addressing** — not just blockchain.

---

## 2.4 HashCash PoW Mechanics

The sender computes a **stamp** such that:

$$ \text{Hash}(ver, date, resource, ext, rand, counter) < 2^{256 - d} $$

where $d$ is the **bit difficulty** (number of leading zero bits required).

- Probability of a random hash having $d$ leading zero bits: $P = 2^{-d}$
- Expected number of trials: $E = 2^d$
- Expected computation time: $T = 2^d \times t_h$ where $t_h$ is the time per hash

---

## 2.5 Consensus Protocol Comparison (Analytical)

| Property | PoW | PoS | PBFT | DPoS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Cost** | Very High | Low | Low | Low |
| **Throughput (TPS)** | ~7 (BTC) | ~30 (ETH) | ~1000 | ~3000 |
| **Finality** | Probabilistic | Probabilistic | Absolute | Absolute |
| **51% Attack Cost** | Hashrate Cost | Stake Cost | 1/3 Byzantine | 1/3 Delegates |
| **Sybil Resistance** | High (compute) | High (capital) | Low (permissioned) | High (stake) |
| **Byzantine Tolerance** | $< 50\%$ | $< 50\%$ | $< 33.3\%$ | $< 33.3\%$ |
| **Decentralization** | High | Medium | Low | Low |

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Block Hash Linkage** | $H_i = H(\text{Header}(B_i))$ | Cryptographic link to next block |
| **HashCash Stamp Validity** | $H(s) \bmod 2^d = 0$ | PoW stamp validity condition |
| **Expected HashCash Trials** | $E = 2^d$ | Average work to find stamp |
| **Bitcoin Difficulty** | $D = \frac{D_{max}}{T_i}$ | Retargeted every 2016 blocks |
| **Target from Difficulty** | $T = T_{max} / D$ | Numerical target for PoW |
| **Merkle Root (n=4)** | $H(H(H(t_1)\Vert H(t_2)) \Vert H(H(t_3)\Vert H(t_4)))$ | Root for 4 transactions |
| **SPV Proof Size** | $O(\log_2 n)$ | Verification complexity |

| **51% Attack Hashrate** | $h_{attacker} > 0.5 \cdot h_{total}$ | Condition to rewrite chain |

> [!NOTE] **Examination Tip (KTU)**
> When asked for the **expected number of trials** in HashCash, always state the assumption: *uniform distribution of hash outputs* and *independent trials*. This is a common 2-mark follow-up.

---

## 2.7 Why These Mechanisms Exist (Engineering Rationale)

1. **Hash Chains** — Provide **integrity & immutability** without a trusted third party. A single hash link broken invalidates the entire subsequent chain.
2. **PoW (HashCash)** — Makes Sybil attacks expensive. Creating a fake identity is free; creating a fake identity *with valid work* is computationally expensive.
3. **Merkle Trees** — Enable **lightweight verification** (SPV) without downloading the full chain — critical for mobile wallets.
4. **Consensus Protocols** — Solve the **Byzantine Generals Problem** to achieve agreement despite adversarial participants.
5. **Smart Contracts** — Replace **legal intermediaries** with deterministic code execution.

---

## 2.8 Smart Contract Architecture (Ethereum Model)

A smart contract has the following on-chain attributes:

| Attribute | Description |
|---|---|
| `address` | 160-bit unique identifier (derived from public key) |
| `bytecode` | Compiled EVM (Ethereum Virtual Machine) instructions |
| `storage` | Persistent key-value store (32-byte slots) |
| `nonce` | Counter to prevent replay attacks |
| `balance` | Wei (10⁻¹⁸ ETH) held by the contract |

**Execution model:** Every node runs the EVM deterministically; the state root after execution is included in the block.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Merkle Root Computation — Worked Example (4 Transactions)

**Given:** Four transactions $t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4$ with the following SHA-256 hashes (illustrative 8-bit values for pedagogy):

$$ H(t_1) = h_1 = \texttt{10110010} $$

$$ H(t_2) = h_2 = \texttt{01011101} $$

$$ H(t_3) = h_3 = \texttt{11100011} $$

$$ H(t_4) = h_4 = \texttt{00010111} $$

**Step 1:** Concatenate adjacent transaction hash pairs.

$$ h_{12} = h_1 \Vert h_2 = \texttt{10110010 01011101} $$

$$ h_{34} = h_3 \Vert h_4 = \texttt{11100011 00010111} $$

**Step 2:** Hash each pair to get the second level.

$$ h_{12}' = H(h_{12}) $$

$$ h_{34}' = H(h_{34}) $$

**Step 3:** Concatenate the second-level hashes.

$$ h_{1234} = h_{12}' \Vert h_{34}' $$

**Step 4:** Hash once more to get the Merkle root.

$$ \text{MerkleRoot} = H(h_{1234}) $$

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Insight**
> Note the binary tree shape — for $n$ transactions, the depth is $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ and the proof size is also $\log_2 n$ hashes. For 1024 transactions, only **10 hashes** are needed to prove inclusion — not 1024!

---

## 3.2 HashCash Stamp Derivation — Expected Work

**Problem:** A HashCash stamp uses SHA-256 and requires the hash to have $d = 20$ leading zero bits. Compute the expected number of hash trials.

**Step 1:** Identify the probability of success per trial.
Each hash output is uniformly distributed over $2^{256}$ possible values. The number of values with at least $d$ leading zero bits is $2^{256-d}$.

$$ P(\text{success per trial}) = \frac{2^{256-d}}{2^{256}} = 2^{-d} $$

**Step 2:** Recognize this as a geometric distribution.
The number of trials $X$ to first success follows a Geometric distribution with parameter $p = 2^{-d}$.

$$ E[X] = \frac{1}{p} = \frac{1}{2^{-d}} = 2^d $$

**Step 3:** Substitute $d = 20$.

$$ E[X] = 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ trials} $$

**Step 4:** Compute expected time assuming a hash rate of $r = 10^9$ hashes/second.

$$ T_{\text{expected}} = \frac{2^{20}}{10^9} = \frac{1{,}048{,}576}{10^9} \approx 1.05 \text{ milliseconds} $$

**Conclusion:** A modern CPU can compute a 20-bit HashCash stamp in about a millisecond — easy for legitimate senders, still cheap to spam at this difficulty. This is why real systems use 30+ bits.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Note for KTU Valuation**
> Always show the substitution step explicitly. Examiners award 1 mark for the formula $E[X] = 2^d$, 1 mark for the substitution, and 1 mark for the numerical answer with units.

---

## 3.3 Bitcoin Difficulty Adjustment — Step-by-Step

**Problem:** Bitcoin's protocol retargets difficulty every 2016 blocks to maintain a 10-minute average block time. Given the protocol's constant:

$$ \text{Expected time for 2016 blocks} = 2016 \times 10 \text{ min} = 20{,}160 \text{ minutes} = 2 \text{ weeks} $$

**Step 1:** Compute the actual time taken $T_{\text{actual}}$ for the last 2016 blocks.

$$ T_{\text{actual}} = t_{\text{block 2016}} - t_{\text{block 0}} $$

**Step 2:** Compute the new difficulty $D_{\text{new}}$ as a ratio.

$$ D_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{old}} \times \frac{T_{\text{expected}}}{T_{\text{actual}}} $$

**Step 3:** Apply the 4× adjustment guardrails (clamps).

$$ D_{\text{new}} = \min\left(\max\left(D_{\text{new}},\ \frac{D_{\text{old}}}{4}\right),\ 4 \times D_{\text{old}}\right) $$

**Step 4:** Compute the new target.

$$ T_{\text{new}} = \frac{T_{\text{max}}}{D_{\text{new}}} $$

where $T_{\text{max}}$ is the maximum possible target (easiest difficulty).

> [!NOTE] **Why the 4× Clamp?**
> Prevents rapid oscillations — if miners leave/join en masse, the difficulty can change by at most 4× per adjustment period. This ensures predictable monetary policy.

---

## 3.4 PBFT Consensus Phases — Detailed Steps

The Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT) algorithm operates in three phases to reach consensus among $N$ nodes tolerating up to $T = \lfloor (N-1)/3 \rfloor$ Byzantine faults.

**Phase 1: Pre-prepare**
- The primary (leader) node broadcasts a `PRE-PREPARE` message containing the proposed block $B$ with sequence number $n$ and view $v$.
- All backups log this message.

**Phase 2: Prepare**
- Each backup validates the proposal and broadcasts a `PREPARE` message to all other nodes.
- Each node waits until it has received $2T$ matching `PREPARE` messages (i.e., from a quorum).
- It then forms a `PREPARED CERTIFICATE`.

**Phase 3: Commit**
- Each node broadcasts a `COMMIT` message.
- Each node waits until it has received $2T + 1$ matching `COMMIT` messages.
- Once collected, the block is committed to the local state and a `REPLY` is sent to the client.

**Total message complexity:** $O(N^2)$ per consensus round.

**Why $N \geq 3T + 1$?**
The classical bound: to ensure that among $2T + 1$ responding nodes, at least $T + 1$ are honest (since at most $T$ can be Byzantine), we need $N - T \geq 2T + 1 \Rightarrow N \geq 3T + 1$.

---

## 3.5 Smart Contract Execution: Gas Cost Analysis

**Problem:** An Ethereum smart contract executes the following sequence:
- 1 transaction (base cost: 21,000 gas)
- 5 SSTORE operations (write to storage: 20,000 gas each)
- 3 SLOAD operations (read from storage: 2,100 gas each)
- 1 CALL to another contract (700 gas + value transfer)

Compute the total gas used and transaction fee (gas price = 50 Gwei, 1 ETH = 10⁹ Gwei).

**Step 1:** Sum base transaction cost.

$$ G_{\text{base}} = 21{,}000 \text{ gas} $$

**Step 2:** Sum storage write costs.

$$ G_{\text{SSTORE}} = 5 \times 20{,}000 = 100{,}000 \text{ gas} $$

**Step 3:** Sum storage read costs.

$$ G_{\text{SLOAD}} = 3 \times 2{,}100 = 6{,}300 \text{ gas} $$

**Step 4:** Sum contract call costs.

$$ G_{\text{CALL}} = 1 \times (700 + 9{,}000) = 9{,}700 \text{ gas} $$

**Step 5:** Total gas.

$$ G_{\text{total}} = 21{,}000 + 100{,}000 + 6{,}300 + 9{,}700 = 137{,}000 \text{ gas} $$

**Step 6:** Compute fee in Gwei.

$$ \text{Fee} = 137{,}000 \times 50 = 6{,}850{,}000 \text{ Gwei} $$

**Step 7:** Convert to ETH.

$$ \text{Fee} = \frac{6{,}850{,}000}{10^9} = 0.00685 \text{ ETH} $$

---

## 3.6 Double-Spend Attack Probability — Analytical

**Problem:** An attacker controls fraction $q = 0.3$ of the total hash rate. The honest network has $p = 0.7$. Compute the probability that the attacker can catch up after $z = 6$ confirmations.

The probability of attacker catching up is given by the **Gambler's Ruin / Binomial formula**:

$$ P_{\text{attack}} = 1 - \sum_{k=0}^{z} \binom{z+k-1}{k} \left( \frac{q}{p} \right)^k \left(1 - \frac{q}{p}\right)^z \cdot (\text{normalization}) $$

A simpler approximation for large $z$ is:

$$ P_{\text{attack}} \approx \left(\frac{q}{p}\right)^z = \left(\frac{0.3}{0.7}\right)^6 = (0.4286)^6 \approx 0.0059 $$

So for $q = 0.3$, the attack probability is $\approx 0.59\%$ after 6 confirmations.

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Takeaway**
> Each additional confirmation exponentially reduces the attack probability by a factor of $(q/p)$. This is why exchanges wait for **6 confirmations** (Bitcoin) or **12 confirmations** (Bitcoin Cash) before crediting deposits.

---

## 3.7 51% Attack Threshold — Formal Derivation

**Given:** A PoW blockchain with total hash rate $H_{\text{total}}$ and honest nodes controlling $H_{\text{honest}}$.

**Step 1:** Attacker needs to outpace the honest chain, so:

$$ H_{\text{attacker}} > H_{\text{honest}} $$

**Step 2:** Since $H_{\text{total}} = H_{\text{attacker}} + H_{\text{honest}}$:

$$ H_{\text{attacker}} > H_{\text{total}} - H_{\text{attacker}} $$

$$ 2 H_{\text{attacker}} > H_{\text{total}} $$

$$ H_{\text{attacker}} > \frac{H_{\text{total}}}{2} $$

**Step 3:** In terms of fraction:

$$ f_{\text{attacker}} > 0.5 $$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Implication**
> The "51% attack" is a **cost threshold**, not a strict integer. Practically, the attacker needs to maintain a slight edge over a long period to rewrite history — the longer the chain to rewrite, the lower the practical probability of success even with $>50\%$ hashrate.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define a Merkle tree and explain how it enables Simplified Payment Verification (SPV) in Bitcoin.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Merkle tree** is a binary tree of cryptographic hash values in which the leaves are hashes of individual transactions and each internal node is the hash of the concatenation of its two child nodes. The single root (Merkle root) is included in the block header and represents a compact cryptographic summary of all transactions in the block.

For a block containing $n$ transactions, the Merkle tree has depth $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$. A lightweight client (SPV client) that does not store the full blockchain can verify that a transaction $t_i$ is included in a block by requesting only the $\log_2 n$ sibling hashes along the path from $t_i$ to the root, then re-computing the root locally and comparing it with the published Merkle root.

This reduces storage and bandwidth from $O(n)$ to $O(\log_2 n)$ — making mobile wallets feasible.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Briefly explain the concept of a 51% attack on a Proof-of-Work blockchain. What is required for it to succeed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **51% attack** occurs when a single entity (or colluding group) controls more than 50% of the total network hash rate in a PoW blockchain. With majority hash power, the attacker can:

1. Mine blocks faster than the honest network.
2. Build a private alternate chain in secret.
3. Publish it after enough blocks are mined to override the honest chain.

Once published, network nodes adopt the longer chain as the canonical truth, allowing the attacker to **double-spend** coins (spend them on the honest chain, then erase that transaction in the rewritten chain).

The attack requires sustained hash power $> 50\%$ of the network, costing billions of dollars in hardware and electricity on major chains like Bitcoin — making it economically infeasible for large networks but viable on small altcoins.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **blockchain data structure** in detail. Discuss the role of cryptographic hash pointers in achieving immutability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **HashCash** proof-of-work system. A HashCash stamp uses SHA-256 and requires the hash output to have the first 28 bits as zero. Calculate the **expected number of hash trials** and the **expected time** to find a valid stamp, assuming a hash rate of $5 \times 10^9$ hashes/second. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Blockchain Data Structure (7 marks):**

A blockchain is an **ordered, cryptographically linked list of blocks**. Each block $B_i$ contains:

- **Block Header:**
  1. Version (4 bytes)
  2. Previous Block Hash (32 bytes) — hash of $B_{i-1}$'s header
  3. Merkle Root (32 bytes) — root hash of transaction tree
  4. Timestamp (4 bytes)
  5. Difficulty Target / nBits (4 bytes)
  6. Nonce (4 bytes)

- **Block Body:** List of transactions $t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_k$.

The blocks are linked by **cryptographic hash pointers**:

$$ H_i = \text{SHA-256}(\text{Header}(B_i)) $$

$$ \text{Header}(B_i) \text{ includes } H_{i-1} $$

**Role of hash pointers in immutability:**

If an adversary alters transaction $t_j$ in block $B_{i-1}$, the Merkle root of $B_{i-1}$ changes, hence $H_{i-1}$ changes, hence the `Previous Block Hash` field in $B_i$ becomes invalid. To re-establish consistency, the adversary must:

1. Re-mine $B_{i-1}$ (finding a new nonce satisfying the difficulty target) — requires $2^d$ hashes on average.
2. Re-mine $B_i$ (same).
3. Re-mine all subsequent blocks $B_{i+1}, B_{i+2}, \ldots, B_n$.

This requires re-doing all the PoW work for the entire suffix, which is computationally prohibitive — this is the **immutability guarantee**.

**Diagram (textual):**

```
[ B_n ] <-- H_{n-1} <-- [ B_{n-1} ] <-- H_{n-2} <-- ... <-- [ B_0 Genesis ]
  |                       |
  v                       v
Merkle Root t1..tk      Merkle Root t1..tk
```

**Immutability comes from:**
- One-way property of hash function (cannot find pre-image)
- Avalanche effect (tiny change $\Rightarrow$ totally different hash)
- PoW requirement (re-mining is expensive)
- Distributed consensus (must outpace honest nodes)

**(7 marks breakdown: 2 marks for diagram/structure, 2 marks for hash pointer concept, 2 marks for immutability reasoning, 1 mark for conclusion.)**

---

**(b) HashCash — Expected Work Calculation (7 marks):**

HashCash is a proof-of-work system where the sender must compute a stamp $s$ such that:

$$ \text{SHA-256}(s) \text{ has at least } d \text{ leading zero bits} $$

The validity condition is:

$$ \text{SHA-256}(s) \bmod 2^{d} = 0 $$

**Probability per trial:**

The hash function output is uniformly distributed over $2^{256}$ possible values. The probability that a random hash has $d$ leading zero bits is:

$$ p = \frac{2^{256-d}}{2^{256}} = 2^{-d} $$

**Expected number of trials (Geometric distribution):**

The number of trials $X$ until first success follows a Geometric distribution:

$$ E[X] = \frac{1}{p} = \frac{1}{2^{-d}} = 2^d $$

**Substituting $d = 28$:**

$$ E[X] = 2^{28} = 268{,}435{,}456 \text{ trials} $$

**Expected time with hash rate $r = 5 \times 10^9$ hashes/sec:**

$$ T_{\text{expected}} = \frac{E[X]}{r} = \frac{268{,}435{,}456}{5 \times 10^9} $$

$$ T_{\text{expected}} = 0.0537 \text{ seconds} \approx 53.7 \text{ milliseconds} $$

**Conclusion:** Even at 28-bit difficulty, a modern GPU can find a valid HashCash stamp in ~54 ms — completely feasible for a legitimate user but adds noticeable cost to a spammer sending millions of emails. **(7 marks breakdown: 2 marks for HashCash concept, 2 marks for probability formula, 2 marks for substitution and final answer with units, 1 mark for conclusion.)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** What are **consensus protocols** in blockchain? Compare **Proof of Work (PoW)**, **Proof of Stake (PoS)**, and **Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT)** with respect to energy cost, throughput, finality, and Byzantine fault tolerance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the architecture and execution model of **smart contracts** using the Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM). A smart contract execution consumes a total of **245,000 gas** and the gas price is set at **35 Gwei**. Compute the total transaction fee in ETH. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Consensus Protocols — Comparison (7 marks):**

A **consensus protocol** is a fault-tolerant algorithm that allows a distributed network of mutually distrusting nodes to agree on a single state of the ledger, even when some nodes are malicious (Byzantine).

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | PoW | PoS | PBFT |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Cost** | Very High (mining hardware + electricity) | Low (no mining) | Low (no mining) |
| **Throughput (TPS)** | ~7 (Bitcoin) | ~30 (Ethereum 2.0) | ~1000 (permissioned) |
| **Finality** | Probabilistic (needs confirmations) | Probabilistic (Casper FFG adds economic finality) | Absolute (immediate after commit) |
| **Byzantine Tolerance** | $< 50\%$ (hash rate) | $< 50\%$ (stake) | $< 33.3\%$ (formula: $N \geq 3T+1$) |
| **Sybil Resistance Mechanism** | Computational cost | Economic stake | Permissioned identity |
| **Examples** | Bitcoin, Litecoin | Ethereum 2.0, Cardano | Hyperledger Fabric |

**Detailed explanation of each:**

- **PoW (Proof of Work):** Miners compete to solve a hash puzzle (HashCash-style). First to find a valid nonce broadcasts the block. Consensus follows the **longest chain rule**. Highly secure but extremely energy-intensive.

- **PoS (Proof of Stake):** Validators are chosen to propose blocks proportional to their stake. Malicious behavior leads to **slashing** (loss of staked coins). Energy-efficient and used by Ethereum post-Merge.

- **PBFT (Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance):** Voting-based protocol operating in **3 phases** (Pre-prepare, Prepare, Commit) among a known set of validators. Tolerates up to $T$ Byzantine nodes where $N \geq 3T + 1$. Provides immediate finality; suitable for permissioned/consortium chains.

**(7 marks breakdown: 2 marks for definition, 3 marks for comparison table, 2 marks for individual explanations.)**

---

**(b) Smart Contract Architecture & Gas Calculation (7 marks):**

**Ethereum Smart Contract Architecture:**

A smart contract in Ethereum is a program that runs on the **EVM (Ethereum Virtual Machine)** — a stack-based, deterministic execution environment replicated on every full node.

**Key components:**

1. **Bytecode:** Compiled EVM instructions deployed via a special transaction whose `to` field is empty.
2. **Contract Account:** Each contract has a 160-bit address, balance (in Wei), storage (persistent key-value), and a nonce.
3. **State Trie:** Contract storage is part of a global Merkle Patricia Trie.
4. **Gas Mechanism:** Every EVM opcode has a fixed gas cost; the total gas consumed is multiplied by `gasPrice` to compute the fee.

**Execution model:**

When a user sends a transaction calling a contract function, every miner:
- Loads the contract bytecode.
- Executes it deterministically.
- Updates the state trie.
- Includes the new state root in the block.

**Computation of transaction fee:**

Given:
- Total gas consumed: $G = 245{,}000$
- Gas price: $P = 35$ Gwei

Fee in Gwei:

$$ \text{Fee}_{\text{Gwei}} = G \times P = 245{,}000 \times 35 = 8{,}575{,}000 \text{ Gwei} $$

Convert to ETH (since $1$ ETH $= 10^9$ Gwei):

$$ \text{Fee}_{\text{ETH}} = \frac{8{,}575{,}000}{10^9} = 0.008575 \text{ ETH} $$

If 1 ETH = ₹2,50,000, the fee in INR is:

$$ \text{Fee}_{\text{INR}} = 0.008575 \times 2{,}50{,}000 = ₹2{,}143.75 $$

**(7 marks breakdown: 3 marks for architecture/EVM explanation, 2 marks for gas formula, 2 marks for final numerical answer with units and currency conversion.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in This Module**
> 1. **Confusing PoW with HashCash:** HashCash is a **specific** PoW scheme (Adam Back, 1997, pre-dates Bitcoin). Bitcoin's PoW builds on HashCash's idea but adds the chain structure. Students often use these terms interchangeably — this costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Writing `$2^{256}$` when you mean `$2^{256-d}$`:** In HashCash expected-work derivations, forgetting the subtraction in the exponent gives wrong numerical answers. Always double-check the probability formula.
> 3. **Forgetting units in gas calculations:** A gas answer in plain "245000 × 35 = 8575000" without unit conversion to ETH loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing up finality types:** PoW/PoS have **probabilistic** finality; PBFT/PoA have **absolute/deterministic** finality. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 5. **Forgetting the $N \geq 3T + 1$ Byzantine tolerance formula:** Just writing "tolerates 1/3 faults" without the formal inequality loses 1 mark in 14-mark questions.
> 6. **Not drawing the Merkle tree diagram:** A textual description of Merkle trees without a diagram loses 2 marks in part (a) of any 7-mark question.
> 7. **Conflating DApps with smart contracts:** DApps = frontend + backend (smart contracts) + storage + tokens. Smart contracts are just one component. A common 2-mark conceptual error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Blockchain** = Append-only, cryptographically linked list of blocks, distributed across P2P network.
- [x] **Block** = Header (version, prev hash, merkle root, timestamp, difficulty, nonce) + Body (transactions).
- [x] **Cryptographic hash function** must be deterministic, pre-image resistant, collision resistant, with avalanche effect.
- [x] **SHA-256** produces 256-bit output; Bitcoin uses **double SHA-256**.
- [x] **Merkle tree** enables $O(\log_2 n)$ proof of inclusion (SPV).
- [x] **HashCash** (Adam Back, 1997) = sender computes a partial hash collision; valid stamp requires $d$ leading zero bits; expected trials = $2^d$.
- [x] **Consensus** = protocol for distributed agreement under adversarial conditions.
- [x] **Byzantine Generals Problem** tolerance formula: $N \geq 3T + 1$ (for PBFT).
- [x] **PoW** = miners solve hash puzzle; energy-intensive; tolerates $<50\%$ Byzantine.
- [x] **PoS** = validators selected by stake; energy-efficient; uses slashing for misbehavior.
- [x] **PBFT** = 3-phase voting (Pre-prepare, Prepare, Commit); absolute finality; tolerates $<33.3\%$ Byzantine.
- [x] **DApp** = Frontend + Smart Contracts + Decentralized Network + Token Incentive.
- [x] **Smart Contract** = Self-executing code on blockchain; immutable once deployed; runs on EVM (Ethereum).
- [x] **Gas** = Computational unit in Ethereum; $\text{Fee} = \text{gasUsed} \times \text{gasPrice}$.
- [x] **51% Attack** = Controlling $>50\%$ hashrate to rewrite history; double-spend attack vector.
- [x] **Double-spend probability** after $z$ confirmations $\approx (q/p)^z$ where $q$ = attacker hashrate fraction.
- [x] **Smart contract vulnerabilities:** Reentrancy, integer overflow, front-running, oracle manipulation.
- [x] **Network attacks:** Eclipse, Sybil, DDoS, BGP hijack.

### 🧠 Critical Equations to Memorize

| Equation | Purpose |
|---|---|
| $E[\text{trials}] = 2^d$ | HashCash expected work |
| $P_{ds} \approx (q/p)^z$ | Double-spend probability |
| $N \geq 3T + 1$ | PBFT Byzantine bound |
| $D_{new} = D_{old} \cdot T_{expected} / T_{actual}$ | Bitcoin difficulty retarget |
| $\text{Fee} = \text{gasUsed} \times \text{gasPrice}$ | Ethereum transaction fee |
| $\text{Proof Size} = O(\log_2 n)$ | Merkle SPV complexity |
| $H_i = \text{SHA-256}(\text{Header}(B_i))$ | Block hash linkage |

### 🎯 KTU Exam Strategy Tips

1. **Always draw diagrams** for blockchain, Merkle tree, and PBFT questions — they fetch 1–2 bonus marks.
2. **Show substitution steps** in numerical problems — partial marking is generous.
3. **Mention real-world examples** (Bitcoin for PoW, Ethereum for smart contracts, HashCash for email spam) to demonstrate applied understanding.
4. **Compare** when asked — examiners expect at least 3 comparison parameters in a 7-mark protocol question.
5. **End with a concluding sentence** linking theory to engineering utility (e.g., "Hence Merkle trees are essential for resource-constrained mobile wallets").

---

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — SECURE COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS (PECPT633)**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme — NEP 2020 Aligned Outcome-Based Education*

topics.title: Scalability and Privacy Challenges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT633 - SECURE COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Scalability and Privacy Challenges # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Scalability Challenges — Formal Definition In the context of secure communication protocols, **Scalability** refers to the ability of a system to maintain acceptable performance metrics (throughput, latency, storage, and computational overhead) as the number of participants, transactions, or data volume grows. A scalable protocol sustains **Throughput (TPS)**, **Latency**, and **Security guarantees** simultaneously when the network size expands from hundreds to millions of nodes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Scalability is the *practical bottleneck* of every distributed secure protocol. KTU expects students to identify *why* cryptographic security often *conflicts* with performance scaling — this is a high-frequency exam theme. ### 1.1.1 Intuitive Analogy — The Highway Analogy Imagine a single-lane village road (a small secure network) where every car (transaction) is hand-verified by a trusted guard. It works perfectly when there are 10 cars per hour. Now imagine 10,000 cars per hour — the guard becomes a bottleneck, cars pile up, and to "scale" you either: - **Widen the road** (increase block size → trade-off with decentralization), - **Add more guards** (more validators → trade-off with consensus speed), or - **Build flyovers** (Layer-2 solutions like Lightning Network / payment channels). This trade-off is famously called the **Blockchain Trilemma** (coined by Vitalik Buterin), which states that a protocol can only achieve **two of the three** properties simultaneously: | Property | Meaning | |---|---| | **Decentralization** | No single point of control | | **Security** | Resistance to attacks (51%, Sybil, etc.) | | **Scalability** | High TPS with low latency | --- ## 1.2 Privacy Challenges — Formal Definition **Privacy** in secure communication protocols refers to protecting three distinct properties: 1. **Identity Privacy** — Hiding *who* is communicating. 2. **Transaction Privacy** — Hiding *what* is being communicated (content, amount). 3. **Linkability Privacy** — Hiding the *relationship* between multiple actions of the same entity. > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition (KTU Standard):** A privacy-preserving protocol is one where, given the public ledger or transmitted packets, a computationally bounded adversary cannot determine the sender, receiver, message content, or correlate transactions with probability significantly greater than random guessing. ### 1.2.1 Intuitive Analogy — The Transparent Post-Office Picture a post office where every letter is copied onto a public bulletin board. Anyone can see *who sent what to whom and when*. This is essentially how **pseudonymous blockchains** (Bitcoin, Ethereum) work — addresses are pseudonyms, not real names, but forensic analysis can often **de-anonymize** users. Privacy challenges are the tools and techniques to make this bulletin board unreadable to outsiders while still being verifiable by everyone. --- ## 1.3 Why These Challenges Co-exist (The Coupling Problem) Scalability and privacy are *tightly coupled* because: - **More participants** → more metadata → harder privacy. - **Privacy techniques** (e.g., zero-knowledge proofs) are computationally **expensive** → hurt scalability. - **Scaling solutions** (e.g., sharding) often **partition trust**, complicating cross-shard privacy. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often treat scalability and privacy as independent. Examiners explicitly test the **coupling** — e.g., "How does sharding affect transaction privacy?" Be prepared for cross-conceptual questions. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Scalability — Core Bottleneck Analysis ### 2.1.1 The Transaction Pipeline Model Every secure distributed protocol (blockchain or otherwise) processes a transaction through these stages: $$ T_{total} = T_{prop} + T_{valid} + T_{consensus} + T_{finality} $$ Where: - $T_{prop}$ = Propagation delay (gossip protocol time) - $T_{valid}$ = Cryptographic verification time (signature, hash checks) - $T_{consensus}$ = Time to reach agreement (PoW, PoS, PBFT) - $T_{finality}$ = Time until transaction is irreversible > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Metric:** Bitcoin's $T_{total} \approx 3600\text{ s}$ (6 blocks × 10 min for probabilistic finality), giving a throughput of ~7 TPS. Visa's centralized network handles ~65,000 TPS. This **5-order-of-magnitude gap** is the scalability chasm. ### 2.2 Privacy — The Threat Model The standard adversarial model assumes a **Honest-but-Curious (HbC)** adversary who: - Follows the protocol honestly, - But passively observes all public data to infer private information. Formally, privacy is captured by **indistinguishability**: $$ \Pr[\mathcal{A}(view) = 1] \leq \frac{1}{2} + \epsilon(\lambda) $$ Where $\lambda$ is the security parameter and $\epsilon(\lambda)$ is a *negligible function*. This is the foundation of zero-knowledge privacy proofs. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Note | |---|---|---| | Throughput | $TPS = \dfrac{N_{tx}}{T_{block}}$ | Transactions per second | | Block Size Limit | $S_{block} = k \cdot s_{tx,\text{avg}}$ | Bytes; $k$ = max tx per block | | Scalability Coefficient | $S_c = \dfrac{\Delta TPS}{\Delta N_{nodes}}$ | Should remain $\geq$ constant | | Trilemma Constraint | $D \cdot Sec \cdot Scal \leq C_{max}$ | Bounded by network physics | | ZK Proof Size (zk-SNARK) | $\vert \pi \vert \approx 288$ bytes | Constant, regardless of computation | | ZK Proof Size (zk-STARK) | $\vert \pi \vert = O(\log^2 N)$ | Polylogarithmic in $N$ | | Ring Signature Size | $O(r)$ | $r$ = ring size (decoy signers) | | Communication Complexity (PBFT) | $O(n^2)$ | $n$ = number of validators | | Communication Complexity (PoW) | $O(n)$ | Gossip-based, scales linearly | | Latency (Finality) | $T_{finality} = k \cdot T_{block}$ | $k$ = confirmations needed | | Anonymity Set | $\mathcal{A}_{set} = \vert \{\text{possible senders}\} \vert$ | Larger = better privacy | | Shannon Entropy (Privacy) | $H = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Bits; higher = more private | | Storage Growth | $D(t) = D_0 + r \cdot t$ | Linear in time; $r$ = growth rate | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Rule:** Memorize the **Communication Complexity classes** for consensus protocols. PBFT's $O(n^2)$ is THE classic reason Byzantine-fault-tolerant protocols don't scale — frequently asked in 14-mark questions. --- ## 2.4 Privacy-Enhancing Technologies (PETs) — Classification | PET Class | Mechanism | Privacy Property | Scalability Cost | |---|---|---|---| | **Ring Signatures** | Mix real signature with decoys | Sender anonymity | $O(r)$ signature size | | **Stealth Addresses** | One-time derived keys | Receiver anonymity | Extra key derivation | | **Confidential Tx (CT)** | Pedersen commitments + range proofs | Amount hiding | ~10× tx size | | **zk-SNARKs** | Succinct non-interactive ZK proofs | Full privacy | Trusted setup needed | | **zk-STARKs** | Transparent setup, hash-based | Full privacy, post-quantum | Larger proofs (~45 KB) | | **Homomorphic Encryption** | Compute on encrypted data | Computation privacy | 1000×–10000× slowdown | | **Mixers / Tumblers** | Pool & redistribute funds | Unlinkability | Trust in mixer, latency | | **MIX Networks (MIXnet)** | Onion-routing for messages | Network-layer privacy | Bandwidth overhead | --- ## 2.5 Scalability Solutions — Taxonomy ### A. Layer-1 (On-Chain) Solutions 1. **Increased Block Size** — Bitcoin Cash, Bitcoin SV. 2. **Consensus Upgrade** — PoW → PoS (Ethereum's Merge), HotStuff. 3. **Sharding** — Split state into parallel chains (Ethereum 2.0, Near). 4. **DAG-Based Ledgers** — IOTA Tangle, Hashgraph, Nano. ### B. Layer-2 (Off-Chain) Solutions 1. **State Channels** — Lightning Network (Bitcoin), Raiden (Ethereum). 2. **Sidechains** — Plasma, Liquid, Polygon. 3. **Rollups** — Optimistic Rollups (Arbitrum, Optimism) and ZK-Rollups (zkSync, StarkNet). 4. **Payment Channel Networks (PCN)** — Hub-and-spoke or mesh routing. ### C. Non-Cryptographic Optimizations - **Batch verification** of signatures. - **Aggregated signatures** (BLS, Schnorr). - **State pruning** and **archival nodes** separation. - **Database sharding** in traditional distributed systems. --- ## 2.6 The Fundamental Trade-off Equation A useful conceptual formula for the coupling: $$ \frac{Privacy \cdot Scalability}{Decentralization} \leq \kappa $$ Where $\kappa$ is a protocol-specific constant. Increasing one forces a decrease in another (formally a *Pareto frontier* problem). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** This is why no production system simultaneously achieves "fully private, fully scalable, and fully decentralized." Real systems (e.g., Monero = private + decentralized but ~5 TPS; Solana = scalable + decentralized but transparent) pick different points on this frontier. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Throughput vs. Block Size Trade-off **Problem Setup:** Given a network with propagation delay $T_{prop}$, block size $B$ (bytes), and block interval $T_{block}$, derive the maximum sustainable throughput. ### Step 1 — Define the Constraint For a block to propagate to all nodes before the next block is mined, we need: $$ T_{prop}(B) \leq T_{block} $$ ### Step 2 — Propagation Delay Model Empirically (and theoretically justified by network coding bounds), propagation delay grows with block size: $$ T_{prop}(B) = T_0 + \alpha \cdot B $$ Where: - $T_0$ = fixed overhead (gossip handshake, header validation) - $\alpha$ = per-byte propagation coefficient (seconds/byte) - Typical $\alpha \approx 3 \times 10^{-9}$ s/byte (from Bitcoin measurements) ### Step 3 — Substitute $$ T_0 + \alpha \cdot B \leq T_{block} $$ Solving for $B$: $$ B \leq \frac{T_{block} - T_0}{\alpha} $$ ### Step 4 — Convert to Throughput If average transaction size is $s_{tx}$: $$ TPS_{max} = \frac{B_{max}}{s_{tx} \cdot T_{block}} = \frac{T_{block} - T_0}{\alpha \cdot s_{tx} \cdot T_{block}} $$ ### Step 5 — Apply to Bitcoin With $T_0 = 0.08$ s, $\alpha = 3 \times 10^{-9}$ s/byte, $T_{block} = 600$ s, $s_{tx} = 250$ bytes: $$ B_{max} = \frac{600 - 0.08}{3 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.99 \times 10^{11} \text{ bytes} \approx 199 \text{ GB} $$ $$ TPS_{max} = \frac{199 \times 10^9}{250 \times 600} \approx 1.33 \times 10^6 \text{ TPS (theoretical)} $$ **Conclusion:** The *protocol-defined* cap of 1 MB blocks limits Bitcoin to ~7 TPS, but the *network physics* allows far more. The constraint is **artificial policy**, not physics — a key insight for KTU essays. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Privacy via Anonymity Set Entropy **Setup:** In a ring signature scheme with ring size $r$, one real signer is hidden among $r-1$ decoys. Compute the Shannon entropy of the sender's anonymity. ### Step 1 — Probability Distribution Assuming uniform random decoy selection: $$ p_i = \frac{1}{r} \quad \text{for all } i = 1, 2, \ldots, r $$ ### Step 2 — Apply Shannon Entropy Formula $$ H = -\sum_{i=1}^{r} p_i \log_2 p_i = -\sum_{i=1}^{r} \frac{1}{r} \log_2 \frac{1}{r} = -r \cdot \frac{1}{r} \cdot (-\log_2 r) = \log_2 r $$ ### Step 3 — Interpretation For Monero (default $r = 16$): $$ H_{Monero} = \log_2 16 = 4 \text{ bits} $$ This means the adversary has only a $1/16 = 6.25\%$ chance of guessing the true signer, equivalent to a 4-bit password. ### Step 4 — Scaling Cost The signature size grows linearly: $$ \vert \sigma \vert = r \cdot \vert \sigma_{base} \vert + O(\lambda) $$ For $r = 16$ and $\vert \sigma_{base} \vert \approx 32$ bytes: $$ \vert \sigma \vert \approx 16 \times 32 + 96 = 608 \text{ bytes} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Coupling Insight:** Doubling ring size doubles privacy entropy (+1 bit) but doubles signature size, hurting scalability. This is the **mathematical essence** of the privacy-scalability trade-off. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Protocol | TPS (claimed) | Privacy | Decentralization | Trade-off Choice | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Bitcoin** | 7 | Pseudonymous (weak) | High | Security + Decentralization | | **Ethereum (L1)** | 30 | Pseudonymous | High | Smart contracts > TPS | | **Solana** | 65,000 | Transparent | Medium | TPS > Decentralization | | **Monero** | 5 | Strong (ring + CT) | High | Privacy > TPS | | **Zcash** | 30 | Optional (zk-SNARK) | High | Optional privacy, regulatory | | **Lightning (L2)** | 1,000,000+ | Inherits L1 | High | Channel liquidity needed | | **IOTA Tangle** | 1,000+ | Weak | Medium | DAG > chain | | **Hedera Hashgraph** | 10,000+ | Optional | Low (governed) | Speed > decentralization | | **Hyperledger Fabric** | 3,000+ | Configurable | Permissioned | Enterprise > openness | --- ## 3.4 ZK-Rollup Scalability Derivation **Setup:** A ZK-Rollup batches $N$ Layer-2 transactions into a single Layer-1 transaction. Compute the effective throughput gain. ### Step 1 — Baseline L1 Cost Let $C_{L1}$ = cost to post one tx on L1 (gas units, bytes, etc.). ### Step 2 — L2 Cost Components - Per-tx execution on L2: $C_{exec}$ - Proof generation (amortized): $C_{proof} / N$ - Data availability (calldata): $N \cdot C_{data}$ ### Step 3 — Effective L1 Equivalent Cost $$ C_{effective} = N \cdot C_{exec} + C_{proof} + N \cdot C_{data} $$ Since $C_{proof}$ is constant (~300K gas for a zk-SNARK) and $N \cdot C_{data}$ is on-chain: $$ C_{effective} \approx C_{proof} + N \cdot C_{data} \quad \text{(dominant for large N)} $$ ### Step 4 — Amortized Cost per Transaction $$ \bar{C} = \frac{C_{effective}}{N} = \frac{C_{proof}}{N} + C_{data} $$ As $N \to \infty$: $$ \lim_{N \to \infty} \bar{C} = C_{data} $$ **Conclusion:** The fixed proof cost $C_{proof}$ is **amortized to zero**. Throughput is bounded only by data availability bandwidth, not by computation. This is why zk-Rollups are theoretically superior to optimistic rollups for scalability. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** The amortization principle — "fixed cost divided by N goes to zero" — is the *unifying insight* across all Layer-2 scaling solutions. Examiners love this in 14-mark problems. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Blockchain Trilemma — Conceptual Map ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef core fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#e94560,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef prop fill:#0f3460,stroke:#16c79a,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#ffffff classDef trade fill:#533483,stroke:#f9b208,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#ffffff tri["Blockchain Trilemma
Pick any TWO"]:::core dec["Decentralization
No single point of control
N = 10,000+ nodes"]:::prop sec["Security
Resists 51%, Sybil, DoS
Adversary bound"]:::prop scal["Scalability
High TPS, low latency
10,000+ TPS"]:::prop tri --> dec tri --> sec tri --> scal btc["Bitcoin
Decentralized + Secure
NOT Scalable (7 TPS)"]:::trade sol["Solana
Scalable + Secure
NOT Decentralized (low nodes)"]:::trade mkr["MakerDAO / Sidechains
Decentralized + Scalable
NOT as Secure (small validator set)"]:::trade dec --> btc sec --> btc scal --> sol sec --> sol dec --> mkr scal --> mkr ``` --- ## 4.2 Layer-2 Scaling Architecture (Lightning Network Pattern) ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef l1 fill:#2c1810,stroke:#f5a623,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef l2 fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4fc3f7,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef user fill:#0d4f3c,stroke:#66bb6a,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#ffffff alice["User A
Wallet Node"]:::user bob["User B
Wallet Node"]:::user carol["User C
Wallet Node"]:::user dave["User D
Wallet Node"]:::user hub1["Hub Node X
High Liquidity"]:::user hub2["Hub Node Y
High Liquidity"]:::user chain["BITCOIN MAINNET
Layer-1 Blockchain
Settlement Layer"]:::l1 ch_ab["Payment Channel A-B
Multisig 2-of-2"]:::l2 ch_bc["Payment Channel B-C
Multisig 2-of-2"]:::l2 ch_cd["Payment Channel C-D
Multisig 2-of-2"]:::l2 ch_ax["Channel A-X
Multisig 2-of-2"]:::l2 ch_xy["Channel X-Y
Multisig 2-of-2"]:::l2 ch_yd["Channel Y-D
Multisig 2-of-2"]:::l2 alice <--> ch_ab bob <--> ch_ab bob <--> ch_bc carol <--> ch_bc carol <--> ch_cd dave <--> ch_cd alice <--> ch_ax hub1 <--> ch_ax hub1 <--> ch_xy hub2 <--> ch_xy hub2 <--> ch_yd dave <--> ch_yd ch_ab -. "On-chain
Open/Close" .-> chain ch_bc -. "On-chain
Open/Close" .-> chain ch_ax -. "On-chain
Open/Close" .-> chain ch_xy -. "On-chain
Open/Close" .-> chain ``` **Key Insight:** Alice can pay Dave *off-chain* by routing A → X → Y → D using HTLCs (Hash Time-Locked Contracts), without ever touching the mainnet. Final settlement hits the L1 chain only when channels open/close. --- ## 4.3 Privacy Technique Stack — Layered Defense Model ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef net fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#e94560,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef tx fill:#0f3460,stroke:#16c79a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef cry fill:#533483,stroke:#f9b208,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff obs["Public Adversary
Observes all data"]:::net subgraph L3["LAYER 3: Network Privacy"] tor["Tor / I2P
Onion Routing"] dandelion["Dandelion++
Stem and Fluff Phases"] vpn["VPN / MIXnet"] end subgraph L2["LAYER 2: Transaction Privacy"] ring["Ring Signatures
Hide Sender"] stealth["Stealth Addresses
Hide Receiver"] ct["Confidential Tx
Hide Amount"] end subgraph L1["LAYER 1: Cryptographic Privacy"] zksnark["zk-SNARKs
Prove without Revealing"] zkstark["zk-STARKs
Post-Quantum ZK"] he["Homomorphic Encryption
Compute on Ciphertext"] end obs --> L3 L3 --> L2 L2 --> L1 L1 --> obs tor --> ring dandelion --> ring ring --> zksnark stealth --> zksnark ct --> zkstark zksnark --> he ``` --- ## 4.4 Sharding Architecture — Horizontal Scaling Pattern ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef coord fill:#2c1810,stroke:#f5a623,stroke-width:2.5px,color:#ffffff classDef shard fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4fc3f7,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef tx fill:#0d4f3c,stroke:#66bb6a,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#ffffff coord["Beacon Chain / Coordinator
Random Shard Assignment
Cross-Shard Coordination"]:::coord s1["Shard 1
State: 0-999
Validators: 128"]:::shard s2["Shard 2
State: 1000-1999
Validators: 128"]:::shard s3["Shard 3
State: 2000-2999

State: 3000-3999
Validators: 128"]:::shard tx1["Tx: Alice pays Bob
State: 1500"]:::tx tx2["Tx: Charlie pays Dave
State: 2500"]:::tx tx3["Tx: Eve pays Frank

S2 ↔ S3"]:::tx coord --> s1 coord --> s2 coord --> s3 coord --> s4 tx1 --> s2 tx2 --> s3 tx3 --> s1 tx4 -. "Async Receipt" .-> s2 tx4 -. "Async Receipt" .-> s3 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Privacy Concern in Sharding:** When a user's state is in Shard 2, an adversary can narrow the anonymity set to *only* Shard 2's users, weakening privacy. This is called **Shard Isolation Privacy Loss** — a favorite KTU question. --- ## 4.5 Decision Flowchart: Choosing a Scalability Solution ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef q fill:#0f3460,stroke:#16c79a,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#ffffff classDef ans fill:#533483,stroke:#f9b208,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef end fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#e94560,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff start["Need to scale
secure protocol?"]:::q q1["Trustless
