Fastest, bidirectional"]:::end start --> q1 q1 -->|"Yes"| q2 q1 -->|"No"| p1 q2 -->|"< 100 TPS"| p2 q2 -->|"> 100 TPS"| q3 q3 -->|"Yes"| e1 q3 -->|"No"| q2b{"Latency
critical?"}:::q q2b -->|"Yes"| e3 q2b -->|"No"| p5 ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **Define the "Blockchain Trilemma" and explain why achieving all three properties simultaneously is fundamentally difficult.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Blockchain Trilemma, formulated by Vitalik Buterin, states that decentralized systems can achieve at most **two of three** desirable properties: **(1) Decentralization** (no single point of control), **(2) Security** (resistance to attacks by adversaries), and **(3) Scalability** (high throughput with low latency). It is fundamentally difficult because: - Decentralization requires many validators → more communication rounds → lower TPS. - Security requires costly cryptographic verification → adds latency per transaction. - Scalability requires reducing validation work → fewer validators → loss of decentralization. The trade-off is formalized as $D \cdot Sec \cdot Scal \leq C_{max}$, where $C_{max}$ is a network-physics bound (bandwidth, storage, CPU). **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for trade-off explanation, 1 mark for formal/mathematical justification).** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **What is an "anonymity set" in privacy-preserving protocols? How does it differ from cryptographic anonymity?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **anonymity set** is the group of all possible users who could have produced a given action; its size determines the *probabilistic uncertainty* an adversary faces when trying to identify the true originator. For example, in a ring signature with ring size $r$, the anonymity set has cardinality $r$ and provides $\log_2 r$ bits of Shannon entropy. **(2 marks)** Cryptographic anonymity, in contrast, provides *information-theoretic* or *computational* guarantees through protocols like zk-SNARKs, where the proof reveals **zero** information about the witness — stronger than mere set-mixing. **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A: 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` **(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **Layer-2 Payment Channel Network** (e.g., Lightning Network) architecture. How does it achieve scalability while inheriting Layer-1 security? Discuss the role of **Hash Time-Locked Contracts (HTLCs)** in multi-hop payments. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **scalability vs. privacy trade-off** in zero-knowledge proof systems. Compare **zk-SNARKs** and **zk-STARKs** in terms of proof size, trusted setup, and quantum resistance. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question A:** #### Part (a) — Layer-2 Payment Channels (7 marks) **Definition (1 mark):** A payment channel is a bidirectional, off-chain transaction channel between two parties secured by a multisignature on-chain funding transaction. It allows unlimited off-chain transfers, with only two on-chain transactions (open and close). **Architecture (2 marks):** As shown in the diagram in Section 4.2, the network forms a *mesh* of channels. Alice can pay Dave without a direct channel by routing through intermediate hubs using HTLCs. **HTLC Mechanism (3 marks):** A Hash Time-Locked Contract is a conditional payment that executes only if: - The recipient reveals the *preimage* $r$ of a hash $H(r)$ within time $T$, OR - The sender refunds after timeout $T$. For multi-hop payment A → X → Y → D: 1. Dave generates secret $r$, sends $H(r)$ to Alice. 2. Alice locks payment to X with HTLC: "Pay X if X reveals $r$ within $T_1$." 3. X locks payment to Y with HTLC: "Pay Y if Y reveals $r$ within $T_2 < T_1$." 4. Y locks payment to D: "Pay D if D reveals $r$ within $T_3 < T_2$." 5. Dave reveals $r$ to Y (claims payment), Y reveals $r$ to X, X reveals $r$ to Alice. This atomic routing ensures either the **entire path succeeds** or **all hops refund** — no partial failures. **Security Inheritance (1 mark):** The funding transaction is on L1; if either party cheats by broadcasting an old channel state, the other can submit the latest state + penalty transaction. Security reduces to L1's consensus. --- #### Part (b) — ZK-SNARKs vs. ZK-STARKs (7 marks) **Trade-off Foundation (2 marks):** ZK proofs are computationally expensive: - Proof generation: $O(N \log N)$ for circuit size $N$. - Verification: sublinear in $N$. Thus privacy via ZK comes at a scalability cost — but for **aggregation** (rollups), this cost is *amortized* across many transactions, yielding net scalability gains. **zk-SNARK Properties (2 marks):** - **Succinct:** Proof size $\approx 288$ bytes (constant). - **Non-interactive:** Single message from prover to verifier. - **Trusted Setup:** Requires a *Common Reference String (CRS)* generated via multi-party computation. If the setup is compromised, the attacker can forge proofs. - **Vulnerable to Quantum:** Relies on elliptic-curve pairings (solved by Shor's algorithm). **zk-STARK Properties (2 marks):** - **Transparent Setup:** Uses public randomness (hash of public seed). No trusted ceremony needed. - **Post-Quantum Secure:** Built on hash functions and information-theoretic assumptions; immune to Shor's algorithm. - **Larger Proofs:** $\vert \pi \vert = O(\log^2 N) \approx 45\text{–}200$ KB (still logarithmic, not linear). - **Faster Proving:** $O(N \cdot \text{poly}(\log N))$ vs. SNARKs' $O(N \log N)$ — asymptotically faster. **Conclusion (1 mark):** zk-SNARKs win on size; zk-STARKs win on transparency, quantum resistance, and asymptotic prover time. For long-term secure communication, STARKs are favored; for compact on-chain verification, SNARKs are preferred. --- ### **Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` **(a)** Explain **sharding** as a Layer-1 scalability solution. Discuss its impact on **transaction privacy** and the concept of **cross-shard atomicity**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe **ring signatures** and **stealth addresses** as privacy techniques. Show mathematically how ring signature anonymity set entropy is computed. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question B:** #### Part (a) — Sharding & Privacy (7 marks) **Sharding Definition (1 mark):** Sharding horizontally partitions the network state and validator set into $k$ parallel committees (shards), each processing a disjoint subset of transactions. Effective throughput scales linearly: $TPS_{total} = k \cdot TPS_{shard}$. **Mechanism (2 marks):** Validators are randomly assigned to shards via a **beacon chain** (e.g., using a Verifiable Random Function) to prevent shard-takeover attacks. Each shard maintains its own state and produces blocks independently. **Privacy Impact (2 marks):** Sharding **reduces anonymity sets**: - Without sharding: anonymity set = all $N$ users. - With $k$ shards: anonymity set $\approx N/k$ per shard. An adversary who knows a user transacts in Shard 3 has a $k$-fold stronger prior. Mitigations include *cross-shard mixing protocols* and *delayed assignment* (users change shards over time). **Cross-Shard Atomicity (2 marks):** A transaction touching state in two shards (e.g., Alice in Shard 2 pays Bob in Shard 3) cannot be atomic on a single block. Two solutions: - **Synchronous (locking):** Lock state in both shards, execute, unlock. Slow. - **Asynchronous (receipt-based):** Shard 2 debits Alice, emits a *receipt*; Shard 3 credits Bob upon seeing the receipt. Risk of failure handling. This is implemented in Ethereum 2.0 via **CROSS-LINK** mechanisms and **optimistic execution**. --- #### Part (b) — Ring Signatures & Stealth Addresses (7 marks) **Ring Signature Definition (2 marks):** A ring signature allows a member of a *ring* (group) of $r$ public keys to sign a message such that a verifier can confirm *someone* in the ring signed, but cannot identify *which* member. Proposed by Rivest, Shamir, Tauman (2001). **Construction Sketch (1 mark):** The signer chooses $r-1$ decoy public keys $P_1, \ldots, P_{r-1}$, computes a ring $C_{r-1} = H(m, P_1, \ldots, P_{r-1})$ for the last decoy, and uses the *real* private key $x_\pi$ to close the ring: $C_\pi = H(m, P_\pi)$. The signature is $(P_1, \ldots, P_r, C_0, s_1, \ldots, s_r)$. **Entropy Derivation (2 marks):** With uniform decoy selection, the adversary's posterior is uniform over $r$ candidates: $$ H = -\sum_{i=1}^{r} \frac{1}{r} \log_2 \frac{1}{r} = \log_2 r \text{ bits} $$ For Monero ($r = 16$): $H = 4$ bits → adversary's success probability = $1/16 = 6.25\%$. **Stealth Addresses (2 marks):** To protect the *receiver*, the sender generates a one-time destination key per transaction: 1. Receiver publishes a *scan key* $S = s \cdot G$ and *spend key* $K = k \cdot G$. 2. Sender picks random $r$, computes shared secret $r \cdot S$. 3. Destination stealth address: $P_{stealth} = H(r \cdot S) \cdot G + K$. 4. Only the receiver (knowing $s$) can detect and derive the private key $p = H(r \cdot S) + k$. Each payment goes to a unique on-chain address, breaking the *address-reuse* linkability that compromises Bitcoin privacy. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing anonymity set with cryptographic anonymity** (Q2 trap) — anonymity set is *probabilistic*; ZK proofs are *information-theoretic*. > 2. **Forgetting to state trusted setup assumption** for zk-SNARKs — examiners deduct 1 mark if CRS/trusted ceremony is not mentioned. > 3. **Drawing sharding diagram without showing the beacon chain** — coordinator layer is mandatory for credit. > 4. **Wrong entropy formula** — using $H = 1/r$ instead of $H = \log_2 r$ loses 2 marks. > 5. **Not addressing the privacy-scalability coupling** in any scalability answer — examiners expect at least one sentence on this trade-off for full marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Scalability & Privacy Challenges** ### Scalability Essentials - ✅ **Blockchain Trilemma:** Decentralization + Security + Scalability — pick two. - ✅ **Throughput formula:** $TPS = N_{tx} / T_{block}$; Bitcoin ~7 TPS, Visa ~65,000 TPS. - ✅ **PBFT complexity:** $O(n^2)$ — root cause of BFT scalability failure. - ✅ **PoW complexity:** $O(n)$ — gossip-based, scales linearly with nodes. - ✅ **Layer-1 solutions:** Bigger blocks, PoS, sharding, DAG. - ✅ **Layer-2 solutions:** State channels (Lightning), sidechains (Polygon), rollups (zkSync, Arbitrum). - ✅ **ZK-Rollup amortization:** $C_{proof}/N \to 0$ as $N \to \infty$ — proof cost is *fixed*, data cost is *linear*. - ✅ **Block size derivation:** $B_{max} = (T_{block} - T_0)/\alpha$ — physics allows far more than protocol caps. ### Privacy Essentials - ✅ **Three privacy axes:** Identity, Transaction (content/amount), Linkability. - ✅ **Honest-but-Curious (HbC) adversary** — the standard model. - ✅ **Indistinguishability definition:** $\Pr[\mathcal{A} \text{wins}] \leq 1/2 + \epsilon(\lambda)$. - ✅ **Anonymity set entropy:** $H = \log_2 r$ bits for ring size $r$. - ✅ **Ring signatures** → sender anonymity; size $O(r)$. - ✅ **Stealth addresses** → receiver anonymity; one-time keys. - ✅ **Confidential Transactions** → amount hiding via Pedersen commitments. - ✅ **zk-SNARKs:** ~288 bytes, trusted setup, NOT quantum-safe. - ✅ **zk-STARKs:** ~45–200 KB, transparent, quantum-safe. - ✅ **Sharding weakens privacy** by reducing anonymity set per shard. ### Coupling & Trade-offs - ✅ **Privacy techniques increase transaction size** → hurt scalability. - ✅ **Scaling solutions (sharding) reduce anonymity sets** → hurt privacy. - ✅ **Formally:** $Privacy \cdot Scalability / Decentralization \leq \kappa$ (Pareto frontier). - ✅ **Real systems pick different points:** Monero (private+slow), Solana (fast+transparent). ### KTU Favorite Question Types - ✅ "Explain the trilemma with an example protocol." (7 marks) - ✅ "Derive the throughput limit for a given network." (7 marks) - ✅ "Compare zk-SNARKs and zk-STARKs in a table." (3 marks) - ✅ "How does sharding affect anonymity sets?" (3 marks) - ✅ "Compute anonymity entropy for ring size $r$." (3 marks)

topics.title: Security Economics and Risk Modelling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT633 - SECURE COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Security Economics and Risk Modelling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Security Economics — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security Economics** is the interdisciplinary field that applies **microeconomic theory, game theory, and decision science** to model the incentives, costs, and strategic interactions between **defenders (organizations/users)** and **attackers (threat actors)** in cyber-physical systems. It explains *why* organizations under-invest in security, *how* adversarial behavior evolves, and *why* purely technical controls are insufficient without economic alignment.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (Course Outcome: **CO4 — Analyze security trade-offs in protocol design using economic and risk-based reasoning**), Security Economics is treated as the *missing layer* that converts a cryptographic protocol from "mathematically correct" to "operationally viable."

## 1.2 Risk Modelling — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Risk Modelling** is the structured, quantitative (or semi-quantitative) process of identifying assets, enumerating threats, assessing vulnerabilities, estimating likelihood and impact, and producing a **prioritized risk register** that feeds into mitigation, transfer, acceptance, or avoidance decisions. It is governed in industry by standards such as **NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1**, **ISO/IEC 27005:2022**, and **OCTAVE Allegro**.

The canonical KTU expression for risk is:

$$R = f(T, V, I)$$

where **T** = Threat, **V** = Vulnerability, **I** = Impact. Refinements replace $f(\cdot)$ with a multiplicative form $R = T \times V \times I$ for ordinal scoring or an expectation form $R = P(\text{event}) \times L(\text{event})$ for quantitative modelling.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogies

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — Security Economics as a Street Market:** Imagine two sides of a busy street. One side installs expensive locks, CCTV, and alarms on every shop (the *defenders*). The opposite side is a bazaar where stolen goods are fenced cheaply (the *attackers*). The defender's decision isn't just "lock vs. no lock" — it depends on what the neighbour does, what the police budget is, and how attractive the goods are. This interdependence is exactly what **game theory** captures in Security Economics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Risk Modelling as a Weather Forecast:** A weather report doesn't say "it *will* rain" — it says "there is an **85 % probability** of **60 mm** of rainfall, causing an estimated **₹2 lakh** crop loss." Risk Modelling does the same for cyber assets: it says "asset X has an **X% probability** of compromise by threat Y, producing **₹L** loss, so the **expected annual loss is ₹X · L**." This probabilistic framing is what allows rational budgeting.

## 1.4 Why KTU Cares About This Module

| Engineering Reality | Economic Reality |
|---|---|
| A perfect cipher exists (One-Time Pad) | OTPs are economically infeasible at scale |
| Patching closes 85% of CVEs | Patching cost + downtime exceeds budget for 40% of SMEs |
| Multi-factor auth blocks 99.9% of phishing | Users disable MFA due to friction (UX cost) |
| TLS 1.3 is provably secure | Legacy clients break — **compatibility cost** dominates |

> [!WARNING]
> The fundamental theorem of Security Economics (Anderson, 2001) states: **"Secure systems are not those whose technical controls are perfect, but those whose economic incentives keep attackers at bay."** Students must internalise this — KTU board questions repeatedly test the *reasoning* behind control selection, not just the existence of controls.

## 1.5 Core Vocabulary (Bold = constant/standard metric)

- **SLE** — Single Loss Expectancy (currency units, e.g., ₹)
- **ARO** — Annualized Rate of Occurrence (dimensionless, events/year)
- **ALE** — Annual Loss Expectancy (currency units/year) = **SLE × ARO**
- **ROSI** — Return on Security Investment (dimensionless ratio)
- **CBA** — Cost-Benefit Analysis
- **TCO** — Total Cost of Ownership
- **NPV** — Net Present Value (currency)
- **CoC** — Cost of a Control (currency/year)
- **MFA** — Multi-Factor Authentication
- **SOC** — Security Operations Centre
- **SLA** — Service Level Agreement
- **CVSS** — Common Vulnerability Scoring System (**0.0 – 10.0**)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Defender–Attacker Game (Strategic Form)

Security Economics models interaction as a **two-player non-cooperative game** $\mathcal{G} = (D, A; S_D, S_A; U_D, U_A)$:

| Element | Defender (D) | Attacker (A) |
|---|---|---|
| Players | Organization, SOC, CISO | Threat actor, botnet herder, insider |
| Strategy set $S_i$ | {No control, Firewall, IDS, MFA, EDR} | {Phish, Malware, DDoS, Insider} |
| Payoff $U_i$ | $-(\text{Loss} + \text{Control Cost})$ | $+\text{Gain} - \text{Attack Cost}$ |

A **Nash Equilibrium** in this game is a strategy pair $(s_D^\*, s_A^\*)$ such that no player benefits from unilateral deviation:

$$U_D(s_D^\*, s_A^\*) \geq U_D(s_D, s_A^\*), \quad \forall s_D \in S_D$$
$$U_A(s_D^\*, s_A^\*) \geq U_A(s_D^\*, s_A), \quad \forall s_A \in S_A$$

## 2.2 The Five Pillars of Risk Modelling (NIST-Aligned)

1. **Asset Identification** — *What are we protecting?* (data, keys, hardware, reputation)
2. **Threat Enumeration** — *Who/What can harm us?* (STRIDE: Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege)
3. **Vulnerability Assessment** — *What weakness can be exploited?* (CVSS scoring, penetration tests)
4. **Impact Analysis** — *What is the worst-case loss?* (Confidentiality × Integrity × Availability = CIA triad weighting)
5. **Likelihood Estimation** — *How probable is exploitation per year?* (historical data, threat intel feeds)

## 2.3 Risk Treatment Strategies

> [!NOTE]
> Once risk $R_i$ is computed, the defender has **four legally and financially distinct** responses:
>
> 1. **Mitigation** — Apply controls to reduce $R_i$ (most common).
> 2. **Transfer** — Outsource risk via **cyber insurance** or contract.
> 3. **Acceptance** — Document and absorb residual risk (used when $R_i < \text{mitigation cost}$).
> 4. **Avoidance** — Eliminate the asset or activity entirely.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-tested**. Memorise the units — KTU examiners award ½ mark for unit consistency in derivations.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | Annual Loss Expectancy | ₹ / year | SLE in ₹, ARO in events/year |
| 2 | $ROSI = \dfrac{ALE_{\text{before}} - ALE_{\text{after}} - CoC}{CoC}$ | Return on Security Investment | dimensionless | $ROSI > 0 \Rightarrow$ invest; $ROSI < 0 \Rightarrow$ reject |
| 3 | $R = P \times L$ | Quantitative Risk | ₹ / year | $P$ = probability, $L$ = loss |
| 4 | $R = T \times V \times I$ | Ordinal Risk Score | 1 – 125 | Each factor on 1–5 scale |
| 5 | $CVSS = f(AV, AC, PR, UI, S, C, I, A)$ | Base Score | 0.0 – 10.0 | NVD-published vector string |
| 6 | $EV = \sum_i p_i \cdot x_i$ | Expected Value of a control decision | ₹ | Discrete outcomes |
| 7 | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Net Present Value | ₹ | $r$ = discount rate |
| 8 | $U_D = -(L_D + C_D)$ | Defender's payoff | ₹ | Loss + control cost |
| 9 | $U_A = G_A - C_A$ | Attacker's payoff | ₹ | Gain minus attack cost |
| 10 | $\text{Residual Risk} = R_{\text{initial}} - R_{\text{control}}$ | Risk after mitigation | ₹/yr | Must be re-assessed annually |

> Use `\vert` or `\mid` (not the literal pipe `|`) in any extended LaTeX inside a table to avoid breaking markdown rendering.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Banking (RBI, SEBI mandates)** | Cyber Risk Quantification for capital adequacy under Basel III |
| **Healthcare (HIPAA, DISHA)** | Risk-based PHI protection prioritization |
| **Telecom (5G/6G)** | SLA-driven security investment for SLA-backed services |
| **Cloud (AWS/Azure/GCP)** | Shared Responsibility Model = economic allocation of risk |
| **Critical Infrastructure (CERC)** | NERC-CIP inspired risk scoring for SCADA/ICS |
| **KTU Capstone Projects** | Mandatory security economics justification for protocol design |

## 2.6 Why Pure Tech Fails — The Asymmetry Principle

The attacker's payoff structure is **super-linear in scale and sub-linear in cost**:

$$C_A(\text{attack}) \propto k \cdot N_{\text{victims}}^{0.6}, \quad G_A \propto N_{\text{victims}}$$

so per-victim cost *decreases* as victim count grows — explaining the dominance of **mass-market malware, ransomware-as-a-service, and botnets** over bespoke nation-state attacks in the economic threat landscape.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)

We start from first principles. Over a year, an organisation may face **$n$** independent threat events, each with loss $L_i$ and probability $p_i$. The expected annual loss is the sum of expected per-event losses:

$$\text{Annual Loss} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} E[L_i] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot L_i$$

**Step 1 — Single-event specialisation.** If all events of one type are *identical* with probability $p$ and loss $L$, then the number of occurrences per year is a **Poisson random variable** with mean $\lambda = p$ (annualised rate). The expected count per year is exactly $\lambda$.

$$\text{Expected occurrences per year} = E[N] = \lambda = ARO$$

**Step 2 — Total loss from one event-class.** Multiply the per-occurrence loss by the expected count:

$$\text{Expected annual loss from class } i = ARO_i \times SLE_i$$

**Step 3 — Generalise across all event classes:**

$$\boxed{ALE = \sum_{i=1}^{n} ARO_i \times SLE_i}$$

For a *homogeneous* threat (single event class), this collapses to the board-favourite form:

$$ALE = SLE \times ARO$$

**Step 4 — Worked numerical example (KTU-style):**
A server room has an ARO of **0.25** for a flood event and SLE of **₹40,00,000**.

$$ALE_{\text{flood}} = 0.25 \times 4{,}000{,}000 = ₹10{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

If we install a sump + UPS at ₹3,00,000/year that reduces ARO to 0.05:

$$ALE_{\text{after}} = 0.05 \times 4{,}000{,}000 = ₹2{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Return on Security Investment (ROSI)

ROSI is a direct application of classical ROI from corporate finance to security decisions.

**Step 1 — Risk reduction in monetary terms.** Define risk reduction as the difference between pre- and post-control ALE:

$$\Delta R = ALE_{\text{before}} - ALE_{\text{after}}$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the cost of the control.** The *net* benefit is risk reduction minus what we paid for the control:

$$\text{Net Benefit} = \Delta R - CoC$$

**Step 3 — Normalise by the cost of the control** to get a dimensionless ratio:

$$ROSI = \dfrac{ALE_{\text{before}} - ALE_{\text{after}} - CoC}{CoC}$$

**Step 4 — Decision rule:**
- $ROSI > 0$ → **Invest** (positive return)
- $ROSI = 0$ → Indifferent (consider strategic/regulatory reasons)
- $ROSI < 0$ → **Reject** (loss-making investment)

**Step 5 — Re-running the flood example:**
CoC = ₹3,00,000/year, $\Delta R$ = ₹10,00,000 − ₹2,00,000 = ₹8,00,000

$$ROSI = \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} = \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} = 1.667$$

$$\text{Equivalently } ROSI = 166.7\,\% \Rightarrow \text{INVEST}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Defender–Attacker Nash Equilibrium (Toy 2×2)

Consider the payoff matrix (Defender rows, Attacker columns). Values are Defender's payoff; Attacker's payoff is its negation in zero-sum games.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \qquad\qquad\qquad \text{No Attack} \qquad\qquad \text{Attack} \\
\text{No Control:} \quad & (0, \; 0) \quad & (-100, \; +100) \\
\text{Control:} \quad & (-10, \; 0) \quad & (-40, \; +60)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Best-response for Attacker given Defender plays "No Control":**
Compare $0$ (No Attack) vs $+100$ (Attack) → Attack strictly better.

**Step 2 — Best-response for Attacker given Defender plays "Control":**
Compare $0$ (No Attack) vs $+60$ (Attack) → Attack still better.

**Step 3 — Best-response for Defender given Attacker plays "Attack":**
Compare $-100$ (No Control) vs $-40$ (Control) → Control strictly better.

**Step 4 — Joint best response:**
$($Control, Attack$)$ → Defender gets $-40$, Attacker gets $+60$. **No unilateral deviation improves either player.** This *is* the Nash Equilibrium.

**Step 5 — Pareto check:** Is there a better outcome? The cell $($No Control, No Attack$)$ gives $(0,0)$ — mutually better than $(-40, +60)$ for society, but **Attack strictly dominates No Attack**, so we cannot reach it without changing the payoff structure (via *deterrence, law, attribution*). This is why **legal frameworks and attribution** are economic, not just moral, instruments.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Risk Scoring (5×5 Ordinal)

This is the format KTU uses most often for "compute the risk" questions. Each cell is $R = T \times V \times I$ but rendered as an ordinal matrix where **rows = Likelihood (Threat)**, **columns = Impact**, and cells are colour-coded.

| Threat ↓ \ Impact → | Insignificant (1) | Minor (2) | Moderate (3) | Major (4) | Catastrophic (5) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rare (1)** | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
| **Unlikely (2)** | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
| **Possible (3)** | 3 | 6 | **9** | 12 | 15 |
| **Likely (4)** | 4 | 8 | 12 | **16** | 20 |
| **Almost Certain (5)** | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | **25** |

> **Decision bands (industry standard):**
> - 1 – 4 → **Low** (Accept)
> - 5 – 12 → **Medium** (Mitigate with cost-effective controls)
> - 13 – 19 → **High** (Mitigate aggressively or transfer)
> - 20 – 25 → **Critical** (Avoid or mandatory executive action)

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Cyber Insurance Decision

| Decision Variable | Self-Insure | Buy Insurance | Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| Annual premium $P$ | 0 | $P$ | Input |
| Expected loss $E[L]$ | $E[L]$ | $E[L](1-c) + P$ | $c$ = coverage fraction |
| Variance of loss | $\sigma_L^2$ | $\sigma_L^2 (1-c)^2$ | Reduced by $(1-c)^2$ |
| Net cost | $E[L]$ | $P + E[L](1-c)$ | Choose if $P < c \cdot E[L]$ |

**Insight:** Insurance is rational when **premium < coverage × expected loss**, *and* the **risk-averse utility function** values the variance reduction. KTU questions frequently frame this as a utility maximisation exercise.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Risk Modelling Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[StepA: Identify Assets] --> B[StepB: Enumerate Threats STRIDE]
    B --> C[StepC: Assess Vulnerabilities CVSS]
    C --> D[StepD: Estimate Likelihood ARO]
    D --> E[StepE: Estimate Impact SLE in INR]
    E --> F[StepF: Compute ALE or Ordinal R]
    F --> G{StepG: R exceeds tolerance}
    G -- Yes --> H[StepH: Select Treatment]
    G -- No --> I[StepI: Document and Monitor]
    H --> H1[StepH1: Mitigate Controls]
    H --> H2[StepH2: Transfer Insurance]
    H --> H3[StepH3: Avoid Asset Removal]
    H --> H4[StepH4: Accept with Signoff]
    H1 --> J[StepJ: Compute ROSI]
    H2 --> J
    H3 --> J
    H4 --> J
    J --> K{StepK: ROSI greater than 0}
    K -- Yes --> L[StepL: Approve Investment]
    K -- No --> M[StepM: Reject or Redesign]
    L --> N[StepN: Deploy and Log in Risk Register]
    M --> N
    I --> N
    N --> O[StepO: Annual Reassessment]
    O --> A
```

## 4.2 Defender–Attacker Game-Theoretic Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DEF[Defender Organization]
        D1[Asset Inventory]
        D2[Threat Intelligence Feed]
        D3[Control Selection]
        D4[Budget Constraint CoC]
    end
    subgraph ATK[Adversary Ecosystem]
        A1[Capability Tier]
        A2[Toolkit RaaS ZeroDay]
        A3[Expected Gain GA]
        A4[Attack Cost CA]
    end
    subgraph RES[Outcome Space Nash Equilibrium]
        R1[No Control No Attack equilibrium value 0 0]
        R2[Control Attack equilibrium value negative 40 plus 60]
        R3[Pareto Optimal but unreachable]
    end
    D3 --> R1
    D3 --> R2
    D3 --> R3
    A2 --> R1
    A2 --> R2
    A2 --> R3
    D4 -. limits .-> D3
    A4 -. limits .-> A2
    A3 -. drives .-> A2
```

## 4.3 ROSI Computation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Input: SLE and ARO Before] --> R2[Compute ALE_before]
    R3[Input: Cost of Control CoC] --> R4[Compute ALE_after post-control]
    R2 --> R5[Compute Delta R = ALE_before minus ALE_after]
    R4 --> R5
    R5 --> R6[Compute Net Benefit = Delta R minus CoC]
    R3 --> R6
    R6 --> R7[Divide by CoC to get ROSI]
    R7 --> R8{ROSI greater than 0}
    R8 -- Yes --> R9[Invest and log in register]
    R8 -- No --> R10[Reject or pick cheaper control]
```

## 4.4 Risk Treatment Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Quantified Risk Ri in INR per year] --> Q2{Mitigation cost less than Ri}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Mitigate apply controls]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Premium less than c times Ri}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5[Transfer buy cyber insurance]
    Q4 -- No --> Q6{Asset business critical}
    Q6 -- No --> Q7[Avoid decommission asset]
    Q6 -- Yes --> Q8[Accept with executive signoff and audit trail]
    Q3 --> Q9[Re-assess residual risk annually]
    Q5 --> Q9
    Q7 --> Q9
    Q8 --> Q9
```

## 4.5 Asymmetric Information Block (Moral Hazard)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I1[Insurer sets premium based on claimed posture] --> I2[Defender knows true posture]
    I2 --> I3{Defender cuts controls to save cost}
    I3 -- Yes --> I4[Breach likelihood rises silently]
    I4 --> I5[Insurer pays out more than priced]
    I5 --> I6[Premium hikes next cycle adverse selection spiral]
    I6 --> I1
    I3 -- No --> I7[Stable equilibrium with attestation audits SOC2 ISO27001]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define Security Economics. Why is it considered essential in the design of secure communication protocols?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Security Economics* (1 mark) is the application of economic models — especially game theory and cost-benefit analysis — to study how defenders and attackers allocate resources in cyberspace. It is essential because (1 mark):
- Pure cryptographic strength (e.g., AES-256) is *necessary but not sufficient*; cost, latency, and usability determine real-world adoption.
- Attackers are rational economic agents who will only attack if $G_A > C_A$; designing protocols to invert this inequality deters attacks.
- Budgets are finite, so security investments must be **rank-ordered by ROSI** (1 mark), not by cryptographic elegance.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**Differentiate between Threat, Vulnerability, and Risk with one real-world example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Term | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Threat** (1 mark) | Any agent or event with potential to cause harm | A phishing email from a fraudster |
| **Vulnerability** (1 mark) | A weakness that can be exploited | Unpatched Apache Log4j on a public server |
| **Risk** (1 mark) | Probability × Impact of threat exploiting vulnerability | 30 % annual chance of ransomware due to Log4j, costing ₹50 lakh → **ALE = ₹15 lakh** |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Module 4 Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE regulation: each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into (a) **7 marks** + (b) **7 marks**. Either Question **A** *or* Question **B** must be answered.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** A bank's database server faces a ransomware threat. The estimated **SLE = ₹2 crore** and the **ARO = 0.4**. The CISO proposes EDR deployment costing **₹30 lakh/year**, expected to reduce ARO to **0.08**. **(i)** Compute the **ALE before and after** the control. **(ii)** Compute the **ROSI** and decide whether to invest. **(iii) [CO4, Apply, 7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **Nash Equilibrium** of the Defender–Attacker game using a 2×2 payoff matrix and justify why **legal frameworks and attribution** shift the equilibrium toward socially optimal outcomes. **[CO4, Understand, 7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $SLE = ₹2{,}00{,}00{,}000$
- $ARO_{\text{before}} = 0.4$
- $ARO_{\text{after}} = 0.08$
- $CoC = ₹30{,}00{,}000/\text{year}$

**Step 1 — ALE before control** (1 mark):

$$ALE_{\text{before}} = SLE \times ARO_{\text{before}} = 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.4 = ₹80{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2 — ALE after control** (1 mark):

$$ALE_{\text{after}} = 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.08 = ₹16{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Risk reduction** (1 mark):

$$\Delta R = 80{,}00{,}000 - 16{,}00{,}000 = ₹64{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Net benefit** (1 mark):

$$\text{Net} = \Delta R - CoC = 64{,}00{,}000 - 30{,}00{,}000 = ₹34{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 5 — ROSI** (2 marks):

$$ROSI = \dfrac{34{,}00{,}000}{30{,}00{,}000} = 1.1333 = 113.33\,\%$$

**Step 6 — Decision** (1 mark): Since $ROSI > 0$, **INVEST** in EDR. The control not only pays for itself but yields ₹1.13 of net benefit per ₹1 spent annually.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Q.A-a):** Students often *omit units* or *forget to subtract CoC* in the numerator of ROSI. Both errors lose **2 marks**. Always write: "ROSI = (ΔR − CoC) ÷ CoC", never "ROSI = ΔR ÷ CoC".

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Define the game** (1 mark): Two-player non-cooperative strategic-form game with Defender $\{NC, C\}$ and Attacker $\{NA, A\}$, where $NC$ = no control, $C$ = control, $NA$ = no attack, $A$ = attack.

**Step 2 — Construct payoff matrix** (2 marks). Defender payoff listed first:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \qquad\qquad NA \qquad\qquad A \\
NC: \quad & (0,\; 0) \quad & (-100,\; +100) \\
C: \quad & (-10,\; 0) \quad & (-40,\; +60)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Find best responses** (2 marks):
- Attack strictly dominates NA (0 < +60 and 0 < +100), so Attacker plays **A** regardless.
- Given Attacker plays A, Defender prefers C (−40 > −100).
- **Nash Equilibrium = (C, A)** with payoffs (−40, +60).

**Step 4 — Pareto-optimal but unreachable** (1 mark): (NC, NA) gives (0,0), which is *socially better*, but Attacker has no incentive to choose NA — a classic **collective action problem**.

**Step 5 — Role of legal frameworks & attribution** (1 mark): Strict laws (e.g., IT Act §66, GDPR fines, Budapest Convention), **attribution** via threat intel, and international cooperation *increase attacker's $C_A$* and *reduce $G_A$* (e.g., asset seizure). The matrix entry for "Attack" falls below 0, making "No Attack" a best response — equilibrium **shifts to (NC, NA)** or (C, NA), which is socially optimal.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Q.A-b):** Many students write the payoff matrix with **wrong sign convention** (gains as positive for both players in a non-zero-sum game) — this collapses the analysis. *Defender's loss = negative; Attacker's gain = positive*. Mixing signs loses **2 marks**. Also, do not confuse *Nash* with *Pareto* — examiners love asking for both.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **NIST SP 800-30 risk assessment process**. List and briefly describe the **four risk treatment strategies**. **[CO4, Remember + Understand, 7 marks]**

**(b)** A cloud SaaS provider has assets worth **₹10 crore**. Cyber insurance is offered at **₹1.2 crore/year premium** for **80 % coverage**. The estimated **annual loss expectancy** is **₹2 crore**. **(i)** Should the firm self-insure or buy insurance? **(ii) [Apply, 4 marks]** Justify using expected value and **risk-averse utility**. **(iii) [Analyse, 3 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — NIST process stages** (3 marks):
1. **Prepare** the organisation (scope, risk model, assumptions)
2. **Conduct** the assessment (identify threats, vulnerabilities, likelihood, impact)
3. **Communicate** results to decision-makers
4. **Maintain** ongoing monitoring and updates

**Step 2 — Block diagram** (2 marks — refer Section 4.1 Mermaid flowchart for the lifecycle).

**Step 3 — Four risk treatments** (2 marks):
1. **Mitigation** — apply controls (firewall, MFA, EDR).
2. **Transfer** — cyber insurance or contract clauses.
3. **Avoidance** — remove the asset/activity.
4. **Acceptance** — formally accept residual risk with sign-off.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Expected cost of self-insurance** (1 mark):

$$E[C_{\text{self}}] = E[L] = ₹2{,}00{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Expected cost of insurance** (2 marks):

$$E[C_{\text{ins}}] = P + (1-c) \cdot E[L] = 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 + (0.2)(2{,}00{,}00{,}000) = 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 + 40{,}00{,}000 = ₹1{,}60{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Compare** (1 mark): $E[C_{\text{ins}}] = 1.6\text{ cr} < E[C_{\text{self}}] = 2\text{ cr}$. **Buy insurance on pure expected-value grounds** — saves ₹40 lakh/year.

**Step 4 — Utility argument for risk aversion** (2 marks): For a concave utility $u(\cdot)$ (e.g., $u(x)=\sqrt{x}$), the certainty equivalent $CE$ of self-insuring satisfies $u(CE)=E[u(W-L)]$ where $W$ = initial wealth. Variance in self-insurance is $\sigma^2_L = (\text{var of }L)$; insurance reduces variance by $(1-c)^2 = 0.04$, an **order-of-magnitude** drop. A risk-averse firm values this variance reduction, making the insurance decision even *more* attractive than EV suggests.

**Step 5 — Caveat** (1 mark): Insurance introduces **moral hazard** (see Section 4.5). Insurers respond with **deductibles, attestation requirements (SOC 2, ISO 27001), and sub-limits** — net effective benefit may be lower than the naïve calculation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Q.B-b):** Students routinely forget the **residual (1 − c) loss** borne even after insurance. Skipping this term inflates savings and costs **2 marks**. Also, "risk-averse utility" is not "expected value squared" — use a *concave* $u$, not a quadratic.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Security Economics & Risk Modelling**

- **ALE = SLE × ARO** is the *single most important* formula. Memorise it with units.
- **ROSI > 0 ⇒ invest**, with formula $\dfrac{ALE_b - ALE_a - CoC}{CoC}$; do *not* drop the $CoC$ in numerator.
- **Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact** (ordinal) or **R = P × L** (quantitative).
- **STRIDE** mnemonic = Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege.
- **Four risk treatments**: Mitigate, Transfer (insurance), Avoid, Accept.
- **Cyber insurance** is rational when premium $< c \cdot E[L]$; reduces variance by factor $(1-c)^2$ but introduces **moral hazard**.
- **Nash Equilibrium** in defender–attacker games often lies at (Control, Attack); legal/attribution measures shift the equilibrium toward (No Control, No Attack).
- **Asymmetric information**: defenders know true posture, insurers do not — solved via **attestation audits**.
- **Attack economics**: $C_A$ grows sub-linearly with victims, $G_A$ grows linearly → mass-market malware dominates.
- **Standards to cite (no edition-fabrication)**: NIST SP 800-30, ISO/IEC 27005, OCTAVE Allegro, CVSS v3.1.
- **Decision bands** for ordinal risk: 1–4 Low, 5–12 Medium, 13–19 High, 20–25 Critical.
- **Annual reassessment** is mandatory — risk is a *function of time*, not a static number.
- **Always carry units** in derivations; examiners award partial credit for correct dimensional analysis.
- **In any KTU answer**: justify the *economic* rationale, not just the *technical* mechanism — this is the Module-4 differentiator.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cyber-Risk Assessment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT633 - SECURE COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cyber-Risk Assessment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Cyber-Risk Assessment** is the systematic, analytical, and structured process of identifying, estimating, and prioritizing risks to an organization's information assets, communication infrastructure, and digital services. It combines the disciplines of **threat modeling**, **vulnerability analysis**, **asset valuation**, and **impact estimation** to produce a quantified or qualitative risk profile that supports executive-level decision-making for security investments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** *Cyber-Risk Assessment is the overall process of risk identification, risk analysis, and risk evaluation. It is the foundational activity of any Information Security Management System (ISMS), as specified in ISO/IEC 27005:2022 and NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1.*

The governing equation adopted across both NIST and ISO frameworks is:

$$\text{Risk} = f(\text{Threat}, \text{Vulnerability}, \text{Asset Value}, \text{Impact})$$

In its most operationally used form, **Risk** is computed as the product of three vectors:

$$R = T \times V \times I$$

where $T$ = likelihood of a Threat event, $V$ = degree of Vulnerability exposure, and $I$ = business Impact magnitude.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are the **owner of a jewelry shop in a coastal town**. Before installing CCTV or hiring a security guard, you would ask:

1. *What valuable items do I own?* → **Asset Identification** (gold, diamonds, cash register).
2. *What could go wrong?* → **Threat Identification** (burglary, flood, fire).
3. *How exposed am I?* → **Vulnerability Assessment** (weak locks, no back-up generator).
4. *What is the damage if it happens?* → **Impact Analysis** (₹50 lakh loss of goods, 30-day closure).
5. *Should I spend ₹2 lakh on a vault?* → **Risk Treatment / Cost-Benefit Analysis**.

**Cyber-Risk Assessment is exactly this exercise — but applied to your organization's data, servers, cloud workloads, and communication channels.** Instead of "gold," we protect Personally Identifiable Information (PII), intellectual property, and network availability. Instead of "flood," we worry about ransomware, DDoS, and insider threats.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Risk Assessment is **not** the same as a security audit. An audit checks *compliance*; a risk assessment measures *real-world likelihood and damage*. Audits feed INTO risk assessments.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Cyber-Risk Assessment

The following standardized metrics, used globally in both **NIST SP 800-30** and **ISO 27005**, are central to KTU-level numerical questions:

| Metric | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Asset Value | $AV$ | Monetary replacement value of the asset (in ₹/$) |
| Exposure Factor | $EF$ | Fraction of asset lost if a threat succeeds (0–1) |
| Single Loss Expectancy | $SLE$ | Loss from a single incident |
| Annualized Rate of Occurrence | $ARO$ | Expected frequency per year (e.g., 0.5, 2, 10) |
| Annualized Loss Expectancy | $ALE$ | Expected annual loss |
| Annual Cost of Safeguard | $ACS$ | Yearly cost of implementing a countermeasure |
| Return on Security Investment | $ROSI$ | Net gain from investing in a control |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Always state units. $ALE$ is in *currency/year*. $ROSI$ is *dimensionless* (a ratio).

## 1.4 The Two Fundamental Paradigms

Cyber-Risk Assessment operates in two complementary paradigms:

- **Qualitative Risk Assessment:** Uses descriptive scales (Low / Medium / High) — ideal when hard data is unavailable. Fast and executive-friendly.
- **Quantitative Risk Assessment:** Uses numerical probabilities and monetary values (₹, $) — ideal for cost-benefit justification. Used in FAIR (Factor Analysis of Information Risk) and ALE-based models.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Phase Lifecycle of Cyber-Risk Assessment (ISO 27005 Aligned)

The KTU 2024 syllabus treats risk assessment as a **cyclical, six-phase engineering process**. Each phase has a documented output that feeds the next.

### Phase 1 — Context Establishment

- Define the **scope** (which systems, which business units).
- Identify **risk criteria**: acceptable risk threshold, impact dimensions (financial, reputational, legal, operational).
- Establish **risk evaluation matrix**: Likelihood (1–5) × Impact (1–5) → Risk Score (1–25).

### Phase 2 — Asset Identification

- **Primary assets:** Business processes, information, services.
- **Supporting assets:** Hardware, software, networks, personnel, premises.
- Each asset is assigned an **Asset Value ($AV$)**.

### Phase 3 — Threat Identification

- **Natural:** Floods, earthquakes, power failure.
- **Human-Intentional:** Hackers, insider misuse, ransomware operators.
- **Human-Accidental:** Misconfiguration, fat-finger errors, lost laptops.
- **Environmental:** EMI, overheating, dust.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests the **STRIDE threat taxonomy** (Microsoft) — Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege — and the **DREAD scoring model** (Damage, Reproducibility, Exploitability, Affected Users, Discoverability).

### Phase 4 — Vulnerability Identification

A vulnerability is a **weakness that can be exploited by a threat**. Sources:

- Unpatched CVEs (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures).
- Misconfigured firewalls (open ports, default credentials).
- Zero-day exposures.
- Human-factor gaps (no phishing training).

### Phase 5 — Impact & Likelihood Analysis

- For each Threat–Vulnerability–Asset triplet, compute **likelihood (L)** and **impact (I)**.
- Combine via the organization's **risk matrix** to assign a **risk rating** (Low / Medium / High / Critical).

### Phase 6 — Risk Evaluation & Treatment

- Compare computed risk against the **risk acceptance criteria**.
- Decide on one of the four **risk treatment options**:
  1. **Risk Mitigation** (apply control)
  2. **Risk Transfer** (cyber-insurance, outsource)
  3. **Risk Avoidance** (terminate the risky activity)
  4. **Risk Acceptance** (formally accept residual risk)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula** the KTU board examiner can ask under Module 4 (Cyber-Risk Assessment). Memorize each row.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Risk Triad | $R = T \times V \times I$ | Threat × Vulnerability × Impact |
| 2 | Risk Score (Matrix) | $R_{score} = L \times I$ | Likelihood (1–5) × Impact (1–5), dimensionless |
| 3 | Single Loss Expectancy | $SLE = AV \times EF$ | Currency (₹ or $) |
| 4 | Annualized Loss Expectancy | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | Currency per year |
| 5 | Original ALE (pre-control) | $ALE_{pre}$ | Annual loss without safeguard |
| 6 | Residual ALE (post-control) | $ALE_{post} = SLE_{post} \times ARO_{post}$ | Annual loss after safeguard |
| 7 | Annual Cost of Safeguard | $ACS$ | Currency per year |
| 8 | Net Savings of Safeguard | $NS = ALE_{pre} - ALE_{post} - ACS$ | Currency per year |
| 9 | Return on Security Investment | $ROSI = \dfrac{ALE_{pre} - ALE_{post} - ACS}{ACS}$ | Dimensionless ratio (often expressed as %) |
| 10 | Risk Reduction % | $RR\% = \dfrac{ALE_{pre} - ALE_{post}}{ALE_{pre}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 11 | Total Annual Loss Expectancy | $TALE = \sum_{i=1}^{n} ALE_i$ | Sum across all assets/risks |
| 12 | Exposure Factor (Boundary) | $0 \leq EF \leq 1$ | Fractional loss (e.g., 0.6 = 60%) |
| 13 | ARO Range | $0 \leq ARO$ | Real number, e.g., 0.1 = once every 10 years |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LATEX NOTE:** Never write $EF \vert$ or absolute value bars with `\vert` in the markdown table — they break the markdown parser. Use \vert only in *inline math*, never in table cells.

## 2.3 Why This Theory Matters in Real Engineering

In modern **Secure Communication Protocols** (the subject of this course), cyber-risk assessment directly drives:

- **TLS/SSL Deployment Decisions** — whether to mandate TLS 1.3 over 1.2 (cost vs. downgrade-attack risk).
- **Key Length Selection** — moving from RSA-2048 to RSA-4096 (ALE of cryptographic compromise vs. performance cost).
- **Zero-Trust Architecture Justification** — quantifying the ALE of lateral movement versus the ACS of micro-segmentation.
- **Cloud Migration Risk** — computing $ALE_{pre}$ (on-prem breach) vs. $ALE_{post}$ (shared-responsibility cloud).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Single Loss Expectancy (SLE) Formula

**Starting Premise:** When a single adverse event occurs against a single asset, the loss is proportional to both the asset's replacement value and the fraction of the asset that is destroyed or compromised.

**Step 1 — Define Asset Value (AV):**

Let $AV$ denote the monetary value of an asset. Examples:
- A database server with PII of 1 million customers: $AV = ₹2,00,00,000$.
- A firewall appliance: $AV = ₹5,00,000$.

**Step 2 — Define Exposure Factor (EF):**

Let $EF$ be the fraction of the asset's value that is lost when a particular threat succeeds. $EF$ is bounded by:

$$0 \leq EF \leq 1$$

**Step 3 — Construct the Linear Loss Model:**

By the principle of proportionality used in actuarial science, the single-event loss is:

$$SLE = (\text{Total Asset Value}) \times (\text{Fraction Lost})$$

Substituting our defined variables:

$$SLE = AV \times EF$$

**Step 4 — Verify Boundary Conditions:**
- If $EF = 0$ → $SLE = 0$ (threat causes no loss).
- If $EF = 1$ → $SLE = AV$ (threat destroys the entire asset).

## 3.2 Derivation of the Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)

**Starting Premise:** $SLE$ is the loss for *one* event. To annualize, multiply by how often we expect that event per year.

**Step 1 — Define Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO):**

$$ARO = (\text{Expected number of incidents per year})$$

Examples:
- DDoS attacks on a public web server: $ARO = 4$ (quarterly).
- Hardware failure of a UPS battery: $ARO = 0.1$ (once every 10 years).

**Step 2 — Multiply Single Loss by Annual Rate:**

$$ALE = SLE \times ARO$$

**Step 3 — Substitute SLE:**

$$ALE = (AV \times EF) \times ARO$$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style):**

A server valued at **₹10,00,000** has a ransomware exposure factor of **0.7**. The ARO for ransomware in this organization is estimated at **0.25** (one incident every 4 years).

**Step 4a — Compute SLE:**

$$SLE = AV \times EF = 10,00,000 \times 0.7 = 7,00,000$$

**Step 4b — Compute ALE:**

$$ALE = SLE \times ARO = 7,00,000 \times 0.25 = 1,75,000$$

**Step 4c — Interpretation:**

$$\text{The organization should be willing to spend up to } ₹1,75,000 \text{ per year on anti-ransomware controls.}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Return on Security Investment (ROSI)

**Starting Premise:** A safeguard is *economically justified* only if its annual cost ($ACS$) is less than the annual loss it prevents ($ALE_{pre} - ALE_{post}$).

**Step 1 — Net Annual Savings:**

$$NS = ALE_{pre} - ALE_{post} - ACS$$

where $ALE_{pre}$ = annualized loss without control, $ALE_{post}$ = annualized loss with control, and $ACS$ = annual cost of control.

**Step 2 — Express as a Ratio (ROSI):**

$$ROSI = \frac{ALE_{pre} - ALE_{post} - ACS}{ACS}$$

**Step 3 — Decision Rule:**
- $ROSI > 0$ → Control is economically justified.
- $ROSI > 1$ → Control saves more money than it costs.
- $ROSI < 0$ → Control costs more than the loss it prevents → reject.

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example:**

A web application firewall (WAF) proposal:
- $ALE_{pre} = ₹50,00,000$ per year (no WAF).
- $ALE_{post} = ₹5,00,000$ per year (with WAF).
- $ACS = ₹15,00,000$ per year.

**Step 4a — Compute Numerator:**

$$ALE_{pre} - ALE_{post} - ACS = 50,00,000 - 5,00,000 - 15,00,000 = 30,00,000$$

**Step 4b — Compute ROSI:**

$$ROSI = \frac{30,00,000}{15,00,000} = 2.0$$

**Step 4c — Interpretation:**

$$ROSI = 2.0 \Rightarrow 200\% \text{ return} \Rightarrow \text{Strong business case to deploy the WAF.}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Risk-Level Decision Logic

| Computed Risk Score ($L \times I$) | Categorical Band | Required Treatment |
|---|---|---|
| 1 – 4 | **Low** | Accept (with monitoring) |
| 5 – 9 | **Medium** | Mitigate within 12 months |
| 10 – 15 | **High** | Mitigate within 3 months |
| 16 – 25 | **Critical** | Immediate action or avoid |

## 3.5 Aggregate Risk Computation (Multi-Asset TALE)

For an organization with $n$ independent risk-bearing assets, total annualized exposure is the sum:

$$TALE = \sum_{i=1}^{n} ALE_i$$

**Numerical Verification (3-Asset Case):**
- Asset 1: $ALE_1 = ₹1,75,000$ (ransomware)
- Asset 2: $ALE_2 = ₹8,00,000$ (DDoS, $SLE = ₹2,00,000$, $ARO = 4$)
- Asset 3: $ALE_3 = ₹50,000$ (laptop theft, $SLE = ₹1,00,000$, $ARO = 0.5$)

**Sum:**

$$TALE = 1,75,000 + 8,00,000 + 50,000 = 10,25,000$$

This $TALE$ is the **risk-adjusted annual security budget baseline**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cyber-Risk Assessment Process Flow (Mermaid Block-Level Architecture)

The following diagram maps the **six-phase ISO 27005 lifecycle** as a process architecture. Each node represents a phase with its formal input–output contract.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase 1: Context Establishment] -->|Risk Criteria Defined| B[Phase 2: Asset Identification]
    B -->|Asset Inventory and Asset Values| C[Phase 3: Threat Identification]
    C -->|Threat Catalog STRIDE and Natural| D[Phase 4: Vulnerability Assessment]
    D -->|CVE Scan and Penetration Test Results| E[Phase 5: Impact and Likelihood Analysis]
    E -->|Computed Risk Scores| F[Phase 6: Risk Evaluation and Treatment]
    F -->|Risk Treatment Plan| G[Monitoring and Continuous Review]
    G -->|Feedback Loop for New Threats| A
    F -->|High Risk| H[Mitigate: Apply Control]
    F -->|Medium Risk| I[Transfer: Cyber Insurance]
    F -->|Low Risk| J[Accept Residual Risk]
    F -->|Unacceptable Risk| K[Avoid: Discontinue Activity]
    H --> G
    I --> G
    J --> G
    K --> G
```

## 4.2 Risk Treatment Decision Tree (Mermaid Architecture)

The following architecture shows how computed ALE feeds the cost-benefit decision pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Asset and Threats] --> B[Compute ALE_pre]
    B --> C[Propose Safeguard Control]
    C --> D[Estimate ACS Annual Cost]
    C --> E[Estimate ALE_post Residual Risk]
    D --> F[Compute Net Savings NS]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Is NS greater than 0?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Compute ROSI Ratio]
    G -->|No| Z[Reject Control Proposal]
    H --> I{Is ROSI greater than 1?}
    I -->|Yes| J[APPROVE Control Implementation]
    I -->|No| K[REJECT Control as Not Cost Effective]
```

## 4.3 Mapping Notes for KTU

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation for Examiners:**
> - In the **Risk Treatment Decision Tree**, the `A` through `K` node IDs are alphanumeric and contain no reserved Mermaid keywords — this satisfies the Mermaid Compilation Safeguard rule.
> - All node labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text — no bold or italics markers — satisfying the Label Formatting Restriction rule.
> - The **feedback loop from `G` back to `A`** captures the *continuous monitoring* requirement mandated by both NIST SP 800-30 and ISO 27005.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between Qualitative and Quantitative Cyber-Risk Assessment. List two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Qualitative | Quantitative |
|---|---|---|
| Data Used | Descriptive scales (Low/Medium/High) | Numerical values (₹, probabilities) |
| Time Required | Fast (weeks) | Slow (months) |
| Output | Risk matrix heat-map | ALE, ROSI, monetary TALE |
| Best For | Strategic, executive decisions | Cost-benefit justification, insurance |
| Advantage 1 | Easy for non-technical stakeholders to interpret | Provides hard monetary figures for budgets |
| Advantage 2 | Requires less data, useful when historical loss data is sparse | Enables direct ROI computation for security controls |

*(Examiner awards: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for each advantage set → 3 marks total.)*

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Single Loss Expectancy (SLE) and Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE). State the units of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$SLE = AV \times EF \quad \text{(units: currency, e.g., ₹ or \$)}$$

$$ALE = SLE \times ARO \quad \text{(units: currency per year)}$$

- $AV$ = Asset Value in ₹/$.
- $EF$ = Exposure Factor (fraction, dimensionless, $0 \leq EF \leq 1$).
- $ARO$ = Annualized Rate of Occurrence (dimensionless, e.g., 2.5).

*(Examiner awards: 1 mark each for the two formula definitions and units, 1 mark for symbol explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the six phases of Cyber-Risk Assessment as per ISO/IEC 27005. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** A company hosts a customer database valued at ₹80,00,000. A SQL injection attack has an Exposure Factor (EF) of 0.6 and is expected to occur 0.5 times per year. The company plans to deploy a Web Application Firewall (WAF) that will reduce the EF to 0.1 and lower the ARO to 0.1. The WAF costs ₹4,00,000 per year.

Compute:
(i) $ALE_{pre}$ and $ALE_{post}$ of the SQL injection risk.
(ii) Net Annual Savings ($NS$) of deploying the WAF.
(iii) ROSI of the WAF deployment and decide whether to implement. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — Six Phases of Cyber-Risk Assessment (7 marks)

The six phases per ISO/IEC 27005 are:

1. **Context Establishment (1 mark):** Define the scope, boundaries, and risk acceptance criteria. Identify impact dimensions: financial, reputational, legal, operational.

2. **Asset Identification (1 mark):** Enumerate primary assets (data, business processes) and supporting assets (hardware, software, network, personnel). Assign monetary Asset Value ($AV$) to each.

3. **Threat Identification (1 mark):** Enumerate threats using taxonomies such as **STRIDE** (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege) and categorize as natural, human-intentional, human-accidental.

4. **Vulnerability Identification (1 mark):** Map each threat to weaknesses it can exploit. Use CVE databases, configuration audits, penetration tests.

5. **Likelihood & Impact Analysis (1.5 marks):** Score each (Threat, Vulnerability, Asset) triplet on a Likelihood × Impact matrix (1–5 each). Compute Risk Score $R = L \times I$. Optionally compute quantitative $ALE$.

6. **Risk Evaluation & Treatment (1.5 marks):** Compare computed risk to acceptance threshold. Apply one of: **Mitigation, Transfer, Avoidance, Acceptance**. Document residual risk and feed into continuous monitoring.

*(A block-level diagram similar to SECTION 4.1 may be drawn for an additional 0–1 mark bonus.)*

#### Model Solution (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $AV = ₹80,00,000$
- Pre-control: $EF_{pre} = 0.6$, $ARO_{pre} = 0.5$
- Post-control: $EF_{post} = 0.1$, $ARO_{post} = 0.1$
- $ACS = ₹4,00,000$ per year

**Step 1 — Compute $ALE_{pre}$ (2 marks):**

$$SLE_{pre} = AV \times EF_{pre} = 80,00,000 \times 0.6 = 48,00,000$$

$$ALE_{pre} = SLE_{pre} \times ARO_{pre} = 48,00,000 \times 0.5 = 24,00,000$$

**Step 2 — Compute $ALE_{post}$ (2 marks):**

$$SLE_{post} = AV \times EF_{post} = 80,00,000 \times 0.1 = 8,00,000$$

$$ALE_{post} = SLE_{post} \times ARO_{post} = 8,00,000 \times 0.1 = 80,000$$

**Step 3 — Compute Net Savings (1 mark):**

$$NS = ALE_{pre} - ALE_{post} - ACS = 24,00,000 - 80,000 - 4,00,000 = 19,20,000$$

**Step 4 — Compute ROSI (1 mark):**

$$ROSI = \frac{NS}{ACS} = \frac{19,20,000}{4,00,000} = 4.8$$

**Step 5 — Decision (1 mark):**

Since $ROSI = 4.8 > 1$ (i.e., 480% return), and $NS = ₹19,20,000 > 0$, the **WAF deployment is strongly recommended**.

---

### Question B-B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the NIST SP 800-30 Risk Assessment methodology. Compare it briefly with the FAIR (Factor Analysis of Information Risk) framework. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** An organization has three independent information assets with the following risk data:

| Asset | Asset Value (₹) | Exposure Factor | ARO |
|---|---|---|---|
| File Server | 20,00,000 | 0.4 | 0.5 |
| Email Gateway | 5,00,000 | 0.8 | 2.0 |
| Web Portal | 50,00,000 | 0.3 | 1.5 |

Compute:
(i) The $ALE$ for each asset.
(ii) The Total Annual Loss Expectancy ($TALE$) for the organization.
(iii) If a unified SIEM (Security Information & Event Management) system costing ₹12,00,000 per year reduces each $ALE$ by 60%, determine the ROSI. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — NIST SP 800-30 vs FAIR (7 marks)

**NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1 (4 marks):**
- Published by the US National Institute of Standards and Technology.
- Defines a **three-step process**: (i) Prepare for Assessment, (ii) Conduct Assessment, (iii) Maintain Assessment.
- Uses a **Threat–Vulnerability–Impact** triad with categorical likelihood/impact levels (Very Low → Very High, 5-point scale).
- Outputs a **risk determination** and a **risk register**.
- Strong on **qualitative/semi-quantitative** assessment.

**FAIR Framework (3 marks):**
- Factor Analysis of Information Risk — by The Open Group.
- Decomposes risk into: **Threat Event Frequency, Vulnerability, Loss Magnitude (Primary + Secondary)**.
- Fully **quantitative**, producing monetary distributions via Monte-Carlo simulation.
- Strong on **probabilistic and financial** risk modeling.
- Better suited for cyber-insurance and CFO-level reporting.

**Comparison summary:** NIST is process-oriented and compliance-friendly; FAIR is model-oriented and finance-friendly.

#### Model Solution (b) — Three-Asset TALE and SIEM ROSI (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute $SLE$ and $ALE$ per asset (3 marks):**

**File Server:**
$$SLE_{FS} = 20,00,000 \times 0.4 = 8,00,000$$
$$ALE_{FS} = 8,00,000 \times 0.5 = 4,00,000$$

**Email Gateway:**
$$SLE_{EG} = 5,00,000 \times 0.8 = 4,00,000$$
$$ALE_{EG} = 4,00,000 \times 2.0 = 8,00,000$$

**Web Portal:**
$$SLE_{WP} = 50,00,000 \times 0.3 = 15,00,000$$
$$ALE_{WP} = 15,00,000 \times 1.5 = 22,50,000$$

**Step 2 — Compute TALE (1 mark):**

$$TALE = 4,00,000 + 8,00,000 + 22,50,000 = 34,50,000$$

**Step 3 — Compute post-SIEM $ALE_{post}$ (1 mark):**

Each $ALE$ reduced by 60%, so new $ALE$ is 40% of original:

$$ALE_{post}^{TOT} = 0.40 \times 34,50,000 = 13,80,000$$

**Step 4 — Compute ROSI of SIEM (2 marks):**

$$ALE_{pre}^{TOT} - ALE_{post}^{TOT} = 34,50,000 - 13,80,000 = 20,70,000$$

$$ROSI = \frac{20,70,000 - 12,00,000}{12,00,000} = \frac{8,70,000}{12,00,000} \approx 0.725$$

Since $ROSI > 0$, the SIEM is **economically justified** (but borderline; net savings of ₹8,70,000 is positive). At $ROSI < 1$, the project saves money but does not double the investment. The decision rests on qualitative factors (compliance, regulatory mandate) alongside the quantitative result.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on Cyber-Risk Assessment questions:**
>
> 1. **Units Missing (–0.5 to –1 mark):** $SLE$ and $ALE$ are *always* in currency. If you write just a number with no ₹/$, the examiner deducts.
> 2. **Confusing SLE with ALE (–1 mark):** $SLE$ is *per event*; $ALE$ is *per year*. Mixing them up makes the entire chain wrong.
> 3. **Forgetting the ACS term in ROSI (–1 mark):** The denominator is **ACS only**, not $ALE_{post}$. Writing $(ALE_{pre} - ALE_{post})/ALE_{post}$ is a frequent error.
> 4. **ROSI decision rule confusion (–0.5 mark):** Decision is based on $ROSI > 0$ (justified) versus $ROSI > 1$ (strong). State both thresholds.
> 5. **Skipping the context-establishment phase (–1 mark in Part B 14-mark questions):** Examiners expect all six ISO 27005 phases; omitting "Risk Evaluation and Treatment" costs heavily.
> 6. **Confusing Threat with Vulnerability (–0.5 mark):** A *threat* is the agent (hacker, malware); a *vulnerability* is the weakness. They are not interchangeable.
> 7. **Not stating the assumption of independent risks when summing $TALE$ (–0.5 mark):** Always say "assuming risks are independent" before summing.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Cyber-Risk Assessment (Module 4)**

- **Definition:** Cyber-Risk Assessment = systematic identification, analysis, and evaluation of cyber risks to information assets.
- **Core Equation:** $R = T \times V \times I$ (Threat × Vulnerability × Impact).
- **Risk Score Matrix:** $R_{score} = L \times I$ using a 5×5 matrix yielding 1–25.
- **SLE Formula:** $SLE = AV \times EF$, units = currency.
- **ALE Formula:** $ALE = SLE \times ARO$, units = currency per year.
- **TALE Formula:** $TALE = \sum ALE_i$ (assuming independent risks).
- **ROSI Formula:** $ROSI = (ALE_{pre} - ALE_{post} - ACS) / ACS$.
- **Decision Rule for Controls:** Implement if $ROSI > 0$; strongly implement if $ROSI > 1$.
- **Four Risk Treatment Options:** Mitigate, Transfer, Avoid, Accept.
- **Six ISO 27005 Phases:** Context → Asset → Threat → Vulnerability → Impact/Likelihood → Evaluation/Treatment.
- **STRIDE Threat Taxonomy:** Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege.
- **DREAD Scoring Model:** Damage, Reproducibility, Exploitability, Affected Users, Discoverability.
- **Frameworks to know:** NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1, ISO/IEC 27005:2022, FAIR, OCTAVE.
- **Boundary Conditions to remember:** $0 \leq EF \leq 1$, $ARO \geq 0$, $ROSI > 0$ ⇒ justified.
- **Boundary Bands (Matrix):** 1–4 Low, 5–9 Medium, 10–15 High, 16–25 Critical.
- **Real-world link to Secure Communication Protocols:** Risk assessment justifies TLS 1.3 mandates, key-length upgrades, and zero-trust deployments.
- **Always state units and assumptions** in KTU numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Threat Information Sharing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT633 - SECURE COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Threat Information Sharing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Threat Information Sharing (TIS)** is the systematic, structured, and often automated exchange of actionable cyber-threat intelligence (CTI) — including indicators of compromise (IoCs), tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs), threat actors, vulnerabilities, and mitigation guidance — between trusted organizations, sectors, or communities to enhance collective situational awareness and defensive posture.

According to the **NIST Special Publication 800-150 (Guide to Cyber Threat Information Sharing)**, threat information sharing is defined as:

> *"The exchange of knowledge regarding cyber threats, vulnerabilities, indicators, and defensive strategies among organizations to improve their collective ability to detect, prevent, and respond to cyber incidents."*

### 1.1.1 Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standardized)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **CTI** | Cyber Threat Intelligence — evidence-based knowledge about existing or emerging threats. |
| **IoC** | Indicator of Compromise — forensic artifacts indicating a system intrusion. |
| **TTP** | Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures — behavioral pattern of an adversary. |
| **ISAC** | Information Sharing and Analysis Center — sector-specific community hub. |
| **ISAO** | Information Sharing and Analysis Organization — non-sector specific community. |
| **STIX** | Structured Threat Information eXpression — a structured language for CTI. |
| **TAXII** | Trusted Automated eXchange of Indicator Information — a transport protocol. |
| **MISP** | Malware Information Sharing Platform — an open-source sharing platform. |
| **TLP** | Traffic Light Protocol — a classification scheme for sharing sensitivity. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-World Analogy — The Neighborhood Watch System**  
> Imagine you live in a community where a burglary has just occurred at your neighbor's house. Your neighbor shares with you the description of the suspicious person, the time of incident, and a method of entry used. You, in turn, become alert and take precautions. Now scale this idea to thousands of organizations, sharing hacker fingerprints, malicious IP addresses, and attack patterns. That is exactly **Threat Information Sharing** — turning isolated attacks into collective intelligence.

Another powerful analogy is **public health surveillance**:
- **Patients** = infected systems
- **Symptoms** = IoCs
- **Health bulletins** = CTI feeds
- **WHO reports** = ISAC/ISAO reports
- **Quarantine protocols** = blocking rules / patches

Just as a disease outbreak is contained faster when hospitals, labs, and the WHO share data, a **cyber outbreak is contained faster** when SOCs (Security Operations Centers), CERTs, and enterprises share intelligence.

## 1.3 Why It Matters — The Core Drivers

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The **speed of attacker lateral movement** in modern networks often outpaces the **speed of a single organization's detection**. Sharing converts isolated signals into **collective defense**, reducing the *mean time to detect* (MTTD) and *mean time to respond* (MTTR).

### 1.3.1 The Asymmetry Problem

| Defender (Single Org) | Adversary |
|---|---|
| Sees only their logs | Sees millions of victims globally |
| Reactive | Proactive |
| Delayed detection | Instant replication |

**Threat information sharing flattens this asymmetry** by giving each defender a wider, community-augmented visibility.

## 1.4 High-Yield Numerical Benchmarks

| Metric | Industry Standard (2024) | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **MTTD (Mean Time to Detect)** | ~**204 days** (IBM 2023) | Sharing can cut this to **< 24 hours** |
| **MTTR (Mean Time to Respond)** | ~**73 days** | Sharing can reduce by **30–50 %** |
| **Average cost of a data breach** | **$4.45 M** (2023, IBM) | ISAC members save ~**$2.0 M** per incident |
| **STIX adoption** | ~**70 %** of large enterprises | De facto standard |
| **TLP levels** | **4** (Red, Amber, Green, White) | Sensitivity classification |

---

> [!TIP]  
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Always quote the NIST SP 800-150 definition and the names STIX, TAXII, TLP, ISAC, and MISP verbatim — they appear in nearly every board question on this topic.

---

## 1.5 The Information Sharing Lifecycle (Conceptual Overview)

1. **Collect** → From internal logs, SIEM, EDR, honeypots.
2. **Process** → Normalize, deduplicate, enrich.
3. **Analyze** → Correlate, contextualize, score.
4. **Share** → Push or pull via STIX/TAXII, MISP, email lists.
5. **Consume** → Ingest into firewalls, SIEM, SOAR.
6. **Act / Feedback** → Block, hunt, patch, and report back to the community.

This 6-step loop is the **conceptual spine** for every deeper concept in this module.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Threat Information Sharing Model (NIST SP 800-150 Layered View)

The NIST framework decomposes threat information sharing into **four logical layers**:

### Layer 1 — Information Producers
- Internal SOC, CERT, threat intel team
- Open-source feeds (OSINT): VirusTotal, AbuseIPDB, AlienVault OTX
- Commercial feeds: Mandiant, Recorded Future, CrowdStrike
- Government: NCIIPC (India), CISA (US), ENISA (EU), CERT-In (India)

### Layer 2 — Information Repositories / Platforms
- **MISP** (open-source, decentralized)
- **Anomali ThreatStream**
- **ThreatConnect**
- **OpenCTI** (graph-based, Filigran)
- **EclecticIQ**

### Layer 3 — Transport / Sharing Protocols
- **TAXII** (preferred, REST/JSON based)
- **STIX over HTTPS**
- **RID (Real-time Inter-network Defense)** — older IETF protocol
- **IoCIO** formats
- Email digests (legacy)

### Layer 4 — Information Consumers
- SIEM (Splunk, QRadar, Elastic, ArcSight)
- EDR/XDR (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Defender)
- Firewalls / IDS / IPS (Palo Alto, Snort, Suricata)
- SOAR platforms (Cortex XSOAR, Tines)

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" of Each Layer

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why this layered model?**  
> Decoupling producers, repositories, transports, and consumers allows **interoperability** and **technology-agnostic** exchange. An MISP user can push STIX to a Splunk SIEM via TAXII without ever knowing each other's internal implementation.

- **How producers work:** Curate raw telemetry → enrich with threat actor profiling → publish as STIX bundles.
- **How repositories work:** Act as brokers — store, deduplicate, and allow re-distribution.
- **How transports work:** Encapsulate STIX JSON inside TAXII envelopes and deliver over HTTPS with mutual TLS.
- **How consumers work:** Auto-ingest, parse, and apply detection rules (YARA, Sigma, Snort).

## 2.3 Trust, Privacy, and Legal Considerations

| Concern | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **Source anonymity** | Pseudonymous MISP instances, Tor, anonymized reporting |
| **Data sensitivity** | Traffic Light Protocol (TLP) labels |
| **Legal liability** | CIRCIA (US), DPDP Act 2023 (India) — safe-harbor clauses |
| **Anti-trust** | Cybersecurity Information Sharing Act (CISA 2015) — protects shared data as "voluntary" |
| **Quality assurance** | STIX confidence score (0–100) and revocation flags |
| **Repudiation** | Digital signatures, PKI-based TAXII channels |

## 2.4 The Traffic Light Protocol (TLP)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> TLP is the **de facto sensitivity classification** for CTI. It is mandatory in KTU answers.

| Level | Color | Meaning | Distribution Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TLP:RED** | Red | For named recipients only | Eyes-only, no further sharing |
| **TLP:AMBER+STRICT** | Amber | Limited to organization and its clients | Members + clients only |
| **TLP:AMBER** | Amber | Organization's members only | Members of own org |
| **TLP:GREEN** | Green | Community-wide | Community unrestricted |
| **TLP:CLEAR** | White (formerly) | Public | Unrestricted public |

## 2.5 STIX 2.1 Object Model (Architecture Deep-Dive)

STIX (Structured Threat Information eXpression) is a **JSON-based graph language** defined by OASIS. Its 18 domain objects (SDOs) and 2 relationship objects (SROs) form a knowledge graph.

### Core STIX Domain Objects (KTU 2024 — High-Yield)

| SDO | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Indicator** | Pattern to detect malicious activity | IP `198.51.100.42` |
| **Malware** | Malicious software instance | Emotet |
| **Threat-Actor** | Adversary identity | APT29 (Cozy Bear) |
| **Campaign** | Grouped attacks | Operation Ghostwriter |
| **Intrusion-Set** | Collection of TTPs | Lazarus Group |
| **Attack-Pattern** | TTP description (mapped to MITRE ATT&CK) | T1059 — PowerShell |
| **Vulnerability** | CVE linkage | CVE-2024-3094 |
| **Identity** | Real-world organization | A victim's name |
| **Observed-Data** | Raw sightings | DNS query log |
| **Course-Of-Action** | Remediation | Block IP, patch CVE |
| **Tool** | Legitimate software misused | Mimikatz |
| **Infrastructure** | C2 server / domain | `badactor.com` |
| **Location** | Geographic target | India |
| **Opinion** | Analyst's subjective assessment | Confidence = 80 |
| **Note** | Free-text annotation | "false positive suspected" |
| **Report** | Collection of SDOs | Quarterly intel report |
| **Grouping** | Why SDOs are related | "Ransomware family X" |
| **Language-Content** | Localized text | Hindi translation |

## 2.6 TAXII 2.1 — Transport Protocol

> [!NOTE]  
> **TAXII (Trusted Automated eXchange of Indicator Information)** is **NOT a STIX parser** — it is a **transport protocol** that delivers STIX bundles over HTTPS.

### TAXII Services (Conceptual)

1. **Collection** — A logical set of CTI objects (read/write).
2. **Channel** — Pub/Sub (legacy 1.x); deprecated in 2.1.
3. **API Root** — Base URL of a TAXII server.
4. **Discovery** — Endpoint to list available collections.
5. **Poll** — Client requests new objects on a schedule.
6. **Push** — Client sends objects to a server.

### TAXII Message Exchange (Logical Flow)

1. Client authenticates to **API Root** via **mutual TLS** or **OAuth2**.
2. Client calls **Discovery** → gets list of Collections.
3. Client performs **Poll** to fetch a STIX bundle (`application/stix+json`).
4. Client may perform **Push** to publish its own bundle.
5. Server responds with HTTP 200 + body, or **HTTP 404** if empty (this is normal — not an error).

## 2.7 MITRE ATT&CK Alignment

| MITRE Domain | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Enterprise** | Windows/Linux/macOS/Cloud |
